It is measured in kilometers (km) and is a primary parameter in the JONSWAP spectrum for fetch-limited seas. > **(b) Significant Wave Height ($H_s$ or $H_{1/3}$):** It is defined as the mean height of the highest one-third of waves in a wave record. It is related to the zeroth spectral moment as $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4\sigma$ for narrow-band spectra. It is the most commonly reported wave height in ship routing and weather charts. > **(c) Fully Developed Sea:** A theoretical sea state in which the wind has blown for an indefinitely long time over an indefinitely large fetch. The sea has reached equilibrium — energy input from wind is balanced by dissipation from whitecapping. The Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) spectrum describes this condition. *(Award: 1 mark for each correct definition with units/relevance.)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **State and explain the Phillips and Miles mechanisms of wave generation by wind.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): > **Phillips' Mechanism (1957):** Treats wind as a **random field of pressure fluctuations** (turbulent eddies). Each eddy produces a pressure pulse on the water surface. If the pulse frequency matches the natural frequency of a wave component, **resonance** occurs, and the wave amplitude grows **linearly with time**. Dominant in the **initial stages** of wave growth. > **Miles' Mechanism (1957):** An existing wave deflects the wind streamlines, creating **asymmetric pressure** on the wave (higher pressure on the leeward face, lower on the windward face). This produces **net positive work** on the wave, causing **exponential growth** of amplitude with time. Dominant for **mature waves**. > **Combined Picture:** Phillips initiates the wave; Miles amplifies it. Together they explain the observed growth rates of wind-generated waves. *(Award: 1.5 marks for Phillips, 1.5 marks for Miles.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts, each carrying **7 marks**. The total for a question is 14 marks. Internal choice is provided between alternative full questions. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the deep-water dispersion relation $C^2 = g\lambda / 2\pi$ for a linear (Airy) progressive wave, starting from the velocity potential and applying both free-surface boundary conditions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A ship is operating in a sea where the wind speed at 10 m elevation is $U_{10} = 20$ m/s. Assuming a fully developed sea, calculate: - (i) The significant wave height $H_s$ - (ii) The peak period $T_p$ - (iii) The maximum expected individual wave height $H_{max}$ - (iv) The phase speed $C$ of the peak-frequency wave - (v) The peak wavelength $\lambda_p$ #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Refer to the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1** above. Award marks as follows: - 1 mark: Writing the velocity potential form - 1 mark: Stating the linearized DFSBC - 1 mark: Evaluating the LHS for $\eta$ and $\partial \phi/\partial t$ - 1 mark: Stating the KFSBC - 1 mark: Evaluating the LHS and RHS of KFSBC - 1 mark: Equating and simplifying to the dispersion relation - 1 mark: Deep-water specialization and final boxed answer **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $U_{10} = 20$ m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Calculate $H_s$ using the PM spectrum relation:** $$ H_s = \frac{0.0213 \, U_{10}^2}{g} = \frac{0.0213 \times (20)^2}{9.81} $$ $$ H_s = \frac{0.0213 \times 400}{9.81} = \frac{8.52}{9.81} \approx 0.868 \text{ m} $$ Wait, let me check the formula. The correct PM formula is: $$ H_s = 0.0213 \cdot \frac{U_{10}^2}{g} $$ With $U_{10} = 20$ m/s: $$ H_s = 0.0213 \times \frac{400}{9.81} = 0.0213 \times 40.77 = 0.868 \text{ m} $$ > [!WARNING] > **EXAMINER'S PITFALL:** A common error is using $H_s = 0.0213 \cdot U_{10}^2$ (forgetting to divide by $g$). The correct dimensionally consistent formula is $H_s = 0.0213 \cdot U_{10}^2 / g$. Check units: $U^2/g$ has units of (m²/s²)/(m/s²) = m. ✓ **Step 2: Calculate peak period $T_p$:** $$ T_p = \frac{0.728 \, U_{10}}{g^{0.5}} = \frac{0.728 \times 20}{\sqrt{9.81}} $$ $$ T_p = \frac{14.56}{3.132} \approx 4.648 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3: Calculate $H_{max}$:** $$ H_{max} \approx 1.86 \, H_s = 1.86 \times 0.868 \approx 1.614 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: Calculate peak wavelength $\lambda_p$:** Using the deep-water dispersion relation: $\lambda_p = g T_p^2 / (2\pi)$ $$ \lambda_p = \frac{9.81 \times (4.648)^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 21.60}{6.283} $$ $$ \lambda_p = \frac{211.95}{6.283} \approx 33.74 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5: Calculate phase speed $C_p$:** $$ C_p = \frac{\lambda_p}{T_p} = \frac{33.74}{4.648} \approx 7.26 \text{ m/s} $$ Or equivalently, $C_p = gT_p/(2\pi) = 9.81 \times 4.648 / 6.283 \approx 7.26$ m/s ✓ **Summary of Results:** | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Significant wave height | $H_s$ | 0.868 m | | Peak period | $T_p$ | 4.648 s | | Maximum individual wave height | $H_{max}$ | 1.614 m | | Peak wavelength | $\lambda_p$ | 33.74 m | | Phase speed (peak) | $C_p$ | 7.26 m/s | *(Award: 1 mark for each correct sub-step with proper unit and significant figures.)* --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a clear sketch, explain the **Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) spectrum** for a fully developed sea. State the key formula, the physical meaning of each parameter, and the conditions under which it applies. Discuss its limitations. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **JONSWAP spectrum** and compare it with the PM spectrum. Explain the role of the **peak enhancement factor $\gamma$** and show how $\gamma = 1$ reduces JONSWAP to PM. For a fetch of $F = 100$ km and wind speed $U_{10} = 15$ m/s, estimate the significant wave height $H_s$ using the JONSWAP empirical relations. #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — PM Spectrum (7 marks):** > **Definition:** The Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) spectrum (1964) is a fully developed sea spectrum derived from North Atlantic wave data, expressed as a function of frequency $\omega$ (rad/s) and wind speed $U_{10}$. **Key Formula:** $$ S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{\alpha g^2}{\omega^5} \exp\left[-\beta\left(\frac{g}{U_{10}\omega}\right)^4\right] $$ with $\alpha = 0.0081$ and $\beta = 0.74$. **Physical meaning of parameters:** - $g$ = gravitational acceleration (m/s²) - $U_{10}$ = wind speed at 10 m elevation (m/s) - $\omega$ = angular wave frequency (rad/s) - $\alpha$ = Phillips' constant (dimensionless) - The $\omega^{-5}$ tail is the equilibrium range (energy cascade to high frequencies, balanced by whitecapping) - The exponential term provides a low-frequency cutoff (no wave can be slower than the wind) **Conditions for application:** - Fully developed sea (long fetch, long duration) - Open ocean (deep water) - Stationary, homogeneous wind field **Limitations:** - Does not account for fetch or duration (only valid for the asymptotic limit) - Underestimates peak in growing seas - Not valid in coastal or shallow water - Does not represent directional spreading (only 1D frequency spectrum) **Sketch (Mermaid):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["frequency omega rad per s"] --> B["PM SPECTRUM"] B --> C["Peak at omega_p"] B --> D["omega to the minus 5 tail"] C --> E["omega_p = 0.84 g divided by U10"] E --> F["Hs = 0.0213 U10 squared by g"] E --> G["Tp = 0.728 U10 by g to the 0.5"] ``` **Part (b) — JONSWAP Comparison (7 marks):** > **Background:** JONSWAP (Joint North Sea Wave Project, 1973) was a major field measurement campaign in the North Sea. The resulting spectrum describes **growing seas** under fetch-limited conditions. **JONSWAP Formula:** $$ S_{J}(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega) \cdot \gamma^{\exp\left[-\frac{(\omega - \omega_p)^2}{2 \sigma^2 \omega_p^2}\right]} $$ where: - $S_{PM}(\omega)$ = the underlying Pierson-Moskowitz spectrum - $\gamma$ = **peak enhancement factor** (typically 3.3, with mean value from JONSWAP data) - $\sigma$ = spectral width parameter - $\sigma = 0.07$ for $\omega \leq \omega_p$ - $\sigma = 0.09$ for $\omega > \omega_p$ - $\omega_p$ = peak frequency (now fetch-dependent) **Reduction to PM:** Setting $\gamma = 1$ makes the exponential term identically 1, so $S_J = S_{PM}$. ✓ **Comparison Table (build in answer):** | Feature | PM | JONSWAP | |:---|:---|:---| | Sea type | Fully developed | Growing | | Fetch | Infinite | Finite (parameter) | | Peak shape | Smooth | Sharp ($\gamma = 3.3$) | | Empirical coefficients | $\alpha = 0.0081$, $\beta = 0.74$ | Adds $\gamma = 3.3$, $\sigma$ | | Use | Open ocean | Coastal, North Sea | **JONSWAP Empirical Relations (Hasselmann et al., 1973):** $$ \frac{g H_s}{U_{10}^2} = 0.0016 \left(\frac{g F}{U_{10}^2}\right)^{0.5} $$ $$ \frac{g T_p}{U_{10}} = 0.286 \left(\frac{g F}{U_{10}^2}\right)^{0.33} $$ **Numerical Calculation:** **Given:** $F = 100$ km $= 10^5$ m, $U_{10} = 15$ m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Compute the dimensionless fetch:** $$ \tilde{F} = \frac{gF}{U_{10}^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 10^5}{(15)^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 10^5}{225} = 4360 $$ **Step 2: Compute $H_s$:** $$ H_s = 0.0016 \cdot \frac{U_{10}^2}{g} \cdot \tilde{F}^{0.5} $$ $$ H_s = 0.0016 \times \frac{225}{9.81} \times (4360)^{0.5} $$ $$ H_s = 0.0016 \times 22.94 \times 66.03 $$ $$ H_s = 0.0367 \times 66.03 \approx 2.42 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3: (Optional, for completeness) Compute $T_p$:** $$ T_p = 0.286 \cdot \frac{U_{10}}{g} \cdot \tilde{F}^{0.33} $$ $$ T_p = 0.286 \times \frac{15}{9.81} \times (4360)^{0.33} $$ $$ T_p = 0.286 \times 1.529 \times 13.92 \approx 6.09 \text{ s} $$ **Final Answer for Part (b): $H_s \approx 2.42$ m**, $T_p \approx 6.09$ s > [!WARNING] > **EXAMINER'S PITFALL — JONSWAP Numerical Errors:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert km to m** when computing $gF/U_{10}^2$. Always use SI units (m, s, m/s). > 2. **Confusing the 0.5 exponent with 0.33** — the 0.5 is for $H_s$, the 0.33 is for $T_p$. > 3. **Mixing up $\alpha = 0.0081$ (PM constant) with $0.0016$ (JONSWAP coefficient)** — they are different! > 4. **Not stating assumptions** — always state "assuming deep water, fetch-limited conditions, neutral stability." --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 MARK-LOSING MISTAKES IN THIS TOPIC:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $g$ in the denominator** of $H_s$ formulas. Always check dimensional consistency: $U^2/g$ has units of meters. > > 2. **Confusing $H_s = 4\sigma$ with $H_s = 4\sigma^2$** — the standard deviation $\sigma$ is the square root of variance $m_0$, so $H_s = 4\sigma = 4\sqrt{m_0}$. > > 3. **Mixing up $T_z$ (zero-crossing period) and $T_p$ (peak period).** Approximate relation: $T_z \approx 0.777 T_p$ or $T_p \approx 1.286 T_z$ for PM/JONSWAP spectra. > > 4. **Treating irregular sea like regular waves** — for ship dynamics, the response is a random process; use spectral methods (RAO × spectrum), not single-wave deterministic analysis. > > 5. **Forgetting units in the JONSWAP dimensionless fetch** — $F$ must be in **meters**, not km. Always convert. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Wind Generated Waves** > > **A. Definitions (must know verbatim):** > - Wind-generated waves = irregular, short-crested, 3D waves under active wind influence > - Fetch = unobstructed distance of wind action > - Duration = time of wind action > - $H_s = H_{1/3} = 4\sigma$ = mean of highest 1/3 waves > - Fully developed sea = asymptotic equilibrium between wind input and dissipation > > **B. Two Generation Mechanisms:** > - **Phillips (1957):** Random pressure fluctuations → **linear** growth, $\partial A / \partial t = $ const. > - **Miles (1957):** Shear-flow instability → **exponential** growth, $A = A_0 e^{\beta t}$ > - Phillips initiates; Miles amplifies > > **C. Key Formulas (must memorize):** > - Deep-water dispersion: $C_0^2 = g\lambda/(2\pi)$ or $C_0 = gT/(2\pi)$ > - Shallow-water: $C_s = \sqrt{gd}$ > - PM spectrum: $H_s = 0.0213 U_{10}^2 / g$, $T_p = 0.728 U_{10} / g^{0.5}$ > - JONSWAP: $H_s = 0.0016 (U_{10}^2/g)(gF/U_{10}^2)^{0.5}$, $T_p = 0.286(U_{10}/g)(gF/U_{10}^2)^{0.33}$ > - Steepness limit: $S = H/\lambda \leq 1/7$ > > **D. Spectral Forms:** > - **PM (1964):** Fully developed, smooth peak, $\omega^{-5}$ tail > - **JONSWAP (1973):** Growing seas, $\gamma = 3.3$ peak enhancement > - **Bretschneider (1958):** Two-parameter, older, used in IACS rules > - **TMA:** Finite depth modification of JONSWAP > > **E. Key Statistics (Longuet-Higgins 1952, Rayleigh distribution):** > - $H_{1/3} = 4.004 \sqrt{m_0}$ > - $H_{1/10} = 5.09 \sqrt{m_0}$ > - $H_{avg} = 2.51 \sqrt{m_0}$ > - $H_{max} \approx 1.86 H_s$ (in ~1000 wave record) > - $H_{1/100} \approx 6.67 \sqrt{m_0}$ > > **F. Engineering Relevance:** > - Seakeeping analysis (RAO × wave spectrum → response spectrum) > - Added resistance in waves → fuel and routing > - Mooring design (slow-drift forces) > - Structural design (slamming, fatigue) > - Operability windows for offshore vessels > > **G. Common KTU Exam Traps:** > - Always convert km → m for fetch > - Always state "fully developed" or "fetch-limited" before applying formulas > - $H_{1/3}$ is a statistical average, not a deterministic value > - The wave period $T_p$ in PM/JONSWAP is the **peak spectral period**, not zero-crossing > - The dispersion relation is for **linear waves** (Airy theory); nonlinear effects (Stokes, cnoidal) are for shallow/intermediate depths

topics.title: Sinusoidal and Trochoidal Wave Theories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sinusoidal and Trochoidal Wave Theories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Ocean Wave Theories** are mathematically formulated models that describe the kinematic (motion) and dynamic (pressure, forces) behaviour of water waves propagating on the surface of a fluid medium. They provide closed-form solutions to the governing Laplace equation for velocity potential, subject to the free-surface and bottom boundary conditions.

Two foundational theories dominate the **KTU Ship Dynamics Module 1** syllabus:

1. **Sinusoidal (Airy) Wave Theory** — Also called *Linear Wave Theory* or *Small-Amplitude Wave Theory*. Proposed by George Biddell Airy (1845). Assumes wave steepness $H/\lambda \le 1/50$.
2. **Trochoidal (Gerstner) Wave Theory** — Proposed by Franz Joseph von Gerstner (1802). A *Finite-Amplitude* theory valid for steeper waves up to $H/\lambda \approx 1/10$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ship Dynamics Context:** Wave theories form the **forcing function** input for all seakeeping analyses — heave, pitch, roll responses, added resistance, and slamming loads are all computed against wave kinematics predicted by these theories.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **crowd doing "the wave"** in a stadium:

- **Sinusoidal Theory** is like watching the crowd from very far away. Each person only bobs up and down a tiny bit, and the wave looks like a smooth, perfectly symmetric **sine curve** drawn on paper. Mathematically simple, but only realistic for tiny ripples.
- **Trochoidal Theory** is like watching a powerful ocean swell up close. The crests are **sharp and pointy**, the troughs are **flat and wide**, and water particles are dragged forward with the wave (not just bobbing vertically). It's the shape of a **rolling coin travelling along a ceiling** — a geometric curve called a *trochoid*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two theories?** Sinusoidal theory is mathematically elegant and sufficient for design-grade calculations on most merchant ships (where wave slopes are small). Trochoidal theory becomes necessary for **steep storm waves, fishing vessels, and high-speed craft** where the linear assumption breaks down.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard naval-architecture metrics must be memorised:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (deep ocean) or $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (fresh)
- **Wave Height** $H$ — vertical distance from trough to crest (m)
- **Wave Length** $\lambda$ — horizontal distance between successive crests (m)
- **Wave Period** $T$ — time for one crest to pass a fixed point (s)
- **Wave Amplitude** $a = H/2$
- **Water Depth** $d$ — still-water depth (m)
- **Wave Number** $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ (rad/m)
- **Angular Frequency** $\omega = 2\pi/T$ (rad/s)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Sinusoidal (Airy) Wave Theory — Logical Breakdown

### Governing Assumptions
1. Fluid is **inviscid** (no viscosity) and **incompressible**.
2. Flow is **irrotational** → velocity potential $\phi$ exists.
3. Wave amplitude is **infinitely small**: $H/\lambda \to 0$.
4. Flow is **two-dimensional** (long-crested waves, no variation in $y$).
5. **Constant depth** $d$; horizontal seabed.
6. Surface tension is neglected; only gravity acts as the restoring force.

### Governing Equation

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

This is the **Laplace equation**, subject to:

- **Bottom BC:** $\partial\phi/\partial z = 0$ at $z = -d$
- **Kinematic FSBC:** $\partial\eta/\partial t = \partial\phi/\partial z$ at $z = \eta$
- **Dynamic FSBC:** $\partial\phi/\partial t + g\eta = 0$ at $z = \eta$ (linearised)

### Solution Form

$$\phi(x,z,t) = \frac{a g}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

## 2.2 Trochoidal (Gerstner) Wave Theory — Logical Breakdown

### Geometric Foundation
The free surface is described as a **trochoid** — the locus of a point on a circle of radius $r$ rolling along a straight ceiling above the still water level.

### Parametric Equations of Particle Motion

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= x_0 + r \, e^{kz} \cdot \sin(kx_0 - \omega t) \\
z &= z_0 - r \, e^{kz} \cdot \cos(kx_0 - \omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

At the free surface, $r = a = H/2$, giving the **trochoidal profile** that has sharper crests and flatter troughs than a sine wave.

### Key Distinguishing Feature
> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Gerstner theory, water particles travel in closed CIRCLES of exponentially decreasing radius** (radius $= a \cdot e^{kz}$). Critically, the flow is **rotational** — violating the Airy irrotational assumption — but it captures the **forward mass transport** observed in real waves.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference (KTU Exam Ready)

| **Parameter** | **Sinusoidal (Airy) Theory** | **Trochoidal (Gerstner) Theory** |
|---|---|---|
| Surface Profile | $\eta = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | $\eta = -a \cos(kx_0 - \omega t)$ (trochoidal) |
| Dispersion Relation | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ | Same as Airy |
| Phase Velocity $c$ | $c = \dfrac{\omega}{k} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{k}\tanh(kd)}$ | $c = \dfrac{g}{\omega} = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$ |
| Deep-water $c$ ($d/\lambda > 0.5$) | $c_0 = \sqrt{g\lambda/(2\pi)}$ | $c_0 = gT/(2\pi)$ |
| Shallow-water $c$ ($d/\lambda < 0.05$) | $c_s = \sqrt{gd}$ | $c_s = \sqrt{gd}$ |
| Group Velocity $c_g$ | $c_g = \dfrac{c}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2kd}{\sinh(2kd)}\right)$ | $c_g = c/2$ (deep water) |
| Particle Path | Closed ellipses (deep: circles) | **Closed circles** (all depths) |
| Horizontal Velocity $u$ | $u = ak\sigma \cdot \cos(kx-\omega t)$ | From parametric $x$-equation |
| Vertical Velocity $w$ | $w = ak\sigma \cdot \sin(kx-\omega t)$ | From parametric $z$-equation |
| Dynamic Pressure $p_d$ | $p_d = \rho g a \sigma \cos(kx-\omega t)$ | Linearised to match Airy |
| Validity Limit | $H/\lambda \le 1/50$ | $H/\lambda \le 1/10$ |
| Crests | Symmetric (rounded) | **Sharper, peaked** |
| Troughs | Symmetric (rounded) | **Flatter, wider** |
| Flow Type | Irrotational | **Rotational** |
| Mass Transport | None (Stokes drift is 2nd order) | **First-order forward drift** |

*Where $\sigma = \cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]/\cosh(kd)$ and $a = H/2$.*

### Table 2.2 — Dimensionless Depth Regimes (Critical for KTU Problems)

| **Regime** | **Condition** | **Simplification** |
|---|---|---|
| Deep water | $d/\lambda \ge 0.5$ | $\tanh(kd) \to 1$ |
| Transitional | $0.05 < d/\lambda < 0.5$ | Full hyperbolic terms retained |
| Shallow water | $d/\lambda \le 0.05$ | $\tanh(kd) \to kd$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sinusoidal Theory** powers **ship motion prediction codes** (e.g., strip theory in HYDROSTAR, WAMIT panel codes linearised).
- **Trochoidal Theory** informs **whale-back hull form design**, **slamming load estimates**, and **breaking-wave criteria** in extreme sea states.
- Both are the bedrock inputs for **RAO (Response Amplitude Operator)** curves plotted in KTU ship dynamics lab.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Wave Celerity (Phase Velocity) — Airy Theory

**Step 1 — Governing PDE and Boundary Conditions**

For incompressible, irrotational 2D flow:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Bottom Boundary Condition**

No flow through seabed at $z = -d$:

$$\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\right|_{z=-d} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Assume Separable Solution**

$$\phi(x,z,t) = f(z) \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Substituting into Laplace equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = -k^2 f(z) \sin(kx - \omega t) \\
&\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = f''(z) \sin(kx - \omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

Adding and equating to zero:

$$f''(z) - k^2 f(z) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE**

General solution:

$$f(z) = A \cosh(kz) + B \sinh(kz)$$

Applying bottom BC: $f'(-d) = Ak\sinh(-kd) + Bk\cosh(-kd) = 0$

$$\Rightarrow B = A \tanh(kd)$$

Therefore:

$$f(z) = A\!\left[\cosh(kz) + \tanh(kd)\sinh(kz)\right]$$

Using the identity $\cosh(A+B) = \cosh A \cosh B + \sinh A \sinh B$:

$$f(z) = A \cdot \frac{\cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\cosh(kd)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Linearised Free-Surface Boundary Conditions**

Combined linearised FSBC:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} &= -\omega^2 f(z) \sin(kx - \omega t) \\
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} &= f'(z) \sin(kx - \omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

At $z = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
-\omega^2 f(0) + g f'(0) &= 0 \\
f(0) &= A \cdot \cosh(kd)/\cosh(kd) = A \\
f'(0) &= Ak \cdot \sinh(kd)/\cosh(kd) = Ak\tanh(kd)
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$-\omega^2 A + g \cdot Ak \tanh(kd) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \boxed{\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)}$$

This is the **Dispersion Relation**. Squareroot and using $c = \omega/k$:

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}\tanh(kd)} = \sqrt{\frac{g\lambda}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\right)}}$$

## 3.2 Deep-Water and Shallow-Water Limits

**Deep-water limit** — $kd \to \infty$, so $\tanh(kd) \to 1$:

$$c_0 = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{g\lambda}{2\pi}} = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$$

**Shallow-water limit** — $kd \to 0$, so $\tanh(kd) \to kd$:

$$c_s = \sqrt{g \cdot kd/k} = \sqrt{gd}$$

## 3.3 Particle Velocity Components (Airy Theory)

$$\begin{aligned}
u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} &= \frac{a g k}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \omega t) \\
w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} &= \frac{a g k}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\sinh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Substitution using dispersion relation:** $agk/\omega = a\omega \cdot \cosh(kd)/(k\sinh(kd) + ...) $ — *cleanest form uses amplitude $a$*:

$$u = a\omega \cdot \frac{\cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx-\omega t)$$

$$w = a\omega \cdot \frac{\sinh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx-\omega t)$$

## 3.4 Particle Trajectory in Deep Water

At $z = 0$, integrating velocities over time in deep water ($kd \to \infty$, $\sinh(kd) \approx \cosh(kd)$):

$$\xi = -\frac{u}{\omega} \cdot ... = a e^{kz} \cdot \sin(kx-\omega t)$$

The trajectory is a **circle of radius $a e^{kz}$** — exactly matching Gerstner's geometric premise!

## 3.5 Derivation of Trochoidal Surface Profile

Consider a circle of radius $r$ rolling along a line at height $a$ above still water level. The position of a point on the rim after the circle has rolled through angle $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= r\theta + R\sin\theta \quad \text{(horizontal position of point)} \\
y &= R - R\cos\theta \quad \text{(vertical position)}
\end{aligned}$$

When the rolling circle's perimeter point traces the wave surface, the resulting curve is a **trochoid**.

Setting $R = a$ (wave amplitude) and identifying $\theta = kx - \omega t$ (phase angle), the surface displacement becomes:

$$\eta(x,t) = a - a\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

i.e., a trochoid where the **peak crest height is $2a$** and the **trough depth is $0$** (referenced from SWL at $y = a$).

**Crest sharpness ratio** (compared to sinusoid):
- Sinusoidal crest: symmetric, height $a$
- Trochoidal crest: **peaked**, maximum height $2a$
- This is why real ocean waves look "pointy on top, flat on bottom" — exactly what Gerstner theory captures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Wave Theory Selection Logic (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Wave Steepness H over lambda] --> B{H over lambda less than 1 over 50}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Sinusoidal Airy Theory]
    B -- No --> D{H over lambda less than 1 over 10}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Trochoidal Gerstner Theory]
    D -- No --> F[Use Stokes 2nd or 3rd Order]
    C --> G{Large ship design or linear response}
    E --> H[Steep storm waves or slamming]
    G --> I[Compute RAO via strip theory]
    H --> J[Whale back hull or breaking wave check]
    F --> K[High speed craft or extreme seas]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Comparison Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        A1[Wave Height H] --> SYS
        A2[Wave Length lambda] --> SYS
        A3[Water Depth d] --> SYS
        A4[Time t] --> SYS
    end
    SYS{KTU Wave Theory Engine}
    SYS -- Sinusoidal Path --> OUT1[Eta, u, w, p in closed form]
    SYS -- Trochoidal Path --> OUT2[Parametric x z profile]
    OUT1 --> APP[Application Layer]
    OUT2 --> APP
    APP --> USE1[Ship Motion RAO]
    APP --> USE2[Slamming Load]
    APP --> USE3[Added Resistance]
```

## 4.3 Particle Orbit Diagram (Schematic Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SURF[Free Surface z equals 0] --> D1[Deep Water d over lambda greater than 0.5]
    SURF --> D2[Shallow Water d over lambda less than 0.05]
    D1 --> O1[Orbit: Perfect Circle]
    D1 --> R1[Radius decreases exponentially with depth]
    D2 --> O2[Orbit: Flattened Ellipse]
    D2 --> R2[Major axis horizontal, decay with depth]
    O1 --> AIRY[Sinusoidal: Closed Orbits, Irrotational]
    O2 --> AIRY
    O1 --> GER[Trochoidal: Also Circular but with Forward Drift]
    O2 --> GER
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Sinusoidal Wave Theory. State its assumptions and limitations. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Sinusoidal (Airy) Wave Theory is a linear, small-amplitude wave theory that describes ocean surface waves using trigonometric (sine/cosine) functions. The free surface profile is given by:

$$\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

**Key Assumptions:**

- Inviscid, incompressible fluid
- Irrotational flow (velocity potential $\phi$ exists)
- Small wave steepness: $H/\lambda \le 1/50$
- Two-dimensional long-crested waves
- Constant depth; horizontal seabed

**Limitations:** Inaccurate for steep storm waves; fails to predict mass transport; symmetric profile doesn't match real ocean wave shapes.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Sinusoidal and Trochoidal wave theories in any four aspects. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Sinusoidal (Airy)** | **Trochoidal (Gerstner)** |
|---|---|---|
| Wave steepness limit | $H/\lambda \le 1/50$ | $H/\lambda \le 1/10$ |
| Profile shape | Symmetric sinusoidal | Asymmetric, sharp crests, flat troughs |
| Flow type | Irrotational | Rotational |
| Particle path | Elliptical orbits | **Circular** orbits |
| Mass transport | Neglected at 1st order | Forward drift included |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sinusoidal Theory Numerical Problem `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

A ship operates in sea conditions where the wave height $H = 3$ m and wavelength $\lambda = 80$ m, in water depth $d = 25$ m.

**(a)** Determine the wave period, phase velocity, and group velocity. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the maximum horizontal and vertical particle velocities at the still water level and at $z = -10$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Given: $H = 3$ m, $\lambda = 80$ m, $d = 25$ m.

Check regime: $d/\lambda = 25/80 = 0.3125$ → **transitional water** (must use full hyperbolic form).

**Step 1 — Wave Number:**
$$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{80} = 0.0785 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Step 2 — Dispersion relation for wave period:**
$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$$

$$kd = 0.0785 \times 25 = 1.9635 \text{ rad}$$

$$\tanh(1.9635) = 0.9597$$

$$\omega^2 = 9.81 \times 0.0785 \times 0.9597 = 0.7391 \text{ (rad/s)}^2$$

$$\omega = 0.8597 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = 7.31 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 3 — Phase velocity:**
$$c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{0.8597}{0.0785} = \boxed{10.95 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Group velocity:**
$$c_g = \frac{c}{2}\left[1 + \frac{2kd}{\sinh(2kd)}\right]$$

$$\sinh(2kd) = \sinh(3.927) = 26.78$$

$$c_g = \frac{10.95}{2}\left[1 + \frac{2(1.9635)}{26.78}\right] = 5.475 \times [1 + 0.1466]$$

$$\boxed{c_g = 6.28 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part (b):**

Amplitude $a = H/2 = 1.5$ m.

Maximum particle velocities occur when $\cos(kx - \omega t) = 1$ for $u$ and $\sin(kx - \omega t) = 1$ for $w$:

$$u_{max} = a\omega \cdot \frac{\cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\sinh(kd)}$$

$$w_{max} = a\omega \cdot \frac{\sinh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\sinh(kd)}$$

**At $z = 0$ (still water level):**

$k(z+d) = 0.0785 \times 25 = 1.9635$

$\cosh(1.9635) = 3.695$, $\sinh(1.9635) = 3.558$, $\sinh(kd) = \sinh(1.9635) = 3.558$

$$u_{max,0} = 1.5 \times 0.8597 \times \frac{3.695}{3.558} = \boxed{1.34 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$w_{max,0} = 1.5 \times 0.8597 \times \frac{3.558}{3.558} = \boxed{1.29 \text{ m/s}}$$

**At $z = -10$ m:**

$k(z+d) = 0.0785 \times 15 = 1.178$

$\cosh(1.178) = 1.842$, $\sinh(1.178) = 1.367$

$$u_{max,-10} = 1.5 \times 0.8597 \times \frac{1.842}{3.558} = \boxed{0.667 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$w_{max,-10} = 1.5 \times 0.8597 \times \frac{1.367}{3.558} = \boxed{0.495 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Trochoidal Theory Derivation `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the parametric equations of particle motion for Gerstner's trochoidal wave theory and show that the surface profile is a trochoid. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A trochoidal wave has height $H = 4$ m, length $\lambda = 60$ m in deep water. Calculate the wave celerity, period, and the radius of the circular orbit at $z = -5$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Assume each water particle moves in a circle of radius $r$ about a mean position $(x_0, z_0)$. As the wave passes, the centre also advances with phase speed $c$.

**Parametric equations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= x_0 + r \, e^{kz} \sin(kx_0 - \omega t) \\
z &= z_0 - r \, e^{kz} \cos(kx_0 - \omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

where the exponential $e^{kz}$ accounts for radius decrease with depth.

**At the free surface** ($z \approx 0$), $r = a = H/2$, and the surface profile obtained by eliminating $x_0$ is a **trochoid** — the curve traced by a point on the rim of a circle of radius $a$ rolling along a line at height $a$ above SWL.

**Derivation of trochoid:**

Let a circle of radius $a$ roll without slipping along a horizontal line at $y = a$. After rotating through angle $\theta$, the centre is at $(a\theta, a)$, and a rim point is at:

$$x = a\theta - a\sin\theta, \quad y = a - a\cos\theta$$

For $\theta = kx - \omega t$ (phase angle), this becomes the trochoidal wave profile:

$$\eta(x,t) = a\!\left[1 - \cos(kx - \omega t)\right]$$

Crest occurs at $\theta = 0$: $\eta_{crest} = 0$, trough at $\theta = \pi$: $\eta_{trough} = 2a$. **Thus crests are sharper and troughs flatter than sinusoidal.**

**Part (b):**

Given: $H = 4$ m, $\lambda = 60$ m, deep water.

**Step 1 — Wave celerity:**
$$c_0 = \sqrt{\frac{g\lambda}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 60}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{93.74} = \boxed{9.68 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Period:**
$$T = \frac{\lambda}{c_0} = \frac{60}{9.68} = \boxed{6.20 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 3 — Wave number:**
$$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{60} = 0.1047 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Step 4 — Orbit radius at $z = -5$ m (deep water):**
$$r(z) = a \cdot e^{kz} = 2 \cdot e^{0.1047 \times (-5)} = 2 \cdot e^{-0.5235}$$

$$r(-5) = 2 \times 0.5927 = \boxed{1.185 \text{ m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the depth regime check** ($d/\lambda$): Many students blindly plug deep-water formulas. Always compute $d/\lambda$ first.
> 2. **Sign errors in $\sinh$ vs $\cosh$**: At $z = -d$, $\sinh(kd)/\sinh(kd) = 1$; at $z = 0$, the ratio is $\cosh(kd)/\sinh(kd) = \coth(kd)$. Don't confuse numerator and denominator.
> 3. **Wrong amplitude**: $a = H/2$, NOT $H$.
> 4. **In Gerstner theory**, particles move in **circles**, not ellipses — common error to confuse with Airy theory.
> 5. **Group velocity ≠ phase velocity**: In deep water, $c_g = c/2$. Don't assume they're equal.
> 6. **KTU requires units in final answers** — always write m/s, s, rad/m explicitly for full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Sinusoidal = Linear = Airy** theory uses $H/\lambda \le 1/50$; profile is $\eta = a\cos(kx - \omega t)$.
- [x] **Trochoidal = Gerstner** theory valid up to $H/\lambda \le 1/10$; **sharper crests, flatter troughs**.
- [x] **Dispersion relation** $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$ is **fundamental** — must memorise.
- [x] **Deep-water limit**: $\tanh(kd) \to 1$, $c_0 = gT/(2\pi)$, $c_g = c/2$.
- [x] **Shallow-water limit**: $\tanh(kd) \to kd$, $c_s = \sqrt{gd}$, $c_g \approx c$.
- [x] **Particle path**: Airy → elliptical (circular deep); Gerstner → **circular** at all depths.
- [x] **Flow nature**: Airy is irrotational; Gerstner is **rotational** with mass transport.
- [x] **Dynamic pressure** $p_d = \rho g a \sigma \cos(kx-\omega t)$ where $\sigma = \cosh[k(z+d)]/\cosh(kd)$.
- [x] **Trochoid geometry**: rim point on a rolling circle of radius $a$.
- [x] **Always identify depth regime first** — $d/\lambda > 0.5$ deep, $< 0.05$ shallow.
- [x] **Practical use**: Airy for ship-motion RAO; Gerstner for steep seas, slamming, whale-back hulls.
- [x] **KTU 14-mark problems** always combine (a) derivation/concept + (b) numerical computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wave Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Wave Spectrum** (also called an **Energy Spectrum** or **Power Spectral Density Function**, denoted $S(\omega)$ or $S(f)$) is a **statistical descriptor of ocean waves** that expresses how the wave energy of a random sea is distributed as a function of angular frequency $\omega$ (or frequency $f$). It is the **Fourier transform of the auto-correlation function** of the random sea-surface elevation $\zeta(t)$, which is mathematically modelled as a **stationary, ergodic, zero-mean Gaussian random process**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the wave spectrum forms the **fundamental link between oceanographic sea-state description and ship motion analysis**. It replaces the simplistic, deterministic regular wave with a realistic representation of the ocean, which is a superposition of an infinite number of infinitesimal regular wave components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wave Spectrum is the cornerstone of stochastic seakeeping theory. Every ship response (RAO-based motion, added resistance, slamming, fatigue, operability) ultimately requires the wave spectrum as input.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are listening to an orchestra playing a complex symphony. Your ear does not hear "a sound" — it hears a blend of many instruments (violins, flutes, drums) each playing at a slightly different pitch. If you ran this music through a spectrum analyzer, it would tell you exactly **how much sound energy exists at each pitch (frequency)**.

A wave spectrum is the **"frequency analyzer" of the ocean**:

- **Regular wave (Airy wave):** One single note played on a piano — clean, simple, deterministic.
- **Real ocean (random sea):** A noisy symphony of countless waves of different sizes and frequencies arriving from every direction.
- **Wave spectrum:** The graph that tells you, "At frequency 0.1 rad/s, there is *this much* wave energy; at 0.5 rad/s, *that much*; and so on."

> [!NOTE]
> The area under the curve $S(\omega)$ between two frequencies equals the **total wave energy** contained in that frequency band. So a spectrum is essentially an "energy histogram" of the ocean.

### Why Not Just Use Regular Waves?

| Limitation of Regular Waves | What Wave Spectrum Solves |
| :--- | :--- |
| Real ocean has waves of many sizes | Spectrum captures **distribution of energy vs. frequency** |
| Cannot predict ship responses statistically | Spectrum enables **probabilistic** analysis (e.g., 1 in 10⁴ exceedance) |
| Cannot compute fatigue damage | Spectrum + RAO → **spectral fatigue analysis** |
| Cannot compute operability limits | Spectrum → **short-term response statistics** |

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
- **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1025$ **kg/m³**
- **Significant wave height:** $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ **m**
- **Peak angular frequency:** $\omega_p$ **rad/s**
- **Energy per unit area of sea surface:** $E = \rho g \int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega = \rho g \, m_0$ **J/m²**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Random Sea as a Stochastic Process

Real ocean waves are random because they arise from **superposition of countless wavelets** generated by wind, swell, and atmospheric pressure fluctuations. Mathematically:

$$\zeta(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \cos(\omega_i t + \phi_i)$$

As $N \to \infty$, by the **Central Limit Theorem**, $\zeta(t)$ becomes a **Gaussian random process** with:

- Mean: $\bar{\zeta} = 0$
- Variance: $\sigma^2 = m_0 = \int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega$
- Skewness $\approx 0$, Kurtosis $\approx 3$ (in linear theory)

### 2.2 Definition via Wiener–Khinchin Theorem

The **auto-correlation function** $R(\tau)$ and the **spectrum** $S(\omega)$ are Fourier transform pairs:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R(\tau) e^{-i\omega \tau}\,d\tau$$

$$R(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(\omega) e^{i\omega \tau}\,d\omega$$

For a real, even sea-surface process, $R(\tau)$ is real and even, and $S(\omega)$ is a **one-sided spectrum** for $\omega \geq 0$:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_0^\infty R(\tau) \cos(\omega \tau)\,d\tau$$

### 2.3 The $n$-th Spectral Moment

$$m_n = \int_0^\infty \omega^n S(\omega)\,d\omega$$

The moments are the building blocks of all ship-dynamics statistics:

| Symbol | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m_0$ | $\int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega$ | Variance of sea elevation $\sigma^2$ |
| $m_1$ | $\int_0^\infty \omega\,S(\omega)\,d\omega$ | Used for mean period $T_1$ |
| $m_2$ | $\int_0^\infty \omega^2\,S(\omega)\,d\omega$ | Used for zero-crossing period $T_z$ |
| $m_{-1}$ | $\int_0^\infty \omega^{-1}\,S(\omega)\,d\omega$ | Used for energy period $T_e$ |
| $m_4$ | $\int_0^\infty \omega^4\,S(\omega)\,d\omega$ | Used for $T_2$, bandwidth |

### 2.4 Derived Sea-State Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Significant wave height $H_s$ | $4\sqrt{m_0}$ | Mean of highest 1/3 waves |
| Zero-crossing period $T_z$ | $2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$ | Mean time between up-crossings of mean level |
| Mean period $T_1$ | $2\pi\,m_0/m_1$ | First moment-based period |
| Energy period $T_{-1}$ | $2\pi\,m_{-1}/m_0$ | Period at spectral peak energy density |
| Spectral width $\epsilon$ | $\sqrt{1 - (m_2^2/(m_0 m_4))}$ | Narrow-band indicator ($\epsilon \to 0$ for narrow sea) |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{array}{ll}
\textbf{Standard Spectra} & \\
\text{Pierson-Moskowitz (PM):} & S(\omega)=\dfrac{A}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{\omega^4}\right),\ A=0.0081\,g^2,\ B=0.74\,(g/U_{19.5})^4 \\[6pt]
\text{PM in } H_s \text{ form:} & S(\omega)=\dfrac{5}{16}\dfrac{H_s^2\,\omega_p^4}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{5}{4}\left(\dfrac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^{\!4}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{JONSWAP:} & S(\omega)=\dfrac{\alpha g^2}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{5}{4}(\omega_p/\omega)^4\right)\gamma^{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\omega-\omega_p)^2}{2\sigma^2\omega_p^2}\right)} \\[6pt]
\text{Bretschneider (2-param):} & S(\omega)=\dfrac{5}{8}\dfrac{H_s^2}{T_z^4\,\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{5}{4}(1/\omega T_z)^4\right) \\[6pt]
\textbf{Wiener-Khinchin} & S(\omega)=\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!R(\tau)e^{-i\omega\tau}d\tau \\[4pt]
\textbf{Moments} & m_n=\displaystyle\int_0^\infty \omega^n S(\omega)\,d\omega \\[4pt]
\textbf{Significant Wave Height} & H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} \\[4pt]
\textbf{Zero-crossing Period} & T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2} \\[4pt]
\textbf{Energy Period} & T_e = 2\pi\,m_{-1}/m_0 \\[4pt]
\textbf{Response Spectrum (ship)} & S_R(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega)\vert^2 S(\omega) \\[4pt]
\textbf{Response Variance} & \sigma_R^2 = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty S_R(\omega)\,d\omega
\end{array}
}
$$

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in Ship Dynamics

1. **Seakeeping Analysis:** Ship responses (heave, pitch, roll) in irregular seas are obtained by convolving the wave spectrum with the **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** via $S_R(\omega) = \vert \text{RAO}(\omega)\vert^2 S(\omega)$.
2. **Fatigue Analysis:** Long-term fatigue uses $S(\omega)$ weighted by exceedance probability.
3. **Added Resistance in Waves:** Computed as spectral integral of resistance RAO × wave spectrum.
4. **Slamming & Whipping:** Short-term extremes derived from spectral bandwidth.
5. **Operability Assessment:** $H_s$ and $T_z$ from spectrum define operational envelopes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — From Random Wave Superposition to Spectral Density

**Start:** Model sea elevation as a superposition of $N$ regular wavelets:

$$
\zeta(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \cos(\omega_i t + \phi_i)
$$

**Step 1 — Variance of $\zeta(t)$:** Since phases $\phi_i$ are independent and uniformly distributed in $[0, 2\pi)$:

$$
\overline{\zeta^2(t)} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^2
$$

because $\overline{\cos^2(\omega_i t + \phi_i)} = 1/2$.

**Step 2 — Introduce spectral density:** Define the spectrum such that:

$$
\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^2 \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega
$$

This defines $S(\omega)\,d\omega$ as the **contribution to variance from waves in the band** $[\omega, \omega+d\omega]$.

**Step 3 — Wave amplitude from spectral density:** Each wavelet has energy $\frac{1}{2}\rho g a_i^2$. The energy density per unit frequency is $\rho g\,S(\omega)$. The amplitude of a component at $\omega$ is:

$$
a(\omega) = \sqrt{2\,S(\omega)\,d\omega}
$$

**Step 4 — Continuous form (superposition integral):**

$$
\boxed{\;\zeta(t) = \int_0^\infty \sqrt{2\,S(\omega)\,d\omega}\;\cos(\omega t + \phi(\omega))\;}
$$

This is the **Rice representation** of the Gaussian random sea.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Significant Wave Height from Spectrum

**Start:** In a **narrow-band Gaussian sea**, wave amplitudes follow the **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$
p(a) = \frac{2a}{m_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{a^2}{m_0}\right), \quad a \geq 0
$$

**Step 1 — Mean of highest 1/3 amplitudes:** Define exceedance probability $P(A > a) = \exp(-a^2/m_0)$. The fraction $1/3$ corresponds to:

$$
\exp\!\left(-\frac{a_{1/3}^2}{m_0}\right) = \frac{1}{3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{a_{1/3}^2}{m_0} = \ln 3
$$

**Step 2 — Mean of conditional distribution above $a_{1/3}$:**

$$
\overline{a}_{1/3} = \frac{\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty a\,p(a)\,da}{\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty p(a)\,da} = \frac{\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty a\,(2a/m_0)\exp(-a^2/m_0)\,da}{(1/3)}
$$

Evaluate numerator by substitution $u = a^2/m_0$, $du = 2a\,da/m_0$:

$$
\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty \frac{2a^2}{m_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{a^2}{m_0}\right)da = \int_{\ln 3}^\infty m_0\, e^{-u}\,du = m_0\, e^{-\ln 3} = \frac{m_0}{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\overline{a}_{1/3} = \frac{m_0/3}{1/3} = m_0\,\frac{1}{e^{-\ln 3}} \quad \text{(recheck)}
$$

Cleaner derivation (textbook form): For Rayleigh distribution with parameter $\sigma^2 = m_0$:

$$
\overline{a}_{1/3} = 2\sqrt{m_0}\!\left[\frac{\sqrt{\pi/2}\,\text{erfc}(\sqrt{\ln 3}/\sqrt{2}) - \sqrt{\ln 3}\,e^{-\ln 3}/3}{\text{erfc}(\sqrt{\ln 3}/\sqrt{2})}\right]
$$

Numerical evaluation gives $\overline{a}_{1/3} \approx 2\sqrt{m_0}$.

**Step 3 — Significant wave height** (crest-to-trough, i.e., $H \approx 2a$):

$$
\boxed{\;H_s = 2\,\overline{a}_{1/3} \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}\;}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation C — Zero-Crossing Period from Spectrum

**Start:** Number of zero-upcrossings per unit time for a stationary Gaussian process:

$$
N_0^+ = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}
$$

**Step 1 — Mean zero-crossing period:**

$$
T_z = \frac{1}{N_0^+} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m_0}{m_2}}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Proof of zero-crossing rate:** For a Gaussian process with stationary zero mean, crossings of level $x=0$ occur at rate:

$$
N_0^+ = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{\sigma_{\dot\zeta}}{\sigma_\zeta}
$$

where $\dot\zeta = d\zeta/dt$. Since $\dot\zeta(t)$ is also stationary with:

$$
\sigma_\zeta^2 = m_0, \qquad \sigma_{\dot\zeta}^2 = \int_0^\infty \omega^2 S(\omega)\,d\omega = m_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_0^+ = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation D — Pierson-Moskowitz Spectral Formula

The PM spectrum is derived from the assumption of a **fully developed sea** with wind blowing steadily over a long fetch. Two asymptotic conditions are imposed:

1. **High-frequency tail** must follow **Phillips' equilibrium** $S(\omega) \sim \omega^{-5}$.
2. **Peak frequency** $\omega_p$ depends only on wind speed $U$ and gravity: dimensional analysis gives $\omega_p \propto g/U$.

**Step 1 — Form:** Try $S(\omega) = \dfrac{A}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B}{\omega^4}\right)$. The $\omega^{-5}$ comes from Phillips' equilibrium; the exponential enforces a peak.

**Step 2 — Find peak frequency:** Set $dS/d\omega = 0$:

$$
\frac{dS}{d\omega} = -\frac{5A}{\omega^6}\exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^4}\right) + \frac{A}{\omega^5}\cdot\frac{4B}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^4}\right) = 0
$$

Simplify:

$$
-\frac{5}{\omega^6} + \frac{4B}{\omega^{10}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{4B}{\omega^{10}} = \frac{5}{\omega^6} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_p^4 = \frac{4B}{5}
$$

So:

$$
\boxed{\;B = \dfrac{5}{4}\omega_p^4\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Experimental fit to North Atlantic data:**

- $\omega_p = 0.84\,g/U_{19.5}$ (peak angular frequency)
- Substituting: $B = 0.74\,(g/U_{19.5})^4$
- $A = 0.0081\,g^2$ (Phillips' constant fit)

**Step 4 — In terms of significant wave height:** Using $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 0.0213\,U_{19.5}^2/g$:

$$
\boxed{\;S(\omega) = \dfrac{5}{16}\dfrac{H_s^2\,\omega_p^4}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{5}{4}\left(\dfrac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^{\!4}\right)\;}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation E — JONSWAP Spectrum (Fetch-Limited Sea)

The PM spectrum assumes infinite fetch. For **fetch-limited** development (e.g., young seas in a finite ocean region), the **Joint North Sea Wave Project (JONSWAP, 1973)** introduced a **peak enhancement factor** $\gamma$:

$$
S_{JS}(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega)\cdot \gamma^{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\omega-\omega_p)^2}{2\sigma^2\omega_p^2}\right)}
$$

with

$$
\sigma = \begin{cases} 0.07, & \omega \leq \omega_p \\ 0.09, & \omega > \omega_p \end{cases}
$$

Typical values:
- $\gamma = 1.5$ to $6$, **mean value $\gamma = 3.3$**
- $\alpha = 0.076\,(gF/U^2)^{-0.22}$ — Phillips' constant (function of dimensionless fetch)
- $\omega_p = 22\,(g^2/(U F))^{1/3}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow — From Ocean Physics to Ship Response

The following **Mermaid block-level architecture flow** maps the chain of reasoning from physical sea state to engineering ship responses, demonstrating exactly where the wave spectrum fits.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OCEAN ENVIRONMENT] --> B[Wind Forcing]
    B --> C[Random Sea Surface zeta of t]
    C --> D[Stationary Gaussian Random Process]
    D --> E[Auto Correlation R of tau]
    E -->|Fourier Transform| F[WAVE SPECTRUM S of omega]
    F --> G[Spectral Moments mn]
    G --> H1[Hs = 4 sqrt m0]
    G --> H2[Tz = 2 pi sqrt m0 over m2]
    G --> H3[Te = 2 pi m-1 over m0]
    F --> I[Standard Spectral Forms]
    I --> I1[Pierson Moskowitz PM]
    I --> I2[JONSWAP JS]
    I --> I3[Bretschneider IT]
    I --> I4[ISSC Modified]
    F --> J[Ship RAO H of i omega]
    J --> K[Response Spectrum SR]
    K --> K1[Roll Response]
    K --> K2[Pitch Response]
    K --> K3[Heave Response]
    K1 --> L[Short Term Statistics]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Slamming Probability]
    L --> N[Fatigue Damage]
    L --> O[Operability Assessment]
    L --> P[Added Resistance in Waves]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Anatomy of a Typical Wave Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Frequency axis omega] --> B[Low freq tail omega to power of -5]
    A --> C[Peak frequency omega p]
    A --> D[High freq tail Phillips equilibrium]
    C --> E[Peak spectral density S of omega p]
    E --> F[Area under curve equals m0]
    F --> G[Variance sigma squared]
    G --> H[Significant height Hs equals 4 sigma]
    B --> I[Long period swell component]
    D --> J[Short period wind sea component]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — PM vs JONSWAP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea State Selection] --> B{Fetch Condition?}
    B -->|Fully Developed infinite fetch| C[Use Pierson Moskowitz]
    B -->|Fetch Limited finite fetch| D[Use JONSWAP]
    C --> E[S of omega = A over omega to power of 5 times exponential]
    D --> F[S of omega = PM times gamma to exponent]
    E --> G[Single Peak broad spectrum]
    F --> H[Sharper Peak with gamma enhancement]
    G --> I[Hs from U19.5 only]
    H --> J[Hs and gamma from fetch and U]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Spectral Analysis Pipeline for Ship Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
