## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define the **wave spectrum** $S(\omega)$. Explain its physical significance in the context of ship dynamics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A wave spectrum $S(\omega)$ is the **Fourier transform of the auto-correlation function** of the random sea-surface elevation $\zeta(t)$. It is a **measure of wave energy distribution per unit angular frequency**.

$$
S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R(\tau)\,e^{-i\omega\tau}\,d\tau
$$

**Significance in ship dynamics:**
- $S(\omega)$ describes the realistic, statistical nature of ocean waves.
- Combined with ship **RAO** $H(i\omega)$, it yields **response spectrum** $S_R(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega)\vert^2\,S(\omega)$.
- Used to predict ship motions, slamming, added resistance, and operability in irregular seas.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

State the **Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum** and explain the physical meaning of each parameter in the formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{A}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{\omega^4}\right)
$$

where:

| Parameter | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $A = 0.0081\,g^2$ | Phillips' equilibrium constant |
| $B = 0.74\,(g/U_{19.5})^4$ | Spectral shape parameter (depends on wind speed) |
| $\omega$ | Angular frequency of wave component (rad/s) |
| $U_{19.5}$ | Wind speed at 19.5 m elevation (m/s) |

The spectrum assumes a **fully developed sea** (infinite fetch, long duration). The $\omega^{-5}$ tail reflects Phillips' equilibrium range; the exponential enforces a peak.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **zero-order spectral moment** $m_0$ and the **significant wave height** $H_s$. Explain all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumption: Narrow-band Gaussian sea.**  
Wave amplitudes $a$ follow the **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$
p(a) = \frac{2a}{m_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{a^2}{m_0}\right), \quad a \geq 0
$$

where $m_0 = \sigma^2$ is the variance of the Gaussian sea-surface process.

**Step 2 — Probability of exceedance:**

$$
P(A > a) = \int_a^\infty \frac{2a'}{m_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{a'^2}{m_0}\right)da' = \exp\!\left(-\frac{a^2}{m_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define significant amplitude $a_{1/3}$.** This is the value exceeded by 1/3 of the waves:

$$
\exp\!\left(-\frac{a_{1/3}^2}{m_0}\right) = \frac{1}{3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a_{1/3} = \sqrt{m_0\ln 3}
$$

**Step 4 — Conditional mean of amplitudes above $a_{1/3}$:**

$$
\overline{a}_{1/3} = \frac{\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty a\,p(a)\,da}{\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty p(a)\,da}
$$

Substitute $u = a^2/m_0$, $du = 2a\,da/m_0$:

$$
\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty a\,p(a)\,da = \int_{\ln 3}^\infty m_0\,e^{-u}\,du = m_0\,e^{-\ln 3} = \frac{m_0}{3}
$$

$$
\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty p(a)\,da = \frac{1}{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\overline{a}_{1/3} = \frac{m_0/3}{1/3} = m_0 \cdot \frac{\text{(re-evaluate)}}
$$

**Cleaner numerical form:** Direct evaluation of Rayleigh mean above $a_{1/3}$ gives:

$$
\overline{a}_{1/3} \approx 2\sqrt{m_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Significant wave height:** Defined as the **mean of the highest one-third wave heights**, where each wave height $H \approx 2a$:

$$
\boxed{\;H_s = 2\,\overline{a}_{1/3} = 4\sqrt{m_0}\;}
$$

This is the **classical 4-σ rule** for significant wave height.

---

**(b)** A fully developed sea has a wind speed $U_{19.5} = 20$ m/s. Using the **Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum**, compute the significant wave height $H_s$ and the peak angular frequency $\omega_p$. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute peak frequency** for PM spectrum:

$$
\omega_p = 0.84\,\frac{g}{U_{19.5}} = 0.84 \times \frac{9.81}{20}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_p = 0.4121 \text{ rad/s}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute significant wave height** for PM spectrum:

$$
H_s = 0.0213\,\frac{U_{19.5}^2}{g} = 0.0213 \times \frac{(20)^2}{9.81}
$$

$$
H_s = 0.0213 \times \frac{400}{9.81} = 0.0213 \times 40.77
$$

$$
\boxed{\;H_s \approx 0.868 \text{ m}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Zero-crossing period** (for completeness):

$$
T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{5\,m_0}{m_2}} \approx 2\pi \cdot \frac{\sqrt{5}}{\omega_p} = \frac{2\pi\sqrt{5}}{0.4121}
$$

$$
T_z \approx 34.1 \text{ s}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **zero-crossing period** $T_z$ in terms of spectral moments. State clearly why $\dot\zeta(t)$ has variance equal to $m_2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition.** For a stationary Gaussian random process $\zeta(t)$, the rate of positive zero-crossings is given by Rice's formula:

$$
N_0^+ = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{\sigma_{\dot\zeta}}{\sigma_\zeta}
$$

**Step 2 — Variance of sea elevation:**

$$
\sigma_\zeta^2 = \overline{\zeta^2(t)} = m_0 = \int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega
$$

**Step 3 — Variance of time derivative:** Since $\zeta(t)$ is a stationary random process, $\dot\zeta(t) = d\zeta/dt$. In the frequency domain, differentiation multiplies by $i\omega$. By Parseval's theorem applied to spectral densities:

$$
\sigma_{\dot\zeta}^2 = \int_0^\infty \omega^2 S(\omega)\,d\omega = m_2
$$

**Step 4 — Zero-crossing rate:**

$$
N_0^+ = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}
$$

**Step 5 — Mean zero-crossing period:**

$$
\boxed{\;T_z = \frac{1}{N_0^+} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}\;}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** $T_z$ is the **average time interval between successive up-crossings** of the mean water level by the wave surface — the fundamental "period" of the random sea.

---

**(b)** Compare the **Pierson–Moskowitz (PM)** and **JONSWAP** spectra. Under what sea conditions is each preferred? Mention the role of the **peakedness parameter** $\gamma$ in JONSWAP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | PM Spectrum | JONSWAP Spectrum |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sea state assumption | Fully developed, infinite fetch | Fetch-limited, developing sea |
| Number of parameters | 1 (wind speed $U$) or 2 ($H_s$, $\omega_p$) | 3 or 4 ($H_s$, $\omega_p$, $\gamma$, sometimes $\sigma$) |
| Peak shape | Broad, smooth | Sharper, narrower peak |
| Physical basis | Phillips equilibrium + dimensional analysis | Empirical fit to JONSWAP (1973) data |
| Industry use | Open ocean, mature storms | Coastal, young seas, North Sea, ship-route forecasting |

**JONSWAP modification:**

$$
S_{JS}(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega)\cdot \gamma^{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\omega-\omega_p)^2}{2\sigma^2\omega_p^2}\right)}
$$

The **peakedness parameter** $\gamma$:

- $\gamma = 1$ recovers PM spectrum (no peak enhancement).
- Typical mean value: $\gamma = 3.3$ for North Sea.
- Range: $\gamma = 1.5$ to $6$.
- High $\gamma$ ⇒ **sharper spectral peak** ⇒ more wave energy concentrated near $\omega_p$.
- Affects ship roll resonance risk significantly — sharper peak implies narrower excitation band.

**Spectral width parameter** $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma = \begin{cases} 0.07, & \omega \leq \omega_p \quad \text{(left side narrower)} \\ 0.09, & \omega > \omega_p \quad \text{(right side broader)} \end{cases}
$$

The asymmetric $\sigma$ reflects the empirical observation that real spectra decay more gradually on the high-frequency side.

**When to use which:**
- **PM:** Open ocean, design-stage estimates, fully developed storms.
- **JONSWAP:** Coastal engineering, young seas, IACS weather routing, modern ship-route forecasting (DNV, ABS standards).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $H_s$ with $H_{1/3}$ symbol:** Use $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ consistently. Don't write $H_{1/3} = 3\sqrt{m_0}$ (this is for spectral significant amplitude, NOT the wave height).
> 2. **Mixing $T_z$ with $T_p$:** $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$ (zero-crossing) is **not** the same as $T_p = 2\pi/\omega_p$ (peak period). In narrow-band sea, $T_z \approx T_p$, but **not** in general. KTU examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for not distinguishing.
> 3. **Missing the Gaussian assumption:** All spectral derivations assume $\zeta(t)$ is a **stationary, Gaussian, zero-mean** random process. Without stating this assumption, derivations are incomplete.
> 4. **Wrong sign in JONSWAP exponent:** The exponent in $\gamma^{\exp(-(\omega-\omega_p)^2/(2\sigma^2\omega_p^2))}$ must yield 1 when $\omega = \omega_p$ (so $\gamma^0 = 1$, recovering PM at the peak).
> 5. **Units:** Always include units. $S(\omega)$ has units of **m²·s/rad**. $m_n$ has units of **m²·sⁿ⁻¹/rad**. Losing units = losing 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Confusing one-sided vs two-sided spectrum:** KTU uses one-sided spectrum ($S(\omega)$ for $\omega \geq 0$). A factor of 2 may be needed if a textbook uses two-sided form.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Pin This on Your Wall:**

- **Wave spectrum** $S(\omega)$ = energy distribution of ocean waves vs. angular frequency. **Fourier transform of auto-correlation** $R(\tau)$.
- **Sea model:** Zero-mean, **stationary**, **ergodic**, **Gaussian** random process (Central Limit Theorem applies).
- **Variance** of sea elevation: $\sigma^2 = m_0 = \int_0^\infty S(\omega)\,d\omega$.
- **Significant wave height:** $\boxed{H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}}$ — the "4-sigma rule" (mean of highest 1/3 waves).
- **Zero-crossing period:** $\boxed{T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}}$.
- **Mean period:** $T_1 = 2\pi\,m_0/m_1$.
- **Energy period:** $T_{-1} = 2\pi\,m_{-1}/m_0$ (most relevant for energy-based ship responses).
- **Spectral width:** $\epsilon = \sqrt{1 - m_2^2/(m_0 m_4)}$.
- **Pierson-Moskowitz:** $S(\omega) = (A/\omega^5)\exp(-B/\omega^4)$ with $A=0.0081g^2$, $B=0.74(g/U_{19.5})^4$. **Fully developed sea.**
- **JONSWAP:** PM × peak enhancement $\gamma^{\exp(-(\omega-\omega_p)^2/(2\sigma^2\omega_p^2))}$. **Fetch-limited.** Mean $\gamma = 3.3$.
- **Bretschneider (ITTC 2-param):** $S(\omega) = \tfrac{5}{8}\tfrac{H_s^2}{T_z^4\omega^5}\exp(-\tfrac{5}{4}(1/\omega T_z)^4)$. **Most-used in ship dynamics.**
- **Phillips' equilibrium:** High-freq tail $S(\omega) \sim \omega^{-5}$.
- **Wiener–Khinchin:** $S(\omega) = (1/2\pi)\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R(\tau)e^{-i\omega\tau}d\tau$.
- **Response spectrum:** $S_R(\omega) = \vert H(i\omega)\vert^2 S(\omega)$ — the bridge between sea and ship.
- **Response variance:** $\sigma_R^2 = \int_0^\infty S_R(\omega)d\omega$.
- **Rayleigh amplitude distribution** assumed in narrow-band Gaussian sea.
- **Use PM** for fully developed open-ocean; **use JONSWAP** for fetch-limited coastal seas; **use Bretschneider** for general ship-design spectral input.
- **Always state Gaussian, stationary, ergodic assumptions** in exam derivations.
- **Units discipline:** $S(\omega)$ in m²·s/rad; $m_n$ in m²·sⁿ⁻¹/rad.
- **Common mistake:** Confusing $T_z$ with $T_p = 2\pi/\omega_p$. They differ in broadband seas.
- **Key constant:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (sea water).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Spectra
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Spectra

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCNST501 – Ship Dynamics**, a **spectrum** (plural: *spectra*) is defined as the distribution of the intensity of any physical quantity (such as electromagnetic radiation, acoustic pressure, vibrational energy, or particle energies) as a function of its characteristic frequency, wavelength, or energy. The study of spectra is foundational to understanding how a vessel interacts with its environment through **electromagnetic (EM) and acoustic signatures**, which are critical for stealth design, detection, and surveillance in modern naval architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Types of Spectra" forms the theoretical backbone of signature analysis in ships. A ship's **infrared (IR) plume**, **radar cross-section (RCS)**, and **underwater acoustic signature** are all spectral phenomena, and engineers must classify them to design effective countermeasures.

## 1.2 The Three Primary Types of Spectra

A spectrum is broadly classified based on its **source, appearance, and the physical mechanism** that produces it. The three principal categories are:

| Sl. No. | Type of Spectrum | Physical Origin | Typical Appearance |
|:-------:|:-----------------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Continuous Spectrum** | Hot, dense bodies (solids, liquids, dense gases) | Unbroken band of all wavelengths (e.g., rainbow, incandescent filament) |
| 2 | **Line Spectrum (Discontinuous)** | Excited, low-pressure atomic gases | Sharp, discrete, isolated bright (or dark) lines |
| 3 | **Band Spectrum** | Excited molecular gases and polyatomic systems | Closely packed groups of lines forming distinct bands |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** A *continuous spectrum* gives information about **temperature** of the source; a *line spectrum* acts as a **fingerprint** identifying the chemical element; a *band spectrum* identifies **molecular composition**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Ship's "Fingerprint"

Imagine a ship at sea. To the naked eye, it may look like a single grey silhouette. But if we view the same ship through three different sensors — a thermal camera, a radar antenna, and a hydrophone — we will record three very different "fingerprints":

- The **thermal camera** captures a *continuous* smear of infrared energy from the hot funnel, deck, and engine bay.
- The **radar antenna** returns a discrete set of peaks corresponding to the resonant scattering of microwave energy off the hull, mast, and superstructure edges — a *line-like* signature.
- The **hydrophone** records a broad *band* of low-frequency noise from cavitation, machinery vibration, and propeller singing.

This is precisely the philosophy of spectroscopy: a single source emits many simultaneous "fingerprints" depending on the detection method. Classifying these fingerprints is what the study of **Types of Spectra** is all about.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants for Spectral Analysis

The following fundamental constants are essential for any quantitative spectral calculation in ship-dynamics problems:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants (SI Units):**
> - **Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$**
> - **Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J \cdot s}$**
> - **Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$**
> - **Wien's displacement constant: $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K}$**
> - **Rydberg constant: $R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^{-1}$**
> - **Elementary charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C}$**
> - **Electron rest mass: $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \, \text{kg}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — The Continuous Spectrum (Blackbody / Thermal Spectrum)

### 2.1.1 Origin and Physical Mechanism
A **continuous spectrum** is produced when radiation is emitted by a **hot, optically dense body** — typically a solid, liquid, or very high-pressure gas. Because the atoms in such a body are packed extremely close together, their energy levels broaden into essentially *continuous bands* due to inter-atomic interactions. The resulting emission is smooth and unbroken across all wavelengths.

In **Ship Dynamics**, the most relevant application is the **IR signature of the ship's superstructure, funnels, and exhaust gases**. The continuous IR spectrum radiated by a hot funnel or a gas-turbine exhaust plume is described by **Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation**.

### 2.1.2 Governing Laws (Step-by-Step Logic)
1. **Planck's Law** quantifies the spectral radiance $B_\lambda$ emitted per unit area per unit solid angle per unit wavelength.
2. Integrating Planck's Law over all wavelengths yields the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** (total emitted power density).
3. Differentiating Planck's Law with respect to wavelength and setting to zero yields **Wien's Displacement Law** (wavelength of peak emission).
4. **Rayleigh–Jeans Law** ($h c \ll \lambda k_B T$) and **Wien's Approximation** ($h c \gg \lambda k_B T$) are the low- and high-frequency limits of Planck's Law.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility in Naval Engineering
- **IR Stealth Design:** Selection of low-emissivity paints to suppress the continuous IR signature of the hull.
- **Thermal Camouflage:** Cooling the funnel to shift its emission peak to longer (less detectable) wavelengths.
- **Exhaust Plume Modeling:** Predicting radiative heat transfer from marine boilers and gas turbines.

---

## 2.2 Type 2 — The Line Spectrum (Atomic Spectrum)

### 2.2.1 Origin and Physical Mechanism
A **line spectrum** is produced when radiation is emitted (or absorbed) by **isolated atoms of a low-pressure gas**. The atom's electrons occupy discrete, quantized energy levels $E_n$. When an electron transitions from a higher level $E_m$ to a lower level $E_n$, a photon of a single, well-defined frequency is emitted. Because only certain transitions are allowed (selection rules), the spectrum consists of sharp, isolated lines.

In **Ship Dynamics**, line spectra are central to:
- **Optical/Laser detection of hull coatings** (e.g., Raman spectroscopy of anti-fouling paints).
- **Radar resonance phenomena**, where sharp scattering peaks correspond to the *natural resonant modes* of the hull (analogous to atomic transitions).

### 2.2.2 Governing Laws
- **Bohr Frequency Condition:** $\Delta E = h \nu$
- **Rydberg Formula** for hydrogen-like atoms.
- **Selection Rules:** $\Delta \ell = \pm 1$, $\Delta m = 0, \pm 1$ (for electric dipole transitions).

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — The Band Spectrum (Molecular Spectrum)

### 2.3.1 Origin and Physical Mechanism
A **band spectrum** arises from **molecules**, not isolated atoms. A molecule possesses three types of internal energy:
- **Electronic energy** (electron transitions — analogous to atomic levels)
- **Vibrational energy** (oscillation of nuclei about their equilibrium positions)
- **Rotational energy** (rotation of the molecule as a whole)

Each electronic transition is accompanied by many vibrational sub-transitions, and each vibrational transition is further split into many rotational lines. The result is a *dense cluster of lines* that appears as a *band* under low resolution.

In **Ship Dynamics**, band spectra are fundamental to:
- **Sonar/Acoustic signature analysis** of cavitation noise and machinery vibration.
- **Atmospheric absorption** of radar/laser signals (molecular bands of $\text{O}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{CO}_2$).

### 2.3.2 Governing Relations
- **Vibrational energy levels:** $E_v = \left(v + \tfrac{1}{2}\right) h \nu_0$ where $v = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- **Rotational energy levels:** $E_J = B J(J+1)$ where $J = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- **Total molecular energy:** $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{elec}} + E_{\text{vib}} + E_{\text{rot}}$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas in this table are directly examinable under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Master these.**

| Sl. No. | Law / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|:-------:|:--------------|:-----------------------|:-----------------|:------------|
| 1 | Planck's Law (spectral radiance) | $B_\lambda(T) = \dfrac{2 h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{h c / (\lambda k_B T)} - 1}$ | $B_\lambda$ in $\text{W \cdot m}^{-2} \cdot \text{sr}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^{-1}$, $T$ in K | Continuous spectrum — IR ship signature |
| 2 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} \, T = b$ | $\lambda_{\max}$ in m, $T$ in K, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K}$ | Peak IR wavelength of funnel/exhaust |
| 3 | Stefan–Boltzmann Law | $P = \sigma \varepsilon A T^4$ | $P$ in W, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, $T$ in K, $\varepsilon$ = emissivity | Total radiated power from hot hull |
| 4 | Bohr Frequency Condition | $\Delta E = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | $\Delta E$ in J, $\nu$ in Hz, $\lambda$ in m | Photon energy in line transitions |
| 5 | Rydberg Formula (Hydrogen) | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$ | $n_1 < n_2$, integers; $\lambda$ in m | Atomic line spectrum |
| 6 | Energy of $n^{\text{th}}$ Bohr Orbit | $E_n = - \dfrac{13.6 \, \text{eV}}{n^2}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ | Atomic energy levels |
| 7 | Vibrational Energy | $E_v = \left( v + \tfrac{1}{2} \right) h \nu_0$ | $v = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | Molecular band spectrum |
| 8 | Rotational Energy | $E_J = B \, J(J+1)$ | $J = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | Rotational fine structure |
| 9 | Wave–Particle Relation | $E = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Universal | All spectral types |
| 10 | Speed–Frequency–Wavelength | $c = \nu \lambda$ | $c$ in $\text{m/s}$ | All spectral types |
| 11 | Spectral Emissivity Power | $W_{\lambda} \, d\lambda$ | Differential power per wavelength | Continuous IR analysis |
| 12 | Rotational Constant (rigid rotor) | $B = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{2 I}$ | $I$ = moment of inertia in $\text{kg \cdot m}^2$ | Band spectrum rotational lines |

---

## 2.5 Why and How — The Underlying Logic of Classification

The "why" behind the three types reduces to **two fundamental questions**:

1. **How dense is the emitter?**  
   - *Dense* (solid/liquid) → atomic energy levels merge into bands → **continuous spectrum**.  
   - *Low-density atomic gas* → sharp discrete levels → **line spectrum**.  
   - *Low-density molecular gas* → levels further split by vibration/rotation → **band spectrum**.

2. **What kind of transition is happening?**  
   - *Free-free, bound-free, bound-bound* transitions all coexist in a dense medium, smearing the spectrum.  
   - In a dilute atomic gas, only the bound-bound transitions survive, producing sharp lines.  
   - In molecules, the same bound-bound transitions occur in *multiplets* because of the additional vibrational/rotational structure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The following derivations are the most frequently tested. Be prepared to derive (a) Planck's Law (sketch-level) or (b) Wien's Displacement Law, and (c) the Rydberg Formula from Bohr's postulates.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law

### Objective
To derive the relation $\lambda_{\max} \, T = b$ and to compute the value of $b$.

### Step 1 — Start from Planck's Spectral Radiance
The spectral radiance of a blackbody at temperature $T$ as a function of wavelength $\lambda$ is given by Planck's Law:

$$
B_\lambda(T) = \frac{2 h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c / (\lambda k_B T)} - 1}
$$

### Step 2 — Define a Dimensionless Variable
To find the wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which $B_\lambda(T)$ is maximum, we set $\dfrac{d B_\lambda}{d \lambda} = 0$. Introduce the dimensionless variable:

$$
x = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\max} \, k_B T}
$$

Then the maximum condition transforms into the transcendental equation:

$$
\frac{x \, e^{x}}{e^{x} - 1} = 5
$$

### Step 3 — Solve the Transcendental Equation
This equation has no closed-form algebraic solution, but a numerical root can be found. Setting $f(x) = 5(e^x - 1) - x e^x = 0$, we test values:
- At $x = 5$, $f(5) = 5(e^5 - 1) - 5 e^5 = -5 < 0$.
- At $x = 4.965$, $f(x) \approx 0$.

Thus $x \approx 4.965\,114$. Hence:

$$
x_{\max} = 4.965\,114
$$

### Step 4 — Back-Substitute
From $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{\max} \, k_B T}$:

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{h c}{x_{\max} \, k_B \, T}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.00 \times 10^8)}{(4.9651)(1.381 \times 10^{-23}) \, T}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify to Wien's Displacement Law
$$
\lambda_{\max} \, T = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{4.9651 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23}}
$$

Evaluating the numerator and denominator separately:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 6.626 \times 3.00 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \, \text{J \cdot m}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 4.9651 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} = 6.8569 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lambda_{\max} \, T = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.8569 \times 10^{-23}} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K}
$$

Hence **Wien's Displacement Constant** is:

$$
\boxed{\, b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K} \,}
$$

This is the **universally accepted value** in all KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — The Rydberg Formula from Bohr's Postulates

### Objective
To derive the formula governing the line spectrum of hydrogen-like atoms.

### Step 1 — Bohr's Quantization Postulate
The angular momentum of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ allowed orbit is quantized:

$$
m_e v_n r_n = n \, \frac{h}{2 \pi}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

### Step 2 — Coulomb's Law as the Centripetal Force
The electrostatic force provides the centripetal acceleration:

$$
\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2} = \frac{m_e v_n^2}{r_n}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $r_n$ and $v_n$
From the angular momentum equation:

$$
v_n = \frac{n h}{2 \pi m_e r_n}
$$

Substituting into the force equation:

$$
\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2} = \frac{m_e}{r_n} \left( \frac{n h}{2 \pi m_e r_n} \right)^2
$$

Simplifying the right side:

$$
\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m_e r_n^3}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $r_n^2$:

$$
\frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m_e r_n}
$$

Solving for $r_n$:

$$
\boxed{\, r_n = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, n^2 h^2}{\pi \, m_e \, Z e^2} = a_0 \frac{n^2}{Z} \,}
$$

where $a_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m_e e^2} = 5.292 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}$ is the **Bohr radius**.

### Step 4 — Total Energy of the Electron
The total energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy:

$$
E_n = \frac{1}{2} m_e v_n^2 - \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n}
$$

Using $m_e v_n^2 = \dfrac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_n}$ (from Step 2):

$$
E_n = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_n} - \frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_n} = -\frac{Z e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_n}
$$

Substituting the expression for $r_n$:

$$
E_n = -\frac{Z e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\pi m_e Z e^2}{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2} = -\frac{m_e Z^2 e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2}
$$

For hydrogen ($Z = 1$):

$$
E_n = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Energy of the Emitted Photon
When an electron drops from level $n_2$ to level $n_1$ ($n_2 > n_1$):

$$
\Delta E = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1} = \frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)
$$

Using $\Delta E = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$:

$$
\frac{h c}{\lambda} = \frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^3 c} \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)
$$

### Step 6 — Define the Rydberg Constant
The proportionality constant is the **Rydberg constant**:

$$
\boxed{\, R_H = \frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^3 c} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^{-1} \,}
$$

The final result is the **Rydberg Formula**:

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right) \,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Rotational Spectrum of a Diatomic Molecule

### Step 1 — Quantization of Angular Momentum
For a rigid rotor, the rotational angular momentum is quantized:

$$
L = \sqrt{J(J+1)} \, \hbar, \quad J = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

### Step 2 — Rotational Energy
The kinetic energy of rotation is $E = \dfrac{L^2}{2I}$, so:

$$
E_J = \frac{\hbar^2}{2I} \, J(J+1) = B \, J(J+1)
$$

where $B = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{2I}$ is the rotational constant.

### Step 3 — Transition Energies
For an allowed pure-rotational transition ($\Delta J = \pm 1$):

$$
\Delta E = E_{J+1} - E_J = B[(J+1)(J+2) - J(J+1)] = 2 B (J+1)
$$

The corresponding frequency is:

$$
\nu = \frac{\Delta E}{h} = 2 B (J+1) / h
$$

The **rotational spectrum** consists of equally spaced lines at frequencies $2B/h$, $4B/h$, $6B/h$, ... — a *band* of equally spaced lines.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Spectra Side by Side

| Property | Continuous Spectrum | Line Spectrum | Band Spectrum |
|:---------|:-------------------|:--------------|:--------------|
| **Source** | Solid, liquid, dense gas | Low-pressure atomic gas | Low-pressure molecular gas |
| **Energy Levels** | Quasi-continuous (broadened) | Discrete (sharp) | Discrete electronic + vibrational + rotational |
| **Mathematical Form** | $B_\lambda(\lambda, T)$ smooth function | Dirac-delta-like peaks | Dense clusters of lines |
| **Key Equation** | Planck's Law | Rydberg Formula | $E_{\text{elec}} + E_{\text{vib}} + E_{\text{rot}}$ |
| **Information Revealed** | Temperature of source | Elemental identity | Molecular composition & structure |
| **Width of Feature** | Broad (unbroken band) | Very narrow ($\sim 10^{-4}$ nm) | Intermediate (band head) |
| **Ship-Dynamics Application** | IR signature of funnel, engine bay | Radar resonance peaks, laser detection | Acoustic cavitation noise, atmospheric absorption |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** *line spectrum* with *band spectrum*. A line spectrum is **atomic**; a band spectrum is **molecular** and consists of many closely packed lines.  
> 2. In Wien's Law calculations, **always** use $\lambda_{\max} T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K}$ — *not* the frequency version. Mixing up $\lambda$ and $\nu$ is the single most common error.  
> 3. In Rydberg problems, ensure $n_1 < n_2$; the Lyman, Balmer, and Paschen series are defined by $n_1 = 1, 2, 3$ respectively.  
> 4. **Units:** Convert nm → m and cm⁻¹ → m⁻¹ before substitution. Marks are routinely lost here.  
> 5. Continuous spectrum questions require **Planck's Law**; line spectrum questions require the **Rydberg Formula**. Do not cross-apply.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Differentiate clearly between a continuous spectrum, a line spectrum, and a band spectrum. Give one example of each in the context of ship-dynamics applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Continuous Spectrum | Line Spectrum | Band Spectrum |
|:-------|:-------------------|:--------------|:--------------|
| **Source** | Hot, dense body (solid/liquid) | Low-pressure atomic gas | Low-pressure molecular gas |
| **Nature** | Smooth, unbroken band of all wavelengths | Sharp, discrete, isolated lines | Closely spaced cluster of lines forming a band |
| **Origin** | Broadened/overlapping atomic energy levels | Quantized electronic transitions in isolated atoms | Electronic + vibrational + rotational transitions in molecules |
| **Ship Example** | IR radiation from a hot funnel or engine bay | Optical emission lines from sodium/mercury vapour in a stack plume | Underwater acoustic signature of cavitation noise |

*(Allocate 1 mark per spectrum type with correct example.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]`
**State and explain Wien's Displacement Law. A ship's funnel has a surface temperature of 600 K. Find the wavelength at which its IR emission peaks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Wien's Displacement Law:** The wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which the spectral radiance of a blackbody is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature $T$:

$$
\lambda_{\max} \, T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{600} = 4.83 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 4830 \, \text{nm}
$$

This wavelength lies in the **mid-infrared (MIR)** band, the typical detection range of naval IR search-and-track systems. *(1 mark — statement; 1 mark — explanation; 1 mark — numerical result with units.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 between two sub-parts (a) and (b). The internal choice means the student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary 2024]`

**Derive Planck's Law of blackbody radiation in qualitative terms and use it to obtain (i) Wien's Displacement Law and (ii) the Stefan–Boltzmann Law. Apply the results to compute the total radiated power from a ship's funnel of area 2.5 m² at 700 K, assuming it behaves as a blackbody.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law and Stefan–Boltzmann Law (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Write Planck's spectral radiance formula:

$$
B_\lambda(T) = \frac{2 h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c / (\lambda k_B T)} - 1}
$$

**Step 2:** For Wien's Displacement Law, set $\dfrac{d B_\lambda}{d \lambda} = 0$. Introduce $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{\max} k_B T}$ to obtain:

$$
x \, e^{x} = 5(e^{x} - 1)
$$

Numerical solution: $x = 4.9651$.

**Step 3:** Back-substitute to get:

$$
\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{h c}{x \, k_B} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{4.9651 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23}} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K}
$$

**Step 4:** For Stefan–Boltzmann, integrate $B_\lambda$ over all wavelengths and the entire solid angle ($\pi$ sr from a flat emitter):

$$
E_b = \pi \int_{0}^{\infty} B_\lambda(T) \, d\lambda
$$

Let $u = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$, so $\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{u k_B T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{h c}{u^2 k_B T} \, du$. Then:

$$
E_b = \pi \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 h c^2}{(h c / u k_B T)^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{u} - 1} \cdot \frac{h c}{u^2 k_B T} \, du
$$

Simplify the powers of $h c$ and $k_B T$:

$$
E_b = 2 \pi h c^2 \cdot \frac{(k_B T)^5}{(h c)^5} \cdot \frac{h c}{k_B T} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^{u} - 1} \, du
$$

The integral evaluates to $\dfrac{\pi^4}{15}$. Therefore:

$$
E_b = 2 \pi h c^2 \cdot \frac{(k_B T)^4}{(h c)^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4
$$

The bracketed constant is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**:

$$
\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W \cdot m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}
$$

Hence:

$$
E_b = \sigma T^4
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application to the Ship's Funnel (7 marks)

**Given:**  
- Area: $A = 2.5 \, \text{m}^2$  
- Temperature: $T = 700 \, \text{K}$  
- Emissivity: $\varepsilon = 1$ (blackbody assumption)  
- $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W \cdot m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$

**Formula:**

$$
P = \sigma \varepsilon A T^4
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $T^4$:**

$$
T^4 = (700)^4 = 7^4 \times 10^8 = 2401 \times 10^8 = 2.401 \times 10^{11} \, \text{K}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the product $A T^4$:**

$$
A T^4 = 2.5 \times 2.401 \times 10^{11} = 6.0025 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power:**

$$
P = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 1 \times 6.0025 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
P = 5.670 \times 6.0025 \times 10^{3} = 34.03 \times 10^{3} \, \text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, P \approx 34.0 \, \text{kW} \,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 34 kW continuous IR emission at the funnel makes the ship easily detectable by modern IRST (Infrared Search and Track) systems at ranges exceeding 10 km. This is the rationale for funnel-cooling systems and IR-suppression coatings on stealth vessels.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the radii of the allowed electron orbits in a hydrogen-like atom, and hence obtain the Rydberg formula for the line spectrum of hydrogen. The first line of the Balmer series has wavelength 656.3 nm. Calculate the corresponding transition and the energy of the photon emitted.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Rydberg Formula (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Apply Bohr's angular-momentum quantization:

$$
m_e v_n r_n = \frac{n h}{2 \pi}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Coulomb's law as centripetal force:

$$
\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2} = \frac{m_e v_n^2}{r_n}
$$

**Step 3:** Eliminate $v_n$ to obtain the orbit radius:

$$
r_n = \frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m_e Z e^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the total energy:

$$
E_n = -\frac{m_e Z^2 e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Bohr frequency condition for transition $n_2 \to n_1$:

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right), \quad R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6:** Identify spectral series:
- **Lyman series:** $n_1 = 1$ (Ultraviolet)  
- **Balmer series:** $n_1 = 2$ (Visible)  
- **Paschen series:** $n_1 = 3$ (Infrared)  
- **Brackett series:** $n_1 = 4$ (Far IR)  
- **Pfund series:** $n_1 = 5$ (Far IR)

---

#### Part (b) — Identification of the Balmer Line and Photon Energy (7 marks)

**Given:** $\lambda = 656.3 \, \text{nm} = 6.563 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}$

**Step 1 — Identify the series and transition:**

The Balmer series has $n_1 = 2$. The first line of the Balmer series (H-$\alpha$) corresponds to the transition $n_2 = 3 \to n_1 = 2$.

**Step 2 — Verify using the Rydberg formula:**

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2} \right) = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9} = \frac{9 - 4}{36} = \frac{5}{36} = 0.1389
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \times 0.1389 = 1.524 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^{-1}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{1.524 \times 10^6} = 6.563 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} = 656.3 \, \text{nm} \quad \checkmark
$$

The transition is confirmed: $n = 3 \to n = 2$ (Balmer-$\alpha$, red H-$\alpha$ line).

**Step 3 — Compute the photon energy:**

$$
E = h \nu = \frac{h c}{\lambda}
$$

$$
E = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6.563 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.563 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.029 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}
$$

Convert to electron-volts:

$$
E = \frac{3.029 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.89 \, \text{eV}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, E_{\text{photon}} = 3.03 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J} = 1.89 \, \text{eV} \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering context:** This red H-$\alpha$ emission at 656.3 nm is the dominant visible line in many plasma-based ship systems (e.g., magnetohydrodynamic thrusters, ion-propulsion diagnostics). Spectrometric monitoring of this line is used to characterise plasma temperature in marine engineering research.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1: Types of Spectra**

- **Three types of spectra:** Continuous (hot dense body), Line (atomic gas), Band (molecular gas).
- **Continuous spectrum** → governed by **Planck's Law**, **Wien's Displacement Law**, **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**.
- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m \cdot K}$.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** $P = \sigma \varepsilon A T^4$, with $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W \cdot m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$.
- **Line spectrum** → governed by the **Rydberg Formula** and **Bohr's Quantization Postulate**.
- **Rydberg Formula:** $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$, with $R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^{-1}$.
- **Spectral series identification:** Lyman ($n_1=1$, UV), Balmer ($n_1=2$, Visible), Paschen ($n_1=3$, IR), Brackett ($n_1=4$, Far IR), Pfund ($n_1=5$, Far IR).
- **Bohr orbit radius:** $r_n = a_0 \dfrac{n^2}{Z}$, with $a_0 = 5.292 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}$.
- **Energy levels (hydrogen):** $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6 \, \text{eV}}{n^2}$.
- **Band spectrum** → electronic + vibrational + rotational energy, with $E_v = (v + \tfrac{1}{2}) h \nu_0$ and $E_J = B J(J+1)$.
- **Photon energy relation:** $E = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ — applies to *all* three spectrum types.
- **Selection rule for atomic transitions:** $\Delta \ell = \pm 1$.
- **Selection rule for rotational transitions:** $\Delta J = \pm 1$.
- **Ship-dynamics application links:**  
  - Continuous spectrum ↔ IR ship signature (funnel, exhaust plume).  
  - Line spectrum ↔ Atomic emission from plasma thrusters & radar resonance modes.  
  - Band spectrum ↔ Underwater acoustic cavitation noise and atmospheric absorption.
- **Common KTU traps:** (1) Mixing $\lambda_{\max}$ and $\nu_{\max}$ versions of Wien's Law. (2) Forgetting that $n_1 < n_2$ in Rydberg. (3) Unit errors between nm and m. (4) Confusing atomic line spectrum with molecular band spectrum.
- **Numericals to master:** (i) Peak IR wavelength from a hot surface; (ii) Wavelength of a hydrogen spectral line; (iii) Total radiated power from a hot surface; (iv) Photon energy from wavelength.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Motions in Regular Waves** refer to the dynamic oscillatory response of a floating vessel subjected to a long-crested, monochromatic (single-frequency) sinusoidal wave excitation. The motion of the ship is mathematically described as a **six-degrees-of-freedom (6-DOF) rigid-body oscillation problem**, governed by Newton's second law applied to a floating body in an inviscid, incompressible, irrotational fluid, with hydrodynamic loads decomposed into **added mass**, **radiation damping**, and **restoring** components.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1, the focus is on **linear, frequency-domain, potential-flow-based seakeeping analysis** of a ship advancing (or stationary) in regular head/beam/oblique waves.

## 1.2 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)

A ship, treated as a **rigid body**, can oscillate in six independent modes. Three are **translational** and three are **rotational**.

| S.No | KTU Notation | Motion Name | Axis of Motion | Physical Description |
|------|--------------|-------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $x_1$ | **Surge** | Longitudinal (x) | Forward-backward translation |
| 2 | $x_2$ | **Sway** | Transverse (y) | Sideways translation |
| 3 | $x_3$ | **Heave** | Vertical (z) | Up-down translation |
| 4 | $x_4$ | **Roll** | About x-axis | Rotation about longitudinal axis |
| 5 | $x_5$ | **Pitch** | About y-axis | Rotation about transverse axis |
| 6 | $x_6$ | **Yaw** | About z-axis | Rotation about vertical axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The standard origin is typically placed at the **center of gravity (G)** of the ship, with the x-axis pointing **forward**, y-axis pointing to **port (left)**, and z-axis pointing **upward** (right-handed coordinate system).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cork floating in a bathtub** while you create regular ripples with your finger:

- The cork bobs **up and down** (this is **heave**).
- It tips **forward and backward** as waves pass (**pitch**).
- It rocks **side to side** (**roll**).
- The water around it has to "make way" — this is the **added mass** effect. It is as if the cork has grown heavier in water than in air.
- The cork also experiences a **resistance** to motion from the water — this is **damping**.
- A **buoyancy/spring-like force** pulls the cork back to its rest position — this is the **hydrostatic restoring force**.

So a ship in regular waves behaves like a **mass-spring-damper system** with six such systems (one per DOF), where the "spring" comes from **gravity/buoyancy** and the "damper" comes from **wave radiation**.

## 1.4 Regular Waves — Key Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regular Wave Definition:** A wave with constant amplitude $\zeta_a$, constant frequency $\omega_e$, and constant wavelength $\lambda$. It is described by:
> $$\zeta(x,t) = \zeta_a \cos(kx - \omega_e t)$$

where:
- $\zeta_a$ = wave amplitude (m) — **constant**
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ = wave number (rad/m)
- $\omega_e$ = **encounter frequency** (rad/s) — frequency as perceived by the moving ship
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)

> [!NOTE]
> The **encounter frequency** $\omega_e$ differs from the **absolute wave frequency** $\omega$ when the ship has a forward speed $U$. For head seas (waves from the front):
> $$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 U}{g} = \omega\left(1 - \frac{\omega U}{g}\right)$$
> For following seas: $\omega_e = \omega + \frac{\omega^2 U}{g}$.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ (standard KTU value for seawater)
- **Fresh water density:** $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Wave steepness limit:** $H/\lambda \leq 1/7$ (for linear/Airy wave theory validity)
- **Ship Block Coefficient range:** $C_B = 0.55$ to $0.85$ (typical for cargo/tanker ships)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Equation of Motion — Uncoupled Form

For a ship undergoing small oscillations in regular waves, the linearized equation of motion for each of the 6 DOFs takes the form of a **forced, second-order, ordinary differential equation** in the time domain:

$$(m + a_{nn}(\omega_e))\ddot{x}_n + b_{nn}(\omega_e)\dot{x}_n + c_{nn}\,x_n = F_{n_0}(\omega_e)\, e^{i\omega_e t}$$

where, for the $n^{th}$ mode of motion:

- $m$ = **ship mass** (or moment of inertia for rotational modes), in kg or kg·m²
- $a_{nn}(\omega_e)$ = **added mass coefficient** (frequency-dependent), in kg or kg·m²
- $b_{nn}(\omega_e)$ = **radiation damping coefficient** (frequency-dependent), in N·s/m or N·m·s
- $c_{nn}$ = **hydrostatic restoring coefficient** (frequency-INDEPENDENT), in N/m or N·m/rad
- $F_{n_0}(\omega_e)$ = **exciting force amplitude** (from incident/diffracted waves), in N or N·m
- $x_n(t)$ = the $n^{th}$ generalized coordinate (displacement or rotation)
- $\omega_e$ = **encounter frequency** (rad/s)

## 2.2 Why "Linear" and "Frequency-Dependent"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why linear?** Because the KTU Module 1 framework assumes small motions and small wave steepness ($H/\lambda \leq 1/7$). This allows us to **superpose** responses and use Airy wave theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why frequency-dependent?** The added mass $a_{nn}$ and damping $b_{nn}$ depend on $\omega_e$ because the water "remembers" the history of ship motion — this is captured by the **Cummins equation** (Convolution formulation). In the frequency domain, this history becomes multiplication by a frequency-dependent coefficient.

## 2.3 The Six Restoring Coefficients

These come from **Archimedes' principle** — when the ship is displaced from its equilibrium position, buoyancy shifts and creates a restoring moment/force.

| Mode $n$ | $c_{nn}$ (Restoring Term) | Physical Origin |
|----------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| Surge (1) | $0$ | No hydrostatic restoring — ship can drift |
| Sway (2) | $0$ | No hydrostatic restoring — ship can drift |
| Heave (3) | $\rho g A_W$ | Waterplane area $A_W$ |
| Roll (4) | $\rho g \overline{GM} \cdot \nabla$ | **GM** (metacentric height) × displacement volume $\nabla$ |
| Pitch (5) | $\rho g \overline{GM_L} \cdot \nabla$ | Longitudinal GM × displacement volume |
| Yaw (6) | $0$ | No hydrostatic restoring in pure yaw |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surge, sway, and yaw have zero hydrostatic restoring forces.** This means their equations of motion in regular waves are **first-order** at low frequencies (dominated by added mass and damping only) and the ship can drift indefinitely under steady wave forces. This is a frequent KTU exam question!

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\zeta(x,t) = \zeta_a \cos(kx - \omega_e t)$ | Regular wave elevation | m |
| 2 | $\omega_e = \omega - (kU\cos\mu)$ | Encounter frequency (head seas, $\mu=180°$) | rad/s |
| 3 | $(m + a_{nn})\ddot{x}_n + b_{nn}\dot{x}_n + c_{nn}x_n = F_{n_0}e^{i\omega_e t}$ | Equation of motion (uncoupled) | N or N·m |
| 4 | $\omega_{n,0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{c_{nn}}{m + a_{nn}(\omega_{n,0})}}$ | Natural frequency (transcendental) | rad/s |
| 5 | $X_n(\omega_e) = \dfrac{F_{n_0}(\omega_e)}{-\omega_e^2(m+a_{nn}) + i\omega_e b_{nn} + c_{nn}}$ | Frequency response (complex) | m or rad |
| 6 | $\text{RAO}_n = \dfrac{\vert X_n \vert}{\zeta_a}$ | Response Amplitude Operator | m/m or rad/m |
| 7 | $\text{RAO}_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1 - (\omega_e/\omega_{n,0})^2\right)^2 + (2\zeta_r \omega_e/\omega_{n,0})^2}}$ | RAO magnitude (damped SDOF form) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\zeta_r = \dfrac{b_{nn}}{2\sqrt{c_{nn}(m+a_{nn})}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{n,0} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m + a_{nn}}{c_{nn}}}$ | Natural period | s |
| 10 | $\nabla = (m/\rho)$ | Displaced volume (Archimedes) | m³ |
| 11 | $k = \omega^2/g$ | Dispersion relation (deep water) | rad/m |
| 12 | $k = \omega^2/(g\tanh kh)$ | Dispersion relation (finite depth $h$) | rad/m |
| 13 | $\lambda = 2\pi/k$ | Wavelength | m |
| 14 | $c = \omega/k$ | Wave celerity | m/s |
| 15 | $F_{D, \text{Froude-Krylov}} = \rho g A_W \zeta_a$ | Approx. heave exciting force | N |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** The table above uses `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute value bars (e.g., $\vert X_n \vert$) instead of the standard `|` pipe symbol. This is to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. In your KTU exam answer sheets, you can freely use the pipe symbol.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) is the **single most important output** of a ship-motion analysis. It tells naval architects:

- **At what wave frequency will the ship resonate** (peak RAO value)?
- **How large will the motion be** in a given sea state?
- **Is the ship safe?** (Avoidance of synchronous roll / parametric roll)
- **Crew comfort** levels (motion sickness incidence — ISO 2631).
- **Cargo securing** requirements.
- **Operability** of helicopters, weapon systems, or deck equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Software Used:** KTU expects familiarity with conceptual outputs of codes like **WAMIT** (3D panel method), **AQWA** (ANSYS), **NASTRAN**, **LAMP** (Lloyd's), and **VERES** (FEM-based). Strip theory codes: **MITSUI**, **STRIP**, and the classical **Salvesen–Tuck–Faltinsen** method.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heave Equation of Motion from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Newton's Second Law applied to a ship in heave (z-direction).

### Step 1 — Identify all vertical forces acting on the ship

For a ship heaving in regular waves, the vertical force balance at any instant $t$ is:

$$F_{\text{net},z} = m \ddot{z}(t)$$

The four contributing forces are:

1. **Inertia force from ship mass:** $F_{\text{inertia}} = m\ddot{z}$
2. **Hydrodynamic added mass force:** $F_{\text{added}} = a_{33}\ddot{z}$ (water "carried along" with ship)
3. **Radiation damping force:** $F_{\text{damp}} = b_{33}\dot{z}$ (energy radiated as waves)
4. **Hydrostatic restoring force:** $F_{\text{restore}} = -\rho g A_W \, z$ (linearized buoyancy change)
5. **Wave exciting force:** $F_{\text{exc}} = F_{3_0} \cos(\omega_e t)$ (Froude-Krylov + diffraction)

### Step 2 — Apply D'Alembert's Principle

Moving all motion-dependent terms to the LHS and external forcing to the RHS:

$$m\ddot{z} + a_{33}\ddot{z} + b_{33}\dot{z} + \rho g A_W \, z = F_{3_0}\cos(\omega_e t)$$

### Step 3 — Group terms

$$(m + a_{33})\ddot{z} + b_{33}\dot{z} + (\rho g A_W) z = F_{3_0}\cos(\omega_e t)$$

This is the **standard heave equation** in KTU Module 1 form.

### Step 4 — Identify the system parameters

$$\boxed{(m + a_{33})\ddot{z} + b_{33}\dot{z} + c_{33}z = F_{3_0}\cos(\omega_e t)}$$

with the **heave restoring coefficient**:

$$c_{33} = \rho g A_W$$

and the **undamped natural frequency**:

$$\omega_{3,0} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g A_W}{m + a_{33}(\omega_{3,0})}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Harmonic Response (Heave RAO)

Assume the steady-state solution is harmonic with the encounter frequency:

$$z(t) = \text{Re}\{Z\, e^{i\omega_e t}\} = Z_0 \cos(\omega_e t - \phi)$$

### Step 1 — Substitute into the equation of motion

Time derivatives:
- $\dot{z}(t) = i\omega_e Z e^{i\omega_e t}$
- $\ddot{z}(t) = -\omega_e^2 Z e^{i\omega_e t}$

The forcing term in complex form: $F = F_{3_0} e^{i\omega_e t}$

### Step 2 — Plug in and divide by $e^{i\omega_e t}$

$$(m + a_{33})(-\omega_e^2 Z) + b_{33}(i\omega_e Z) + c_{33} Z = F_{3_0}$$

### Step 3 — Factor out $Z$

$$Z \left[ -\omega_e^2 (m + a_{33}) + i\omega_e b_{33} + c_{33} \right] = F_{3_0}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the complex amplitude $Z$

$$Z(\omega_e) = \frac{F_{3_0}}{-\omega_e^2 (m + a_{33}) + i\omega_e b_{33} + c_{33}}$$

This is the **complex frequency response function**.

### Step 5 — Express as a magnitude and phase

The magnitude (steady-state heave amplitude) is:

$$|Z(\omega_e)| = \frac{F_{3_0}}{\sqrt{\left[c_{33} - \omega_e^2(m+a_{33})\right]^2 + (\omega_e b_{33})^2}}$$

The phase lag (between motion and forcing) is:

$$\phi(\omega_e) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_e b_{33}}{c_{33} - \omega_e^2(m+a_{33})}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Define the RAO

Dividing the motion amplitude by the wave amplitude $\zeta_a$:

$$\boxed{\text{RAO}_{\text{heave}} = \frac{|Z|}{\zeta_a} = \frac{F_{3_0}/\zeta_a}{\sqrt{\left[c_{33} - \omega_e^2(m+a_{33})\right]^2 + (\omega_e b_{33})^2}}}$$

The numerator $F_{3_0}/\zeta_a$ is called the **exciting force coefficient** or **Froude-Krylov transfer function**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Non-Dimensionalized RAO in Classical Damped-SDOF Form

Divide numerator and denominator of $|Z|$ by $c_{33}$:

$$|Z| = \frac{F_{3_0}/c_{33}}{\sqrt{\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{\omega_e}{\omega_{3,0}}\right)^2\right]^2 + \left(2\zeta_r \dfrac{\omega_e}{\omega_{3,0}}\right)^2}}$$

where we define the **frequency ratio** $\Omega = \omega_e/\omega_{3,0}$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta_r$:

$$\boxed{\zeta_r = \frac{b_{33}}{2\sqrt{c_{33}(m+a_{33})}} = \frac{b_{33}}{2(m+a_{33})\omega_{3,0}}}$$

Therefore:

$$|Z| = \frac{F_{3_0}/c_{33}}{\sqrt{(1-\Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta_r \Omega)^2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Encounter Frequency for Head Seas

A ship of speed $U$ sailing in **head seas** (waves from the bow, encounter angle $\mu = 180°$) experiences an encounter frequency different from the absolute wave frequency.

**Step 1:** Wave phase at the bow at time $t$ is $\Phi = kx - \omega t$. With ship moving forward, the bow position at time $t$ is $x = -Ut$ (relative to a fixed reference). The phase of the wave at the bow becomes:

$$\Phi(t) = k(-Ut) - \omega t = -(kU + \omega)t$$

**Step 2:** The apparent (encounter) angular frequency is the negative of the phase rate:

$$\omega_e = kU + \omega$$

Wait — KTU standard convention (head seas) is:

$$\boxed{\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 U}{g} = \omega\left(1 - \frac{\omega U}{g}\right)}$$

**Step 3:** This comes from the dispersion relation $k = \omega^2/g$ (deep water) substituted in $kU$:

$$kU = \frac{\omega^2 U}{g}$$

**Step 4:** For general encounter angle $\mu$ (measured from bow):

$$\boxed{\omega_e = \omega - kU \cos\mu = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 U \cos\mu}{g}}$$

**Step 5:** For **beam seas** ($\mu = 90°$): $\cos\mu = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_e = \omega$.
**Step 6:** For **following seas** ($\mu = 0°$): $\omega_e = \omega(1 - U\omega/g) \cdot$ ... wait, with $\cos\mu = +1$, $\omega_e = \omega - \omega^2 U/g$ (reduced). For head seas $\cos\mu = -1$ in this convention so the sign flips. Always check sign convention per lecturer.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Coupling Between Heave and Pitch (KTU Favourite)

For a ship, the equations of motion in heave ($z$) and pitch ($\theta$) are **NOT truly independent** — there is a coupling through:

- **Off-diagonal added mass:** $a_{35}, a_{53}$ — coupling through forward-motion of water.
- **Off-diagonal damping:** $b_{35}, b_{53}$.
- **Off-diagonal restoring:** $c_{35} = c_{53} = -\rho g \oint z_W \, x \, d\ell$ (the "waterplane moment").

The coupled 2×2 system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(m + a_{33})\ddot{z} + a_{35}\ddot{\theta} + b_{33}\dot{z} + b_{35}\dot{\theta} + c_{33}z + c_{35}\theta &= F_3 e^{i\omega_e t} \\
a_{53}\ddot{z} + (I_{yy} + a_{55})\ddot{\theta} + b_{53}\dot{z} + b_{55}\dot{\theta} + c_{53}z + c_{55}\theta &= F_5 e^{i\omega_e t}
\end{aligned}
$$

In **matrix form**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
m + a_{33} & a_{35} \\
a_{53} & I_{yy} + a_{55}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix}
\ddot{z} \\ \ddot{\theta}
\end{Bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
b_{33} & b_{35} \\
b_{53} & b_{55}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix}
\dot{z} \\ \dot{\theta}
\end{Bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
c_{33} & c_{35} \\
c_{53} & c_{55}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix}
z \\ \theta
\end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix}
F_3 \\ F_5
\end{Bmatrix}
e^{i\omega_e t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Because the pitch motion $\theta$ contributes to the vertical displacement at any point along the ship length $x$ from the CG: $z(x) = z_G - x\theta$. So even at the **bow** (large $x$), the **heave RAO** is enhanced near the pitch resonance, and vice versa. This is why KTU 14-mark questions often ask for **bow vertical motion RAO**.

## 3.6 Summary of Analytical Procedure (Algorithm for the KTU 14-mark Answer)

1. **Input ship particulars:** $L, B, T, C_B, m, GM, GM_L, A_W, \nabla$.
2. **Compute** $\omega_{n,0}$ iteratively from transcendental equation (Step 4, §3.1).
3. **For each** $\omega_e$ (sweep over wave periods $T = 4$ s to $T = 20$ s):
   - Read/compute $a_{nn}(\omega_e), b_{nn}(\omega_e), F_{n_0}(\omega_e)$ from strip theory or 3D panel method.
   - Compute complex $Z(\omega_e)$ from §3.2 Step 4.
   - Compute $\text{RAO}_n = |Z|/\zeta_a$.
4. **Plot RAO vs** $T$ or $\omega_e$.
5. **Identify resonance peak** and corresponding critical period $T_{\text{crit}} \approx 2\pi/\omega_{n,0}$.

## 4.5 Physical Interpretation of Each DOF

| DOF | Natural Period (Typical) | Resonance Consequence |
|-----|-------------------------|------------------------|
| Heave | 5–10 s | Slamming, deck wetness |
| Pitch | 6–12 s | Bow emergence, propeller racing |
| Roll | 10–25 s | Synchronous roll, capsize risk |
| Surge | None (zero restoring) | Low-frequency drift |
| Sway | None (zero restoring) | Low-frequency drift |
| Yaw | None (zero restoring) | Course-keeping issues |
