# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) for ship motions. Why is it considered the most important output of a regular-wave seakeeping analysis? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** is defined as the **complex (or magnitude) ratio of the steady-state amplitude of a ship motion response to the amplitude of the incident regular wave**, expressed as a function of encounter frequency $\omega_e$ (or equivalently, wave period $T$ or wavelength $\lambda$).

$$\text{RAO}_n(\omega_e) = \frac{|X_n(\omega_e)|}{\zeta_a} = \frac{|X_n(\omega_e)|}{\zeta_a}$$

For a translational mode like heave, RAO has units of m/m (dimensionless, scale factor). For a rotational mode like pitch, RAO has units of rad/m.

**Why it is the most important output:**

1. **Frequency-domain transfer function:** Once the RAO is known for a ship, its motion in **any irregular sea** can be predicted by multiplying with the wave spectrum (linear superposition): $S_{xx}(\omega) = \vert \text{RAO}(\omega) \vert^2 S_{\zeta\zeta}(\omega)$.
2. **Resonance identification:** The peak of the RAO curve identifies the **critical wave period** at which the ship is most vulnerable.
3. **Operability assessment:** RAO values at specific frequencies are used in seakeeping criteria (DNV, Lloyd's, ISO 2631) for operability and habitability.
4. **Design feedback:** Modifying hull form, GM, or bilge keels changes the RAO — hence the RAO drives **design iterations**.

*(Self-contained, 3-mark ideal answer)*

---

### Question 2: Explain why the hydrostatic restoring coefficient $c_{nn}$ is zero for surge, sway, and yaw modes of a ship. What are the practical consequences? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The hydrostatic restoring force arises from the change in buoyancy when the ship is displaced or rotated from its equilibrium floating position. The restoring coefficient is computed from the **second derivative of the change in buoyancy potential energy** with respect to the displacement.

**For surge ($c_{11}$):** When the ship is translated forward by $\delta x$ in still water, the **waterplane area and shape remain unchanged**. The displaced volume is the same. Therefore, the change in buoyancy is zero, giving $c_{11} = 0$.

**For sway ($c_{22}$):** Symmetric translation in the transverse direction does not change the displaced volume — only the *position* of the underwater hull changes. Hence $c_{22} = 0$.

**For yaw ($c_{66}$):** Pure rotation about the vertical axis (z-axis) at the waterplane level leaves the waterplane geometry invariant. The displaced volume and its center remain the same. Hence $c_{66} = 0$.

**Practical consequences:**

1. **No equilibrium position:** The ship has no preferred heading or longitudinal position in calm water — it can drift indefinitely under steady second-order wave drift forces.
2. **Low-frequency dynamics dominate:** Without restoring stiffness, the surge, sway, and yaw motions are governed mainly by **added mass and damping** at low frequencies, leading to large-amplitude slow oscillations ("slow-drift motions") in irregular seas.
3. **Course-keeping difficulty:** Vessels with weak heading stability (e.g., ultra-large container ships) may exhibit undesirable yaw oscillations in quartering seas.
4. **Mooring system design:** Zero $c_{11}, c_{22}, c_{66}$ means that **mooring lines and thrusters** must provide the entire low-frequency restoring action when a ship is at berth.

*(3-mark answer — emphasize the physical "no change in displaced volume" argument.)*

---

## PART B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

A displacement-type ship has the following particulars:
- Length $L = 120$ m, Beam $B = 18$ m, Draft $T = 6.0$ m
- Displacement $\nabla = 12{,}000$ m³ (volume)
- Waterplane area $A_W = 2000$ m²
- Metacentric height $\overline{GM} = 1.2$ m, Longitudinal GM $\overline{GM_L} = 90$ m
- Radius of gyration in pitch $k_{55} = 0.25 L$
- At a particular encounter frequency, the heave added mass $a_{33} = 0.20 \times m$ and heave damping $b_{33} = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N·s/m
- Wave amplitude $\zeta_a = 1.0$ m
- Heave exciting force amplitude $F_{3_0} = 8.0 \times 10^5$ N
- Sea water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

#### Part (a) — 7 marks: Compute the heave natural frequency $\omega_{3,0}$ and natural period $T_{3,0}$. Comment on whether the ship's heave resonance is in the typical wave-encounter frequency range.

**Step 1: Compute ship mass $m$.**
$$m = \rho \nabla = 1025 \times 12{,}000 = 1.23 \times 10^7 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Compute heave restoring coefficient $c_{33}$.**
$$c_{33} = \rho g A_W = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2000 = 2.011 \times 10^7 \; \text{N/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute effective heave mass $(m + a_{33})$.**
$$a_{33} = 0.20 \times m = 0.20 \times 1.23 \times 10^7 = 2.46 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg}$$
$$m + a_{33} = 1.23 \times 10^7 + 2.46 \times 10^6 = 1.476 \times 10^7 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 4: Compute natural frequency $\omega_{3,0}$.**
$$\omega_{3,0} = \sqrt{\frac{c_{33}}{m + a_{33}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.011 \times 10^7}{1.476 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{1.3625} = 1.167 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute natural period $T_{3,0}$.**
$$T_{3,0} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_{3,0}} = \frac{2\pi}{1.167} = 5.385 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 6: Engineering comment.**
Typical ocean wave periods are 4 s to 14 s. The computed $T_{3,0} = 5.39$ s falls **squarely within the operational range** of ocean wave encounter periods for a ship of 120 m length. Therefore, the ship is **vulnerable to heave resonance** in head or bow seas of period ~5.4 s, which corresponds to a wavelength $\lambda = g T^2 / (2\pi) = 9.81 \times 29 / 6.28 = 45.3$ m (a wavelength comparable to half the ship length $L/\lambda \approx 2.65$, which is the typical heave-resonance range of $L/\lambda \approx 1.5$ to $3.0$).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks: For the given encounter frequency, compute the heave steady-state amplitude $|Z|$, the phase lag $\phi$, and the heave RAO.

Assume the encounter frequency $\omega_e = 1.20$ rad/s (slightly above resonance).

**Step 1: Compute the stiffness term $c_{33} - \omega_e^2 (m + a_{33})$.**
$$\omega_e^2 = 1.20^2 = 1.44$$
$$\omega_e^2 (m + a_{33}) = 1.44 \times 1.476 \times 10^7 = 2.125 \times 10^7 \; \text{N/m}$$
$$c_{33} - \omega_e^2 (m + a_{33}) = 2.011 \times 10^7 - 2.125 \times 10^7 = -1.143 \times 10^6 \; \text{N/m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the damping term $\omega_e b_{33}$.**
$$\omega_e b_{33} = 1.20 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 = 2.4 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the magnitude of the denominator.**
$$\sqrt{(-1.143 \times 10^6)^2 + (2.4 \times 10^5)^2} = \sqrt{1.306 \times 10^{12} + 5.76 \times 10^{10}} = \sqrt{1.364 \times 10^{12}} = 1.168 \times 10^6 \; \text{N/m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the heave amplitude.**
$$|Z| = \frac{F_{3_0}}{1.168 \times 10^6} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^5}{1.168 \times 10^6} = 0.685 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Compute the phase lag $\phi$ (motion lags excitation).**
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2.4 \times 10^5}{-1.143 \times 10^6}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.21)$$
Since the stiffness term is **negative** (above resonance), the heave motion is in **anti-phase** with the wave at this frequency:
$$\phi = 180° - \tan^{-1}(0.21) = 180° - 11.86° = 168.1° \approx 168°$$

**Step 6: Compute the RAO.**
$$\text{RAO}_{\text{heave}} = \frac{|Z|}{\zeta_a} = \frac{0.685}{1.0} = 0.685 \; \text{m/m}$$

**Step 7: Compute the damping ratio $\zeta_r$ (extra insight, for full marks).**
$$\zeta_r = \frac{b_{33}}{2(m + a_{33})\omega_{3,0}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2 \times 1.476 \times 10^7 \times 1.167} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{3.444 \times 10^7} = 0.00581$$
This is a **low damping ratio** (~0.6%), confirming that the heave response near resonance will be **sharply peaked** — typical of a slender ship hull.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

A container ship of length $L = 200$ m sails at speed $U = 15$ knots ($= 7.72$ m/s) in deep water. The ship encounters regular head seas (encounter angle $\mu = 180°$) of absolute wave period $T = 8$ s. The pitch hydrostatic restoring coefficient is $c_{55} = 1.5 \times 10^9$ N·m/rad. The pitch added mass moment of inertia at the encounter frequency is $a_{55} = 0.30 \times I_{yy}$. The pitch moment of inertia $I_{yy} = m k_{55}^2$ with $k_{55} = 0.25 L$. Ship mass $m = 4.0 \times 10^7$ kg. The pitch exciting moment amplitude is $M_{5_0} = 1.2 \times 10^8$ N·m. Pitch damping $b_{55} = 1.5 \times 10^8$ N·m·s/rad. Wave amplitude $\zeta_a = 1.5$ m.

#### Part (a) — 7 marks: Compute the encounter frequency $\omega_e$, the pitch natural frequency $\omega_{5,0}$, and the natural period $T_{5,0}$. Verify whether the encounter frequency is below, at, or above pitch resonance.

**Step 1: Compute absolute wave frequency $\omega$.**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{8} = 0.7854 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute wave number $k$ using deep-water dispersion.**
$$k = \frac{\omega^2}{g} = \frac{0.7854^2}{9.81} = \frac{0.6168}{9.81} = 0.0629 \; \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute encounter frequency for head seas.**

Using the KTU convention $\omega_e = \omega - (kU\cos\mu)$, with $\cos(180°) = -1$:

$$\omega_e = 0.7854 - (0.0629)(7.72)(-1) = 0.7854 + 0.4856 = 1.271 \; \text{rad/s}$$

> *[Note: For head seas, the encounter frequency is HIGHER than the absolute frequency because the ship is moving INTO the waves.]*

**Step 4: Compute pitch moment of inertia $I_{yy}$.**
$$k_{55} = 0.25 L = 0.25 \times 200 = 50 \; \text{m}$$
$$I_{yy} = m k_{55}^2 = 4.0 \times 10^7 \times 50^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{11} \; \text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 5: Compute effective pitch inertia $(I_{yy} + a_{55})$.**
$$a_{55} = 0.30 \times I_{yy} = 0.30 \times 1.0 \times 10^{11} = 3.0 \times 10^{10} \; \text{kg·m}^2$$
$$I_{yy} + a_{55} = 1.0 \times 10^{11} + 3.0 \times 10^{10} = 1.3 \times 10^{11} \; \text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 6: Compute pitch natural frequency $\omega_{5,0}$.**
$$\omega_{5,0} = \sqrt{\frac{c_{55}}{I_{yy} + a_{55}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{1.3 \times 10^{11}}} = \sqrt{1.154 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1074 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 7: Compute pitch natural period $T_{5,0}$.**
$$T_{5,0} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_{5,0}} = \frac{2\pi}{0.1074} = 58.5 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 8: Compare $\omega_e$ with $\omega_{5,0}$.**

Since $\omega_e = 1.271 \; \text{rad/s} \gg \omega_{5,0} = 0.1074 \; \text{rad/s}$, the encounter frequency is **FAR ABOVE** the pitch natural frequency.

The frequency ratio is:
$$\Omega = \frac{\omega_e}{\omega_{5,0}} = \frac{1.271}{0.1074} = 11.83 \gg 1$$

This means the ship is in the **mass-dominated (high-frequency) regime**, where pitch response is small and decays as $1/\omega_e^2$. The ship is **NOT in pitch resonance** for this sea state.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks: Compute the pitch steady-state amplitude, phase, and RAO. State the practical implication of your answer.

**Step 1: Compute stiffness term $c_{55} - \omega_e^2 (I_{yy} + a_{55})$.**
$$\omega_e^2 = 1.271^2 = 1.615$$
$$\omega_e^2 (I_{yy} + a_{55}) = 1.615 \times 1.3 \times 10^{11} = 2.100 \times 10^{11} \; \text{N·m/rad}$$
$$c_{55} - \omega_e^2 (I_{yy} + a_{55}) = 1.5 \times 10^9 - 2.100 \times 10^{11} = -2.085 \times 10^{11} \; \text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2: Compute damping term $\omega_e b_{55}$.**
$$\omega_e b_{55} = 1.271 \times 1.5 \times 10^8 = 1.907 \times 10^8 \; \text{N·m·s/rad}$$

**Step 3: Compute the magnitude of the denominator.**
$$\sqrt{(-2.085 \times 10^{11})^2 + (1.907 \times 10^8)^2} = \sqrt{4.347 \times 10^{22} + 3.637 \times 10^{16}}$$
$$\approx 2.085 \times 10^{11} \; \text{N·m/rad}$$ (damping term is negligible compared to stiffness)

**Step 4: Compute pitch amplitude.**
$$|\Theta| = \frac{M_{5_0}}{2.085 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^8}{2.085 \times 10^{11}} = 5.755 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{rad}$$

Converting to degrees:
$$|\Theta| = 5.755 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.0330°$$

**Step 5: Compute the phase lag.**
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.907 \times 10^8}{-2.085 \times 10^{11}}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-9.146 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 180° - 0.0524° \approx 179.95°$$

**Step 6: Compute the pitch RAO.**
$$\text{RAO}_{\text{pitch}} = \frac{|\Theta|}{\zeta_a} = \frac{5.755 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5} = 3.84 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 7: Practical implication.**

The pitch RAO is **very small** ($3.84 \times 10^{-4}$ rad/m), corresponding to a pitch amplitude of only 0.033° per meter of wave amplitude. This indicates that:

1. The ship behaves essentially as a **rigid body** in pitch for this short-period sea state — the waves are too rapid for the ship to follow.
2. The **bow vertical motion** at $x = L/2 = 100$ m from CG is $z_{\text{bow}} = |\Theta| \times 100 = 0.0576$ m ≈ 5.8 cm — negligible.
3. **No risk of bow emergence or slamming** at this ship speed and sea state.
4. However, this calculation is for a **single regular wave component**. In a real ocean spectrum with longer-period components (say $T = 12$–$15$ s), the encounter frequency would drop closer to $\omega_{5,0}$, and pitch response could grow by 1–2 orders of magnitude. Therefore, **operability assessments must integrate over the full wave spectrum**, not just the peak frequency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of encounter frequency:** In head seas, $\omega_e > \omega$ (not less!). Mixing up signs loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Units of RAO:** Heave RAO is dimensionless (m/m), pitch RAO is **rad/m** (NOT dimensionless). Examiner deducts if you write "m/m" for pitch.
> 3. **Forgetting to add $a_{nn}$ to $m$:** Many students compute $\omega_{n,0} = \sqrt{c_{nn}/m}$ — this is WRONG. The added mass term MUST be included, and since $a_{nn}$ depends on frequency, the equation is **transcendental** and solved iteratively.
> 4. **Confusing restoring stiffness for roll vs pitch:** Roll uses $c_{44} = \rho g \overline{GM} \nabla$ (with $\overline{GM} \approx 1$–$2$ m for typical ships), while pitch uses $c_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM_L} \nabla$ (with $\overline{GM_L} \approx 50$–$200$ m — much larger). Hence pitch natural frequency is much lower than roll natural frequency.
> 5. **Phase angle convention:** Some textbooks define $\phi$ as the phase of motion *leading* the wave, others as *lagging*. State your convention clearly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **6-DOF**: Surge, Sway, Heave, Roll, Pitch, Yaw. Three translational + three rotational.
- [x] **Regular wave equation**: $\zeta(x,t) = \zeta_a \cos(kx - \omega_e t)$. Linear, monochromatic, long-crested.
- [x] **Encounter frequency** in head seas: $\omega_e = \omega + \omega^2 U / g$ (HIGHER than $\omega$).
- [x] **Equation of motion** (uncoupled form): $(m + a_{nn})\ddot{x} + b_{nn}\dot{x} + c_{nn}x = F_{n_0}e^{i\omega_e t}$.
- [x] **Three hydrodynamic forces/moments**: Added inertia (frequency-dependent), radiation damping (frequency-dependent), hydrostatic restoring (frequency-INdependent).
- [x] **Wave excitation forces**: Froude–Krylov (incident wave pressure on instantaneous wetted hull) + diffraction (scattering by the hull).
- [x] **Restoring coefficients**: Heave $c_{33} = \rho g A_W$, Roll $c_{44} = \rho g \overline{GM} \nabla$, Pitch $c_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM_L} \nabla$. **Surge, Sway, Yaw → ZERO restoring.**
- [x] **Natural frequency**: $\omega_{n,0} = \sqrt{c_{nn}/(m + a_{nn}(\omega_{n,0}))}$ — transcendental (iterative solution).
- [x] **Steady-state response** (complex): $X_n(\omega_e) = F_{n_0} / \left[ -\omega_e^2(m + a_{nn}) + i\omega_e b_{nn} + c_{nn} \right]$.
- [x] **RAO**: $\text{RAO}_n = |X_n| / \zeta_a$. Most important output of seakeeping analysis.
- [x] **Damping ratio**: $\zeta_r = b_{nn}/[2(m + a_{nn})\omega_{n,0}]$. Ships typically have $\zeta_r \sim 0.01$ to $0.10$ — lightly damped.
- [x] **Typical resonance regimes**: Heave $T \approx 5$–$10$ s, Pitch $T \approx 6$–$12$ s, Roll $T \approx 10$–$25$ s.
- [x] **Coupling**: Heave–pitch coupling is the most important (waterplane moment $c_{35} \neq 0$). Roll–pitch coupling is generally weak except for oblique seas.
- [x] **Strip theory** (Salvesen–Tuck–Faltinsen): 2D section-by-section solution integrated along ship length; standard KTU Module 1 tool.
- [x] **3D Panel Methods** (WAMIT, AQWA, NEMOH): Solve the boundary value problem on a meshed hull; required for low-frequency and multi-body problems.
- [x] **Frequency-domain vs time-domain**: Module 1 uses frequency-domain (RAOs). Time-domain (impulse response functions via Cummins equation) is Module 2/3 territory.
- [x] **Limitation of linear theory**: Motion amplitudes $<\!\!< \zeta_a$; wave steepness $H/\lambda \leq 1/7$. Beyond this, nonlinear effects (wave breaking, viscous roll damping, slamming) dominate.
- [x] **Industry link**: RAO × Wave Spectrum → Motion Spectrum → Operability, safety, comfort statistics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equations of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In **Ship Dynamics**, the **Equations of Motion** refer to the set of **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** rigid-body dynamic equations that mathematically describe the motion of a ship in calm water or in waves, formulated with respect to a **body-fixed coordinate system** (origin usually at the ship's center of gravity, G). These equations are derived from **Newton's Second Law** (for translations) and **Euler's Rotational Equations** (for rotations), and are the foundation of seakeeping analysis, maneuvering prediction, and controller design.

Following the **SNAME (1950) notation**, the 6-DOF motions are:

| DOF Index | Translation / Rotation | Symbol | Velocity / Angular Velocity | Force / Moment |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Surge (longitudinal) | $x$ | $u$ | $X$ |
| 2 | Sway (lateral) | $y$ | $v$ | $Y$ |
| 3 | Heave (vertical) | $z$ | $w$ | $Z$ |
| 4 | Roll (about x-axis) | $\phi$ | $p$ | $K$ |
| 5 | Pitch (about y-axis) | $\theta$ | $q$ | $M$ |
| 6 | Yaw (about z-axis) | $\psi$ | $r$ | $N$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PCNST501 establishes the rigid-body kinematics and dynamics governing the motion of a ship. The student must master the 6-DOF vector formulation, the body-fixed vs. earth-fixed frame transformation, and the force/moment decomposition prior to studying wave-induced responses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diving Board" Intuition

Imagine pushing a **3-D boat-shaped fidget toy** floating in a bathtub. When you push it forward, it doesn't just move straight — it also slightly rotates, dips, and wiggles side-to-side. **Equations of Motion are the mathematical "rule book" that predicts exactly how the toy moves** when you apply any push, any twist, or any passing wave.

- The **three translation equations** are like saying *"how fast does the toy's center of mass accelerate in each direction?"* (Newton: F = ma).
- The **three rotation equations** are like saying *"how fast does the toy spin around each of its three axes?"* (Euler: M = Iα).
- The **hydrodynamic, hydrostatic, and wave forces** are the "pushing hands" of the surrounding water on the toy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The full 6-DOF set is **coupled** in general — meaning roll motion, for example, can influence sway and yaw simultaneously. **Decoupling** (separating surge, lateral, and vertical motion groups) is a common KTU-acceptable simplification when the physics justifies it (e.g., symmetric ship, small motions).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Conventions

- **Coordinate origin:** Center of gravity $G$ of the ship at the still water level (or at design waterline — both are conventions; SNAME recommends the centerline plane of symmetry).
- **x-axis:** Positive forward (toward bow).
- **y-axis:** Positive to port (left side facing forward).
- **z-axis:** Positive downward (some texts use positive upward — KTU typically follows **positive downward** to align with hydrostatic pressure convention).
- **Units (SI):** Forces in **N (Newtons)**, masses in **kg**, lengths in **m**, moments in **N·m**, moments of inertia in **kg·m²**, accelerations in **m/s²**, angular accelerations in **rad/s²**.

> [!TIP]
> Always state the **sign convention** assumed in your free-body diagram at the start of every KTU examination answer. A wrong sign convention is the **single most common reason** for losing full marks in ship dynamics problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reference Frames in Ship Dynamics

Two reference frames are used:

1. **Earth-Fixed (Inertial) Frame** $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0)$ — fixed in space; Newton's laws apply directly here.
2. **Body-Fixed (Moving) Frame** $(x, y, z)$ — translates and rotates with the ship; equations are easier to write in this frame but contain Coriolis and centripetal terms.

The transformation between angular velocities in the two frames uses **Euler angles**: roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$.

### 2.2 The 6-DOF Equations — General Vector Form

The total external force vector $\vec{F}_{ext}$ and moment vector $\vec{M}_{ext}$ on the ship equal the rate of change of linear and angular momentum in the **earth-fixed frame**, expressed in the **body-fixed frame**:

$$m \left( \dot{\vec{V}}_{G} + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_{G} \right) = \vec{F}_{ext}$$

$$\frac{d \vec{H}_G}{dt} + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G = \vec{M}_{ext}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of the ship (kg)
- $\vec{V}_G = [u, v, w]^T$ = translational velocity of G in body-fixed frame
- $\vec{\Omega} = [p, q, r]^T$ = angular velocity in body-fixed frame
- $\vec{H}_G = [I_x p, I_y q, I_z r]^T$ = angular momentum about G

### 2.3 Force/Moment Decomposition

The total external force and moment are split into physically meaningful parts:

$$\vec{F}_{ext} = \vec{F}_{HS} + \vec{F}_{HR} + \vec{F}_{EX} + \vec{F}_{W} + \vec{F}_{C}$$

$$\vec{M}_{ext} = \vec{M}_{HS} + \vec{M}_{HR} + \vec{M}_{EX} + \vec{M}_{W} + \vec{M}_{C}$$

Where each subscript denotes:

- **HS** = **Hydrostatic** (buoyancy + gravity restoring; depends on position)
- **HR** = **Hydrodynamic Radiation** (added mass + radiation damping; depends on acceleration and velocity)
- **EX** = **Hydrodynamic Excitation** (Froude–Krylov + diffraction from incident waves)
- **W** = **Wind** force/moment
- **C** = **Current** interaction; also mooring and control forces

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Table

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Key Variables | Engineering Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Surge (Newton) | $m(\dot{u} - vr + wq) = X$ | $u$ = surge velocity | Longitudinal motion prediction |
| 2 | Sway (Newton) | $m(\dot{v} + ur - wp) = Y$ | $v$ = sway velocity | Lateral path control |
| 3 | Heave (Newton) | $m(\dot{w} - uq + vp) = Z$ | $w$ = heave velocity | Vertical motion, slamming |
| 4 | Roll (Euler) | $I_x \dot{p} + (I_z - I_y)qr = K$ | $p$ = roll rate | Stability analysis |
| 5 | Pitch (Euler) | $I_y \dot{q} + (I_x - I_z)rp = M$ | $q$ = pitch rate | Trim, bow emergence |
| 6 | Yaw (Euler) | $I_z \dot{r} + (I_y - I_x)pq = N$ | $r$ = yaw rate | Course keeping |
| 7 | Hydrostatic heave | $Z_{HS} = -\rho g A_{WP} \cdot z$ | $A_{WP}$ = waterplane area | Buoyancy restoring |
| 8 | Hydrostatic roll moment | $K_{HS} = -W \cdot \overline{GM}_T \cdot \phi$ | $\overline{GM}_T$ = transverse metacentric height | Initial stability |
| 9 | Hydrostatic pitch moment | $M_{HS} = -W \cdot \overline{GM}_L \cdot \theta$ | $\overline{GM}_L$ = longitudinal metacentric height | Longitudinal stability |
| 10 | Linearized radiation force | $F_{HR} = -A_{ii}\ddot{\eta}_i - B_{ii}\dot{\eta}_i$ | $A_{ii}$ = added mass, $B_{ii}$ = damping | Frequency-domain RAO |
| 11 | Decoupled roll ODE | $(I_x + A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + W \overline{GM}_T \phi = K_E$ | $K_E$ = excitation moment | Roll decay, natural roll period |
| 12 | Heave-pitch 2-DOF | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} + \mathbf{B}\dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} + \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\eta} = \mathbf{F}_{EX}$ | $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}$ = 2×2 matrices | Vertical plane seakeeping |
| 13 | Kinematic rotation matrix | $\mathbf{R}_{BE} = \mathbf{R}_z(\psi)\mathbf{R}_y(\theta)\mathbf{R}_x(\phi)$ | — | Frame transformation |
| 14 | Angular velocity (body) | $p = \dot{\phi} - \dot{\psi}\sin\theta$, $q = \dot{\theta}\cos\phi + \dot{\psi}\cos\theta\sin\phi$, $r = \dot{\psi}\cos\theta\cos\phi - \dot{\theta}\sin\phi$ | — | Body vs. Euler rates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** For the ESE, you may use the **simplified uncoupled forms** (Equation 11) when the problem statement specifies "small motions" and "symmetric ship." For 14-mark problems involving seakeeping, you should write the **coupled matrix form** (Equation 12).

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where These Equations Are Used

1. **Seakeeping Analysis** — Predicting ship responses (heave, pitch, roll) in regular and irregular waves for design and operability assessment.
2. **Maneuvering Prediction** — Simulating turning circles, zig-zag maneuvers, and course stability for IMO maneuverability standards.
3. **Dynamic Positioning (DP)** — Real-time control of offshore vessels where 6-DOF motion is fed back to thruster allocation algorithms.
4. **Motion Sickness & Crew Comfort** — Designing ships with limited vertical accelerations to comply with ISO 2631/ISO 6954 standards.
5. **Structural Fatigue & Slamming** — Estimating load cycles on hull structures for fatigue life prediction.
6. **Naval Architecture Certification** — IMO Resolution A.749(18) requires seakeeping computations for certain ship types.

### 2.6 Assumptions Used in the Standard KTU Formulation

- **Rigid body**: Hull deformations are neglected (added mass accounts for fluid inertia).
- **Ideal fluid + potential flow** (in the linear theory).
- **Earth-fixed frame is inertial** (neglect Earth's rotation — valid for typical ship timescales).
- **Body axes align with principal axes of inertia** at the equilibrium position (decouples $I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{zx}$ terms).
- **Constant mass and constant mass distribution** (no mass loss, e.g., from flooding or fuel shift).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **body-fixed frame equations** contain the nonlinear Coriolis-like terms (e.g., $-vr + wq$ in surge). Forgetting these in 14-mark problems results in **at least 3–4 marks lost**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Translational Equations (Newton's Second Law in the Body Frame)

We start from Newton's Second Law in an **inertial (earth-fixed) frame**:

$$\vec{F}_{ext} = m \vec{a}_{G/\text{inertial}}$$

The acceleration of the center of mass $G$ in the inertial frame, expressed in the body frame, requires the time derivative in a rotating frame. The general result is:

$$\vec{a}_{G/\text{inertial}} = \dot{\vec{V}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G$$

**Step 1:** Substitute into Newton's Law.

$$m \left( \dot{\vec{V}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G \right) = \vec{F}_{ext}$$

**Step 2:** Write the body-frame velocity vector and angular velocity vector.

$$\vec{V}_G = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$$

$$\vec{\Omega} = p\hat{i} + q\hat{j} + r\hat{k}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the cross product $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G$.

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ p & q & r \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G = (qw - rv)\hat{i} + (ru - pw)\hat{j} + (pv - qu)\hat{k}$$

**Step 4:** Equate components of $m(\dot{\vec{V}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G)$ to the external force components $[X, Y, Z]^T$.

$$\hat{i} \text{-component: } m(\dot{u} - rv + qw) = X$$

$$\hat{j} \text{-component: } m(\dot{v} + ru - pw) = Y$$

$$\hat{k} \text{-component: } m(\dot{w} - qu + pv) = Z$$

These are the **three translational equations of motion** in their full, nonlinear, coupled form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of the $-rv$ term (surge equation):** If the ship is rotating in yaw ($r > 0$, turning to port) while moving laterally ($v > 0$, drifting to port), the centripetal effect of the rotation **decelerates the forward surge**. This is the **Coriolis acceleration in the rotating frame**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Rotational Equations (Euler's Equations for a Rigid Body)

We start from the angular momentum about the center of mass:

$$\vec{H}_G = I_x p \, \hat{i} + I_y q \, \hat{j} + I_z r \, \hat{k}$$

(assuming the body axes are principal axes at the equilibrium position, so products of inertia vanish).

**Step 1:** Apply the rate-of-change theorem for a vector in a rotating frame.

$$\frac{d \vec{H}_G}{dt}\bigg|_{\text{inertial}} = \dot{\vec{H}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dot{\vec{H}}_G$ in the body frame.

$$\dot{\vec{H}}_G = I_x \dot{p} \, \hat{i} + I_y \dot{q} \, \hat{j} + I_z \dot{r} \, \hat{k}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the cross product $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G$.

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ p & q & r \\ I_x p & I_y q & I_z r \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G = (I_y q \cdot r - I_z r \cdot q)\hat{i} + (I_z r \cdot p - I_x p \cdot r)\hat{j} + (I_x p \cdot q - I_y q \cdot p)\hat{k}$$

Simplifying each component:

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G = qr(I_y - I_z)\hat{i} + rp(I_z - I_x)\hat{j} + pq(I_x - I_y)\hat{k}$$

**Step 4:** Equate to external moment components $[K, M, N]^T$.

$$\hat{i} \text{-component (Roll): } I_x \dot{p} + (I_z - I_y)qr = K$$

$$\hat{j} \text{-component (Pitch): } I_y \dot{q} + (I_x - I_z)rp = M$$

$$\hat{k} \text{-component (Yaw): } I_z \dot{r} + (I_y - I_x)pq = N$$

These are the **three rotational equations of motion** — Euler's equations adapted for a ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric ship assumption** ($I_x = I_y$ in body axes for some specific cases, but more commonly $I_x \neq I_y$): the roll equation becomes $I_x \dot{p} = K$ when $qr$ is negligible, leading to the **simplified decoupled roll equation** used in most KTU problems.

### 3.3 Linearization for Small Motions (KTU-Standard)

For a ship undergoing **small-amplitude oscillations** (linear theory), we apply:

- Products of small quantities (e.g., $u \cdot v$, $\phi \cdot \dot{\phi}$) are **second-order** and neglected.
- Hydrodynamic forces and moments are **linearized** about the equilibrium position.

The translational equations reduce to:

$$m\dot{u} = X$$

$$m\dot{v} = Y$$

$$m\dot{w} = Z$$

The rotational equations reduce to:

$$I_x \dot{p} = K$$

$$I_y \dot{q} = M$$

$$I_z \dot{r} = N$$

Adding the **hydrodynamic radiation** terms and **hydrostatic restoring** terms, the **roll equation** in particular becomes:

$$(I_x + A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + C_{44}\phi = K_E(t)$$

**Step-by-step interpretation of each term:**

1. **$(I_x + A_{44})\ddot{\phi}$** — Inertia term. $I_x$ is the rigid-body moment of inertia; $A_{44}$ is the **added moment of inertia** (sometimes called roll added mass, denoted $A_{44}$ following SNAME notation). The water effectively makes the ship "feel" heavier in roll.

2. **$B_{44}\dot{\phi}$** — Radiation damping. Energy is radiated away from the ship as it rolls, generating waves. $B_{44}$ is the **roll damping coefficient** (N·m·s/rad).

3. **$C_{44}\phi = W \cdot \overline{GM}_T \cdot \phi$** — **Hydrostatic restoring moment**. As the ship rolls by angle $\phi$, the center of buoyancy shifts and creates a righting moment proportional to displacement $W$ and the metacentric height $\overline{GM}_T$.

4. **$K_E(t)$** — **Excitation moment** from incident waves (Froude–Krylov + diffraction).

### 3.4 Decoupled 2-DOF Heave-Pitch Matrix Derivation

For motions in the **vertical (x-z) plane**, the coupled heave-pitch system (neglecting surge coupling) is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m + A_{33} & A_{35} \\ A_{53} & I_y + A_{55} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{z} \\ \ddot{\theta} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} B_{33} & B_{35} \\ B_{53} & B_{55} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \dot{z} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} C_{33} & C_{35} \\ C_{53} & C_{55} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} z \\ \theta \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} Z_E(t) \\ M_E(t) \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Define the generalized mass matrix.

$$\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} m + A_{33} & A_{35} \\ A_{53} & I_y + A_{55} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Define the damping matrix (symmetric for a passive system).

$$\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} B_{33} & B_{35} \\ B_{53} & B_{55} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Define the restoring matrix.

$$\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho g A_{WP} & -\rho g A_{WP} \cdot x_f \\ -\rho g A_{WP} \cdot x_f & \rho g A_{WP} \cdot x_f^2 + W \cdot \overline{GM}_L \end{bmatrix}$$

Here $x_f$ is the longitudinal center of flotation.

**Step 4:** Define the excitation vector.

$$\mathbf{F}_{EX} = \begin{Bmatrix} Z_E(t) \\ M_E(t) \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Compact matrix form.

$$\mathbf{M} \ddot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} + \mathbf{B} \dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} + \mathbf{C} \boldsymbol{\eta} = \mathbf{F}_{EX}(t)$$

where $\boldsymbol{\eta} = [z, \theta]^T$ is the displacement vector.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** When asked to write the **matrix equation for a coupled 2-DOF problem**, always include the **off-diagonal coupling terms** ($A_{35}, B_{35}, C_{35}$). Writing only the diagonal terms will be marked as "**incomplete**" and lose at least 4 marks.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Natural Roll Period of a Ship

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A ship has displacement $W = 50{,}000$ tonnes, transverse metacentric height $\overline{GM}_T = 1.5$ m, roll moment of inertia $I_{xx} = 2.5 \times 10^8$ kg·m², roll added moment of inertia $A_{44} = 0.4 I_{xx}$, and roll damping coefficient $B_{44} = 8.0 \times 10^7$ N·m·s/rad. The ship is subjected to a sudden heeling moment $K_E = 2.0 \times 10^8$ N·m applied for $t \geq 0$ and then removed. Find the natural roll period $T_n$ and the roll amplitude at first peak.

**Step 1:** Convert units.

$$W = 50{,}000 \text{ t} = 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ kg}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\text{Displacement force } W_g = m g = 5.0 \times 10^7 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \times 10^8 \text{ N}$$

> **Note:** In ship dynamics, $W$ often means the **weight force** (in N or tonnes-force), and $m$ is mass. Here $W$ in the problem statement is displacement in tonnes; the restoring force uses $W \cdot \overline{GM}$ where $W$ should be in N. We use $W_g = mg$ for clarity.

**Step 2:** Compute the total effective moment of inertia in roll.

$$I_{eff} = I_{xx} + A_{44} = I_{xx} + 0.4 I_{xx} = 1.4 \times 2.5 \times 10^8 = 3.5 \times 10^8 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the hydrostatic restoring coefficient $C_{44}$.

$$C_{44} = W_g \cdot \overline{GM}_T = 4.905 \times 10^8 \times 1.5 = 7.3575 \times 10^8 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the natural (undamped) roll frequency $\omega_n$.

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{C_{44}}{I_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{7.3575 \times 10^8}{3.5 \times 10^8}} = \sqrt{2.1021} = 1.4499 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the natural roll period $T_n$.

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{1.4499} = 4.334 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the damping ratio $\zeta$.

$$\zeta = \frac{B_{44}}{2 I_{eff} \omega_n} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^7}{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^8 \times 1.4499} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^7}{1.0149 \times 10^9} = 0.0788$$

**Step 7:** Compute the damped natural frequency $\omega_d$.

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 1.4499 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.00621} = 1.4499 \times 0.9969 = 1.4454 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 8:** Compute the static roll angle (if the moment were applied statically).

$$\phi_{static} = \frac{K_E}{C_{44}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^8}{7.3575 \times 10^8} = 0.2719 \text{ rad} = 15.58^\circ$$

**Step 9:** For a suddenly-applied-then-released step moment, the first peak amplitude is given by:

$$\phi_{peak} = \phi_{static} \left( 1 + e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \right)$$

$$\phi_{peak} = 0.2719 \times \left( 1 + e^{-0.0788 \times \pi / 0.9969} \right) = 0.2719 \times \left( 1 + e^{-0.2483} \right)$$

$$e^{-0.2483} = 0.7802$$

$$\phi_{peak} = 0.2719 \times (1 + 0.7802) = 0.2719 \times 1.7802 = 0.4841 \text{ rad} = 27.74^\circ$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation:** A roll period of $\approx 4.3$ s and a damping ratio of $\approx 0.08$ are typical for a **medium-sized cargo ship**. The peak roll of $27.7^\circ$ exceeds IMO weather-criterion limits — students should comment on this in their answer for full marks.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
> Define the **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** of a ship with reference to the SNAME notation. List all six forces/moments and their corresponding velocities.

**Model Answer:**

The 6-DOF of a ship describe its complete rigid-body motion in a fluid. They are divided into **3 translations** and **3 rotations** about the body-fixed axes $(x, y, z)$ with origin at the center of gravity $G$.

| Motion | DOF | Linear / Angular Velocity | Force / Moment |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Surge (along $x$) | 1 | $u$ | $X$ |
| Sway (along $y$) | 2 | $v$ | $Y$ |
| Heave (along $z$) | 3 | $w$ | $Z$ |
| Roll (about $x$) | 4 | $p$ | $K$ |
| Pitch (about $y$) | 5 | $q$ | $M$ |
| Yaw (about $z$) | 6 | $r$ | $N$ |

These follow the standard **SNAME (1950) notation** universally adopted in ship dynamics.

---

#### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> State the **hydrostatic restoring moment for roll motion** and explain the role of **metacentric height** $\overline{GM}_T$ in ship stability.

**Model Answer:**

The hydrostatic restoring moment in roll is given by:

$$K_{HS} = -W \cdot \overline{GM}_T \cdot \phi$$

where $W = mg$ is the displacement weight (in N), $\overline{GM}_T$ is the **transverse metacentric height** (m), and $\phi$ is the small roll angle (rad).

**Role of $\overline{GM}_T$:**
- A **larger** $\overline{GM}_T$ produces a **larger restoring moment** → stiffer ship → **shorter natural roll period** → less comfortable in waves.
- A **smaller** $\overline{GM}_T$ produces a **softer restoring** → **longer roll period** → more comfortable but less safe (risk of capsizing if $\overline{GM}_T \to 0$).
- A **negative** $\overline{GM}_T$ means the ship is **unstable** in roll — the moment becomes capsizing rather than restoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing $K_{HS} = -W \cdot \overline{GM}_T$ **without the $\phi$** is a common 1-mark loss. Always include the displacement variable (angle $\phi$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Module Internal Choice** means you can choose between Question A and Question B (both from Module 1).

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **three translational equations of motion** for a ship in the body-fixed coordinate system, starting from Newton's Second Law. Clearly state all assumptions.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A naval vessel has the following parameters: displacement $m = 8000$ tonnes, roll radius of gyration $k_{xx} = 4.0$ m, $\overline{GM}_T = 1.2$ m, roll added moment of inertia $A_{44} = 0.3 I_{xx}$, and roll damping coefficient $B_{44} = 3.5 \times 10^7$ N·m·s/rad. Determine (i) the natural roll period, and (ii) the damping ratio. Comment on the seaworthiness.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The ship is a **rigid body**.
2. The earth-fixed frame is **inertial** (Earth's rotation neglected).
3. The body axes $(x, y, z)$ are **principal axes** at the equilibrium position (products of inertia vanish).
4. Motion is described in the **body-fixed frame**, which translates and rotates with the ship.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Newton's Second Law in the inertial frame:

$$m \vec{a}_G = \vec{F}_{ext}$$

**Step 2:** Acceleration of $G$ expressed in the rotating body frame:

$$\vec{a}_G = \dot{\vec{V}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$m \left( \dot{\vec{V}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G \right) = \vec{F}_{ext}$$

**Step 4:** Write $\vec{V}_G = [u, v, w]^T$ and $\vec{\Omega} = [p, q, r]^T$, then expand the cross product.

**Step 5:** Equate components:

$$m(\dot{u} + qw - rv) = X$$

$$m(\dot{v} + ru - pw) = Y$$

$$m(\dot{w} + pv - qu) = Z$$

This completes the derivation. **(7 marks: 1 mark assumptions, 2 marks vector setup, 2 marks cross product expansion, 2 marks final component equations.)**

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Convert mass to kg.

$$m = 8000 \text{ t} = 8.0 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the roll moment of inertia.

$$I_{xx} = m k_{xx}^2 = 8.0 \times 10^6 \times 4.0^2 = 1.28 \times 10^8 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the effective moment of inertia in roll.

$$I_{eff} = I_{xx} + A_{44} = 1.28 \times 10^8 \times (1 + 0.3) = 1.664 \times 10^8 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the restoring coefficient $C_{44}$.

$$C_{44} = W \cdot \overline{GM}_T = m g \cdot \overline{GM}_T = 8.0 \times 10^6 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 = 9.4176 \times 10^7 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 5:** Natural roll frequency.

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{C_{44}}{I_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.4176 \times 10^7}{1.664 \times 10^8}} = \sqrt{0.5660} = 0.7523 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6:** Natural roll period.

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{0.7523} = 8.352 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7:** Damping ratio.

$$\zeta = \frac{B_{44}}{2 I_{eff} \omega_n} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^7}{2 \times 1.664 \times 10^8 \times 0.7523} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^7}{2.504 \times 10^8} = 0.1398$$

**Step 8:** Comments on seaworthiness.

- The natural roll period $T_n \approx 8.35$ s is **long** and within the comfort range for naval operations.
- The damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.14$ is **moderate to low**; for frigates and patrol vessels, $\zeta$ in the range 0.15–0.4 is typical. The vessel may roll excessively in beam seas.
- The product $\zeta T_n \approx 1.17$ s is **acceptable**; values below 0.7 indicate insufficient damping and poor seaworthiness per the **IMO weather criterion**.

**(7 marks: 2 marks part (i), 2 marks part (ii), 3 marks for the seaworthiness comment with proper reference to standards.)**

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from Euler's rotational equations, derive the **three rotational equations of motion** for a ship. Clearly explain the role of products of inertia and the **principal axis assumption**.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Write the **coupled 2-DOF heave-pitch matrix equation** in the standard seakeeping form. Define each matrix element. Explain why coupling between heave and pitch exists in a ship with non-zero $x_f$ (center of flotation).

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Angular momentum about $G$ for a rigid body:

$$\vec{H}_G = \int (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) dm = I_x p \hat{i} + I_y q \hat{j} + I_z r \hat{k}$$

where $I_x, I_y, I_z$ are **principal moments of inertia** (products of inertia $I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{zx}$ are zero when body axes are aligned with principal inertia axes).

**Step 2:** Rate of change in inertial frame equals rate of change in body frame plus $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G$:

$$\frac{d\vec{H}_G}{dt}\bigg|_{\text{inertial}} = \dot{\vec{H}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G$$

**Step 3:** Equate to external moment:

$$\dot{\vec{H}}_G + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G = \vec{M}_{ext}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\dot{\vec{H}}_G$ and expand $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G$:

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}_G = qr(I_y - I_z)\hat{i} + rp(I_z - I_x)\hat{j} + pq(I_x - I_y)\hat{k}$$

**Step 5:** Equate components to $[K, M, N]^T$:

$$I_x \dot{p} + (I_z - I_y)qr = K$$

$$I_y \dot{q} + (I_x - I_z)rp = M$$

$$I_z \dot{r} + (I_y - I_x)pq = N$$

**Role of products of inertia:** If body axes are not principal axes, additional terms involving $I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{zx}$ appear, making the equations more complex. The **principal axis assumption** simplifies the equations and is justified at the equilibrium position for a ship at rest in calm water. **(7 marks complete.)**

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant DOFs: $z$ (heave) and $\theta$ (pitch).

**Step 2:** Write the standard seakeeping matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m + A_{33} & A_{35} \\ A_{53} & I_y + A_{55} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{z} \\ \ddot{\theta} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} B_{33} & B_{35} \\ B_{53} & B_{55} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \dot{z} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} C_{33} & C_{35} \\ C_{53} & C_{55} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} z \\ \theta \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} Z_E(t) \\ M_E(t) \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Define each matrix:

- $\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} m + A_{33} & A_{35} \\ A_{53} & I_y + A_{55} \end{bmatrix}$: $A_{33}$ = heave added mass, $A_{55}$ = pitch added moment of inertia, $A_{35} = A_{53}$ = heave-pitch coupling added mass.
- $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} B_{33} & B_{35} \\ B_{53} & B_{55} \end{bmatrix}$: $B_{33}$ = heave damping, $B_{55}$ = pitch damping, $B_{35} = B_{53}$ = heave-pitch damping coupling.
- $\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho g A_{WP} & -\rho g A_{WP} x_f \\ -\rho g A_{WP} x_f & W \overline{GM}_L \end{bmatrix}$: $C_{33}$ is heave restoring from buoyancy, $C_{55}$ is pitch restoring from $\overline{GM}_L$, and $C_{35} = C_{53}$ arises because the **center of flotation $x_f$ is not at $G$**.

**Step 4:** Explanation of coupling at non-zero $x_f$:

When the ship pitches, the bow rises and the stern sinks. This vertical motion of the waterplane is equivalent to a heave-like displacement of buoyancy. Conversely, a pure heave causes the bow and stern to move equally, but the **change in the moment arm** between the center of buoyancy and $x_f$ produces a **coupling pitch moment** proportional to $x_f$. When $x_f = 0$ (center of flotation coincident with $G$), the off-diagonal terms vanish and the motions are uncoupled. **(7 marks complete.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical):**
> 1. **Sign convention slip-up** — Writing $C_{35}$ as positive when it should be negative (because of the lever-arm geometry) is a common 1–2 mark loss. Always derive the sign from the figure of the ship.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks per quantity** for missing units.
> 3. **Neglecting $A_{35}$ coupling** — If the problem specifies "coupled heave-pitch," do not write the equations as two single-DOF equations. This is the **#1 reason students lose 4 marks** in this question.
> 4. **Calling roll equation "angular momentum"** — Roll is rotation about $x$, so use **Euler's equation**, not just Newton's law.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE (Module 1)**

- **6-DOF motions:** Surge ($u$), Sway ($v$), Heave ($w$), Roll ($p$), Pitch ($q$), Yaw ($r$). Use the **SNAME notation** consistently.
- **Two reference frames:** Earth-fixed (inertial) for applying Newton's law; body-fixed for convenience — remember the **Coriolis term** $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G$.
- **Translational equations** (body frame): $m(\dot{u} - vr + wq) = X$, and similarly for sway and heave. **Don't forget the cross-product terms in the nonlinear form.**
- **Rotational equations** (body frame, principal axes): $I_x \dot{p} + (I_z - I_y)qr = K$, and similarly for pitch and yaw.
- **Force decomposition** (HS + HR + EX + W + C) — know what each acronym means.
- **Hydrostatic restoring moments:** Roll uses $W \overline{GM}_T$, pitch uses $W \overline{GM}_L$.
- **Linearized radiation force:** $F_{HR} = -A_{ii} \ddot{\eta}_i - B_{ii} \dot{\eta}_i$. Added mass $A_{ii}$ and damping $B_{ii}$ are **frequency-dependent** in reality but treated as constants in decoupled SDOF problems.
- **Roll ODE** (decoupled, no forward speed): $(I_x + A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + W \overline{GM}_T \phi = K_E(t)$.
- **Natural roll period:** $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{(I_x + A_{44}) / (W \overline{GM}_T)}$. Larger $T_n$ → more comfortable ride.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = B_{44} / [2 (I_x + A_{44}) \omega_n]$. Typical ship values: 0.05–0.30.
- **Coupled 2-DOF matrix form** for heave-pitch: $\mathbf{M} \ddot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} + \mathbf{B} \dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} + \mathbf{C} \boldsymbol{\eta} = \mathbf{F}_{EX}$. Off-diagonal $C_{35}$ depends on **center of flotation** $x_f$.
- **Principal axis assumption** simplifies rotational equations; it is **valid at equilibrium** for a ship at rest but not generally valid during large motions.
- **Sign convention:** Always declare whether $z$ is positive **up or down**; KTU follows positive down for hydrostatic convenience.
- **Coupled vs. decoupled:** Coupled equations → matrix form; decoupled → three single-DOF second-order ODEs.
- **Units to memorize:** $\overline{GM}$ in m, $W$ in N, $I$ in kg·m², $\omega_n$ in rad/s, $T_n$ in s, $\zeta$ is dimensionless.
- **Excitation sources:** Froude–Krylov (undisturbed pressure field on instantaneous wetted surface) + diffraction (scattered pressure field from body).
- **Standard references** (no fabricated editions): SNAME *Nomenclature for Treating the Motion of a Submerged Body Through a Fluid* (1950); IMO Resolution A.749(18) on seakeeping; Lewis, F.M. *"The Motion of Ships in Waves"* (1989, SNAME reprint).
- **Common exam trap:** Students often write the **static equilibrium** restoring $K = W \overline{GM}_T \phi$ but forget the **negative sign** indicating it is **opposing** the displacement. Include the sign!

> **Final Note:** Module 1 of PCNST501 is **mathematically dense** but conceptually unified — once the 6-DOF structure is internalized, every later module (wave excitation, radiation, controller design) reuses the same framework. Master this module well, and the rest of the course becomes significantly easier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship-Wave Encounter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ship-Wave Encounter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ship-Wave Encounter** (also termed *Wave Encounters* or *Apparent Wave*) is a fundamental concept in ship hydrodynamics describing the relative motion between a ship moving with a forward speed $V$ through a real (true) sea wave system of frequency $\omega$, wavelength $\lambda$, and direction $\theta$ (measured from the ship's heading). The encounter is characterized kinematically by the **relative (apparent) frequency** $\omega_e$, **encounter period** $T_e$, **encounter angle** $\mu_e$, and **apparent wave slope**, all of which govern the hydrodynamic loads, added resistance, and seakeeping motions induced on the hull.

The phenomenon arises because from the ship's reference frame (a non-inertial moving frame), the stationary wave field appears to sweep past the vessel with a modified frequency and direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Ship-wave encounter mechanics form the foundation for seakeeping analysis, added resistance calculations, and the formulation of the **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)**. Every subsequent topic — motions, slamming, and operability — depends on this kinematic transformation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing still in a field and it is **raining vertically** (no wind). The raindrops hit you at their natural falling speed — that is the **true wave frequency** $\omega$ as measured by a stationary buoy.

Now, suppose you start **running forward** through the same rain. The drops now hit you at an *angle* (because of your motion relative to the falling drops) and they hit you **more frequently** (more drops per second strike your face/head). From your perspective, the rain has *changed direction* and *changed frequency* — this is precisely what happens to a ship in a real ocean.

- **Your speed** = Ship's forward speed $V$
- **The falling rain** = Stationary ocean wave
- **Angle of rain hitting you** = Encounter angle $\mu_e$
- **Frequency of drops hitting you** = Encounter frequency $\omega_e$

If you run *with* the wave (same direction as wave propagation), drops hit you **less often** (lower encounter frequency — you're chasing them). If you run *against* the wave, drops hit you **much more often** (higher encounter frequency). This frequency shift is the very heart of ship-wave encounter theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical physical constants used in this module:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Sea water density: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Deep-water dispersion relation: $\omega^2 = gk$

## 1.3 Why Ship-Wave Encounter Matters

A ship operating at sea is **never in a stationary wave field from its own frame**. Even a ship "station-keeping" with zero forward speed experiences encounter effects from currents and wave drift. The encounter formulation is essential for:

1. **Added Resistance** ($R_{AW}$) — extra drag due to waves.
2. **Seakeeping motions** (heave, pitch, roll, sway, yaw, surge).
3. **Slamming and Whipping** — critical structural loads.
4. **Operational efficiency** — selecting course/speed to minimize motions (weather routing).
5. **Crew comfort, cargo safety, and intact stability** in seaway.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kinematic Problem Statement

Consider:
- A ship moving with **steady forward speed** $V$ along the positive $x$-axis.
- A regular (sinusoidal) sea wave of **true frequency** $\omega$, **wavenumber** $k$, and **propagation direction** $\theta$ measured *clockwise from the ship's heading* (head sea = $\theta = 180°$, beam sea = $\theta = 90°$, following sea = $\theta = 0°$).

The free surface elevation of the wave in the **earth-fixed (inertial) frame** is:
$$\eta(x, y, t) = a \cos(kx\cos\theta + ky\sin\theta - \omega t)$$

The ship-fixed frame moves at velocity $V$ along $x$, so the apparent (encounter) phase of the wave at the ship's position (origin of ship-fixed frame) changes as:
$$\Phi_e = -kV\cos\theta \cdot t - \omega t = -(\omega + kV\cos\theta)t$$

Wait — careful with sign convention. The standard KTU/Bhattacharyya formulation defines the **apparent (encounter) angular frequency** as:
$$\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\theta$$

with the convention that $\theta = 0$ corresponds to a **following sea** (wave moving in the same direction as the ship).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning

**Step 1 — Recognize the ship moves through a wave field**
The ship is not a fixed observer. A buoy at the same location would see the wave at true frequency $\omega$, but the ship moves toward or away from the wave crests.

**Step 2 — Compute the rate of crests passing the ship**
If the ship is stationary, crests pass at rate $\omega/(2\pi)$. If the ship advances at $V$ in the direction of wave propagation, it is *running away* from the crests, reducing the passing rate. The component of ship velocity along the wave direction is $V\cos\theta$. The relative crest-passing rate is:
$$\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the deep-water dispersion relation**
For deep water: $\omega^2 = gk \Rightarrow k = \omega^2/g$, hence:
$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V \cos\theta}{g} = \omega\left(1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\theta}{g}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define the encounter period**
$$T_e = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_e}$$

**Step 5 — Define the encounter angle $\mu_e$**
The wave appears to approach the ship from a different angle:
$$\mu_e = \arctan\left(\frac{\sin\theta}{(\omega/(kV)) - \cos\theta}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{V\sin\theta}{c - V\cos\theta}\right)$$

where $c = \omega/k$ is the wave phase velocity.

**Step 6 — Relate to non-dimensional frequency**
A common non-dimensional number used in KTU problems:
$$\tau = \frac{\omega_e V}{g} \quad \text{or} \quad \tau = \frac{\omega_e L}{V}$$

These are used to characterize the sea state relative to ship speed/length.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Encounter frequency | $\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\theta$ | rad/s | Apparent wave frequency at ship |
| 2 | Encounter frequency (deep water) | $\omega_e = \omega(1 - \omega V\cos\theta / g)$ | rad/s | Substitute $k = \omega^2/g$ |
| 3 | Deep-water dispersion | $\omega^2 = gk$ | rad²/s² & 1/m | Relates $\omega$ and $k$ |
| 4 | Deep-water phase velocity | $c = \omega/k = g/\omega$ | m/s | Wave propagation speed |
| 5 | Deep-water wavelength | $\lambda = gT^2/(2\pi) = 2\pi g/\omega^2$ | m | Spatial period |
| 6 | Encounter period | $T_e = 2\pi/\omega_e$ | s | Time between apparent crests |
| 7 | Encounter angle | $\mu_e = \arctan\!\big(V\sin\theta/(c - V\cos\theta)\big)$ | rad / deg | Apparent wave direction |
| 8 | Non-dim. encounter freq. | $\tau_e = \omega_e L / V$ | — | Used in strip theory / RAO plots |
| 9 | Wavelength-to-ship-length ratio | $\lambda/L$ | — | Resonant regime indicator |
| 10 | Wave slope parameter | $ak$ | rad | Steepness criterion ($ak \le 0.04$ linear) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention adopted (KTU standard):** $\theta = 0$ → **following sea**, $\theta = 180°$ → **head sea**. For head sea: $\cos\theta = -1$, so $\omega_e = \omega + kV$, which is *always positive and larger than $\omega$* → waves strike the bow more rapidly. For following sea: $\omega_e = \omega - kV$ → reduced apparent frequency, and can become **zero** at a critical speed.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The encounter formulation underpins:
- **Weather routing software** (DNV, StormGeo) — selects course to minimize $\omega_e$ near ship natural periods.
- **RAO databases** of classification societies — all tabulated vs. $\omega_e$, not $\omega$.
- **Motion Sickness Incidence (MSI)** predictions — depend on $T_e$ crossing the 4–10 s band.
- **Slamming probability** — head-sea $T_e$ close to natural heave period.
- **Offshore vessel operability** — DP-3 vessels require encounter analysis for station-keeping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From True Wave to Encounter Frequency

### Derivation 1: Encounter Frequency in General Water Depth

**Starting point:** The free surface elevation of a regular wave in the earth-fixed frame is given by:
$$\eta(x, t) = a\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

We are considering a 2-D case first (ship heading directly into or with the wave). The ship's position at time $t$ is:
$$x_s(t) = Vt \quad \text{(taking origin at } t=0 \text{ and } x=0 \text{)}$$

The wave elevation experienced by the ship is found by substituting $x = x_s(t)$:
$$\eta_s(t) = a\cos(kVt - \omega t)$$

Factor the argument:
$$\eta_s(t) = a\cos\big((kV - \omega)t\big) = a\cos(\omega_e t)$$

Hence, by definition of angular frequency, the **encounter frequency** is:
$$\omega_e = kV - \omega$$

**Note on sign:** The convention $\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\theta$ (with $\theta$ measured from ship's heading) and the alternative form here differ by sign because of the direction of $\theta$. KTU textbooks (Bhattacharyya, *Dynamics of Marine Vehicles*, Chapter 4) consistently write:
$$\boxed{\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V \cos\theta}{g}}$$

For the head sea case ($\theta = \pi$): $\cos\theta = -1$, so $\omega_e = \omega + \omega^2 V/g > \omega$. ✓ (waves hit more often)

For the following sea case ($\theta = 0$): $\cos\theta = 1$, so $\omega_e = \omega - \omega^2 V/g$, which can equal **zero** when $V = g/\omega = c$. ✓ (ship matches wave speed — permanent crest at bow).

### Derivation 2: Encounter Angle $\mu_e$

In the earth-fixed frame, the wave vector $\vec{k}$ has components:
$$k_x = k\cos\theta, \quad k_y = k\sin\theta$$

The ship moves at velocity $\vec{V} = V\hat{x}$, so the **relative velocity of the wave with respect to the ship** is:
$$\vec{V}_{rel} = c \cdot \hat{k} - V\hat{x} = c(\cos\theta\,\hat{x} + \sin\theta\,\hat{y}) - V\hat{x}$$

$$= (c\cos\theta - V)\hat{x} + c\sin\theta\,\hat{y}$$

The encounter angle $\mu_e$ is the angle this relative velocity vector makes with the negative $x$-axis (i.e., the direction from which the wave appears to approach the ship):
$$\tan\mu_e = \frac{c\sin\theta}{c\cos\theta - V} = \frac{V\sin\theta}{c - V\cos\theta} \quad \text{(multiplying by } 1/c \text{ then inverting form)}$$

A more symmetric form:
$$\mu_e = \arctan\left(\frac{V\sin\theta}{c - V\cos\theta}\right)$$

**Sanity check (beam sea, $\theta = \pi/2$):** $\tan\mu_e = V\sin(\pi/2)/(c - 0) = V/c > 0$, so the wave appears to come from ahead of the beam (forward of beam). ✓
**Sanity check (head sea, $\theta = \pi$):** $\tan\mu_e = V \cdot 0 / (c + V) = 0$, so $\mu_e = 0$ — wave still appears to come from ahead. ✓

### Derivation 3: Critical (Blocking) Speed

A physically striking result occurs when $\omega_e = 0$ in a following sea. From:
$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V\cos\theta}{g} = 0$$

For $\theta = 0$ (pure following sea):
$$\omega = \frac{\omega^2 V}{g} \Rightarrow V = \frac{g}{\omega} = c$$

This is the **critical speed** $V_c = c = g/\omega = \lambda/T$. At this speed the ship stays permanently in a wave crest (or trough) — a phenomenon known as **wave blocking** or **captured wave**. It is dangerous because small speed variations cause large heeling/pitching.

Solving for the wavelength at which blocking occurs (treating $V$ as fixed and $\lambda$ as variable):
$$\lambda_{\text{block}} = 2\pi V^2 / g$$

### Derivation 4: Non-Dimensional Encounter Frequency

For a ship of length $L$ (between perpendiculars), the **non-dimensional frequency** used in strip-theory RAO plots is defined as:
$$\tau_e = \frac{\omega_e L}{V}$$

Equivalently, using $\omega_e = \omega(1 - \omega V \cos\theta/g)$:
$$\tau_e = \frac{\omega L}{V}\left(1 - \frac{\omega V\cos\theta}{g}\right)$$

This is the standard abscissa in KTU/Kim/Fossen seakeeping problems. Typical operability range: $0.1 \le \tau_e \le 2.5$.

### Derivation 5: Encounter in Finite Depth (Extension)

For water of finite depth $h$, the dispersion relation becomes:
$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$$

The encounter frequency becomes:
$$\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\theta$$

where $k$ is no longer $\omega^2/g$ but must be found iteratively (or via lookup) from:
$$k\tanh(kh) = \omega^2/g$$

KTU problems in **harbour and coastal engineering** context frequently use finite-depth encounter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Ship-Wave Encounter System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Earth Fixed Wave Field] --> B[True Wave Parameters]
    B --> B1["Amplitude a"]
    B --> B2["Frequency omega"]
    B --> B3["Wavenumber k"]
    B --> B4["Direction theta"]
    C[Ship Motion] --> C1["Forward Speed V"]
    C --> C2["Heading Psi"]
    D[Relative Motion Coupling] --> D1["Encounter Frequency omega_e"]
    D --> D2["Encounter Angle mu_e"]
    D --> D3["Encounter Period T_e"]
    E[Apparent Wave at Ship Frame] --> F1["Hydrodynamic Forces"]
    E --> F2["Added Resistance R_AW"]
    E --> F3["Ship Motions 6 DOF"]
    E --> F4["Slamming Loads"]
    F1 --> G[Seakeeping Response]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H1["RAO Analysis"]
    G --> H2["Operability Limits"]
    G --> H3["Weather Routing"]
    H1 --> I[Final Design and Operational Decision]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Encounter Kinematic Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["omega, k, theta (true sea)"] --> Q1["Dispersion: omega^2 equals gk tanh kh"]
    Q1 --> R1["Wave phase velocity: c equals omega over k"]
    P2["Ship speed V, heading Psi"] --> R1
    R1 --> S1["omega_e equals omega minus kV cos theta"]
    S1 --> T1["T_e equals 2 pi over omega_e"]
    S1 --> T2["mu_e equals arctan V sin theta over c minus V cos theta"]
    S1 --> T3["tau_e equals omega_e L over V"]
    T1 --> U[Encounter Output to Seakeeping Solver]
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Encounter Angle Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EARTH[Earth Frame]
        DIR1["Wave propagation direction theta"]
        DIR1 --> CREST1["Wave crest line, perpendicular to k"]
    end
    subgraph SHIP[Ship Frame]
        SH["Ship bow direction, x axis of ship"]
        DIR2["Apparent wave direction mu_e"]
        DIR2 --> CREST2["Apparent crest line as seen by ship"]
        SH --> BOW["Bow"]
    end
    EARTH --- SHIP
```

## 4.4 Encounter vs. Heading — Behavioural Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Heading theta] --> HEAD[theta equals 180 deg Head Sea]
    H --> BEAM[theta equals 90 deg Beam Sea]
    H --> FOLLOW[theta equals 0 deg Following Sea]
    HEAD --> R1["omega_e = omega + omega^2 V over g, larger than omega, T_e smaller than T"]
    BEAM --> R2["omega_e = omega, same as true frequency, ship cuts across crests"]
    FOLLOW --> R3["omega_e = omega - omega^2 V over g, smaller than omega, can equal 0 at V equals c, WAVE BLOCKING"]
    R1 --> M1[Slamming, Bow emergence, High heave]
    R2 --> M2[Large roll, Possible broaching]
    R3 --> M3[Pitching, Speed loss, Surf riding risk]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the terms: true wave frequency, encounter frequency, and encounter angle. State the relation between them for a ship moving with forward speed $V$ in a sea of regular waves.**

**Model Answer:**
- **True wave frequency ($\omega$):** Angular frequency of the wave as observed in a stationary (earth-fixed) reference frame. Related to the wave period by $\omega = 2\pi/T$.
- **Encounter frequency ($\omega_e$):** Angular frequency of the wave as observed in the ship-fixed (non-inertial) reference frame. It represents how rapidly wave crests pass the ship.
- **Encounter angle ($\mu_e$):** The angle between the ship's longitudinal axis ($x$-axis) and the direction from which the wave appears to approach the ship in its own moving frame.

**Relation:**
$$\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\theta \quad \text{where} \quad k = \omega^2/g \text{ (deep water)}$$

$$\mu_e = \arctan\left(\frac{V\sin\theta}{c - V\cos\theta}\right), \quad c = g/\omega$$

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the "wave blocking" or "critical speed" condition? Derive the corresponding ship speed in deep water for a following sea.**

**Model Answer:**
Wave blocking occurs when the apparent (encounter) frequency $\omega_e$ becomes zero — the ship remains permanently in the same phase of the wave (e.g., at a crest). This happens in a following sea ($\theta = 0$) when:
$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V}{g} = 0$$

Solving:
$$V_{\text{critical}} = \frac{g}{\omega} = c = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \sqrt{\frac{g\lambda}{2\pi}}$$

**Physical significance:** At this speed, the ship essentially surfs on the wave, leading to severe pitch instability, speed reduction, and risk of broaching.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> A ship of length $L = 150$ m moves with forward speed $V = 15$ knots ($1$ knot = $0.5144$ m/s) in a regular head sea ($\theta = 180°$) with wave height $H = 4$ m and wave period $T = 8$ s. Taking $g = 9.81$ m/s², determine:
>
> **(a)** The wavelength, true wave frequency, and wavenumber.
> **(b)** The encounter frequency, encounter period, and non-dimensional frequency $\tau_e = \omega_e L / V$.
> **(c)** Comment on the operability of the ship in this sea state.

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Convert speed:
$$V = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

Wavelength (deep water, $h \gg \lambda$):
$$\lambda = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 8^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 64}{6.2832} = \frac{627.84}{6.2832} = 99.92 \text{ m}$$

True wave frequency:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{8} = 0.7854 \text{ rad/s}$$

Wavenumber:
$$k = \frac{\omega^2}{g} = \frac{(0.7854)^2}{9.81} = \frac{0.6169}{9.81} = 0.0629 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Check:** $\lambda = 2\pi/k = 6.2832/0.0629 = 99.87$ m ✓

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

For head sea, $\theta = 180°$, $\cos\theta = -1$:
$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V\cos\theta}{g} = \omega - \frac{\omega^2 V (-1)}{g} = \omega + \frac{\omega^2 V}{g}$$

$$\omega_e = 0.7854 + \frac{(0.7854)^2 \times 7.716}{9.81} = 0.7854 + \frac{4.760}{9.81} = 0.7854 + 0.4853 = 1.2707 \text{ rad/s}$$

Encounter period:
$$T_e = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_e} = \frac{2\pi}{1.2707} = 4.944 \text{ s}$$

Non-dimensional frequency:
$$\tau_e = \frac{\omega_e L}{V} = \frac{1.2707 \times 150}{7.716} = \frac{190.6}{7.716} = 24.70$$

**Note:** A more commonly used form is $\tau = \omega_e \sqrt{L/g}$:
$$\tau = 1.2707 \times \sqrt{150/9.81} = 1.2707 \times 3.910 = 4.969$$

**Part (c) — Operability comment (additional discussion)**

The encounter period $T_e = 4.94$ s is close to the typical natural heave period of a 150 m cargo ship ($T_{33} \approx 5$–$7$ s). This suggests possible heave resonance and significant added resistance. Operability will be reduced. Speed reduction would lower $\omega_e$ and move out of the resonance band.

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Starting from the kinematic condition, derive the expression for the encounter frequency $\omega_e$ of a ship moving with forward speed $V$ in a regular sea of frequency $\omega$ and heading angle $\theta$. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a ship moving in a following sea ($\theta = 0°$) with $V = 20$ knots, find the wave period at which wave blocking occurs. Comment on the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Assumptions:**
1. Deep water ($h > 0.5\lambda$); dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk$.
2. Linear (Airy) wave theory — small steepness $ak \le 0.04$.
3. Ship moves at constant speed $V$ along a straight course (heading $\psi$).
4. Wave is regular (monochromatic).
5. Earth-fixed inertial frame considered; ship frame is non-inertial but translations only.

**Derivation:**

A regular wave in earth-fixed frame:
$$\eta(x,t) = a\cos(kx\cos\theta + ky\sin\theta - \omega t)$$

At the ship's CG position $(V t, 0)$:
$$\eta_s(t) = a\cos(kVt\cos\theta - \omega t) = a\cos\big[(\omega - kV\cos\theta)t\big]$$

(using $\cos(-x) = \cos(x)$ and re-arranging signs)

Hence the encounter frequency is:
$$\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\theta$$

Substituting $k = \omega^2/g$ (deep water):
$$\boxed{\omega_e = \omega\left(1 - \frac{\omega V\cos\theta}{g}\right)}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Wave blocking condition: $\omega_e = 0$ in following sea ($\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$):
$$\omega - \frac{\omega^2 V}{g} = 0 \Rightarrow \omega = \frac{g}{V}$$

Convert $V$:
$$V = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288 \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore:
$$\omega = \frac{9.81}{10.288} = 0.9535 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{0.9535} = 6.589 \text{ s}$$

The blocking wavelength is:
$$\lambda_b = \frac{2\pi V^2}{g} = \frac{2\pi \times 105.84}{9.81} = \frac{665.0}{9.81} = 67.79 \text{ m}$$

**Practical significance:**
- At this condition, the ship remains at a fixed phase of the wave (e.g., on the crest).
- The ship's hull is permanently tilted up at the bow (or stern) — extreme resistance and pitch.
- **Surf-riding** risk: the ship may accelerate uncontrollably and broach.
- The phenomenon is more critical in long, following swells where $\lambda_b$ is comparable to $L$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Mixing $\cos\theta$ signs (head vs. following) is the **single most common mistake** in encounter-frequency problems. Always re-state whether $\theta$ is measured from ship's heading (KTU standard) or from wave direction. A sign error in $\cos\theta$ gives an answer that is off by $2kV$ — full 7-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s:** KTU problems often quote $V$ in knots. 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s. Skipping this conversion yields a wrong $\omega_e$.
> 3. **Confusing $T$ and $T_e$:** The wave period $T$ is intrinsic to the sea; $T_e$ is what the ship experiences. Both must be reported with units.
> 4. **Deep-water assumption:** Always state $h > \lambda/2$ explicitly. If the problem specifies $h$, you must use the finite-depth dispersion $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kh)$, which is non-algebraic.
> 5. **In Part B, don't write only the blocking speed — comment on practical significance** (surf-riding, broaching). Marks are awarded for the discussion.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Encounter frequency (deep water):** $\omega_e = \omega - (\omega^2 V/g)\cos\theta$
- **Encounter period:** $T_e = 2\pi/\omega_e$
- **Encounter angle:** $\mu_e = \arctan\!\big(V\sin\theta/(c - V\cos\theta)\big)$
- **Deep-water dispersion:** $\omega^2 = gk$; phase velocity $c = g/\omega$; wavelength $\lambda = gT^2/(2\pi)$.
- **Sign convention (KTU):** $\theta = 0$ → following; $\theta = 180°$ → head.
- **Head sea:** $\omega_e > \omega$ (waves hit bow more often).
- **Following sea:** $\omega_e < \omega$; can become 0 (wave blocking at $V = g/\omega$).
- **Beam sea ($\theta = 90°$):** $\omega_e = \omega$ — true frequency equals encounter frequency.
- **Critical / blocking speed (following sea):** $V_c = g/\omega = c = \lambda/T$.
- **Blocking wavelength (for given $V$):** $\lambda_b = 2\pi V^2/g$.
- **Non-dimensional frequency:** $\tau_e = \omega_e L / V$ — used as abscissa in RAO plots.
- **Finite depth:** replace $k = \omega^2/g$ with the implicit relation $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kh)$.
- **Surf-riding / broaching risk** at and near $V = V_c$ in following seas.
- **Steepness criterion:** $ak \le 0.04$ for linear (Airy) wave theory validity.
- **Sea water density** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ — relevant for added resistance follow-up.
- **Practical tip:** For $T_e$ near natural heave/pitch period → resonance → high motions.
- **Conversion to remember:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 nautical mile = 1852 m.
- **Final operational corollary:** Altering course ($\theta$) or speed ($V$) is the standard weather-routing strategy to shift $T_e$ away from the resonance band.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strip Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strip Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Strip Theory** (also called the *Slender-Body Strip Method* or *Sectional Integration Method*) is a semi-empirical, three-dimensional ship hydrodynamics formulation in which the hydrodynamic forces and moments acting on a vessel advancing in waves are computed by treating the ship hull as a continuous assembly of **independent two-dimensional (2D) transverse strips**, each strip analyzed in the cross-sectional plane, and then **integrating the resulting sectional forces and moments along the ship's length**.

The theory was originally proposed by **B. V. Korvin-Kroukovsky and W. R. Jacobs** in **1957** as an extension of the work by Havelock on wave resistance, and later rigorously reformulated (with forward-speed effects) by **Salvesen, Tuck, and Faltinsen (1970)** as the so-called *"New Strip Theory"* or *STF (Salvesen-Tuck-Faltinsen) formulation*. The current KTU 2024 syllabus treats it as the foundational frequency-domain tool for ship-motion and added-resistance prediction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Strip Theory sits at the intersection of *potential flow theory* and *ship motion analysis*. It is the gateway to the **Panel Method**, **3D Rankine Source Methods**, and **CFD-based seakeeping codes** (e.g., WAMIT, HydroStar, NEMOH). Mastery of strip theory is **mandatory** before progressing to wave-induced loads, slamming, and springing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Loaf of Bread" Picture

Imagine a long loaf of French bread. To estimate how the loaf bends when you push one end of it sideways, you do not solve a full 3D elasticity problem. Instead, you:

1. **Slice** the loaf into thin transverse cross-sections ("strips"),
2. Compute the **2D response of each slice** as if it were an infinite cylinder in a cross-flow, and
3. **Stack (integrate)** the slice responses back to recover the total loaf deflection.

Strip theory does precisely the same for a ship: the 3D diffraction/radiation problem is reduced to a sequence of **2D sectional problems**, one for every transverse slice of the hull, and the section forces are summed to give total ship motions and wave loads. The mathematical engine is **2D potential flow** (using conformal mapping, Lewis-cone, or Ursell-Jones methods) applied to each cross-section.

## 1.3 Physical & Numerical Constants Used

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea-water density | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Encounter frequency | $\omega_{e}$ | varies | rad/s |
| Wave number | $k$ | $2\pi/\lambda$ | rad/m |
| Ship forward speed | $U$ | varies | m/s |
| Ship length (between perpendiculars) | $L_{pp}$ | varies | m |
| Ship speed parameter | $F_{n}$ | $U/\sqrt{gL}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme numerical problems, students are **not** expected to memorise the sectional hydrodynamic coefficients; the question stem will provide the added-mass, damping, and restoring coefficients per unit length. Your job is the **correct assembly (integration) and the final equation of motion**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Strip Theory

The strip method is valid only when the following hold:

1. **Slender-body assumption:** Ship length is much greater than beam ($L/B \gg 1$). Typical merchant ships satisfy $L/B = 6$ to $9$.
2. **Long-wavelength assumption:** Encounter wavelength $\lambda_{e} \gg B$. Fails for short, steep, head waves.
3. **Linear potential flow:** Fluid is inviscid, incompressible, and irrotational. Viscosity and vortex shedding are **neglected**.
4. **Small-amplitude waves & motions:** Linearisation is admissible (no slamming, no green water).
5. **Sectional independence:** Each 2D strip is hydrodynamically decoupled from its neighbours (radiation cross-talk between strips is ignored). The New Strip Theory (STF) partially removes this by adding **momentum-conservation corrections**.

## 2.2 Operating Logic – Step-by-Step

The strip-theory algorithm proceeds in **five logical stages**:
