1. **Discretise the hull** into $N$ transverse sections (typically $N = 10$ to $21$ along the girth). Each section is at longitudinal position $x_{i}$ with local draught $T(x_i)$ and local beam $B(x_i)$.
2. **Solve the 2D radiation/diffraction problem** for each section, assuming an infinite 2D cylinder oscillating in heave/pitch with the *local* encounter frequency $\omega_{e}$. Outputs: sectional added mass $a_{33}(x)$, $a_{55}(x)$, $a_{35}(x)$ and damping $b_{33}(x)$, $b_{55}(x)$, $b_{35}(x)$ (per unit length).
3. **Account for forward speed** by using the encounter frequency $\omega_{e} = \omega - (k U \cos\mu)$ and applying the STF correction terms (mushroom, time-delay, and cross-coupling integrals).
4. **Integrate section forces** along $L_{pp}$ to obtain total hydrodynamic forces $X$, $Y$, $Z$ and moments $K$, $M$, $N$ on the ship as a whole.
5. **Solve the 6-DOF equations of motion** in the frequency domain to yield Response Amplitude Operators (RAOs) for heave, pitch, roll, etc., from which the **added resistance** $R_{AW}$ is computed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every equation the examiner can reasonably ask**. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute-value and divisibility symbols to preserve markdown-table integrity.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_{e} = \omega - k U \cos\mu$ | Encounter frequency; $\mu$ = wave-to-heading angle |
| 2 | $\lambda_{e} = \dfrac{2\pi g}{U^{2} \cos^{2}\mu}\,\bigl(1 - \sqrt{1 - \tfrac{4 g \omega \cos\mu}{U^{2} \omega^{2}}}\bigr)^{-1}$ | Encounter wavelength for finite-depth free surface |
| 3 | $F_{3}(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!\bigl[a_{33}(x)\ddot{z} + a_{35}(x)\ddot{\theta} + b_{33}(x)\dot{z} + b_{35}(x)\dot{\theta} + c_{33}(x)z + c_{35}(x)\theta\bigr]\,dx$ | Total heave force from strips |
| 4 | $M_{5}(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!x\,\bigl[a_{53}(x)\ddot{z} + a_{55}(x)\ddot{\theta} + b_{53}(x)\dot{z} + b_{55}(x)\dot{\theta} + c_{53}(x)z + c_{55}(x)\theta\bigr]\,dx$ | Total pitch moment about midship |
| 5 | $R_{AW} = \dfrac{\rho g^{2}\zeta_{a}^{2} B^{2}}{2 L} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\dfrac{\vert P(\omega_{e},\mu)\vert \sin^{2}\mu}{\bigl(1 - F_{n}^{2}\cos^{2}\mu\bigr)^{2}}\,d\mu$ | Mean added resistance (Kochin-integral form) |
| 6 | $R_{AW} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\rho g^{2}\zeta_{a}^{2} B^{2}}{L}\,\bigl(\mathrm{RAO}_{\text{heave}}^{2} + \tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\,\mathrm{RAO}_{\text{pitch}}^{2}\bigr)\,\mathcal{K}(k L, F_{n})$ | Engineering approximation; $\mathcal{K}$ is a strip-theory kernel |
| 7 | $\eta_{j}(\omega) = \bigl[\bigl(-\omega^{2}\bigl[\mathbf{M} + \mathbf{A}(\omega)\bigr] - i\omega\mathbf{B}(\omega) + \mathbf{C}\bigr]^{-1}\mathbf{F}_{e}(\omega)\bigr)_{j}$ | Frequency-domain 6-DOF motion (RAO extraction) |
| 8 | $\mathrm{RAO} = \dfrac{\eta_{j,\text{amp}}}{\zeta_{a}}$ | Response Amplitude Operator (per metre of wave) |
| 9 | $A_{33}^{\text{STF}}(x) = a_{33}(x) - \dfrac{U^{2}}{g}\int_{0}^{x}\!a_{33}(\xi)\,d\xi$ (sample term) | STF forward-speed correction (mushroom term) |
| 10 | $\sigma_{R} = \sqrt{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} S_{R}(\omega)\,R_{AW}^{2}(\omega)\,d\omega}$ | Long-term rms added resistance (spectral form) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Strip theory is the **workhorse** of:

- **Concept and contract design** of tankers, bulk carriers, container ships, and naval frigates.
- **Operational guidance**: route planning tools (e.g., *OCTOPUS*, *SWAIM*) embed strip-theory kernels to compute weather-routing in real time.
- **Class-society rule checks**: IACS S21A & S21B wave-induced-bending calculations trace back to strip-theory bending moments.
- **Mooring and station-keeping feasibility** for FPSOs and semi-submersibles (with Newman-style corrections).
- **Model-test correlation**: Towing-tank RAOs are typically reduced using a strip-theory-based identification scheme.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 Part-B (14-mark) questions, examiners expect you to (i) state the **assumptions**, (ii) write the **integrated equation of motion** in matrix form, (iii) substitute numerical section coefficients (given in the question), and (iv) comment on **when strip theory breaks down**. Memorising formula 3 and 4 above in the expanded form is the single highest-yield investment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – From 3D Radiation Potential to Strip Form

Starting from the 3D linearised radiation potential $\Phi_{R}(\mathbf{x},t) = \mathrm{Re}\{\phi_{R}(\mathbf{x})\,e^{-i\omega t}\}$, we assume the **slender-body separation**:

$$
\phi_{R}(x,y,z) \;\approx\; \int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!\phi_{2D}(x'\,;\,y,z;\,\omega)\,w(x-x')\,dx'
$$

where $w$ is a smooth weighting (often a delta in the classical strip method). The local 2D potential $\phi_{2D}$ satisfies the **2D Laplace equation** in the cross-section plane and the **linearised free-surface condition**:

$$
g\,\frac{\partial \phi_{2D}}{\partial z} \;=\; -\omega^{2}\,\phi_{2D} \quad \text{at } z = 0
$$

The sectional force per unit length on the strip due to heave is obtained by integrating pressure $p = -\rho\,\partial\Phi_{R}/\partial t = i\omega\rho\,\phi_{R}$ over the instantaneous wetted section $C(x)$:

$$
f_{3}(x,t) \;=\; -\rho\!\!\oint_{C(x)}\!\!\frac{\partial\Phi_{R}}{\partial t}\,n_{3}\,d\ell
$$

Using Bernoulli's linearised equation and the radiation-potential boundary condition on the oscillating hull ($\partial\phi_{R}/\partial n = V_{n}$), this integral is rewritten in the **classic strip form**:

$$
f_{3}(x,t) \;=\; -\rho\!\!\oint_{C(x)}\!\!i\omega\,\phi_{R}\,n_{3}\,d\ell
$$

Separating $\phi_{R}$ into **radiation** and **diffraction** parts, and using the **Haskind-Newman relations**, the 2D section force is decomposed as:

$$
f_{3}(x,t) \;=\; -a_{33}(x)\ddot{z}(t) - b_{33}(x)\dot{z}(t)
$$

where $a_{33}$ and $b_{33}$ are the **2D added mass** and **damping per unit length**. Summing all strips recovers formula (3) above.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Heave and Pitch Coupled Equations of Motion

The 6-DOF equation of motion in the frequency domain is:

$$
\bigl[-\omega^{2}\bigl(\mathbf{M} + \mathbf{A}(\omega)\bigr) - i\omega\mathbf{B}(\omega) + \mathbf{C}\bigr]\,\boldsymbol{\eta}(\omega) \;=\; \mathbf{F}_{e}(\omega)
$$

For the **vertical-plane (heave–pitch) sub-system** (modes 3 and 5), the strip-theory form is obtained by **assembling** the integrated coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{33}(\omega) &= \int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!a_{33}(x,\omega)\,dx \\
A_{35}(\omega) &= \int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!a_{35}(x,\omega)\,dx \;=\; A_{53}(\omega)\\
A_{55}(\omega) &= \int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!a_{55}(x,\omega)\,dx
\end{aligned}
$$

with identical definitions for $\mathbf{B}(\omega)$. Substituting into the 2-DOF form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Bigl[-\omega^{2}\bigl(M + A_{33}\bigr) - i\omega B_{33} + C_{33}\Bigr]z(\omega) \;+\; \Bigl[-\omega^{2}A_{35} - i\omega B_{35} + C_{35}\Bigr]\theta(\omega) &= F_{3}^{e}(\omega)\\[4pt]
\Bigl[-\omega^{2}A_{53} - i\omega B_{53} + C_{53}\Bigr]z(\omega) \;+\; \Bigl[-\omega^{2}\bigl(I_{55} + A_{55}\bigr) - i\omega B_{55} + C_{55}\Bigr]\theta(\omega) &= F_{5}^{e}(\omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving this $2 \times 2$ complex system yields the heave and pitch RAOs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z(\omega) &= \dfrac{\bigl(-\omega^{2}(I_{55}+A_{55}) - i\omega B_{55} + C_{55}\bigr)F_{3}^{e} - \bigl(-\omega^{2}A_{35} - i\omega B_{35} + C_{35}\bigr)F_{5}^{e}}{\Delta(\omega)}\\[4pt]
\theta(\omega) &= \dfrac{\bigl(-\omega^{2}(M+A_{33}) - i\omega B_{33} + C_{33}\bigr)F_{5}^{e} - \bigl(-\omega^{2}A_{35} - i\omega B_{35} + C_{35}\bigr)F_{3}^{e}}{\Delta(\omega)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta(\omega)$ is the determinant of the impedance matrix.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Added Resistance from the Havelock–Kochin Source

For a ship advancing at speed $U$ in head waves ($\mu = 180^{\circ}$, $\cos\mu = -1$) the mean added resistance equals the **second-order mean drift force**:

$$
R_{AW} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,\zeta_{a}^{2}\,\dfrac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}\!\!\left[\int\!\!\int \phi_{D}^{*}\,\phi_{D}\,dS\right]_{\text{mean}}
$$

Using the **far-field Kochin function** $P(\theta;\omega_{e})$ and the **Havelock–Maruo formula**, this reduces to:

$$
R_{AW} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho g^{2}\zeta_{a}^{2}B^{2}}{2L}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\dfrac{\vert P(\omega_{e},\mu)\vert \sin^{2}\mu}{\bigl(1 - F_{n}^{2}\cos^{2}\mu\bigr)^{2}}\,d\mu
$$

This integral is evaluated **numerically** by discretising the wave-heading circle into $N_{\mu}$ slices (typically $N_{\mu} = 24$ to $48$). In the KTU numerical part, the question will provide tabulated $P(\omega_{e},\mu)$ values; you just perform the **trapezoidal integration** to obtain $R_{AW}$ in kN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic recap** for students: $R_{AW}$ is a *mean* force over a wave cycle. To get the **rms** long-term value, multiply by the spectral density and integrate as in formula 10 of the cheat sheet. The factor $\sin^{2}\mu$ in the numerator reflects the **Michell–Havelock** geometric projection – head seas ($\mu = 180^{\circ}$) and beam seas ($\mu = 90^{\circ}$) give zero contribution at this order, with maximum at $\mu \approx 150^{\circ}$ (bow quartering).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Strip Decomposition Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **sectional-assembly algorithm** used in strip theory. Note the alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords) and clean uppercase labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[SHIP HULL GEOMETRY] --> B0[DISCRETISE INTO N STRIPS]
    B0 --> C0[EACH STRIP AT STATION X]
    C0 --> D0[COMPUTE 2D ENCOUNTER FREQUENCY WE]
    D0 --> E0[2D RADIATION PROBLEM PER STRIP]
    E0 --> F0[SECTIONAL ADDED MASS a33 a35 a55]
    E0 --> G0[SECTIONAL DAMPING b33 b35 b55]
    F0 --> H0[INTEGRATE OVER LENGTH]
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[ASSEMBLE 6 DOF IMPEDANCE MATRIX]
    I0 --> J0[FREQUENCY DOMAIN SOLVER]
    J0 --> K0[RAO HEAVE PITCH ROLL]
    K0 --> L0[ADDED RESISTANCE RAW VIA MICHELL]
    L0 --> M0[KTU FINAL ANSWER]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Section Radiation Process (2D Slice Analysis)

The block diagram below maps how a single 2D cross-section is converted into hydrodynamically meaningful section coefficients.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[INPUT SECTION GEOMETRY] --> S2[LEWIS CONE OR CONFORMAL MAP]
    S2 --> S3[2D POTENTIAL PHI 2D SOLVED]
    S3 --> S4[INTEGRATE PRESSURE OVER WETTED C]
    S4 --> S5[ADDED MASS A33 PER METER]
    S4 --> S6[DAMPING B33 PER METER]
    S4 --> S7[CROSS COUPLED A35 B35]
    S5 --> S8[STORE IN STRIP TABLE]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Strip-Theory Code

The figure below shows how a typical seakeeping program (e.g., **SHIPMO** or **VERES**) organises the strip-theory modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0[HULL FORM OFFSET FILE] --> M1[GEOMETRY PROCESSOR]
    M1 --> M2[2D SECTION LIBRARY]
    M2 --> M3[LEWIS OR URSSELL JONES SOLVER]
    M3 --> M4[STF FORWARD SPEED CORRECTIONS]
    M4 --> M5[ASSEMBLY MATRIX]
    M5 --> M6[LINEAR SOLVER LAPACK ZGESV]
    M6 --> M7[RAO GENERATOR]
    M7 --> M8[ADDED RESISTANCE POST PROCESSOR]
    M8 --> M9[REPORT TO USER]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **STF correction block (M4)** is what separates the *classical* strip theory (Kroukovsky–Jacobs, 1957) from the *New Strip Theory* (Salvesen–Tuck–Faltinsen, 1970). For KTU 2024 questions at the 14-mark level, the examiner may ask you to **list the three STF corrections** – these are: (i) **Mushroom effect** (uniform-flow correction), (ii) **Momentum-conservation correction** (wake adjustment), and (iii) **Time-delay (memory) correction** (using the Lewis-cone forward-speed kernel). Writing these three names alone often fetches 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define strip theory and list any four of its underlying assumptions. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer (≈ 95 words):**

Strip theory is a **slender-body method** in ship hydrodynamics in which the 3D problem of wave-induced motions and loads is replaced by a sequence of 2D radiation/diffraction problems on transverse hull sections; the section forces are integrated along the ship length to obtain the total response. The principal assumptions are:

1. Ship is slender ($L/B \gg 1$).
2. Encounter wavelength is long compared with the beam ($\lambda_{e}/B \gg 1$).
3. Linear potential flow (inviscid, incompressible, irrotational).
4. Small-amplitude waves and motions (no slamming).
5. Sectional independence – 2D strips are decoupled from each other.

### Q2. Differentiate between Classical Strip Theory and New Strip Theory (STF). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

| Aspect | Classical (Kroukovsky–Jacobs) | New Strip Theory (STF) |
|---|---|---|
| Forward-speed effects | Ignored / treated approximately | **Explicitly included** |
| 3D flow coupling between sections | Sectional independence | **Mushroom, momentum & time-delay corrections** |
| Applicable Froude range | $F_{n} \le 0.2$ | $F_{n}$ up to 0.4–0.5 |
| Reference frame | Earth-fixed | Earth-fixed + body-fixed coupling terms |
| Damping in roll | Often under-predicted | Improved by cross-coupling terms |

In short, the New Strip Theory removes the assumption of **sectional independence** by introducing analytical corrections derived from conservation of mass and momentum in the body-fixed frame.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Choice-Bank (14-Mark Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

(a) Starting from the 2D radiation potential, derive the strip-theory expression for the heave force on a ship advancing in regular head waves. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

(b) A slender ship of length $L_{pp} = 120$ m, beam $B = 16$ m, and forward speed $U = 7.7$ m/s is sailing in head waves of height $H = 3$ m and wavelength $\lambda = 100$ m. The 2D section damping coefficient at the midship is $b_{33}(x=0) = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ Ns/m per metre, and varies linearly from $0.4 b_{33}(0)$ at the ends to its full value at the midship. Using the Michell–Havelock integral, compute the **mean added resistance** $R_{AW}$ in kN. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², and the Kochin function magnitude $\vert P \vert = 0.85$ (assume constant). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

1. **Assumptions:** slender body, linear potential flow, small waves, 2D cross-section.
2. **2D radiation potential** satisfies Laplace equation in the cross-plane with linearised free-surface condition $g\,\partial\phi_{2D}/\partial z = \omega^{2}\phi_{2D}$.
3. **Sectional heave force** is obtained by integrating unsteady Bernoulli pressure over the wetted girth $C(x)$:
   $$f_{3}(x,t) = -\rho\oint_{C(x)}\!\frac{\partial\Phi_{R}}{\partial t}\,n_{3}\,d\ell = -a_{33}(x)\ddot{z} - b_{33}(x)\dot{z}$$
4. **Total heave force** is the line integral along the ship:
   $$F_{3}(t) = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!\bigl[-a_{33}(x)\ddot{z} - b_{33}(x)\dot{z}\bigr]dx$$
5. In the frequency domain with $z(t) = \hat{z}\,e^{-i\omega t}$:
   $$F_{3}(\omega) = \bigl[\omega^{2}A_{33} - i\omega B_{33}\bigr]\hat{z}$$
   where $A_{33} = \int a_{33}\,dx$ and $B_{33} = \int b_{33}\,dx$.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

1. **Wave amplitude:** $\zeta_{a} = H/2 = 1.5$ m.
2. **B-squared term:** $B^{2} = 16^{2} = 256$ m².
3. **Froude number:** $F_{n} = U/\sqrt{gL} = 7.7/\sqrt{9.81 \times 120} = 7.7/34.32 = 0.224$.
4. **Head waves:** $\mu = 180^{\circ}$, so $\sin^{2}\mu = 0$, $\cos^{2}\mu = 1$. The geometric Michell weight in the numerator vanishes for **pure head seas** at this order – this signals that the Kochin function is needed and the question is a **bow-quartering** scenario. The examiner has set $\sin^{2}\mu / (1 - F_{n}^{2}\cos^{2}\mu)^{2}$ as a pre-tabulated value $K = 1.32$ (radians).
5. **Linear distribution of damping** – the average over the length is $0.7 \times b_{33}(0)$ (trapezoidal average of $0.4$ at ends and $1.0$ at midship):

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{33,\text{avg}} &= \dfrac{0.4 + 2(1.0) + 0.4}{4}\times b_{33}(0) \\
&= 0.7 \times 1.2 \times 10^{5} = 8.4 \times 10^{4} \text{ Ns/m per m}
\end{aligned}$$

6. **Total damping integral** (assuming $b_{33}$ symmetric):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!b_{33}(x)\,dx &= B_{33,\text{avg}} \times L = 8.4 \times 10^{4} \times 120 \\
&= 1.008 \times 10^{7} \text{ Ns}
\end{aligned}$$

7. **Encounter frequency** for head waves: $\omega_{e} = \omega - kU = \omega(1 - U\omega/g)$ with $\omega = \sqrt{2\pi g/\lambda} = 0.785$ rad/s. Then $\omega_{e} = 0.785(1 - 7.7 \times 0.785/9.81) = 0.785 \times 0.384 = 0.302$ rad/s.
8. **Heave RAO** (using $\vert z/\zeta_{a}\vert \approx 0.32$ – provided in the question stem for this KTU variant).
9. **Substitute into the Havelock formula** (engineering form, formula 6 of the cheat sheet):

$$R_{AW} = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81^{2} \times 1.5^{2} \times 256}{120} \times 0.32^{2} \times 0.85 \times 1.32$$

10. **Evaluate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator factor} &= 1025 \times 96.24 \times 2.25 \times 256 = 5.69 \times 10^{7}\\
\text{Divided by 120} &= 4.74 \times 10^{5}\\
\text{RAO squared} &= 0.1024\\
\text{P factor} &= 0.85\\
\text{K kernel} &= 1.32\\[4pt]
R_{AW} &= 4.74 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1024 \times 0.85 \times 1.32 \\
&= 5.45 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{54.5 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Three common marks-losing errors in this question:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $g^{2}$** in the Michell numerator (most common; -2 marks).
> 2. **Using $H$ instead of $\zeta_{a} = H/2$** for the wave amplitude (-1 mark).
> 3. **Forgetting to convert N to kN** at the end (-0.5 mark).
> 4. Using $F_{n}$ in the denominator of $(1 - F_{n}^{2}\cos^{2}\mu)^{2}$ without squaring $F_{n}$ (very common; -1 mark).
> 5. Mixing the **Froude number with the speed-length ratio** $U/\sqrt{L}$ (dimensionally wrong; -1 mark).

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative]**

(a) With the aid of neat sketches, explain how a ship hull is discretised into strips in the strip-theory method. Discuss why the **midship section** is given the highest weight in the integration. **(7 marks)**

(b) For a vessel operating in beam seas ($\mu = 90^{\circ}$), the heave RAO at encounter frequency $\omega_{e} = 0.6$ rad/s is $\vert \mathrm{RAO}_{\text{heave}}\vert = 0.45$ m/m, and the pitch RAO about midship is $\vert \mathrm{RAO}_{\text{pitch}}\vert = 0.18$ rad/m. Using the strip-theory engineering approximation (formula 6 of the cheat sheet), with $B = 18$ m, $L = 150$ m, $\zeta_{a} = 1.25$ m, and strip-theory kernel $\mathcal{K}(kL,F_{n}) = 1.85$, compute the mean added resistance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

The hull is sliced into $N = 11$ to $21$ transverse stations, conventionally located at the **apparent edges of the waterplane** plus evenly distributed in between. Each section is characterised by its local section coefficient $C_{m}(x)$, beam $B(x)$, and draught $T(x)$. The midship section receives the highest weight because (i) it is the **fattest** part of the hull (largest beam-to-length ratio, biggest displacement per unit length), (ii) the **2D added mass and damping peak** in the midship region, and (iii) the longitudinal lever arm $x$ multiplying the pitch moment term in formula 4 is **maximum** at midship (i.e., $x \to L/2$), thereby dominating $M_{5}$.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

Use formula 6 directly:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{AW} &= \dfrac{\rho g^{2}\zeta_{a}^{2} B^{2}}{L}\,\bigl(\mathrm{RAO}_{z}^{2} + \tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\,\mathrm{RAO}_{\theta}^{2}\bigr)\,\mathcal{K}\\
&= \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81^{2} \times 1.25^{2} \times 18^{2}}{150} \times \bigl(0.45^{2} + \tfrac{150^{2}}{4}\times 0.18^{2}\bigr) \times 1.85
\end{aligned}$$

Compute inside out:

- Prefactor: $1025 \times 96.24 \times 1.5625 \times 324 / 150 = 3.49 \times 10^{5}$.
- $\mathrm{RAO}_{z}^{2} = 0.2025$.
- $\mathrm{RAO}_{\theta}^{2} \times L^{2}/4 = 0.0324 \times 5625 = 182.25$.
- Bracket sum: $0.2025 + 182.25 \approx 182.45$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{AW} &= 3.49 \times 10^{5} \times 182.45 \times 1.85 \\
&= 1.18 \times 10^{8} \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{118\,000 \text{ kN} = 118 \text{ MN}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. Beam seas contribute **maximum drift** in the second-order sense – the $\sin^{2}\mu$ factor in the Michell integral is unity at $\mu = 90^{\circ}$.
> 2. Pitch RAO is in **radians per metre** – the $L^{2}/4$ factor multiplies the *square* of the pitch RAO, NOT the linear pitch angle.
> 3. Many students drop the **$\mathcal{K}$ kernel** – it represents the strip-theory spectral weighting; omitting it leads to a ~45% under-prediction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap – High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist on a single A4 sheet the night before the exam:**

- [ ] **Strip theory** = 2D section analysis + longitudinal integration. Method by Korvin-Kroukovsky & Jacobs (1957).
- [ ] **New Strip Theory (STF)** by Salvesen, Tuck & Faltinsen (1970) adds three corrections: *Mushroom, Momentum, Time-delay*.
- [ ] Encounter frequency: $\omega_{e} = \omega - kU\cos\mu$.
- [ ] Heave force integral: $F_{3} = \int[a_{33}\ddot{z} + a_{35}\ddot{\theta} + b_{33}\dot{z} + b_{35}\dot{\theta} + c_{33}z + c_{35}\theta]dx$.
- [ ] Pitch moment integral: $M_{5} = \int x \cdot [\text{same integrand with 5 replacing 3}]\,dx$.
- [ ] **Michell–Havelock** added resistance: contains $\rho g^{2}\zeta_{a}^{2}B^{2}/(2L)$ prefactor, $\vert P(\omega_{e},\mu)\vert$ Kochin function, and the $\sin^{2}\mu/(1-F_{n}^{2}\cos^{2}\mu)^{2}$ geometric kernel.
- [ ] **RAO = (response amplitude) / (wave amplitude)** – dimensionless, in m/m for heave and rad/m for pitch.
- [ ] **Assumptions (memorise):** slender body, long wave, linear potential, small amplitude, sectional independence.
- [ ] **Failure modes:** short waves ($\lambda/L < 0.5$), blunt bows, high $F_{n} > 0.4$, roll and yaw (strip theory is **weak in roll** because of 3D bilge keel effects).
- [ ] Numerical evaluation: use **trapezoidal rule** for $\int b_{33}dx$; **Simpson's 1/3 rule** is preferred when the question explicitly says "smooth variation".
- [ ] KTU expects **both forms**: expanded equation of motion AND the Havelock integral in the same answer.
- [ ] **Conversion tip:** 1 MN = $10^{3}$ kN = $10^{6}$ N. Always carry units to the last line.
- [ ] Engineering-approximation formula 6 is the **fastest way to score** in 7-mark numerical questions where the section coefficients are not given.
- [ ] Do not confuse **added resistance** $R_{AW}$ (mean over wave cycle) with **instantaneous wave-induced force** $F_{3}(t)$ (oscillatory).
- [ ] **Final sanity check:** added resistance for a 100-m ship in 3-m head waves is typically **50–500 kN**; if your answer is in the MN range, recheck.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship in a Seaway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ship in a Seaway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Grade Definition

A **Ship in a Seaway** refers to the dynamic behaviour of a vessel navigating through ocean waves, where the floating body experiences oscillatory motions in **six degrees of freedom (6-DOF)** due to wave-induced hydrodynamic forces. In the framework of KTU's 2024 Scheme naval architecture syllabus, a seaway is modelled as a stochastic sea surface composed of a superposition of regular sinusoidal wave components, each with characteristic amplitude, frequency, and random phase, typically described by an **energy spectrum** $S(\omega)$.

The mathematical analysis of a ship in a seaway combines potential flow theory, rigid-body dynamics, and stochastic process theory to predict **ship motions** (displacements and rotations), **added wave resistance**, and **slamming/whipping** responses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The course outcome mapping for this topic falls under **CO2**: *"Apply the principles of seakeeping to evaluate ship motions, added resistance, and operational limits in irregular seaways."* Cognitive level: **Apply / Analyse (Bloom Level 3–4)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a bathtub with a floating plastic toy boat. Now shake the water gently with your hand. The boat does not just bob up and down — it tilts forward, rocks sideways, slides a little, and sometimes spins. That is exactly what happens to a real ship at sea, except the bathtub is the **ocean**, and the hand-shaking is **wind-driven waves**.

A ship is essentially a **constrained floating pendulum**:
- It wants to stay upright because of **gravity (restoring force)**.
- It is pushed by **waves (external forcing)**.
- It has **inertia** (resistance to change of motion).
- The surrounding water resists the motion through **damping (radiation)**.

The "battle" between these four forces — inertia, restoring, damping, and wave excitation — determines whether the ship moves gently or dangerously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$**
> - Density of seawater: **$\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$** (freshwater: $1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$)
> - Standard reference area for spectral formulation: **encountered wave spectrum $S_\zeta(\omega_e)$**

### 1.3 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) — Naming Convention

| S.No | Motion | Axis | Type | Common Notation |
|------|--------|------|------|------------------|
| 1 | Surge | $x$ | Translation | $x_1$ or $\xi$ |
| 2 | Sway | $y$ | Translation | $x_2$ or $\eta$ |
| 3 | Heave | $z$ | Translation (vertical) | $x_3$ or $\zeta$ |
| 4 | Roll | about $x$ | Rotation (transverse) | $x_4$ or $\phi$ |
| 5 | Pitch | about $y$ | Rotation (longitudinal) | $x_5$ or $\theta$ |
| 6 | Yaw | about $z$ | Rotation (vertical) | $x_6$ or $\psi$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In a **head/following sea**, surge, heave, and pitch are dominant. In a **beam sea**, sway, heave, roll, and yaw are dominant. This wave-heading dependence is critical for KTU problems.

### 1.4 Two Reference Coordinate Systems

- **Earth-fixed (inertial) frame $OXYZ$**: Fixed in space; useful for describing wave geometry.
- **Body-fixed (ship-fixed) frame $Gxyz$**: Origin at ship's center of gravity $G$; $x$-axis along the longitudinal centerline, $y$ to port, $z$ downward.

The ship's motions are most naturally expressed in the body-fixed frame.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Ship–Wave Interaction — Step-by-Step Logic

When a wave meets a ship, the following sequence of events occurs at every instant:

1. **Wave Excitation Phase:** The undisturbed wave field exerts pressure on the instantaneous wetted surface of the hull. The integrated pressure gives the **wave excitation force** $\vec{F}_{\text{exc}}(t)$.

2. **Radiation Phase:** As the ship moves, it generates outgoing waves (like a moving pressure point). The work done to create these waves manifests as **radiation damping** $B_{ij}(\omega)$. The associated inertia is the **added mass** $A_{ij}(\omega)$.

3. **Diffraction Phase:** The presence of the hull scatters (diffracts) the incoming waves. This contributes to the **diffraction force**, which combines with Froude–Krylov force to give total excitation.

4. **Hydrostatic Restoring Phase:** Gravity tries to pull the ship back to its equilibrium position; buoyancy shifts with the displaced volume, creating **restoring forces/moments** $C_{ij}$.

5. **Equation Assembly:** Newton's second law applied to the rigid body in the body-fixed frame yields the **frequency-domain equations of motion**.

### 2.2 Frequency-Domain Equation of Motion (The Core Equation)

For a single degree of freedom at circular frequency $\omega$:

$$\left[ -\omega^2 \left( M + A(\omega) \right) - i\omega B(\omega) + C \right] X(\omega) = F_{\text{exc}}(\omega)$$

Where:
- $M$ = rigid-body mass / moment of inertia
- $A(\omega)$ = frequency-dependent added mass / added moment of inertia
- $B(\omega)$ = frequency-dependent radiation damping coefficient
- $C$ = hydrostatic restoring coefficient (e.g., $C = \rho g A_{\text{WP}}$ for heave)
- $F_{\text{exc}}(\omega)$ = complex wave excitation amplitude
- $X(\omega)$ = complex motion amplitude (output)

### 2.3 Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)

The **RAO** $\text{RAO}(\omega)$ is the ratio of ship motion amplitude to wave amplitude for a regular wave:

$$\text{RAO}(\omega) = \frac{\vert X(\omega) \vert}{\zeta_a}$$

where $\zeta_a$ is the incident wave amplitude. RAO is a **complex transfer function** — its magnitude is the amplification, and its phase is the lag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** RAO is the **most frequently tested concept** in seakeeping problems. In irregular waves, the spectrum of motion is:
> $$S_X(\omega_e) = \vert \text{RAO}(\omega_e) \vert^2 \, S_\zeta(\omega_e)$$

### 2.4 Wave Encounter Frequency

A ship moving with speed $V$ at heading angle $\mu$ relative to waves of frequency $\omega$ encounters them at a Doppler-shifted frequency:

$$\omega_e = \omega - \frac{\omega^2}{g} V \cos\mu = \omega \left( 1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\mu}{g} \right)$$

Special cases:
- **Head sea** ($\mu = 180°$): $\omega_e = \omega \left( 1 + \dfrac{\omega V}{g} \right)$ — frequency increases.
- **Following sea** ($\mu = 0°$): $\omega_e = \omega \left( 1 - \dfrac{\omega V}{g} \right)$ — frequency decreases.
- **Beam sea** ($\mu = 90°$): $\omega_e = \omega$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\omega_e = \omega(1 - \omega V \cos\mu / g)$ | Wave encounter frequency | rad/s |
| 2 | $T_e = 2\pi / \omega_e$ | Encounter period | s |
| 3 | $\lambda_e = 2\pi g / \omega_e^2$ | Encounter wavelength | m |
| 4 | $C_{33} = \rho g A_{\text{WP}}$ | Heave restoring (waterplane area) | N/m |
| 5 | $C_{44} = \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T$ | Roll restoring (metacentric) | N·m/rad |
| 6 | $C_{55} = \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_L$ | Pitch restoring | N·m/rad |
| 7 | $\text{RAO}(\omega) = \vert X \vert / \zeta_a$ | Response Amplitude Operator | m/m or rad/m |
| 8 | $S_X(\omega_e) = \vert \text{RAO} \vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega_e)$ | Motion spectrum | varies |
| 9 | $\sigma_X^2 = \int_0^\infty S_X(\omega_e) \, d\omega_e$ | Variance of motion | varies |
| 10 | $X_{1/3} = 4\sigma_X$ | Significant amplitude (Rayleigh) | same as $X$ |
| 11 | $R_{\text{add}} = \int_0^\infty \frac{F_e^2(\omega)}{2 B(\omega)} \cdot \frac{2}{\pi} \, d\omega$ | Added resistance (Haskind) | N |
| 12 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{C/(M+A)}$ | Natural frequency (SDOF) | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the daily working tools of a **Naval Architect** in shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock) for designing offshore vessels, FPSOs, and high-speed container ships.

### 2.6 Sea Spectra (Irregular Sea Representation)

Real seas are irregular. KTU typically asks for **Pierson–Moskowitz (PM)** or **ISSC** spectra:

$$S_\zeta(\omega) = \frac{A}{\omega^5} \exp\left( -\frac{B}{\omega^4} \right)$$

where $A = 0.0081 g^2$, $B = 0.74 (g/U_{19.5})^4$, and $U_{19.5}$ is the wind speed at 19.5 m height.

The **significant wave height** $H_s = 4\sigma_\zeta$ where $\sigma_\zeta$ is the standard deviation of surface elevation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Single-DOF Heave Equation in Regular Waves

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup:**
Consider a ship free to heave only (constrained in other DOF). Let $z$ be the instantaneous vertical position of mass center $G$ above equilibrium, $\zeta_a$ the wave amplitude, and $\omega$ the wave circular frequency.

**Step 2 — Force Balance (Newton's Second Law):**
Sum of vertical forces equals mass times vertical acceleration:

$$M \ddot{z} = F_{\text{exc}}(t) - F_{\text{radiation}}(t) - F_{\text{restoring}}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Decompose Forces:**
- Excitation (complex form): $F_{\text{exc}}(t) = \Re\{\hat{F}_e \, e^{i\omega t}\}$
- Radiation: $F_{\text{rad}} = A(\omega) \ddot{z} + B(\omega) \dot{z}$
- Restoring (linearised hydrostatic): $F_{\text{rest}} = \rho g A_{\text{WP}} \cdot z = C_{33} z$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Equation of Motion:**

$$M \ddot{z} + A(\omega) \ddot{z} + B(\omega) \dot{z} + C_{33} z = \Re\{\hat{F}_e \, e^{i\omega t}\}$$

Combine $M$ and $A(\omega)$:

$$\left[ M + A(\omega) \right] \ddot{z} + B(\omega) \dot{z} + C_{33} z = \Re\{\hat{F}_e \, e^{i\omega t}\}$$

**Step 5 — Assume Harmonic Steady-State Solution:**
Let $z(t) = \Re\{\hat{z} \, e^{i\omega t}\}$. Then:

$$\dot{z} = i\omega \hat{z} e^{i\omega t}, \qquad \ddot{z} = -\omega^2 \hat{z} e^{i\omega t}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$\left[ -\omega^2(M + A) + i\omega B + C_{33} \right] \hat{z} = \hat{F}_e$$

$$\therefore \quad \hat{z} = \frac{\hat{F}_e}{-\omega^2(M + A(\omega)) + i\omega B(\omega) + C_{33}}$$

**Step 7 — Define RAO:**
Since $\hat{F}_e = \hat{f}_e \cdot \zeta_a$ where $\hat{f}_e$ is the excitation force per unit wave amplitude:

$$\text{RAO}_{heave}(\omega) = \frac{\hat{z}}{\zeta_a} = \frac{\hat{f}_e}{-\omega^2(M + A(\omega)) + i\omega B(\omega) + C_{33}}$$

**Step 8 — Magnitude and Phase:**

$$\vert \text{RAO} \vert = \frac{\vert \hat{f}_e \vert}{\sqrt{\left[ C_{33} - \omega^2(M+A) \right]^2 + \left[ \omega B \right]^2}}$$

$$\angle \text{RAO} = -\arctan\left( \frac{\omega B}{C_{33} - \omega^2(M+A)} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behaviour of RAO vs $\omega$:**
> - At $\omega \to 0$: $\vert \text{RAO} \vert \to \vert \hat{f}_e \vert / C_{33}$ (quasi-static).
> - At resonance $\omega = \omega_n$: peak amplification limited only by $B$.
> - At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert \text{RAO} \vert \to 0$ (inertia dominates).

### 3.2 Derivation: Encounter Frequency Relation from Wave Phase

**Step 1:** Wave phase at ship position $(x_s(t), 0)$:
$$\Phi(x_s, t) = k x_s \cos\mu - \omega t + \text{phase}$$

Wait — using 2D wave $\zeta = a\cos(kx\cos\mu - \omega t)$ with ship at $x_s = Vt$:

$$\Phi(t) = k V t \cos\mu - \omega t = -(\omega - kV\cos\mu) t$$

**Step 2:** Define encounter frequency so that $\Phi(t) = -\omega_e t + \text{const}$:

$$\omega_e = \omega - kV\cos\mu$$

**Step 3:** Use dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk$, so $k = \omega^2/g$:

$$\boxed{\omega_e = \omega \left( 1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\mu}{g} \right)}$$

This is the final KTU-board-ready expression. Note: a sign-convention mismatch (some texts use $\omega_e = \omega + kV\cos\mu$ for head seas because $\mu=180°$). Always state your convention.

### 3.3 Derivation: Significant Motion Amplitude (Rayleigh Distribution)

**Step 1:** For narrow-band spectrum, the motion envelope $A$ follows Rayleigh PDF:

$$p(A) = \frac{A}{\sigma_X^2} \exp\left( -\frac{A^2}{2\sigma_X^2} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Expected value of the largest one-third:

$$X_{1/3} = \int_{A_0}^\infty A \cdot p(A|A>A_0) \, dA \quad \text{where} \quad \int_{A_0}^\infty p(A) dA = 1/3$$

**Step 3:** Solving using Rayleigh statistics:

$$X_{1/3} = 2 \sigma_X \sqrt{2 \ln 3} \approx 4\sigma_X$$

This is why $H_s = 4\sigma_\zeta$ for waves.

### 3.4 Multi-DOF Matrix Form (Coupled Equations)

The full 6-DOF equation in matrix form:

$$\left[ \mathbf{M} + \mathbf{A}(\omega) \right] \ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{B}(\omega) \dot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{C} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{exc}}(\omega, t)$$

where each entry $\mathbf{M}_{ij}$, $\mathbf{A}_{ij}$, $\mathbf{B}_{ij}$ is a $(6 \times 6)$ matrix coupling motions. For practical KTU problems, couplings are often neglected except:
- **Heave–Pitch coupling** (dominant in head/following seas)
- **Roll–Yaw coupling** (in beam seas, due to $A_{46} \ne 0$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Most exam problems assume either pure roll or pure heave/pitch (uncoupled). If coupling appears, the problem will explicitly provide the off-diagonal terms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ship in a Seaway — Functional Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Input / Wave Generation] --> B[Irregular Sea Surface zeta_x_t]
    B --> C[Spectral Decomposition S_omega]
    C --> D[Regular Wave Component k, omega, mu]
    D --> E[Incident Wave Field]
    E --> F[Hull Wetted Surface]
    F --> G[Froude-Krylov Force]
    F --> H[Diffraction Force]
    G --> I[Wave Excitation Force F_exc]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Equations of Motion Matrix]
    K[Added Mass A_omega] --> J
    L[Radiation Damping B_omega] --> J
    M[Hydrostatic Restoring C] --> J
    J --> N[RAO Calculation]
    N --> O[Motion Spectrum S_X]
    O --> P[Significant Response X_1/3]
    P --> Q{Safety Check}
    Q -->|Acceptable| R[Operate]
    Q -->|Exceeds Limits| S[Alter Course or Speed]
```

### 4.2 6-DOF Motion Schematic (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Trans[Translational DOF]
        X1[Surge x direction]
        X2[Sway y direction]
        X3[Heave z direction]
    end
    subgraph Rot[Rotational DOF]
        X4[Roll about x]
        X5[Pitch about y]
        X6[Yaw about z]
    end
    Trans --> M[6x6 Mass Matrix M]
    Rot --> M
    M --> EQ[Frequency Domain Equation]
    A[6x6 Added Mass A omega] --> EQ
    B[6x6 Damping B omega] --> EQ
    C[6x6 Restoring C] --> EQ
    EQ --> OUT[Motion Response Vector x_1 to x_6]
```

### 4.3 RAO Magnitude vs Frequency — Schematic Trend

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[omega equals 0] -->|Quasi-static| B[RAO approx f_e divided by C]
    B --> C[Resonance Peak at omega_n]
    C -->|omega increases| D[Roll-off 1 divided by omega squared]
    D --> E[RAO approx 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For **roll**, the RAO often shows a sharp peak near the natural roll period (typically 10–25 s for ships). For **heave and pitch**, the peaks are broader and shifted due to higher added mass and different restoring.

### 4.4 Coupling Architecture in Head Sea

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HS[Head Sea Excitation] --> EXC[Excitation Vector F3 heave, F5 pitch]
    EXC --> H[Heave Equation]
    EXC --> P[Pitch Equation]
    H --> C1[Coupling Term C35]
    P --> C2[Coupling Term C53]
    C1 --> P
    C2 --> H
    H --> OUT1[z output]
    P --> OUT2[theta output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The coupling terms $C_{35}$ and $C_{53}$ arise because heaving motion induces a pitching moment and vice versa, especially when the center of buoyancy shifts off midship. This is **NOT** a symmetric beam-sea coupling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question 1. Define Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) and state its significance in seakeeping analysis.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The RAO is the ratio of the steady-state amplitude of a ship motion (translation or rotation) to the amplitude of the incident regular wave at a given frequency and heading.

$$\text{RAO}_i(\omega, \mu) = \frac{X_i(\omega, \mu)}{\zeta_a(\omega)}$$

**Significance:**
1. Acts as a transfer function between wave spectrum and motion spectrum.
2. Allows computation of ship motions in **irregular seas** from regular-wave data.
3. Provides direct insight into resonant frequencies and motion amplification.
4. Essential for predicting operability, crew comfort, and cargo safety.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition with equation, 2 marks for significance points)*

---

#### **Question 2. What is encounter frequency? Why does it differ from the absolute wave frequency?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Encounter frequency $\omega_e$ is the frequency at which a ship moving with velocity $V$ actually "sees" or encounters the waves:

$$\omega_e = \omega \left( 1 - \frac{\omega V \cos\mu}{g} \right)$$

**Reason for difference:**
- A stationary ship observes waves at their natural frequency $\omega$.
- A moving ship effectively samples the wave field at a different rate due to its own velocity component along the wave propagation direction.
- In **head seas** ($\mu = 180°$), encounter frequency is **higher** than wave frequency — ship meets waves faster.
- In **following seas** ($\mu = 0°$), encounter frequency is **lower** — ship runs with the waves.

*(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 2 marks explanation with head/following cases)*

---

### **Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

## 📌 **Question A (14 Marks)**

### **(a)** Derive the frequency-domain equation of motion for a ship in heave in regular waves. Explain the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Starting point — Newton's 2nd law in the vertical direction:**

$$M\ddot{z} = F_{\text{exc}}(t) - F_{\text{rad}}(t) - F_{\text{restoring}}(t)$$

**Step 1 — Identify excitation force:**
The wave exerts pressure variation on the hull. The integrated force has both Froude–Krylov (from undisturbed pressure) and diffraction (from scattering) components:

$$F_{\text{exc}}(t) = \Re\{\hat{F}_e e^{i\omega t}\}$$

**Step 2 — Radiation force** (from ship-generated waves): depends on ship velocity $\dot{z}$ and acceleration $\ddot{z}$:

$$F_{\text{rad}} = A(\omega)\ddot{z} + B(\omega)\dot{z}$$

- $A(\omega)$ = **added mass** — inertia of water that accelerates with the ship.
- $B(\omega)$ = **radiation damping** — energy radiated as outgoing ship-generated waves.

**Step 3 — Hydrostatic restoring force** (gravity-buoyancy balance for small heave $z$):

$$F_{\text{rest}} = \rho g A_{\text{WP}} z = C_{33} z$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$[M + A(\omega)] \ddot{z} + B(\omega) \dot{z} + C_{33} z = \Re\{\hat{F}_e e^{i\omega t}\}$$

**Step 5 — Harmonic steady-state** $z(t) = \Re\{\hat{z} e^{i\omega t}\}$:

$$[C_{33} - \omega^2(M + A(\omega)) + i\omega B(\omega)] \hat{z} = \hat{F}_e$$

$$\therefore \quad \hat{z} = \frac{\hat{F}_e}{C_{33} - \omega^2(M + A(\omega)) + i\omega B(\omega)}$$

*(7 marks — 1 for setup, 2 for each term explanation, 2 for algebraic manipulation, 2 for final boxed expression)*

---

### **(b)** A container ship of mass $1.5 \times 10^7$ kg has heave added mass coefficient $A_{33}(\omega) = 0.8 M$ and damping coefficient $B_{33}(\omega) = 2.5 \times 10^6$ kg/s at wave frequency $\omega = 0.6$ rad/s. The waterplane area is $A_{\text{WP}} = 8000$ m². The wave amplitude is 1.2 m and excitation force amplitude is $1.8 \times 10^7$ N.

**Calculate:**
(i) The heave RAO magnitude.
(ii) The phase lag between heave motion and wave.
(iii) The significant heave amplitude in irregular sea (assume narrow-band).
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) RAO magnitude:**

Total inertia: $M + A_{33} = 1.5 \times 10^7 (1 + 0.8) = 2.7 \times 10^7$ kg

Restoring: $C_{33} = \rho g A_{\text{WP}} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8000 = 8.045 \times 10^7$ N/m

Stiffness term: $C_{33} - \omega^2(M + A_{33}) = 8.045 \times 10^7 - (0.6)^2 \times 2.7 \times 10^7$

$$= 8.045 \times 10^7 - 0.36 \times 2.7 \times 10^7 = 8.045 \times 10^7 - 9.72 \times 10^6$$

$$= 7.073 \times 10^7 \, \text{N/m}$$

Damping term: $\omega B_{33} = 0.6 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 = 1.5 \times 10^6$ N·s/m

Denominator magnitude:

$$\vert D \vert = \sqrt{(7.073 \times 10^7)^2 + (1.5 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{5.002 \times 10^{15} + 2.25 \times 10^{12}} \approx 7.074 \times 10^7 \, \text{N/m}$$

RAO magnitude:

$$\vert \text{RAO} \vert = \frac{\hat{F}_e}{\vert D \vert \cdot \zeta_a} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^7}{7.074 \times 10^7 \times 1.2} = 0.212 \, \text{m/m}$$

**(ii) Phase lag:**

$$\phi = -\arctan\left( \frac{\omega B_{33}}{C_{33} - \omega^2(M+A_{33})} \right) = -\arctan\left( \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{7.073 \times 10^7} \right)$$

$$\phi \approx -\arctan(0.0212) \approx -1.21°$$

**(iii) Significant amplitude in irregular sea:**

Heave variance:

$$\sigma_z^2 = \int_0^\infty \vert \text{RAO}(\omega) \vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega_e) d\omega_e$$

For a single-frequency approximation (per problem instruction):

$$\sigma_z \approx \vert \text{RAO} \vert \cdot \sigma_\zeta$$

Assuming $\sigma_\zeta = H_s / 4$ and using wave amplitude $H = 2\zeta_a \Rightarrow \sigma_\zeta \approx 0.6$ m:

$$\sigma_z = 0.212 \times 0.6 = 0.127 \, \text{m}$$

$$z_{1/3} = 4\sigma_z = 4 \times 0.127 = 0.509 \, \text{m}$$

*(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for each of (i)/(ii)/(iii))*

---

## 📌 **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

### **(a)** Explain the salient features of a Pierson–Moskowitz sea spectrum. How is significant wave height $H_s$ related to the spectral density? **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Salient Features of PM Spectrum:**

1. **Fully developed sea assumption:** Assumes the sea has been blown by a steady wind for a sufficiently long time over a long fetch such that energy input from wind equals dissipation.
2. **Single parameter:** Defined entirely by the wind speed $U_{19.5}$ at 19.5 m reference height.
3. **Mathematical form:**

$$S_\zeta(\omega) = \frac{0.0081 \, g^2}{\omega^5} \exp\left[ -0.74 \left( \frac{g}{U_{19.5} \omega} \right)^4 \right]$$

4. **Has a peak:** Maximum at frequency $\omega_p = 0.84 \, g / U_{19.5}$.
5. **Tail behaviour:** $\sim \omega^{-5}$ decay at high frequency; $\sim \omega^{-5}$ rise at low frequency.
6. **Used for fully developed, open-ocean conditions.**

**Relation of $H_s$ to spectral density:**

The variance of surface elevation is:

$$\sigma_\zeta^2 = \int_0^\infty S_\zeta(\omega) \, d\omega$$

For a narrow-band process (Rayleigh distributed):

$$\boxed{H_s \approx 4\sigma_\zeta = 4\sqrt{\int_0^\infty S_\zeta(\omega) \, d\omega}}$$

This matches the empirical observation that significant wave height is approximately the average height of the highest one-third of waves.

*(7 marks — 3 for PM features, 2 for formula derivation, 2 for connection to $H_s$)*

---

### **(b)** A ship has heave RAO magnitude $\vert \text{RAO}_z \vert = 0.4$ m/m at encounter frequency $\omega_e = 0.8$ rad/s. The encountered wave spectrum is given by:

$$S_\zeta(\omega_e) = \frac{10}{\omega_e^5} \exp\left( -\frac{5}{\omega_e^4} \right) \, \text{m}^2\text{s/rad}$$

**Compute:**
(i) The significant heave amplitude $z_{1/3}$.
(ii) Comment on whether the ship is in resonance with the wave conditions.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Significant heave amplitude:**

Motion spectrum:
$$S_z(\omega_e) = \vert \text{RAO}_z \vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega_e) = (0.4)^2 \cdot \frac{10}{\omega_e^5} \exp\left( -\frac{5}{\omega_e^4} \right)$$

$$= \frac{1.6}{\omega_e^5} \exp\left( -\frac{5}{\omega_e^4} \right)$$

Variance:
$$\sigma_z^2 = \int_0^\infty S_z(\omega_e) \, d\omega_e$$

Let $u = 5/\omega_e^4 \Rightarrow \omega_e^4 = 5/u \Rightarrow \omega_e = (5/u)^{1/4}$

$du = -20/\omega_e^5 \, d\omega_e \Rightarrow d\omega_e/\omega_e^5 = -du/20$

$$\sigma_z^2 = 1.6 \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{\omega_e^5} e^{-5/\omega_e^4} d\omega_e = 1.6 \cdot \frac{1}{20} = 0.08 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$\sigma_z = \sqrt{0.08} = 0.283 \, \text{m}$$

$$z_{1/3} = 4\sigma_z = 4 \times 0.283 = 1.13 \, \text{m}$$

**(ii) Resonance check:**

Natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{C_{33}/(M+A_{33})}$ is **not** given directly. However, the spectral peak occurs at:

$$\frac{dS_\zeta}{d\omega_e} = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_p = (0.84 \, g / U_{19.5})$$

For the spectral form $\sim \omega^{-5} \exp(-5/\omega^4)$, peak is at $\omega_p^4 = 20/5 = 4 \Rightarrow \omega_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ rad/s.

Since $\omega_e = 0.8 < \omega_p$, the ship is **below peak frequency** — not at resonance, but in the energy-rising region. RAO of 0.4 is moderate; if natural heave frequency lies near $\omega_p$, amplification would be severe.

*(7 marks — 3 for (i) integration, 2 for numerical answer, 2 for resonance comment)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $\omega_e$ formula:** Many students forget that $\mu = 180°$ gives $+\omega V/g$ term. Always state your heading convention explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing added mass $A(\omega)$ with rigid-body mass $M$:** They are added — never subtract.
> 3. **Mixing encounter frequency $\omega_e$ with absolute $\omega$** in spectrum integrals. RAOs are usually tabulated vs $\omega_e$.
> 4. **Using $\rho = 1000$ instead of $1025$ kg/m³:** KTU explicitly expects seawater density.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in RAO (m/m for translations, rad/m for rotations).
> 6. **Significant value is $4\sigma$, NOT $3\sigma$ or $\sqrt{2}\sigma$** — this is a classic KTU trap.

---

### 🧠 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ A ship in a seaway has **6 DOF**: surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw.
- ✅ Equation of motion (frequency-domain): $[M+A(\omega)]\ddot{x} + B(\omega)\dot{x} + Cx = F_{\text{exc}}(\omega)$.
- ✅ **RAO** is the transfer function: $\text{RAO} = \vert X \vert / \zeta_a$.
- ✅ **Encounter frequency**: $\omega_e = \omega(1 - \omega V \cos\mu / g)$.
- ✅ Head sea ($\mu=180°$): $\omega_e > \omega$; Following sea ($\mu=0°$): $\omega_e < \omega$.
- ✅ Heave restoring: $C_{33} = \rho g A_{\text{WP}}$; Roll restoring: $C_{44} = \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}_T$.
- ✅ Motion spectrum in irregular sea: $S_X(\omega_e) = \vert \text{RAO} \vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega_e)$.
- ✅ **Significant amplitude**: $X_{1/3} = 4\sigma_X$ (Rayleigh/narrow-band).
- ✅ **Pierson–Moskowitz** spectrum for fully developed sea: $S_\zeta = \dfrac{0.0081 g^2}{\omega^5}\exp[-0.74(g/U_{19.5}\omega)^4]$.
- ✅ Coupling: heave–pitch in head seas; roll–yaw in beam seas.
- ✅ Damping $B(\omega)$ at resonance controls peak RAO — higher $B$ → lower peak.
- ✅ Added mass $A(\omega)$ and damping $B(\omega)$ come from solving the **radiation problem** using strip theory or 3-D panel methods.
- ✅ **Added wave resistance** is computed from energy balance (Haskind's relation) or direct pressure integration.
- ✅ **Sea state scales:** Douglas Sea State, Beaufort Scale, $H_s$, $T_z$ (zero-crossing period) are all equivalent ways to describe irregular sea.
- ✅ Always remember: **seawater $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³**, $g = 9.81$ m/s² for numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pitch and Roll in Irregular Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pitch and Roll in Irregular Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Pitch and Roll in Irregular Waves — Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In ship hydrodynamics, **pitch** and **roll** are the two principal rigid-body rotational motions of a floating vessel responding to an ocean wave field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitch (Ψ):** Angular oscillation of the ship about the **transverse (y) axis**, causing the bow and stern to alternately rise and fall. Conventionally measured in **radians (rad)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roll (Φ):** Angular oscillation of the ship about the **longitudinal (x) axis**, causing the port and starboard sides to alternately lift and depress. Conventionally measured in **radians (rad)**.

An **irregular (random) sea** is a real ocean surface composed of a continuum of sinusoidal wave components with random phases, statistically described by a **wave energy spectrum** $S_\zeta(\omega)$ (units: **m²·s**). Unlike regular waves (single frequency and amplitude), irregular seas demand a **spectral (frequency-domain)** treatment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dish of Rice" Intuition

Imagine a wooden toy boat floating in a basin. If you tap the basin once, you create a single, clean wave — that is a **regular wave**, and the boat rocks predictably. Now imagine a stormy ocean: thousands of wave trains of all sizes and directions crash together into a chaotic, ever-changing surface. The boat now experiences a **statistical storm of motions**, never repeating exactly. 

- **Roll** is like a child rocking side-to-side on a rocking chair — the chair's restoring force and friction (damping) decide how violently it sways for a given push.
- **Pitch** is like a seesaw being pushed by uneven gusts — the bow dives into a trough while the stern climbs a crest, and vice versa.

The "push" comes from the wave. Because the wave is random, we cannot predict one motion at one instant. Instead, we use **averages** — the statistical signature of the motion — to answer engineering questions such as *"How often will the roll exceed 25°?"*

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Wave elevation spectrum | $S_\zeta(\omega)$ | **m²·s** |
| Pitch RAO | $\vert RAO_\psi(\omega) \vert$ | **rad/m** |
| Roll RAO | $\vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert$ | **rad/m** |
| Motion response spectrum | $S_R(\omega)$ | **rad²·s** |
| Motion variance | $\sigma_R^2$ | **rad²** |
| Significant motion amplitude | $R_{1/3}$ | **rad** |
| Mean zero-upcrossing period | $T_z$ | **s** |
| Standard gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> All rotational motions in KTU problems are in **radians**. Convert from degrees using $1° = \pi/180 \approx 0.01745$ rad when needed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Spectral Analysis?

A ship in an irregular sea behaves as a **linear time-invariant dynamic system** excited by a random process. The complete pipeline is:

1. **Decompose** the irregular sea into a Fourier sum of infinitesimal regular wave components.
2. **Solve** the equation of motion for each frequency $\omega$ in regular waves, obtaining the **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)**.
3. **Superpose** the responses statistically (since random phases prevent simple addition).
4. **Integrate** the resulting response spectrum to obtain motion statistics.

The cardinal assumption is **linearity** — valid for moderate sea states where motions are small enough that quadratic damping, viscous effects, and non-linear restoring terms are negligible.

## 2.2 Equation of Motion in the Frequency Domain

### Roll equation (uncoupled, single DOF):

$$(I_{xx} + A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + C_{44}\phi = F_\phi(t)$$

where
- $I_{xx}$ — mass moment of inertia about the longitudinal axis (kg·m²)
- $A_{44}$ — roll added moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $B_{44}$ — roll damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $C_{44} = \rho g \overline{GM} \cdot V$ — roll restoring stiffness (N·m/rad)
- $F_\phi(t)$ — wave-exciting moment about x-axis (N·m)

### Pitch equation (uncoupled, single DOF):

$$(I_{yy} + A_{55})\ddot{\psi} + B_{55}\dot{\psi} + C_{55}\psi = F_\psi(t)$$

Pitch restoring $C_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM_L} \cdot V$ uses the **longitudinal metacentric height** $\overline{GM_L}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The frequency-domain transfer function (RAO) for a single-DOF oscillator excited by a harmonic moment of amplitude $F_0 e^{i\omega t}$ is
>
> $$RAO(\omega) = \frac{1}{(C - \omega^2(I+A)) + i\omega B}$$
>
> Its magnitude squared is the **mechanical admittance**.

## 2.3 Response Spectrum — The Central Result

For a linear system, the **input-output** spectral relationship is:

$$S_R(\omega) = \vert RAO(\omega) \vert^2 \cdot S_\zeta(\omega)$$

This is the most important equation in KTU module-2 problems. It directly couples the sea spectrum to the motion spectrum.

## 2.4 Motion Statistics (Spectral Moments)

Define the **n-th spectral moment** of the response:

$$m_n = \int_0^\infty \omega^n \, S_R(\omega) \, d\omega$$

Then:
- **Variance of motion:** $\sigma_R^2 = m_0 = \int_0^\infty S_R(\omega) \, d\omega$
- **Mean zero-upcrossing period:** $T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{m_0 / m_2}$
- **Significant amplitude:** $R_{1/3} = 4\sigma_R$ (Rayleigh-distributed response)
- **Average amplitude:** $\overline{R} = \sqrt{\pi/2} \cdot \sigma_R \approx 1.25\,\sigma_R$
- **Probability of exceeding amplitude $a$:** $P(R > a) = \exp(-a^2 / (2\sigma_R^2))$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_R(\omega) = \vert RAO \vert^2 \, S_\zeta(\omega)$ | Response spectrum from wave spectrum |
| 2 | $\sigma_R^2 = \int_0^\infty S_R(\omega) \, d\omega$ | Motion variance |
| 3 | $R_{1/3} = 4\sigma_R$ | Significant amplitude |
| 4 | $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$ | Mean zero-upcrossing period |
| 5 | $m_n = \int_0^\infty \omega^n S_R(\omega) d\omega$ | Spectral moments |
| 6 | $P(R>a) = \exp(-a^2/(2\sigma_R^2))$ | Exceedance probability |
| 7 | $C_{44} = \rho g \overline{GM} V$ | Roll restoring stiffness |
| 8 | $C_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM_L} V$ | Pitch restoring stiffness |
| 9 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{C/(I+A)}$ | Natural frequency of roll or pitch |
| 10 | $\zeta = B / (2\sqrt{C(I+A)})$ | Damping ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (or `\vert` in LaTeX) for the **absolute value / magnitude** of the RAO — never the bare pipe character `|`, which breaks markdown tables.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

Predicting pitch and roll in irregular waves underpins:
- **Passenger comfort** (motion sickness incidence relates to RMS roll)
- **Cargo securing** (significant pitch amplitude guides lashing design)
- **Helicopter-deck operability** (pitch limits for safe landing)
- **Weapons-system performance** on naval vessels (stabilization requirements)
- **Structural fatigue** (number of roll cycles above a threshold per voyage)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Regular-Wave RAO to Irregular-Wave Response Spectrum

**Starting point:** Consider the ship in a regular wave of amplitude $\zeta_a$ and frequency $\omega$. The steady-state roll response is

$$\phi(t) = \zeta_a \cdot \vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert \cos(\omega t - \varepsilon)$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the phase lag.

**Step 1 — Express as complex phasor.**

$$\tilde{\phi}(\omega) = \zeta_a \, RAO_\phi(\omega) \, e^{i\omega t}$$

**Step 2 — Superpose N sinusoidal components** (Fourier representation of irregular sea):

$$\zeta(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \zeta_{a,j} \cos(\omega_j t + \theta_j)$$

where $\theta_j$ are random phases uniformly distributed on $[0, 2\pi]$.

**Step 3 — Apply linearity** (superposition holds because the system is linear):

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \zeta_{a,j} \, \vert RAO_\phi(\omega_j) \vert \cos(\omega_j t + \theta_j - \varepsilon_j)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the autocorrelation** of $\phi(t)$:

$$R_\phi(\tau) = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \phi(t)\phi(t+\tau)\,dt$$

**Step 5 — Use orthogonality of sinusoids with random phases.** Cross-terms vanish in the limit, leaving only same-frequency contributions:

$$R_\phi(\tau) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{\zeta_{a,j}^2}{2} \vert RAO_\phi(\omega_j) \vert^2 \cos(\omega_j \tau)$$

**Step 6 — Take the Fourier transform** to obtain the response spectrum. Recognize that $\zeta_{a,j}^2 / 2 = S_\zeta(\omega_j) \Delta\omega$ in the continuum limit:

$$S_\phi(\omega) = \vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert^2 \, S_\zeta(\omega)$$

**Conclusion:** The variance of roll in irregular seas is

$$\sigma_\phi^2 = \int_0^\infty \vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega) \, d\omega$$

An identical derivation holds for pitch with subscript $\psi$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Significant Roll Amplitude from Spectral Area

The significant amplitude $R_{1/3}$ is the mean of the highest one-third of all peak amplitudes. For a **narrow-band** Gaussian process the peaks follow a **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$f(a) = \frac{a}{\sigma_R^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{a^2}{2\sigma_R^2}\right), \quad a \geq 0$$

The mean of the upper one-third is obtained from the truncated expectation:

$$R_{1/3} = \frac{\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty a\, f(a)\, da}{\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty f(a)\, da}$$

with the threshold $a_{1/3}$ satisfying $\int_{a_{1/3}}^\infty f(a)\, da = 1/3$.

Solving the Rayleigh CDF:

$$P(A > a_{1/3}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{a_{1/3}^2}{2\sigma_R^2}\right) = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{a_{1/3}^2}{2\sigma_R^2} = \ln 3 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a_{1/3} = \sigma_R\sqrt{2\ln 3}$$

Substituting back, the integral evaluates to:

$$R_{1/3} = 4\sigma_R$$

Thus the **significant roll or pitch amplitude is exactly four standard deviations** — the single most tested numerical fact in KTU module-2 valuation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Roll Variance Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A ship has a roll RAO given by $\vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert = 0.20 \, \omega / \omega_n$ for $\omega \leq \omega_n$, and $=0.20\,\omega_n/\omega$ for $\omega > \omega_n$, with $\omega_n = 0.6$ rad/s. The sea is described by a Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum $S_\zeta(\omega) = (A/\omega^5)\exp(-B/\omega^4)$ with $A = 0.0081\, g^2$, $B = 0.74\,(g/U_{19.5})^4$, and $U_{19.5} = 15$ m/s. Compute the roll variance numerically.

**Step 1 — Compute spectral constants.**

$$B = 0.74 \cdot (9.81 / 15)^4 = 0.74 \cdot 0.1871 = 0.1384$$

**Step 2 — Build the integrand** $S_\phi(\omega) = \vert RAO_\phi \vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega)$.

For $\omega \leq 0.6$: $\vert RAO \vert^2 = 0.04 \,\omega^2/0.36 = 0.1111\,\omega^2$.

For $\omega > 0.6$: $\vert RAO \vert^2 = 0.04 \cdot 0.36/\omega^2 = 0.0144/\omega^2$.

**Step 3 — Numerical integration** (typical trapezoidal values, illustrative):

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | $\vert RAO \vert^2$ | $S_\zeta(\omega)$ | $S_\phi(\omega)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.2 | 0.00444 | 1.74 | 0.00774 |
| 0.4 | 0.01778 | 0.082 | 0.00146 |
| 0.6 | 0.04000 | 0.021 | 0.00084 |
| 0.8 | 0.02250 | 0.0091 | 0.00021 |
| 1.0 | 0.01440 | 0.0047 | 0.00007 |

**Step 4 — Apply trapezoidal rule** over the dominant range $[0.2, 1.0]$:

$$\sigma_\phi^2 \approx \int_{0.2}^{1.0} S_\phi(\omega) d\omega \approx 0.00118 \;\text{rad}^2$$

**Step 5 — Convert to degrees:**

$$\sigma_\phi = \sqrt{0.00118} = 0.0344\,\text{rad} = 1.97°$$

**Step 6 — Significant amplitude:**

$$R_{1/3} = 4 \times 1.97° = 7.88° \quad \text{(significant single roll amplitude)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The standard procedure for any KTU numerical: (i) tabulate RAO² and spectrum, (ii) multiply pointwise, (iii) integrate numerically, (iv) take square root, (v) multiply by 4 for significant value.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Mean Zero-Upcrossing Period

Zero upcrossings occur when $\phi(t) = 0$ with $\dot{\phi}(t) > 0$. The expected number per unit time is:

$$N_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}$$

Hence

$$T_z = \frac{1}{N_0} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}$$

where $m_n = \int_0^\infty \omega^n S_R(\omega) d\omega$.

This is the **average period between successive upcrossings** of the zero line, equivalent in irregular waves to the regular-wave period in deterministic analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spectral Motion Prediction

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end engineering pipeline** from a real sea to statistical ship-motion output. It mirrors the procedure used in ship-design software (e.g., SESAM, WAMIT-coupled codes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real Ocean Surface"] --> B["Wave Measurement Buoy"]
    B --> C["Estimated Wave Spectrum Szeta omega"]
    C --> D["Select Spectral Form PM or JONSWAP"]
    D --> E["Compute Spectral Moments m0 m1 m2 m4"]
    F["Ship Hull Geometry and Mass Distribution"] --> G["Strip Theory or 3D Panel Method"]
    G --> H["Hydrodynamic Coefficients Added Mass A44 A55 Damping B44 B55 Restoring C44 C55"]
    H --> I["Frequency Domain Equation of Motion"]
    I --> J["Roll RAO and Pitch RAO Magnitudes"]
    C --> K["Spectral Multiplier"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Response Spectrum Sphi omega and Spsi omega"]
    L --> M["Numerical Integration for Spectral Moments"]
    M --> N["Variance sigma squared and Standard Deviation sigma"]
    N --> O["Significant Amplitude R one third equal to 4 sigma"]
    M --> P["Mean Zero Upcrossing Period Tz"]
    N --> Q["Exceedance Probability P of R greater than a"]
    O --> R["Engineering Outputs Comfort Cargo Securing Fatigue Limits"]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Process Schematic — RAO Generation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hydrodynamic Solver
        S1["Discretize Hull into Strips or Panels"] --> S2["Solve Radiation Diffraction at Each omega"]
        S2 --> S3["Compute Exciting Force F0 omega"]
        S2 --> S4["Compute Added Mass and Damping"]
    end
    S3 --> S5["RAO = F0 divided by C minus omega squared I plus A plus i omega B"]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["Magnitude RAO and Phase Epsilon at Each omega"]
    S6 --> S7["Tabulated RAO Curve for Roll and Pitch"]
```

## 4.3 Logic of Variance Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Input: Szeta omega in m squared s"] --> Y["Multiply by RAO squared at each omega"]
    Y --> Z["Obtain Sphi omega in rad squared s"]
    Z --> AA["Integrate from 0 to infinity"]
    AA --> AB["sigma phi squared"]
    AB --> AC["sigma phi = root"]
    AC --> AD["R one third = 4 sigma phi"]
    AC --> AE["Probability of exceedance via Rayleigh"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`S1`, `X`, etc.) and labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text with allowed operators. No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names.
