# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** for roll. Why is its magnitude squared, rather than the magnitude itself, used when computing the roll response spectrum in an irregular sea?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Roll RAO** $RAO_\phi(\omega)$ is defined as the complex ratio of the steady-state roll amplitude to the incident wave amplitude for a regular wave of frequency $\omega$:

$$RAO_\phi(\omega) = \frac{\phi_a(\omega)}{\zeta_a(\omega)}$$

Units: **rad/m**. It encapsulates the hydrodynamic transfer function of the ship at each frequency.

The **magnitude squared** $\vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert^2$ appears because the **response spectrum** is obtained by multiplying the input wave spectrum by the system's mechanical admittance (power transfer function), not the transfer function itself. Squaring the magnitude eliminates the phase angle and yields a real, positive-valued spectral density suitable for statistical integration. The phase information is irrelevant to variance-type statistics, which depend only on energy.

**(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — units and physical meaning; 1 mark — justification of squaring)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State and explain the relationship between the **significant roll amplitude** and the **standard deviation** of roll motion in irregular waves.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a **narrow-band Gaussian random process** (such as ship roll in irregular seas), the peak amplitudes follow a **Rayleigh distribution**. The significant amplitude is defined as the mean of the highest one-third of peak amplitudes:

$$R_{1/3} = 4\sigma_\phi$$

where $\sigma_\phi$ is the standard deviation of the roll angle. It is computed from the response spectrum:

$$\sigma_\phi^2 = \int_0^\infty \vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert^2 \, S_\zeta(\omega) \, d\omega$$

**Physical meaning:** In a 3-hour sea state, the ship will roll through roughly 100 cycles; the highest 33 of these peaks will, on average, attain an amplitude of $4\sigma_\phi$. This is directly analogous to the significant wave height $H_{1/3} = 4\sigma_\zeta$ used in ocean engineering.

**(1 mark — statement; 1 mark — derivation link; 1 mark — physical interpretation)**

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive, starting from the single-DOF equation of motion for ship roll in regular waves, the expression for the **roll response spectrum** $S_\phi(\omega)$ in terms of the wave spectrum $S_\zeta(\omega)$ and the roll RAO. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A frigate of displacement $\Delta = 3500$ tonnes operates in a sea state with significant wave height $H_s = 4$ m and mean zero-upcrossing period $T_z = 8$ s. The roll RAO is approximated as $\vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert = 0.18$ rad/m for $0.4 \leq \omega \leq 1.2$ rad/s and zero outside this band. Compute the **RMS roll angle**, the **significant single roll amplitude** (in degrees), and the **probability that a roll exceeds 10°**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Equation of motion in regular waves.** For a regular wave $\zeta(t) = \zeta_a \cos\omega t$, the roll equation is

$$(I_{xx}+A_{44})\ddot\phi + B_{44}\dot\phi + C_{44}\phi = F_\phi(\omega)\cos\omega t$$

**Step 2 — Harmonic steady-state solution.** Assume $\phi(t) = \phi_a\cos(\omega t-\varepsilon)$:

$$\phi_a = \frac{F_\phi(\omega)}{\sqrt{[C_{44}-\omega^2(I_{xx}+A_{44})]^2 + (\omega B_{44})^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Define RAO.**

$$RAO_\phi(\omega) = \frac{F_\phi(\omega)/\zeta_a}{[C_{44}-\omega^2(I_{xx}+A_{44})] + i\omega B_{44}}$$

so that $\phi_a = \zeta_a \cdot \vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert$.

**Step 4 — Irregular sea representation.**

$$\zeta(t) = \int_0^\infty \cos(\omega t+\theta(\omega))\sqrt{2\,S_\zeta(\omega)\,d\omega}$$

where $\theta(\omega)$ are random phases.

**Step 5 — Linearity superposition** (assumptions: linear system, stationary Gaussian sea, uncorrelated phases).

$$\phi(t) = \int_0^\infty \cos(\omega t+\theta(\omega)-\varepsilon(\omega))\cdot \vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert\sqrt{2\,S_\zeta(\omega)\,d\omega}$$

**Step 6 — Compute autocorrelation and Fourier-transform** to obtain the response spectrum:

$$\boxed{S_\phi(\omega) = \vert RAO_\phi(\omega) \vert^2 \, S_\zeta(\omega)}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Linear time-invariant system. (ii) Weakly nonlinear roll damping is linearized. (iii) Coupling between roll and other modes neglected. (iv) Wave components are statistically independent.

**(7 marks: 2 for eqn of motion + harmonic solution, 2 for RAO definition, 2 for superposition derivation, 1 for assumptions.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Estimate the wave spectrum from $H_s$ and $T_z$.** For a Pierson–Moskowitz-like broadband spectrum,

$$S_\zeta(\omega) \approx \frac{H_s^2}{16\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_\omega}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\omega-\omega_p)^2}{2\sigma_\omega^2}\right)$$

with peak frequency $\omega_p = 2\pi/T_z = 0.785$ rad/s, half-width $\sigma_\omega \approx 0.35$ rad/s, and $H_s = 4$ m giving spectral level near the peak:

$$S_\zeta(\omega_p) \approx \frac{16}{16\sqrt{2\pi}\cdot 0.35} \approx 3.22 \;\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}$$

For engineering-level estimation within the RAO band, take the **mean spectral level** as $S_\zeta \approx 2.0$ m²·s (averaged over $[0.4, 1.2]$ rad/s).

**Step 2 — Compute RMS roll.**

$$\sigma_\phi^2 = \int_{0.4}^{1.2} (0.18)^2 \cdot 2.0 \, d\omega = 0.0324 \times 2.0 \times 0.8 = 0.05184 \;\text{rad}^2$$

$$\sigma_\phi = \sqrt{0.05184} = 0.2277 \;\text{rad} = 13.04°$$

**Step 3 — Significant roll amplitude.**

$$R_{1/3} = 4 \times 13.04° = 52.2°$$

**Step 4 — Exceedance probability for 10°:**

$$P(\phi > 10°) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(10\pi/180)^2}{2\times 0.05184}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.03062}{0.10368}\right) = \exp(-0.2954) = 0.744$$

So roughly **74.4 %** of roll peaks exceed 10°. (This very high probability reflects the wide-band assumption — for narrower spectra the result would be smaller.)

**Note:** When $R_{1/3}$ computed from the spectrum exceeds physically reasonable limits (here > 50°), the linear assumption is being violated. KTU valuation accepts the algebra but flags this for commentary.

**(7 marks: 2 for spectrum estimation, 2 for variance integration, 1 for significant amplitude, 2 for exceedance probability.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** For ship pitch in irregular waves, derive the expression for the **mean zero-upcrossing period** $T_z$ of pitch motion in terms of the spectral moments $m_0$ and $m_2$ of the pitch response spectrum. Explain its physical significance and why it differs from the natural period of pitch in still water. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A containership with pitch natural period $T_n = 7$ s and pitch damping ratio $\zeta = 0.20$ sails in head seas. The wave spectrum at the encounter frequency band may be approximated as constant at $S_\zeta(\omega_e) = 1.5$ m²·s over $0.6 \leq \omega_e \leq 1.1$ rad/s. Compute the **significant pitch amplitude** in degrees, assuming the pitch RAO magnitude in this band is $\vert RAO_\psi \vert = 0.15$ rad/m and zero elsewhere. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition.** A zero upcrossing occurs each time the pitch angle $\psi(t)$ crosses zero with positive slope. Let $N_0$ be the expected number of zero upcrossings per unit time.

**Step 2 — Rice's formula.** For a stationary Gaussian process:

$$N_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}$$

where the spectral moments are

$$m_n = \int_0^\infty \omega^n S_\psi(\omega) \, d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Mean period.**

$$\boxed{T_z = \frac{1}{N_0} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical significance.** $T_z$ is the **statistical average period** of pitch oscillation in the irregular sea. It is the irregular-sea analogue of the regular-wave period.

**Step 5 — Why $T_z \neq T_n$.** The natural period $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{(I_{yy}+A_{55})/C_{55}}$ is a property of the ship alone (in still water). In an irregular sea the ship responds preferentially to frequencies near the resonance, but the **broadband spectrum** excites a range of frequencies, so the centroid of the energy in the response spectrum shifts. In head seas the encounter-frequency distribution further skews the response spectrum, making $T_z$ generally shorter than $T_n$ for slow ships and longer for fast ships.

**(7 marks: 2 for Rice's formula, 2 for $T_z$ expression, 1 for physical meaning, 2 for comparison with $T_n$.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute pitch variance.**

$$\sigma_\psi^2 = \int_{0.6}^{1.1} (0.15)^2 \cdot 1.5 \, d\omega = 0.0225 \times 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.01688 \;\text{rad}^2$$

$$\sigma_\psi = \sqrt{0.01688} = 0.1299 \;\text{rad}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to degrees.**

$$\sigma_\psi = 0.1299 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 7.44°$$

**Step 3 — Significant pitch amplitude.**

$$R_{1/3} = 4 \times 7.44° = 29.8° \approx 30°$$

**Sanity check:** For a containership with $T_n = 7$ s in moderate head seas, a significant pitch amplitude of order 5°–10° is typical. A result of 30° indicates either (i) the RAO value 0.15 rad/m is conservative, or (ii) the assumption of broad-band constant spectrum overestimates energy. The student should comment on this in the solution.

**(7 marks: 2 for variance integral, 2 for standard deviation in degrees, 2 for significant amplitude, 1 for commentary.)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** radian answers to degrees when the question asks for degrees (or vice versa). The factor **180/π = 57.296** must appear explicitly.
> 2. **Using the bare pipe symbol `|` in markdown tables** instead of `\vert` or `\mid`. In KTU answer sheets this isn't a problem, but in any submission with tables the table syntax breaks. Always use parentheses or the word "modulus".
> 3. **Mis-applying the factor 4.** The relation $R_{1/3} = 4\sigma$ holds for **Rayleigh-distributed peaks** (narrow-band Gaussian). If the spectrum is wide-band (significant low-frequency or high-frequency tail), the factor shifts slightly toward **3.8** or **4.2** — but for KTU, **always use 4** unless specifically directed otherwise.
> 4. **Neglecting assumptions.** State linearity, stationarity, narrow-band, and uncoupled-DOF explicitly in derivations. Examiners reward the assumption list.
> 5. **Mixing encounter frequency with absolute frequency.** In head or oblique seas, pitch is governed by the encounter frequency $\omega_e = \omega - \omega^2 V\cos\beta / g$. Use $\omega_e$ in the RAO and spectrum for pitch, but the **physical pitch period** in the ship frame still uses absolute $\omega$.
> 6. **Not integrating over frequency.** A common 2-mark loss is stating $\sigma^2 = \vert RAO \vert^2 \cdot S_\zeta$ at a single frequency. Always integrate over the spectrum.
> 7. **Forgetting units in the answer.** Always write rad² for variance, rad for amplitude, s for period.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pitch** = rotation about transverse y-axis; **Roll** = rotation about longitudinal x-axis. Both in **radians**.
- **Irregular sea** ⇒ use spectral (frequency-domain) analysis with wave spectrum $S_\zeta(\omega)$.
- **Core equation:** $S_R(\omega) = \vert RAO(\omega) \vert^2 \, S_\zeta(\omega)$.
- **Variance:** $\sigma_R^2 = \int_0^\infty S_R(\omega) \, d\omega$.
- **Significant amplitude:** $R_{1/3} = 4\sigma_R$ (Rayleigh, narrow-band).
- **Mean zero-upcrossing period:** $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0 / m_2}$.
- **Exceedance probability:** $P(R>a) = \exp[-a^2/(2\sigma_R^2)]$.
- **Roll restoring stiffness:** $C_{44} = \rho g \overline{GM} V$.
- **Pitch restoring stiffness:** $C_{55} = \rho g \overline{GM_L} V$.
- **Assumptions checklist:** Linear, stationary, Gaussian, uncoupled DOF, narrow-band.
- **Numerical workflow:** Tabulate $\omega$, RAO², $S_\zeta$ → multiply → integrate → take sqrt → multiply by 4 → convert to degrees if needed.
- **Head-sea encounter:** Use encounter frequency $\omega_e$ for pitch RAOs and spectra.
- **Convert rad → deg:** multiply by **180/π**.
- **Linear limits:** If $R_{1/3}$ exceeds ~ 25°, linear theory underestimates true motions and viscous / non-linear effects dominate.
- **KTU priority:** Memorize the **factor 4** and the **integration formula** — these two alone solve ~ 60% of module-2 problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — RESPONSE AMPLITUDE OPERATOR (RAO)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Response Amplitude Operator (RAO)** is a dimensionless, frequency-dependent transfer function that describes the steady-state amplitude of a floating vessel's motion (in any of its six degrees of freedom) per unit amplitude of an incident regular wave. It is the fundamental output of linear ship-motion theory and is mathematically expressed as the complex ratio between the response amplitude and the wave amplitude, evaluated as a function of wave frequency $\omega$ (or equivalently wave period $T$, or wavelength $\lambda$).

Formally, for any degree of freedom $j \in \{1,2,\ldots,6\}$:

$$\boxed{\;H_j(\omega) \;=\; \frac{X_j(\omega)}{\zeta_a} \;=\; \vert H_j(\omega) \vert \, e^{\, i \varepsilon_j(\omega)}\;}$$

where:

- $X_j(\omega)$ = complex amplitude of the $j^{\text{th}}$ motion response.
- $\zeta_a$ = incident regular wave amplitude (in **meters**).
- $\vert H_j(\omega) \vert$ = **modulus** of the RAO (dimensionless, often reported in **m/m** or **deg/m**).
- $\varepsilon_j(\omega)$ = **phase** of the RAO (in **radians**), representing the lead/lag of response relative to wave crest.

The RAO is reported for each of the six rigid-body modes:
1. **Surge** (translation along $x$-axis)
2. **Sway** (translation along $y$-axis)
3. **Heave** (translation along $z$-axis)
4. **Roll** (rotation about $x$-axis)
5. **Pitch** (rotation about $y$-axis)
6. **Yaw** (rotation about $z$-axis)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a child on a swing at different speeds. If you push very slowly, the swing barely moves with each cycle — small response. If you push exactly at the swing's natural rhythm, the swing goes wild — large response. If you push very fast, the swing has no time to react — small response again.

A ship in waves behaves identically. The **RAO is the "swing's responsiveness curve"** plotted against wave frequency. At the ship's **natural (resonant) frequency**, the RAO peaks dramatically (this is dangerous!). At very low or very high wave frequencies, the RAO is small (ship essentially ignores the waves).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for students:** The RAO is essentially the *frequency-response fingerprint* of a ship. Two ships of identical size can have radically different RAOs because mass distribution, damping, and hull shape (which determines hydrodynamic added mass and damping) all differ.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: $\mathbf{g = 9.81 \ m/s^2}$
- Sea water density: $\mathbf{\rho = 1025 \ kg/m^3}$
- Angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi/T$ in **rad/s**
- Wave number: $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ in **rad/m**, related by **dispersion relation** $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$
- RAO units: **m/m** for translations, **rad/m** (commonly re-expressed as **deg/m**) for rotations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Linear Ship Motion Theory

The RAO is derived under the following assumptions (KTU syllabus: **strip theory / 3-D potential theory**):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underlying Assumptions:**
> 1. Fluid is **inviscid, incompressible**, and **irrotational** (potential flow).
> 2. Motion amplitudes are **small** (linearisation justified).
> 3. Ship responds **linearly** to wave forcing (superposition holds).
> 4. **Steady-state** harmonic response is sought (transients neglected).
> 5. Ship moves with **constant forward speed** $U$ in head waves (optional, complicates RAO via Doppler shift).

Under these, the velocity potential is decomposed as:

$$\Phi(x,y,z,t) \;=\; \underbrace{\Phi_I}_{\text{incident}} + \underbrace{\Phi_D}_{\text{diffracted}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{6} \xi_j \, \Phi_{Rj}}_{\text{radiated (6 modes)}}$$

Each component satisfies the **Laplace equation** $\nabla^2 \Phi = 0$, appropriate **free-surface**, **hull-body**, **seabed**, and **radiation** boundary conditions.

### 2.2 Equation of Motion in the Frequency Domain

For the $j^{\text{th}}$ mode, the linearised equation of motion (per unit wave amplitude) becomes:

$$\left[\,-\omega^2\bigl(M_{jj} + A_{jj}(\omega)\bigr) \;+\; i\omega B_{jj}(\omega) \;+\; C_{jj}\,\right] \xi_j(\omega) \;=\; F_j^{\text{ext}}(\omega)$$

The three competing physical mechanisms are:

| # | Term | Physical Meaning | Behaviour vs $\omega$ |
|---|------|------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | $\omega^2 (M+A)$ | Inertia (mass + hydrodynamic added mass) | Dominates at **high** $\omega$ |
| 2 | $i\omega B(\omega)$ | Radiation damping (energy radiated by ship) | Peaks near **resonance** |
| 3 | $C_{jj}$ | Hydrostatic restoring stiffness | Dominates at **low** $\omega$ |
| 4 | $F_j^{\text{ext}}(\omega)$ | Wave excitation force (Froude–Krylov + diffraction) | Drives the system |

The implicit coupling between modes (e.g., heave-pitch) is captured through off-diagonal coefficients $M_{ij}, A_{ij}, B_{ij}, C_{ij}$, but is **often neglected** in introductory treatments yielding 6 independent SDOF oscillators.

### 2.3 Why the RAO Exists — Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world applications of RAO:**
> - **Seakeeping analysis:** Predicting whether a vessel will slam, propeller-emerge, or capsize in a given sea state.
> - **Design optimisation:** Hull-form modifications to reduce roll or pitch amplitudes.
> - **Comfort & operability:** Crew/passenger seasickness thresholds are tied to vertical acceleration $\ddot{z} = -\omega^2 \zeta_a \vert H_{33} \vert$.
> - **Spectral fatigue analysis:** RAO multiplied by wave spectrum yields response spectrum (covered in Module 3).
> - **Mission planning:** Determining operability envelopes for naval auxiliaries, FPSOs, offshore platforms.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorise for KTU 2024 exams**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $H_j(\omega) = X_j(\omega)/\zeta_a$ | Definition of RAO |
| 2 | $X_j(\omega) = \dfrac{F_j^{\text{ext}}(\omega)}{-\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj}) + i\omega B_{jj} + C_{jj}}$ | Solution of forced linear oscillator |
| 3 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{C_{jj}/(M_{jj}+A_{jj}(\omega_n))}$ | Natural frequency (implicit) |
| 4 | $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ | Natural period (roll: typically 8–25 s) |
| 5 | $\zeta_{\text{crit}} = B_{jj}/(2\sqrt{C_{jj}(M_{jj}+A_{jj})})$ | Damping ratio |
| 6 | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ | Dispersion relation (deep: $\omega^2 = gk$) |
| 7 | $\lambda = gT^2/(2\pi)$ | Deep-water wavelength |
| 8 | $\ddot{z}_{\text{rms}} = \sigma_{\ddot{z}} = \omega^2 \zeta_a \vert H_{33}(\omega) \vert$ | Vertical acceleration (slamming predictor) |
| 9 | $\sigma_x^2 = \int_0^\infty \vert H_j(\omega)\vert^2 S_\zeta(\omega) \, d\omega$ | Spectral response variance |
| 10 | $\Phi = \Phi_I + \Phi_D + \sum_j \xi_j \Phi_{Rj}$ | Decomposition of velocity potential |

### 2.5 The Three Physical Regimes of the RAO

1. **Low-frequency regime ($\omega \to 0$):** Restoring stiffness dominates → quasi-static response. RAO tends to a finite limit (e.g., roll RAO → 1 in beam waves).
2. **Resonant regime ($\omega \approx \omega_n$):** Damping is the only thing preventing infinite response. RAO peak height $\propto 1/B_{jj}$ → explains why roll RAOs of lightly-damped ships are very sharp.
3. **High-frequency regime ($\omega \to \infty$):** Inertia dominates. Response $\propto 1/\omega^2$ → RAO $\to 0$. The ship "out-runs" the wave.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Newton's Second Law to the RAO

### Step 1: Starting Point — Time-Domain Equation of Motion

Consider a ship undergoing small oscillations in regular waves. Applying Newton's second law to the $j^{\text{th}}$ mode of motion (e.g., heave), the sum of forces equals mass times acceleration:

$$\sum F_j(t) \;=\; M_{jj}\,\ddot{x}_j(t)$$

The total force is decomposed into three physically distinct contributions:

- **Hydrostatic restoring force** — proportional to displacement (Hookean, like a spring).
- **Radiation damping force** — proportional to velocity (energy radiated as waves).
- **Inertial reaction force** — proportional to acceleration (added-mass effect, water moving with hull).

Adding the **external wave excitation force** on the right-hand side:

$$M_{jj}\,\ddot{x}_j(t) \;+\; \int_{-\infty}^{t} K_{jj}(t-\tau)\,\dot{x}_j(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; C_{jj}\,x_j(t) \;=\; F_j^{\text{ext}}(t)$$

Here, $K_{jj}(t-\tau)$ is the **impulse-response function** (memory kernel) representing the fact that the radiation force at time $t$ depends on the entire history of motion.

### Step 2: Fourier Transform to the Frequency Domain

Taking the Fourier transform (using the convention $\hat{f}(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,e^{-i\omega t}\,dt$), and exploiting the **convolution theorem**:

$$\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\int K_{jj}(t-\tau)\dot{x}_j(\tau)\,d\tau\right\} \;=\; \bigl[i\omega\,\hat{K}_{jj}(\omega)\bigr]\,\hat{x}_j(\omega)$$

The Fourier transform of the kernel is related to hydrodynamic coefficients as:

$$\hat{K}_{jj}(\omega) \;=\; B_{jj}(\omega) \;-\; i\,\omega\,A_{jj}(\omega)$$

The full equation in frequency domain becomes:

$$\bigl[-\omega^2 M_{jj} + i\omega B_{jj}(\omega) - i\omega(-i\omega A_{jj}(\omega)) + C_{jj}\bigr]\hat{x}_j(\omega) \;=\; \hat{F}_j^{\text{ext}}(\omega)$$

Wait — let me be more careful. Substituting $K = B - i\omega A$:

$$i\omega\bigl(B_{jj}(\omega) - i\omega A_{jj}(\omega)\bigr) \;=\; i\omega B_{jj}(\omega) + \omega^2 A_{jj}(\omega)$$

Combining with the $-i\omega M_{jj}\cdot (-i\omega) = -\omega^2 M_{jj}$ term from inertia, and the $-C_{jj}$ from stiffness (sign convention: $+C_{jj}x$ on LHS, so transform gives $C_{jj}\hat{x}$):

$$[-\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj}(\omega)) + i\omega B_{jj}(\omega) + C_{jj}]\,\hat{x}_j(\omega) \;=\; \hat{F}_j^{\text{ext}}(\omega)$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Complex Response Amplitude

Since the external excitation is harmonic, $\hat{F}_j^{\text{ext}}(\omega) = F_j(\omega)\,e^{i\varepsilon_F}$ (a complex quantity encoding magnitude and phase), the response is likewise harmonic:

$$\hat{x}_j(\omega) \;=\; X_j(\omega)\,e^{i\varepsilon_x}$$

Direct algebraic inversion gives the **complex amplitude**:

$$X_j(\omega)\,e^{i\varepsilon_x} \;=\; \frac{F_j(\omega)\,e^{i\varepsilon_F}}{-\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj}(\omega)) \;+\; i\omega B_{jj}(\omega) \;+\; C_{jj}}$$

### Step 4: Define the RAO as a Transfer Function

The **Response Amplitude Operator** is, by definition, the response amplitude per unit wave amplitude. Since $F_j(\omega) = \zeta_a \cdot f_j(\omega)$ where $f_j(\omega)$ is the excitation force per unit wave amplitude (the "force RAO"):

$$H_j(\omega) \;=\; \frac{X_j(\omega)}{\zeta_a} \;=\; \frac{f_j(\omega)}{-\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj}(\omega)) \;+\; i\omega B_{jj}(\omega) \;+\; C_{jj}}$$

Or, equivalently:

$$\boxed{\;H_j(\omega) \;=\; \frac{f_j(\omega)}{C_{jj} - \omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj}) \;+\; i\omega B_{jj}}\;}$$

### Step 5: Magnitude and Phase — Physical Interpretation

Writing the denominator in polar form $D = \vert D\vert e^{i\phi_D}$:

$$\vert D\vert \;=\; \sqrt{\bigl[C_{jj}-\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj})\bigr]^2 \;+\; \bigl[\omega B_{jj}\bigr]^2}$$

$$\phi_D \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega B_{jj}}{C_{jj}-\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj})}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\vert H_j(\omega)\vert \;=\; \frac{\vert f_j(\omega)\vert}{\sqrt{[C_{jj}-\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj})]^2 + [\omega B_{jj}]^2}}$$

$$\varepsilon_j(\omega) \;=\; \varepsilon_F \;-\; \phi_D$$

### Step 6: Identify the Resonance Peak

The maximum of $\vert H_j(\omega)\vert$ occurs when the real part of the denominator vanishes (when $C_{jj} = \omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj})$, i.e., at the natural frequency $\omega_n$). At resonance:

$$\vert H_j(\omega_n)\vert_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\vert f_j(\omega_n)\vert}{\omega_n B_{jj}(\omega_n)}$$

**Physical reading:** the resonant peak height is *inversely proportional to damping*. A lightly-damped roll mode produces a tall, narrow peak — critical for ship safety!

### Step 7: Limiting Cases

**Low-frequency limit** ($\omega \to 0$, quasi-static):

$$\lim_{\omega\to 0} \vert H_j(\omega)\vert \;=\; \frac{\vert f_j(0)\vert}{C_{jj}}$$

For **roll**, $C_{44} = \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}$ where $\overline{GM}$ is the **metacentric height**, so the low-frequency roll RAO is governed by stability.

**High-frequency limit** ($\omega \to \infty$):

$$\lim_{\omega\to\infty} \vert H_j(\omega)\vert \;\sim\; \frac{\vert f_j(\omega)\vert}{\omega^2(M_{jj}+A_{jj}(\infty))} \;\to\; 0$$

For **heave** and **pitch**, the excitation force $f_j \propto \omega^2$ in high-frequency limit, leading to $H_j \to$ constant. For **roll**, $f_4 \propto \omega^2$ also, so $H_4 \to$ constant.

### Step 8: From RAO to Vertical Acceleration (Engineering-relevant)

Acceleration is the second time-derivative of displacement. For harmonic response $x_j(t) = X_j \cos(\omega t + \varepsilon)$:

$$\ddot{x}_j(t) \;=\; -\omega^2 X_j \cos(\omega t + \varepsilon)$$

The acceleration RAO is therefore:

$$\vert H_{\ddot{x}_j}(\omega)\vert \;=\; \omega^2 \vert H_{x_j}(\omega)\vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Slamming criterion:** Vertical acceleration at the bow exceeding $\sim 0.5g$ to $1.0g$ typically marks the onset of **slamming** — this is what RAOs ultimately predict.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RAO Computation — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Geometry Form Data] --> B[Mesh Generation Panel Method]
    B --> C[Boundary Value Problem Solve Laplace Equation]
    C --> D[Hydrodynamic Coefficients Database]
    D --> E[Added Mass A omega]
    D --> F[Damping B omega]
    D --> G[Excitation Force F omega]
    H[Mass Matrix M and Stiffness C] --> I[Equation Assembly]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J[Frequency Loop omega 0 to 3 rad per s]
    J --> K[Compute H omega equals F over denominator]
    K --> L[Store Magnitude and Phase]
    L --> M{More Frequencies?}
    M -->|Yes| J
    M -->|No| N[RAO Curves H magnitude vs omega]
    N --> O[Roll RAO]
    N --> P[Pitch RAO]
    N --> Q[Heave RAO]
    O --> R[Seakeeping Analysis Output]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 RAO Behaviour — Conceptual Frequency-Response Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_FREQ[Low Frequency Long Waves]
        LF1[Quasi Static Regime]
        LF2[RAO tends to Constant Limit]
    end
    subgraph RESONANCE[Resonance Zone]
        RZ1[Natural Frequency omega_n]
        RZ2[RAO Peaks Sharply]
        RZ3[Peak Height inverse of Damping]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_FREQ[High Frequency Short Waves]
        HF1[Inertia Dominates]
        HF2[RAO decays as 1 over omega squared]
    end
    LOW_FREQ --> RESONANCE
    RESONANCE --> HIGH_FREQ
```

## 4.3 Six-DOF Coupling Schematic (Surge-Heave-Pitch Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph COUPLED[Coupled 3 DOF System]
        M33[Mass Heave] --> SUM1[Inertia Sum]
        M35[Mass Pitch Coupling] --> SUM1
        M53[Mass Heave Coupling] --> SUM2[Inertia Sum Pitch]
        M55[Mass Pitch] --> SUM2
    end
    SUM1 --> SOLVE[Matrix Inversion Frequency Loop]
    SUM2 --> SOLVE
    A33[Added Mass Heave] --> SUM1
    A35[Added Mass Coupling] --> SUM1
    A53[Added Mass Coupling] --> SUM2
    A55[Added Mass Pitch] --> SUM2
    B33[Damping Heave] --> DAMPH[Radiation Damping Block]
    B35[Damping Coupling] --> DAMPH
    B53[Damping Coupling] --> DAMPP[Radiation Damping Block]
    B55[Damping Pitch] --> DAMPP
    C33[Stiffness Heave] --> STIFF[Hydrostatic Stiffness Block]
    C35[Stiffness Coupling] --> STIFF
    C53[Stiffness Coupling] --> STIFFP[Hydrostatic Stiffness Block]
    C55[Stiffness Pitch] --> STIFFP
    F3[Wave Force Heave] --> SOLVE
    F5[Wave Force Pitch] --> SOLVE
    SOLVE --> OUTH[Heave RAO]
    SOLVE --> OUTP[Pitch RAO]
```

## 4.4 RAO Interpretation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Identify Wave Frequency omega] --> STEP1[Read H magnitude at this omega]
    STEP1 --> STEP2[Multiply by Wave Amplitude zeta_a]
    STEP2 --> STEP3[Obtain Motion Amplitude X]
    STEP3 --> CHECK{Motion within Safe Limits?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| OK[Operability Confirmed]
    CHECK -->|No| WARN[Slamming or Capsizing Risk]
    WARN --> ACT[Modify Course Speed or Hull Form]
    ACT --> START
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Response Amplitude Operator (RAO). Explain why it is a complex quantity.**

**Model Answer:**
The Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) is a frequency-dependent transfer function that expresses the steady-state amplitude of a ship's motion (per unit amplitude of incident wave) as a function of wave frequency $\omega$. Mathematically,

$$H_j(\omega) = \frac{X_j(\omega)}{\zeta_a}$$

The RAO is complex because the response can lead or lag the wave excitation in phase. The real and imaginary parts (or equivalently the modulus $\vert H_j \vert$ and phase $\varepsilon_j$) encode both the *magnitude* of motion and the *timing* of the response relative to wave crests. The phase is essential because two motions of equal amplitude but opposite phase have very different dynamic implications.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**
**List the three physical mechanisms that govern a ship's roll RAO. State which one controls the peak magnitude.**

**Model Answer:**
The three mechanisms are:
1. **Inertia** (mass + hydrodynamic added mass $M + A(\omega)$) — dominates at high frequencies.
2. **Hydrostatic restoring stiffness** ($C = \rho g \nabla \overline{GM}$) — dominates at low frequencies.
3. **Radiation damping** ($B(\omega)$) — controls energy dissipation at all frequencies.

The **radiation damping** $B(\omega)$ controls the peak magnitude of the roll RAO. At the natural roll period, the peak height is inversely proportional to $B(\omega_n)$. A lightly-damped ship has a tall, narrow peak, making roll resonance potentially catastrophic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Most students **forget the imaginary unit $i\omega B$** in the denominator and treat RAO as purely real → **lose 3–4 marks**.
> - Students often **write $\omega^2 M$ instead of $\omega^2(M+A)$**, forgetting added mass → **lose 2 marks**.
> - **Do not confuse RAO with Response Spectrum** (RAO is per-unit-amplitude in regular waves; response spectrum is statistical, used with irregular sea spectra).
> - Always **state units** explicitly (m/m for translations, deg/m for rotations).
> - For coupled problems, **mention the off-diagonal coupling terms** $A_{35}, B_{35}$, etc., even if neglected numerically.

### Question A (14 Marks) **[Internal Choice 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) for heave motion of a ship in regular head waves, starting from Newton's second law. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vessel has the following heave-mode properties at $\omega = 0.6$ rad/s:
- Mass $M = 8.0 \times 10^6$ kg
- Added mass $A = 6.0 \times 10^6$ kg
- Damping $B = 2.5 \times 10^6$ N·s/m
- Hydrostatic stiffness $C = 3.0 \times 10^6$ N/m
- Excitation force amplitude per unit wave amplitude $f_3 = 1.2 \times 10^6$ N/m
- Wave amplitude $\zeta_a = 1.5$ m

Compute the **heave RAO magnitude** and the **heave motion amplitude**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Internal Choice 2 — Alternative]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a clear sketch of the heave RAO curve, explain the three distinct frequency regimes (low, resonant, high) and identify which physical term dominates each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship has a natural roll period $T_n = 14$ s and a roll-damping ratio $\zeta = 0.05$. If the wave excitation spectrum has a peak at $T_p = 13$ s with significant amplitude, comment qualitatively on the expected roll response and the safety implications. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:** (1) Linear, small-amplitude motion, (2) Inviscid, incompressible, irrotational flow, (3) Regular (monochromatic) wave excitation, (4) Steady-state response, (5) Heave uncoupled from other modes.

**Time-domain equation:**
$$M \ddot{z} + \int_{-\infty}^{t} K(t-\tau)\dot{z}(\tau)d\tau + C z = F^{\text{ext}}(t)$$

**Fourier transform with convolution theorem** ($\mathcal{F}\{K * \dot{z}\} = \hat{K}(\omega) \cdot i\omega \hat{z}(\omega)$):

$$[-i\omega M][i\omega\hat{z}] + i\omega \hat{K}(\omega)\hat{z} + C\hat{z} = \hat{F}^{\text{ext}}$$

Using $\hat{K}(\omega) = B(\omega) - i\omega A(\omega)$:

$$\bigl[-\omega^2(M+A) + i\omega B + C\bigr]\hat{z}(\omega) = \hat{F}^{\text{ext}}(\omega)$$

For a regular wave of amplitude $\zeta_a$, $\hat{F}^{\text{ext}}(\omega) = \zeta_a f_3(\omega)$:

$$\hat{z}(\omega) = \frac{\zeta_a f_3(\omega)}{C - \omega^2(M+A) + i\omega B}$$

$$\boxed{H_3(\omega) = \frac{\hat{z}(\omega)}{\zeta_a} = \frac{f_3(\omega)}{C - \omega^2(M+A) + i\omega B}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical evaluation (7 marks):**

Given: $\omega = 0.6$ rad/s, $M = 8.0\times10^6$ kg, $A = 6.0\times10^6$ kg, $B = 2.5\times10^6$ N·s/m, $C = 3.0\times10^6$ N/m, $f_3 = 1.2\times10^6$ N/m, $\zeta_a = 1.5$ m.

**Inertia term:**
$$\omega^2(M+A) = (0.6)^2 \times (8.0\times10^6 + 6.0\times10^6) = 0.36 \times 14.0\times10^6 = 5.04\times10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

**Damping term:**
$$i\omega B = i \times 0.6 \times 2.5\times10^6 = i\,1.5\times10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

**Stiffness term:** $C = 3.0\times10^6$ N/m (real)

**Denominator:**
$$D = C - \omega^2(M+A) + i\omega B = (3.0 - 5.04)\times10^6 + i\,1.5\times10^6 = -2.04\times10^6 + i\,1.5\times10^6$$

**Modulus of denominator:**
$$\vert D\vert = \sqrt{(-2.04)^2 + (1.5)^2} \times 10^6 = \sqrt{4.1616 + 2.25}\times10^6 = \sqrt{6.4116}\times10^6 \approx 2.532\times10^6$$

**RAO magnitude:**
$$\vert H_3(0.6)\vert = \frac{\vert f_3\vert}{\vert D\vert} = \frac{1.2\times10^6}{2.532\times10^6} \approx 0.474 \text{ m/m}$$

**Heave motion amplitude:**
$$Z = \zeta_a \times \vert H_3\vert = 1.5 \times 0.474 \approx 0.711 \text{ m}$$

**Phase angle:**
$$\varepsilon = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.5}{-2.04}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(-0.735) \approx 143.7^\circ \quad \text{(2nd quadrant, as expected)}$$

The negative real part of the denominator indicates we are **above** the natural frequency — the ship is in the high-frequency, inertia-dominated regime, with the response lagging the wave by ~144°.

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Heave RAO sketch and regimes (7 marks):**

*Sketch description:* Plot $\vert H_3(\omega)\vert$ on y-axis vs $\omega$ on x-axis. Curve starts at finite value $\vert f_3(0)/C_0\vert$ at $\omega=0$, rises gradually, peaks near natural frequency $\omega_n$, then decays as $1/\omega^2$ at high $\omega$.

| Regime | $\omega$ Range | Dominant Term | RAO Behaviour |
|--------|----------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Low / Quasi-static** | $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | $C$ (stiffness) | $\vert H_3\vert \to \vert f_3(0)\vert/C$ (constant) |
| **Resonant** | $\omega \approx \omega_n$ | $i\omega B$ (damping) | Sharp peak, height $\propto 1/B$ |
| **High / Inertia** | $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | $\omega^2(M+A)$ (inertia) | Decays as $1/\omega^2$ |

**Part (b) — Roll safety comment (7 marks):**

Natural roll period $T_n = 14$ s, wave peak period $T_p = 13$ s. Ratio $T_p/T_n = 13/14 \approx 0.93$.

Since $T_p < T_n$, the wave forcing is **near-resonant** (frequency ratio close to 1). Combined with the **low damping ratio** $\zeta = 0.05$ (typical of large ships), the roll RAO will exhibit a **sharp, tall resonance peak**.

**Estimated peak amplification factor** (from sharp-resonance formula):
$$\frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.05} = 10$$

This means roll amplitudes can be amplified by a factor of ~10 over the quasi-static response.

**Safety implications:**
- High probability of **excessive roll angles** (>30°), risking cargo damage, crew injury, and even **parametric roll**.
- Risk of **capsizing** if roll exceeds the angle of vanishing stability.
- **Recommendation:** alter course to alter encounter period, or reduce speed. Also, anti-roll tanks or fin stabilisers may be necessary.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — RAO**

- **Definition:** RAO = (ship response amplitude) / (incident wave amplitude), dimensionless function of $\omega$.
- **Three physical ingredients** in the denominator: **inertia** $[-\omega^2(M+A)]$, **damping** $[+i\omega B]$, **stiffness** $[+C]$.
- **Resonance peak:** RAO magnitude maximised when $\omega^2(M+A) \approx C$. Peak height $\propto 1/B$.
- **Low-$\omega$ limit:** dominated by hydrostatic stiffness → constant quasi-static RAO.
- **High-$\omega$ limit:** dominated by inertia → RAO $\propto 1/\omega^2 \to 0$.
- **Units:** m/m (translations), deg/m or rad/m (rotations).
- **Six DOFs:** surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw — each has its own RAO.
- **Coupling:** heave-pitch and roll-sway-yaw typically coupled through off-diagonal added-mass and damping terms.
- **Phase information:** essential — it tells you whether the ship moves *with* or *against* the wave.
- **Engineering use:** vertical acceleration RAO = $\omega^2 \cdot \vert H_3 \vert$ → predicts slamming.
- **Assumptions:** linear, potential flow, small-amplitude, monochromatic waves.
- **Common mistake:** Forgetting added mass $A(\omega)$ — it's frequency-dependent and can exceed $M$!
- **Exam mantra:** Always write the **complex denominator** explicitly; never drop the $i\omega B$ term.
- **Force RAO vs Motion RAO:** $F_j(\omega) = \zeta_a f_j(\omega)$; motion RAO = force RAO / dynamic stiffness.
- **Forward speed effect:** introduces encounter frequency $\omega_e = \omega - \omega^2 U \cos\beta / g$, shifting the RAO peak (Doppler effect).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ship Motion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Ship Motion Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ship Motion Control** is the branch of naval architecture and control engineering concerned with the design, analysis, and implementation of devices and feedback systems that actively or passively suppress, regulate, or modify the six rigid-body motions of a vessel — **surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, and yaw** — in order to improve seakeeping, habitability, course-keeping, and operational safety.

The four motion degrees of freedom most commonly addressed by control hardware are:

- **Roll** ($\phi$) — rotation about the longitudinal (x) axis.
- **Pitch** ($\theta$) — rotation about the transverse (y) axis.
- **Yaw** ($\psi$) — rotation about the vertical (z) axis.
- **Heave** ($z$) — vertical translation (less commonly controlled).

Mathematically, a controlled ship is described by the matrix equation:

$$[M]\{\ddot{q}\} + [B]\{\dot{q}\} + [C]\{q\} = \{F_{dist}\} + \{F_{ctrl}\}$$

where $\{q\} = \{x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi\}^{T}$, and the control vector $\{F_{ctrl}\}$ is generated by actuators (fins, rudders, tanks, gyros) governed by a feedback law.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis (CO Mapping)**
> - **CO2**: Apply rigid-body and hydrodynamic principles to formulate ship motion equations.
> - **CO3**: Analyze motion-control devices and design compensators for roll, pitch, and yaw stabilization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a moving train. When it sways side to side, you instinctively shift your weight opposite to the motion — your brain acts as a **controller**, your legs as **actuators**, and your inner ear as a **sensor (gyroscope)**. Ship Motion Control works on the *exact same principle* — only at a much larger scale, with rigid metal devices and computer algorithms.

A modern cruise ship uses underwater "wings" called **stabilizer fins** that twist automatically — like an aircraft's ailerons — to push against the rolling sea, keeping your coffee cup level. The two key engineering questions are:

1. *How large must the device be?* (actuator sizing)
2. *How fast and accurately must it react?* (control law design)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea water density | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Displacement | $\Delta$ | 10³ – 10⁵ | tonnes |
| Metacentric height | $GM$ | 0.3 – 1.5 | m |
| Natural roll period | $T_\phi$ | 8 – 25 | s |
| Service speed | $V$ | 10 – 30 | knots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 demands a working knowledge of (i) passive stabilizers, (ii) active fin/rudder systems, (iii) control-tank dynamics, and (iv) feedback controller design (PID / LQR). Memorize the roll equation coefficients in Section 2 — they appear in almost every numerical.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Control Devices

Ship motion-control hardware is split along **two independent axes**:

**(A) By Degree of Freedom**
- Roll stabilizers
- Pitch/flap stabilizers
- Yaw / heading autopilots
- Heave-motion compensators (e.g., drilling vessels)

**(B) By Energy Source**
- **Passive** — no external power; uses hydrodynamic or gravitational restoring forces (bilge keels, passive U-tanks).
- **Active** — uses external power driven by a feedback signal (active fins, rudder roll stabilization).
- **Hybrid / Controllable** — combines both (controllable passive tanks, Magnus-effect rotors).

### 2.2 Roll Stabilization Devices — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Identify the problem.**
Roll has the *lowest* damping among ship motions and is the *most uncomfortable* for passengers and crew. Natural roll damping ($B_{44,\text{natural}}$) covers only **2 – 5%** of critical damping, so an *augmented* damping source is required.

**Step 2: Choose the actuation principle.**
The most common options are:

- **Hydrodynamic lift** (fins, rudder).
- **Hydrodynamic drag** (bilge keels, skegs).
- **Moving mass / gyroscopic moment** (gyros, shifting weights).
- **Liquid sloshing** (anti-roll tanks).

**Step 3: Couple the device to the equation of motion.**
Each device contributes an **additional moment** $M_{\text{ctrl}}(t)$ and, often, an **additional damping term** $B_{44,\text{add}}$ to the roll equation.

**Step 4: Close the feedback loop.**
A sensor (rate gyro, IMU) measures the roll rate $\dot{\phi}$, a controller computes the demanded actuator deflection $\alpha_c$, and the actuator drives the fin/rudder.

### 2.3 Pitch & Yaw Control — Why It Matters

- **Pitch** is critical for SWATHS, hydrofoils, and fast ferries, where bow slamming and emergence events are dangerous. **Stern flaps** and **T-foils** generate lift that opposes the heaving/pitching motion.
- **Yaw** control is essentially *autopilot design*. The rudder produces a side force $Y_R$ that creates a yaw moment $N_R = Y_R \cdot x_R$, where $x_R$ is the longitudinal distance of the rudder from the ship's center of gravity.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities use the **SNU linear-velocity convention** (surge $= u$, sway $= v$, heave $= w$) and **Euler-angle** convention (roll $= \phi$, pitch $= \theta$, yaw $= \psi$).

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_\phi = 2\pi\,k_{xx}/\sqrt{g\,GM}$ | Natural roll period of intact ship | s |
| 2 | $(I_{xx}+A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + \Delta\,GM\,\phi = M_\text{wave} + M_\text{ctrl}$ | Linearized 1-DOF roll equation | SI |
| 3 | $B_{44,\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{\Delta\,GM\,(I_{xx}+A_{44})}$ | Critical roll damping coefficient | N·m·s |
| 4 | $B_{44,\text{add, fin}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f (dC_L/d\alpha)\, l_f^{\,2}$ | Added damping from one fin (per side) | N·m·s |
| 5 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\alpha_f$ | Lift force of a fin at small angle | N |
| 6 | $M_f = F_L\,l_f$ | Roll-control moment from one fin | N·m |
| 7 | $Y_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\delta$ | Rudder side force (small angle) | N |
| 8 | $N_R = Y_R\,x_R$ | Yaw-control moment from rudder | N·m |
| 9 | $G(s) = \dfrac{\phi(s)}{\alpha_f(s)} = \dfrac{K}{s(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)}$ | Open-loop transfer function of roll-plant to fin | rad/rad |
| 10 | $u(t) = K_P e(t) + K_I\!\int e(t)dt + K_D\,\dot e(t)$ | PID control law (yaw & roll autopilots) | varies |
| 11 | $T_\theta = 2\pi\,k_{yy}/\sqrt{g\,GM_L}$ | Natural pitch period (longitudinal GM) | s |
| 12 | $B_{44,\text{add,bilge}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_\text{bk}\,l_\text{bk}^{\,2}\,C_{D,b}$ | Added damping from a bilge keel | N·m·s |

> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in derivations (e.g., $\lvert x \rvert$).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Commercial shipping**: 30–40 % roll reduction directly translates to fuel savings, higher cargo safety, and reduced crew fatigue.
- **Naval vessels**: radar and weapon-line stabilization are *mission-critical*; a 1° roll error can mis-aim a missile by tens of meters.
- **Offshore & DP vessels**: dynamic positioning controllers use the same linearized 3-DOF (surge, sway, yaw) plant model.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linearized Roll Equation of Motion

We start from the **angular-momentum balance** about the longitudinal (x) axis through the ship's center of gravity.

**Step 1 — Rigid-body inertia.**
For a rigid body rotating about the x-axis:

$$M_x = I_{xx}\,\ddot{\phi}$$

where $I_{xx}$ is the mass moment of inertia of the ship about the roll axis.

**Step 2 — Added (hydrodynamic) inertia.**
When a ship rolls, it must accelerate a surrounding mass of water. By experiment and potential-flow theory, this is captured by an *added moment of inertia* $A_{44}$:

$$M_{x,\text{added}} = A_{44}\,\ddot{\phi}$$

**Step 3 — Hydrodynamic damping.**
Viscous and wave-making effects generate a moment proportional to roll rate $\dot{\phi}$:

$$M_{x,\text{damp}} = B_{44}\,\dot{\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Gravitational (stiffness) restoring moment.**
For a small angle $\phi$, the righting arm $GZ \approx GM\sin\phi \approx GM\,\phi$. The restoring moment is therefore:

$$M_{x,\text{rest}} = \Delta\,GM\,\phi$$

> Note: $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ is the weight displacement, and the sign convention makes this a *restoring* (negative-direction) moment.

**Step 5 — External moments.**
The two external moment sources are the wave excitation $M_\text{wave}(t)$ and the control moment $M_\text{ctrl}(t)$ from stabilizers.

**Step 6 — Newton's second law for rotation.**
Summing all moments about the roll axis:

$$(I_{xx}+A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + \Delta\,GM\,\phi = M_\text{wave}(t) + M_\text{ctrl}(t)$$

This is the **linearized 1-DOF roll equation**, the foundation of every roll-control design in KTU Module 2.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Added Damping from a Fin Stabilizer

A fin of span $l_f$ and chord $c_f$ (planform area $A_f = l_f c_f$) is mounted amidships at a vertical lever $l_f$ from the roll axis.

**Step 1 — Lift produced by the fin.**
For small incidence $\alpha_f$ (relative to the flow of speed $V$):

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\alpha_f$$

**Step 2 — Roll moment from one fin.**
The lift acts at the lever arm $l_f$:

$$M_f = F_L\,l_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\alpha_f\,l_f$$

**Step 3 — When controlled, $\alpha_f$ is approximately proportional to the roll rate $\dot{\phi}$**.
A typical active fin controller applies:

$$\alpha_f = K_c\,\dot{\phi}$$

(negative gain so the lift opposes the roll).

**Step 4 — Substitute into the moment expression:**

$$M_{f,\text{add}} = \left[\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,K_c\,l_f^{\,2}\right]\dot{\phi}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the added damping coefficient:**

$$B_{44,\text{add, fin}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,K_c\,l_f^{\,2}$$

This term is **added directly** to $B_{44}$ in the roll equation, increasing the roll damping ratio from 2–5 % to a target of **15–30 %** of critical.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Natural Roll Period $T_\phi$

From the unforced, undamped roll equation:

$$(I_{xx}+A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + \Delta\,GM\,\phi = 0$$

The natural angular frequency is:

$$\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{\Delta\,GM}{I_{xx}+A_{44}}$$

**Step 1 — Express inertia as a radius of gyration.**
By definition $I_{xx}+A_{44} = (I_{xx}+A_{44})/1 = \Delta\,k_{xx}^{\,2}/g$ when $k_{xx}$ is the roll radius of gyration (in metres).

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{\Delta\,GM}{\Delta\,k_{xx}^{\,2}/g} = \dfrac{g\,GM}{k_{xx}^{\,2}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to period:**

$$T_\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi\,\dfrac{k_{xx}}{\sqrt{g\,GM}}$$

This is the *Krishna-Paeo roll period formula*, heavily tested in KTU 2024 examinations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Rudder Force & Yaw Moment

A rudder of area $A_R$ deflected to angle $\delta$ (small) in a flow of speed $V$:

**Step 1 — Side force on the rudder:**

$$Y_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\delta$$

**Step 2 — Yaw moment about the CG (rudder is at longitudinal distance $x_R$ aft of CG):**

$$N_R = Y_R\,x_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,x_R\,\delta$$

**Step 3 — Combined linearized yaw equation:**

$$(I_{zz}+A_{66})\ddot{\psi} + N_{\dot\psi}\dot{\psi} + N_\psi\,\psi = Y_R\,x_R$$

This is the basis of every *autopilot* design — the rudder deflection $\delta$ is driven by a controller to force heading error $\psi \to 0$.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Roll Stabilization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wave Disturbance Mw] --> B[Rigid Ship Plant]
    C[Controller Gain Kc] --> D[Fin Actuator]
    B --> E[Sensor: Rate Gyro]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioner]
    F --> C
    D --> G[Fin Force FL]
    G --> B
    B --> H[Roll Angle phi and Rate dphi]
    H --> I[Safety Limiter plus Saturation]
    I --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Device Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Motion Control Devices] --> B[Passive]
    A --> C[Active]
    A --> D[Hybrid]
    B --> B1[Bilge Keels]
    B --> B2[Passive UTank]
    B --> B3[Parametric Roll Damper]
    C --> C1[Fin Stabilizer]
    C --> C2[Gyroscopic Stabilizer]
    C --> C3[Rudder Roll Stabilizer]
    C --> C4[Stern Flap]
    D --> D1[Controllable Passive Tank]
    D --> D2[Magnus Rotor]
    D --> D3[Moving Weight System]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Feedback Autopilot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Heading psid] --> B[Summing Junction]
    C[Actual Heading psi] --> B
    B --> D[Heading Error e]
    D --> E[PID Controller]
    E --> F[Demanded Rudder Angle deltad]
    F --> G[Rudder Servo]
    G --> H[Rudder Angle delta]
    H --> I[Ship Yaw Plant]
    I --> C
    I --> J[Disturbance Wind Waves Current]
    J --> I
```

### 4.4 Engineering Interpretation of the Block Diagrams

- The **outer feedback loop** (sensor $\rightarrow$ controller $\rightarrow$ actuator) is the *only* difference between a passive device and a control system. Passive devices do not use this loop; they rely on the inherent ship-plus-device hydrodynamics.
- **Saturation blocks** (in diagram 4.1) represent the *physical limits* of fin deflection ($\pm 25°$) and fin rate ($\pm 20°$/s). KTU 2024 questions frequently ask students to size the controller so the demand never exceeds saturation in regular seas (Sea State 4).
- The **PID block** in diagram 4.3 must be *tuned* using the yaw-plant natural frequency and damping; Ziegler–Nichols or LQR methods are both acceptable at M.Tech level, but for B.Tech a **Kvastad–Nomoto first-order** approximation is the standard.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *Define the term "ship motion control". List the four ship motions that are most commonly controlled and identify which one receives the most engineering attention. Justify your answer in one sentence.*

**[Model Answer — 3 marks]**
Ship motion control is the design and implementation of passive and active systems that suppress or regulate a vessel's rigid-body motions (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw) in waves. The four motions most commonly controlled are *roll, pitch, yaw,* and *heave*. Among these, **roll** receives the most engineering attention because it has the lowest natural damping (2–5 % of critical) and is the primary cause of passenger discomfort, cargo damage, and capsizing. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark listing, 1 mark justification.)*

**Q2.** *Distinguish between a passive stabilizer and an active stabilizer. Give one engineering example of each.*

**[Model Answer — 3 marks]**
A **passive stabilizer** requires *no external power*; it extracts energy from the ship's motion itself and dissipates it through hydrodynamic drag or wave radiation. **Example:** a bilge keel — a vertical plate fitted at the bilge that increases roll damping via vortex shedding. An **active stabilizer** *uses external power* and a feedback signal to drive an actuator. **Example:** an electro-hydraulic fin stabilizer that rotates fins in response to a rate-gyro signal. *(1.5 marks definition, 1.5 marks example.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Always state **units** with every numerical answer. Marks are *routinely* deducted when SI units are missing.
> - For a roll-equation question, write the equation in the form $(I_{xx}+A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + \Delta\,GM\,\phi = M_\text{ext}$ *before* substituting values. Students who jump directly to a number lose the "equation-formulation" marks (≈ 2 of 7).
> - Show the *direction sign* of the fin moment explicitly; sign errors are the single most common mistake in fin/torque problems.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A ship has the following roll-axis data: displacement $\Delta = 1.0 \times 10^7$ kg, metacentric height $GM = 1.2$ m, roll radius of gyration $k_{xx} = 4.5$ m, natural roll-damping coefficient $B_{44} = 2.5 \times 10^6$ N·m·s, and added roll inertia $A_{44} = 0.3 \Delta k_{xx}^{\,2}/g$. Calculate:
  1. The natural roll period $T_\phi$.
  2. The critical damping coefficient $B_{44,\text{crit}}$.
  3. The actual damping ratio $\zeta$ of the intact ship.

**(b)** Two identical stabilizer fins are to be fitted amidships, each with area $A_f = 6$ m², lift-curve slope $dC_L/d\alpha = 2\pi$ /rad, and lever arm $l_f = 4$ m. The ship sails at $V = 15$ m/s. Estimate the additional roll-damping coefficient $B_{44,\text{add,fin}}$ produced by the pair of fins, assuming the controller is tuned so that the effective fin incidence $\alpha_f = 0.5\,\dot{\phi}$ (rad, with $\dot{\phi}$ in rad/s). What is the new damping ratio with the fins engaged?

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a)** *(7 marks)*

**1. Natural roll period** (2 marks)

$$T_\phi = \dfrac{2\pi\,k_{xx}}{\sqrt{g\,GM}} = \dfrac{2\pi(4.5)}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.2}} = \dfrac{28.274}{3.431} = 8.24 \text{ s}$$

**2. Critical damping** (3 marks)

First compute the *effective* roll inertia:

$$I_\text{eff} = I_{xx}+A_{44} = \dfrac{\Delta\,k_{xx}^{\,2}}{g}\,(1+0.3) = \dfrac{(1.0\times 10^{7})(4.5)^{2}}{9.81}\times 1.3$$

$$I_\text{eff} = \dfrac{2.025\times 10^{8}}{9.81}\times 1.3 = 2.064\times 10^{7}\times 1.3 = 2.683\times 10^{7} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

Now:

$$B_{44,\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{\Delta\,GM\,I_\text{eff}}$$

$$= 2\sqrt{(1.0\times 10^{7})(9.81)(1.2)(2.683\times 10^{7})}$$

$$= 2\sqrt{3.158\times 10^{15}} = 2\,(5.620\times 10^{7}) = 1.124\times 10^{8} \text{ N·m·s}$$

**3. Damping ratio** (2 marks)

$$\zeta = \dfrac{B_{44}}{B_{44,\text{crit}}} = \dfrac{2.5\times 10^{6}}{1.124\times 10^{8}} = 0.0223 \approx 2.2\%$$

This is the typical 2–5 % range — confirming the textbook claim that *passive* damping alone is insufficient.

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)*

**1. Added damping from one fin** (3 marks)

$$B_{44,\text{add,1 fin}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,K_c\,l_f^{\,2}$$

with $K_c = 0.5$ (gain).

$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times (15)^2 \times 6 \times 2\pi \times 0.5 \times (4)^2$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 225 \times 6 \times 3.1416 \times 0.5 \times 16$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 225 = 1.153\times 10^{5}$$

$$\times 6 = 6.919\times 10^{5} \times 3.1416 = 2.174\times 10^{6} \times 0.5 = 1.087\times 10^{6} \times 16 = 1.739\times 10^{7}$$

So $B_{44,\text{add,1 fin}} \approx 1.74 \times 10^{7}$ N·m·s per fin.

**2. Two fins** (1 mark)

$$B_{44,\text{add,total}} = 2 \times 1.739\times 10^{7} = 3.478\times 10^{7} \text{ N·m·s}$$

**3. New damping ratio** (3 marks)

$$B_{44,\text{new}} = B_{44,\text{intact}} + B_{44,\text{add}} = 2.5\times 10^{6} + 3.478\times 10^{7} = 3.728\times 10^{7}$$

$$\zeta_\text{new} = \dfrac{3.728\times 10^{7}}{1.124\times 10^{8}} = 0.332 = 33.2\%$$

> This is a healthy damping ratio — well above the 20 % target used by most classification societies for a comfortable ship.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the aid of a clear block diagram, describe a **closed-loop fin-roll stabilization system**. Identify the role of the rate gyro, controller, actuator, and the ship itself, and explain how a sign error in the controller gain would manifest as a physical phenomenon (e.g., *roll resonance*).

**(b)** A tanker of displacement $1.5 \times 10^8$ kg, $GM = 1.8$ m, $k_{xx} = 6$ m, and intact damping $B_{44} = 1.0 \times 10^{7}$ N·m·s is equipped with a single rudder of area $A_R = 12$ m², $dC_L/d\alpha = 2\pi$ /rad, located $x_R = 50$ m aft of the centre of gravity. The ship moves at $V = 12$ m/s. For a maximum rudder deflection of $\delta = 25°$, calculate:
  1. The maximum side force $Y_{R,\text{max}}$.
  2. The maximum yaw-control moment $N_{R,\text{max}}$.
  3. Comment on whether this rudder is also suitable for *rudder roll stabilization* (RRS) at this speed.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a)** *(7 marks)*

A **closed-loop fin-roll stabilization system** has the following functional chain:

1. **Rate Gyro (sensor)** — measures instantaneous roll rate $\dot{\phi}(t)$ and outputs a voltage proportional to $\dot{\phi}$. Located near the ship's centre of gravity to minimise cross-coupling with pitch and yaw.
2. **Controller** — electronic unit implementing $u(t) = K_c\,\dot{\phi}(t)$ (or PID). The gain $K_c > 0$ but the *plant gain* is negative (fin lift creates a *negative* roll moment for a *positive* fin incidence), so the *loop gain* must be negative — a typical implementation inverts the sign in firmware.
3. **Actuator** — an electro-hydraulic servo that converts the controller output $u$ into a fin angle $\alpha_f \in [-25°, +25°]$ with a rate limit of ~20°/s.
4. **Ship (plant)** — converts the fin lift into a roll moment through the lever arm $l_f$.

A **sign error** in the controller (positive instead of negative loop gain) produces a *positive feedback* loop. The system then *adds energy* to the roll mode instead of removing it. The physical manifestation is a *growing roll oscillation* at the natural roll period $T_\phi$, culminating in **parametric resonance** if the wave encounter period approaches $T_\phi/2$ or $T_\phi$. The ship may roll over if the malfunction is not detected.

> Provide the block diagram already shown in Section 4.1 to score full marks.

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)*

**1. Maximum side force** (3 marks)

Convert the angle to radians: $\delta = 25° \times \pi/180 = 0.4363$ rad.

$$Y_{R,\text{max}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\delta$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times (12)^2 \times 12 \times 2\pi \times 0.4363$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5 \times 144 = 7.380\times 10^{4} \times 12 = 8.856\times 10^{5} \times 6.2832 = 5.564\times 10^{6} \times 0.4363 = 2.428\times 10^{6} \text{ N}$$

So $Y_{R,\text{max}} \approx 2.43$ MN.

**2. Maximum yaw-control moment** (2 marks)

$$N_{R,\text{max}} = Y_{R,\text{max}} \times x_R = 2.428\times 10^{6} \times 50 = 1.214\times 10^{8} \text{ N·m}$$

**3. Suitability for RRS** (2 marks)

For RRS, the *roll moment* produced by the rudder is $M_R = Y_R \times z_R$, where $z_R$ is the *vertical* lever of the rudder force (typically 2–4 m for a tanker). With $z_R = 3$ m:

$$M_{R,\text{max}} = 2.428\times 10^{6} \times 3 = 7.28 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·m}$$

This is roughly **1.4×** the intact damping of the ship, but a modern RRS scheme needs *4–6×* the intact damping to reduce roll by 50 %. **Conclusion:** at $V = 12$ m/s the rudder *can* contribute to roll reduction, but for the *primary* roll-stabilization duty a dedicated fin stabilizer (or a pair of them) is recommended.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Ship Motion Control**

- **Six rigid-body motions:** surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw. Roll is the most important to control.
- **Linearized 1-DOF roll equation** (memorize):
  $(I_{xx}+A_{44})\ddot{\phi} + B_{44}\dot{\phi} + \Delta\,GM\,\phi = M_\text{wave} + M_\text{ctrl}$
- **Natural roll period:** $T_\phi = 2\pi\,k_{xx}/\sqrt{g\,GM}$ — *independent of displacement*.
- **Critical damping:** $B_{44,\text{crit}} = 2\sqrt{\Delta\,GM\,(I_{xx}+A_{44})}$.
- **Intact damping ratio:** typically **2–5 %** of critical; target after stabilization is **15–30 %**.
- **Fin force:** $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\alpha_f$. Lift grows with *speed squared* — that's why fins are *useless* at low speed.
- **Fin roll moment:** $M_f = F_L\,l_f$. The lever arm $l_f$ is the designer's biggest free parameter.
- **Bilge-keel damping:** proportional to $l_\text{bk}^2$ and drag coefficient $C_{D,b}$ — make them long and rough.
- **Rudder force:** $Y_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,(dC_L/d\alpha)\,\delta$.
- **Rudder moment:** $N_R = Y_R\,x_R$ — the rudder is *long* after the CG, giving it a large moment arm.
- **Passive vs Active vs Hybrid:** passive = no power, active = external power, hybrid = both (e.g., controllable passive tank).
- **PID autopilot:** $u = K_P e + K_I\!\int e\,dt + K_D\,\dot e$ — the workhorse of every commercial heading-control system.
- **Block diagram must show:** disturbance → plant → sensor → controller → actuator → plant (with feedback summing junction).
- **Sign convention:** roll to starboard is *positive*; rudder to port produces *positive* yaw moment; **always check the sign of your control gain**.
- **Units mantra:** *kg, m, s, N, rad* — anything else and you risk losing marks.
- **KTU hot topics:** Krishna roll period, fin force derivation, RRS moment calculation, PID tuning, controller sign-error discussion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Roll and Pitch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control of Roll and Pitch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Roll and Pitch in Ship Dynamics

In the six-degrees-of-freedom (6-DOF) rigid body motion of a surface ship, **roll** and **pitch** are the two primary angular oscillations about the ship's longitudinal ($x$) and transverse ($y$) axes, respectively, taken through the centre of gravity ($G$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Roll ($\phi$)** — Rotation of the hull about the longitudinal (fore-aft) axis, $x$. A ship rolls from side to side under transverse wave action. It is the most violent and uncomfortable of all ship motions because the metacentric height ($GM$) that provides transverse stability also acts as the *restoring spring* in roll.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitch ($\theta$)** — Rotation of the hull about the transverse (port-starboard) axis, $y$. The ship pitches bow-up and bow-down, primarily due to head or following waves. Pitch becomes critical for high-speed vessels, aircraft carriers, and SWATH (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull) ships.

## 1.2 Why Control Roll and Pitch?

Uncontrolled roll and pitch produce four major engineering problems:

| Problem | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| Crew/ Passenger comfort | Seasickness, fatigue, loss of operational efficiency |
| Cargo damage | Shifting of containers, broken machinery, sloshing in tanks |
| Operational loss | Helicopters cannot land on rolling decks, weapons cannot fire |
| Structural fatigue | Cyclic loading on hull girder, whipping in slamming conditions |

Therefore, **roll and pitch control systems (stabilizers)** are mandatory on naval combatants, cruise liners, yachts, and most commercial vessels above **30 m LOA**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Spoon in a Bowl of Soup:**
> Imagine a soup spoon standing upright in a bowl. The spoon *rolls* left and right if you shake the bowl sideways (this is **roll**). The spoon *pitches* forward and backward if you push the bowl toward/away from you (this is **pitch**). 
>
> Now imagine you stick a small fan blade (a **fin**) to the side of the spoon, just below the waterline. When the spoon tries to roll, water rushing past the tilted fin generates a side-force that *pushes the spoon back upright*. This is exactly how a **fin stabilizer** works on a ship — a small wing submerged at the bilge that creates a hydrodynamic counter-moment every time the ship begins to roll.

## 1.4 Classification of Roll/Pitch Control Systems

Roll and pitch control systems are classified into two broad families:

1. **Passive Stabilizers** — No external energy input; rely on tuned hydrodynamics.
   - Bilge Keels
   - Free-Surface (Passive) Anti-Roll Tanks
   - U-Tube (Passive) Anti-Roll Tanks
   - Sloshing Tank Dampers (Tank Stabilizers)
2. **Active Stabilizers** — Require hydraulic/pneumatic/electrical power input.
   - Active Fin Stabilizers
   - Gyroscopic Stabilizers
   - Moving-Weight Stabilizers
   - Rudder-Based Roll Control (RORC)
   - Interceptor / Trim Tab Systems
3. **Hybrid / Controlled Passive** — Combination of both.
   - Controlled Passive Anti-Roll Tanks
   - Flap-Type Fins with feathering control

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically requires treatment of *bilge keels*, *anti-roll tanks*, *fin stabilizers*, and *gyroscopic stabilizers* with their governing hydrodynamic equations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Governing Equation of Roll Motion

A ship undergoing small-angle roll ($\phi$ in radians) in regular beam seas obeys the classical **Conolly linear roll equation**:

$$I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} + 2N_{\phi}\dot{\phi} + \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \phi = M_{ext}(t)$$

Where the three terms on the left side represent, respectively:
- **Inertia term** — moment to accelerate the rotating mass
- **Damping term** — energy dissipation (viscous + wave + bilge keel)
- **Restoring term** — hydrostatic righting moment

The right-hand side is the external excitation moment from waves plus any active control moment.

## 2.2 The Free-Roll Natural Frequency and Period

Setting the external moment to zero and assuming harmonic motion $\phi = \Phi_0 e^{i\omega_n t}$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta \cdot g \cdot GM}{I_{xx}}} = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot GM}{k_{xx}^2}}$$

The **natural roll period** is therefore:

$$T_{\phi} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \cdot k_{xx}\sqrt{\frac{1}{g \cdot GM}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** When the wave encounter period $T_E$ approaches $T_{\phi}$ (typically $T_{\phi} \approx 6\text{–}12$ s for cargo ships, $9\text{–}15$ s for warships), the ship resonates and roll amplitudes can exceed **30°–40°**. The objective of all stabilizers is to shift this resonance and increase damping.

## 2.3 Bilge Keels — Passive Vortex-Generators

**Bilge keels** are longitudinal plates projecting from the turn-of-the-bilge. They are the cheapest, lightest, and most universally used roll damping device.

- They shed **vortices** at their trailing edge during roll motion, dissipating kinetic energy.
- The damping moment is approximately:
$$M_{bk} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \cdot C_{Dbk} \cdot A_{bk} \cdot b_{bk}^2 \cdot \dot{\phi}\vert\dot{\phi}\vert$$
where $A_{bk}$, $b_{bk}$ are the area and span of the bilge keel, and $C_{Dbk}$ is an empirical drag coefficient ($\approx 1.5$ at typical Reynolds numbers).
- They are **purely dissipative** — they add no restoring moment and shift the natural frequency only marginally.
- Typical sizing: 0.05 B to 0.10 B as projected span, length 30–50 % of Lpp.

## 2.4 Anti-Roll Tanks (Passive)

### 2.4.1 Free-Surface (Flotation) Tank
A partial tank open to the sea at the bottom, located high in the ship. As the ship rolls, water *sloshes* from one side to the other with a phase lag, generating a moment that opposes the roll.

The **tank natural period** is tuned to match the ship's roll period:
$$T_{tank} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{l_{tank}}{2g}}$$

where $l_{tank}$ is the tank length (port-starboard).

### 2.4.2 U-Tube Tank
A closed tank (no free surface) divided by a central bulkhead with a bottom opening (water cross-section $A_c$). The water oscillates from one limb to the other, building up dynamic pressure.

Tuning condition:
$$\omega_{tank} = \sqrt{\frac{2gA_d}{l_e A_c}}$$

where $A_d$ is the surface area of each limb and $l_e$ is the effective length of the water column.

## 2.5 Active Fin Stabilizers

A fin is a small hydrofoil extending from the hull side, near amidships, at the turn of the bilge. When the ship rolls, the fin is *commanded* to a small angle $\alpha_f$ so that the relative water flow generates a **lift force**.

$$F_{lift} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \cdot V_r^2 \cdot A_f \cdot C_{L\alpha} \cdot \alpha_f$$

The roll-control moment about the longitudinal axis is:
$$M_{fin} = F_{lift} \cdot l_{arm}$$

where $l_{arm}$ is the vertical distance between the fin centre of pressure and the roll axis (often taken as $G$).

**Effect on roll equation:**
$$I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} + 2N_{\phi}\dot{\phi} + \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \phi = M_{wave}(t) - M_{fin}(t)$$

The fin is typically driven by a closed-loop controller that commands $\alpha_f$ as a function of measured roll rate $\dot{\phi}$:

$$\alpha_f = -K_p \phi - K_d \dot{\phi}$$

This is essentially a **PD (proportional-derivative) feedback** that adds active damping to the roll mode.

## 2.6 Gyroscopic Stabilizers

A heavy spinning flywheel is mounted in a gimbal frame inside the hull. When the ship rolls, gyroscopic precession produces a resisting torque:

$$\vec{M} = \vec{H} \times \vec{\omega}_{precession}$$

where $H = I_{fw}\Omega$ is the flywheel's angular momentum.

The precession rate is $\dot{\phi}$, so the gyroscopic reaction moment is:
$$M_{gyro} = I_{fw}\,\Omega\,\dot{\phi}$$

This adds to the roll damping term, reducing roll amplitude. Typical flywheel: mass $\sim$ 50 t, $\Omega \sim 1000$ rpm, in a vacuum-sealed housing. Used on large yachts, cruise ships, and some naval auxiliaries.

## 2.7 Pitch Control

Pitch control is fundamentally different because there is no hydrostatic restoring moment analogous to $GM$ in pitch (it depends on trim). The primary control devices are:

1. **Bow Flaps / Interceptors** — small transom-like plates at the bow, deflected downward to generate an upward hydrodynamic lift that counters bow-diving in waves.
2. **Stern Flaps** — placed at the transom; reduce pitch by modifying the pressure field on the afterbody.
3. **Active T-Foil** — a forward underwater foil generating lift to lift the bow.
4. **Motion Control Fins** — for submarines, large horizontal fins (sailplanes, fairwater planes) provide pitch control.

The pitch equation in heave-pitch coupling is:
$$I_{yy}\ddot{\theta} + B_{yy}\dot{\theta} + C_{yy}\theta = M_{wave,y}(t) - M_{control,y}(t)$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} + 2N_{\phi}\dot{\phi} + \Delta g GM\,\phi = M_{ext}$ | Conolly roll equation | N·m |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g\,GM}{k_{xx}^2}}$ | Roll natural angular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $T_{\phi} = 2\pi k_{xx}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g\,GM}}$ | Natural roll period | s |
| 4 | $M_{bk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{Dbk} A_{bk} b_{bk}^2 \dot{\phi}\vert\dot{\phi}\vert$ | Bilge keel damping moment | N·m |
| 5 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_r^2 A_f C_{L\alpha}\alpha_f$ | Fin lift force | N |
| 6 | $M_{fin} = F_L \cdot l_{arm}$ | Fin-induced roll moment | N·m |
| 7 | $\alpha_f = -K_p \phi - K_d \dot{\phi}$ | PD control law for fin | rad |
| 8 | $T_{tank} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{l_{tank}}{2g}}$ | Free-surface tank period | s |
| 9 | $\omega_{U} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2gA_d}{l_e A_c}}$ | U-tube tank natural frequency | rad/s |
| 10 | $M_{gyro} = I_{fw}\,\Omega\,\dot{\phi}$ | Gyroscopic reaction moment | N·m |
| 11 | $R_{xx} = \dfrac{\phi_{max,free}}{\phi_{max,controlled}}$ | Roll Reduction Ratio (RRR) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $RRR\,\% = (1-R_{xx}) \times 100$ | % Roll reduction achieved | percent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** A modern cruise ship typically achieves a **Roll Reduction Ratio (RRR) of 80–90 %** with active fin stabilizers, reducing roll from 20° unstabilized to 2–3° stabilized. Bilge keels alone provide RRR of 30–50 % at low speeds.

## 2.9 Selection of Stabilizer — Design Trade-off

| Vessel Type | Recommended Stabilizer | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Tanker / Bulker | Bilge keel + U-tube | Low cost, no moving parts |
| Frigate / Corvette | Active fins (retractable) | Speed 25+ kts, mission-critical |
| Cruise Liner | Fin + gyroscopic (hybrid) | Passenger comfort paramount |
| Yacht (> 40 m) | Gyroscopic / zero-speed fins | Quiet, no drag at anchor |
| Aircraft Carrier | Anti-roll tank + active fins | High GM, low freeboard |
| Container Ship | Bilge keel only | Cost, maintainability |
