<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Deck Wetness** is a seakeeping phenomenon in which the free surface of the sea (green water) reaches or overtops the bulwark/rail of a ship and floods the exposed weather deck. In the framework of KTU 2024 Scheme Ship Dynamics, it is treated as a **statistical exceedance problem** of the relative vertical motion between the ship and the incident wave above the mean freeboard of the vessel.

Mathematically, deck wetness is defined by the event:

$$E_{wet} : z_{r}(t) \geq F_{b}$$

where $z_{r}(t)$ is the relative motion (heave + pitch-induced vertical displacement at a deck location) and $F_{b}$ is the effective freeboard (height of the deck edge above the calm waterline).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Deck wetness is one of the four primary **operational/cargo-damage criteria** in seakeeping analysis (the others being slamming, shipping of green water, and propeller emergence). The KTU board frequently tests the probability formulation based on the **Rayleigh / Ochi approach**.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing on the open top deck of a moving ship during a storm. Every time the bow rises and plunges, the ocean surface can climb up the hull and slap the deck. The fraction of time (or the frequency) with which this happens is exactly what we call **deck wetness**.

Think of it like this:

- The ship is essentially **"dancing"** on the waves (heaving and pitching).
- The waves themselves are also rising and falling.
- The **relative motion** is the *combined up-and-down movement* of the ship with respect to the wave surface.
- Whenever this combined motion pushes water above the rail height, the deck gets wet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A taller ship (more freeboard) is *less* likely to suffer deck wetness. A faster ship in head seas is *more* likely. A ship sailing in beam seas experiences wetness amidships, while head seas cause bow wetness, and following seas cause **"pooping"** (stern wetness).

## Key Physical Metrics (KTU Standard)

- **Probability of Deck Wetness (P_wet):** Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1), representing the fraction of time the deck is wet.
- **Number of Wet Events per Hour (N_wet):** Expressed in **events/hour** or **cycles/hour**.
- **Mean Period of Wet Events (T_wet):** Measured in **seconds**.
- **Critical Relative Motion (z_c):** Equals the freeboard $F_{b}$, expressed in **meters**.
- **Spectral Moments (m₀, m₂):** $m_0$ has units of $m^2$ and $m_2$ has units of $m^2/s^2$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Underlying Physical Theory

The theoretical treatment of deck wetness rests on **three foundational assumptions** used universally in ship dynamics:

1. **Linearity Assumption:** Ship motions (heave, pitch) in regular waves are linear superpositions of six rigid-body modes. This allows spectral methods.
2. **Stationary Gaussian Process:** In irregular (real) seas, the wave elevation $\eta(t)$ and the ship response $\xi(t)$ are modeled as **zero-mean stationary Gaussian random processes**.
3. **Narrow-Band Spectrum Approximation:** The relative motion spectrum is treated as narrow-banded, allowing use of the **Rayleigh distribution** for crest amplitudes.

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

- **Step 1 — Compute the relative motion spectrum.** 
The relative motion between ship and wave surface is:
$$z_{r}(t) = \xi_{ship}(t) - \eta(t)$$
The spectral density of relative motion is:
$$S_{z_r}(\omega) = \vert H_{ship}(\omega) \vert^{2} S_{\eta}(\omega) + S_{\eta}(\omega)$$
where $H_{ship}(\omega)$ is the ship Response Amplitude Operator (RAO) for vertical displacement at the deck-edge location.

- **Step 2 — Compute the spectral moments.**
$$m_{n, z_r} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \omega^{n} S_{z_r}(\omega) \, d\omega$$

- **Step 3 — Apply Rayleigh distribution to crest heights.**
For a narrow-band Gaussian process, the probability that a crest exceeds a threshold $F$ is:
$$P(z_{r} \geq F) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)$$

- **Step 4 — Define the mean zero-crossing period of wet events.**
$$T_{wet} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{0, z_r}}{m_{2, z_r}}}$$

- **Step 5 — Compute expected number of wet events per hour.**
$$N_{wet} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{wet}} \cdot P_{wet} = \dfrac{3600}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2, z_r}}{m_{0, z_r}}} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)$$

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Deck wetness analysis is critical for:
- **Fishing vessels and offshore supply vessels (OSVs):** IMO Res. A.749(18) sets operational limits.
- **Ro-Ro passenger ferries:** Freeboard and bow door integrity depend on wetness.
- **Naval combatants:** Weapon system operability (missile launchers, radar).
- **Cargo ships:** Cargo damage and shifting stability on deck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters in Design:** Increasing freeboard by even **0.5 m** can reduce $P_{wet}$ by an order of magnitude, because of the exponential nature of the Rayleigh distribution. This makes freeboard a primary design variable.

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $z_{r}(t) = \xi(t) - \eta(t)$ | Relative motion between ship and wave | m |
| 2 | $S_{z_r}(\omega) = \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} S_{\eta}(\omega) + S_{\eta}(\omega)$ | Relative motion spectrum | m²·s |
| 3 | $m_{0, z_r} = \int_0^\infty S_{z_r}(\omega) \, d\omega$ | Zeroth spectral moment | m² |
| 4 | $m_{2, z_r} = \int_0^\infty \omega^{2} S_{z_r}(\omega) \, d\omega$ | Second spectral moment | m²/s² |
| 5 | $P_{wet} = \exp\left(-F^{2}/(2 m_{0, z_r})\right)$ | Probability of deck wetness | — |
| 6 | $T_{wet} = 2\pi \sqrt{m_{0, z_r}/m_{2, z_r}}$ | Mean period of wet events | s |
| 7 | $N_{wet} = (3600/T_{wet}) \cdot P_{wet}$ | Wet events per hour | /hour |
| 8 | $z_{critical} = F_b$ | Threshold relative motion | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Convention:** The freeboard $F$ is measured from the **calm waterline** to the **sheer line / bulwark top**, NOT the deck plate. In bow regions, the *effective* freeboard is reduced by pitch-induced submergence of the bow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Probability of Deck Wetness (Rayleigh Approach)

### Starting Premise

We assume the relative motion $z_{r}(t)$ is a **zero-mean, narrow-band stationary Gaussian process** with variance $\sigma_{z_r}^{2} = m_{0, z_r}$.

For a narrow-band Gaussian process, the envelope (crest amplitude) follows a **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$p(A) = \dfrac{A}{m_{0, z_r}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{A^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right), \quad A \geq 0$$

### Step 1: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Envelope

The probability that the envelope is less than threshold $F$ is obtained by integrating $p(A)$ from 0 to $F$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A < F) &= \int_{0}^{F} \dfrac{A}{m_{0, z_r}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{A^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right) dA \\
&= \left[-\exp\left(-\dfrac{A^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)\right]_{0}^{F} \\
&= 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We use the standard integral $\int x e^{-x^2/c} dx = -\frac{c}{2} e^{-x^2/c}$.

### Step 2: Probability of Exceedance (Deck Wetness)

The probability that the relative motion exceeds the freeboard is the complement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wet} &= P(A \geq F) \\
&= 1 - P(A < F) \\
&= 1 - \left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)\right] \\
P_{wet} &= \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the Ochi–Kotik formula (1974)** — the KTU-board standard expression. ∎

## Derivation 2: Mean Period Between Wet Events

### Step 1: Zero-Crossing Period of the Relative Motion

For a stationary Gaussian process with spectral moments $m_0$ and $m_2$, the **mean zero-upcrossing period** is:

$$T_{z} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{0, z_r}}{m_{2, z_r}}}$$

**Derivation Sketch:** The expected number of zero-upcrossings per unit time is $N_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{m_2/m_0}$ (Rice's formula). Inverting gives the period.

### Step 2: Mean Period of Wet Events

A "wet event" corresponds to an upcrossing of the threshold $F$. By Rice's generalized upcrossing theorem, the **mean upcrossing rate** of level $F$ is:

$$\nu(F) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2, z_r}}{m_{0, z_r}}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)$$

**Logic:** Rice's theorem states that the upcrossing rate of level $F$ for a differentiable Gaussian process $X(t)$ is $\nu(F) = \nu_0 \exp(-F^2 / 2\sigma^2)$, where $\nu_0$ is the zero-crossing rate.

The **mean time between wet events** is the reciprocal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wet} &= \dfrac{1}{\nu(F)} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{0, z_r}}{m_{2, z_r}}} \cdot \exp\left(+\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Some KTU textbooks write $T_{wet} = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$ (the *zero-crossing* period of relative motion, used as a base period) and then multiply by $1/P_{wet}$ to get the *inter-event* period. The two conventions differ by a factor of $1/P_{wet}$. Confirm the textbook convention.

## Derivation 3: Number of Wet Events per Hour

Combining the probability and the period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{wet} \, \text{(per hour)} &= \dfrac{3600 \, \text{s/hr}}{T_{wet}} \cdot P_{wet} \\
&= \dfrac{3600}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2, z_r}}{m_{0, z_r}}} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right) \cdot 1 \\
N_{wet} &= \dfrac{3600}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2, z_r}}{m_{0, z_r}}} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The base crossing rate is multiplied by the probability of crossing the level $F$.

## Numerical Demonstration (Sample Calculation)

Suppose $m_{0, z_r} = 0.36 \, m^2$, $m_{2, z_r} = 0.49 \, m^2/s^2$, $F = 1.5 \, m$. Compute the wetness parameters.

**Step A — Probability of Wetness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wet} &= \exp\left(-\dfrac{(1.5)^{2}}{2 \times 0.36}\right) \\
&= \exp\left(-\dfrac{2.25}{0.72}\right) \\
&= \exp(-3.125) \\
&\approx 0.0439
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Deck is wet approximately 4.4% of the time.

**Step B — Mean Period of Wet Events:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wet} &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{0.36}{0.49}} \\
&= 2\pi \times 0.857 \\
&\approx 5.39 \, s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Wet Events per Hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{wet} &= \dfrac{3600}{5.39} \times 0.0439 \\
&\approx 668.8 \times 0.0439 \\
&\approx 29.4 \, \text{events/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** A ship wet for ~4% of time and seeing ~30 events per hour implies a *wet event duration* of $\sim 5$ s, which is consistent with a 5–6 s wave period. This validates the calculation.

## Sensitivity Analysis Summary

| Parameter | Effect on $P_{wet}$ | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Increase $F$ (freeboard) | Exponential **decrease** | Hull height most effective |
| Increase $H_s$ (sea state) | Increases $m_0$ → exponential **increase** | Limit operational sea state |
| Reduce speed in head seas | Decreases $\vert H(\omega)\vert$ → reduces $m_0$ | Speed reduction policy |
| Bow flare angle change | Modifies RAO at bow | Hull form optimization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Figure 1: Block Diagram — Deck Wetness Analysis Pipeline


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "deck wetness" as applied in ship dynamics. List the two key statistical descriptors used to quantify it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Deck wetness is the phenomenon in which sea water overtops the bulwark or rail of a vessel and floods the exposed weather deck during ship operation in waves. It is statistically defined as the event where the **relative vertical motion** $z_r(t)$ between the ship and the wave free surface exceeds the effective **freeboard** $F_b$ at a given deck-edge location.

**Statistical Descriptors (1 mark):**
1. **Probability of Deck Wetness ($P_{wet}$):** Fraction of time the deck is wet.
2. **Number of Wet Events per Hour ($N_{wet}$):** Frequency of green-water impacts on the deck.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Ochi's formula for the probability of deck wetness and explain the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{wet} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_{0, z_r}}\right)$$

**Term Meanings (1.5 marks):**
- $F$ — Effective freeboard (m): height of deck edge above calm waterline, the critical threshold.
- $m_{0, z_r}$ — Zeroth spectral moment of the relative motion ($m^2$): represents the **variance** of the relative vertical displacement.
- The exponential form indicates that wetness is dominated by *rare large crests*; small freeboard or large wave energy drastically raises $P_{wet}$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the probability of deck wetness $P_{wet}$ for a ship operating in irregular head seas, starting from the Rayleigh distribution of the envelope of the relative motion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container ship operates in head seas with significant wave height $H_s = 4 \, m$. The relative motion spectrum yields $m_{0, z_r} = 0.81 \, m^2$ and $m_{2, z_r} = 0.64 \, m^2/s^2$. The effective freeboard at the bow is $F = 2.0 \, m$. Compute (i) the probability of deck wetness, (ii) the mean period of wet events, and (iii) the number of wet events per hour. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. (1 mark) Assume $z_r(t)$ is zero-mean narrow-band stationary Gaussian with variance $\sigma^2 = m_0$.

2. (2 marks) Envelope $A$ follows Rayleigh:
$$p(A) = \dfrac{A}{m_0} \exp\left(-\dfrac{A^{2}}{2 m_0}\right)$$

3. (2 marks) Integrate to get CDF and exceedance probability:
$$P_{wet} = P(A \geq F) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{F^{2}}{2 m_0}\right)$$

4. (1 mark) State Ochi's assumption that the deck is wet whenever the crest amplitude exceeds freeboard.

5. (1 mark) Mention narrow-band approximation as a prerequisite.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) **Probability of deck wetness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wet} &= \exp\left(-\dfrac{(2.0)^{2}}{2 \times 0.81}\right) \\
&= \exp\left(-\dfrac{4.0}{1.62}\right) \\
&= \exp(-2.469) \\
&\approx 0.0846
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) **Mean period of wet events:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wet} &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_0}{m_2}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{0.81}{0.64}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{1.2656} \\
&= 2\pi \times 1.125 \\
&\approx 7.07 \, s
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) **Number of wet events per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{wet} &= \dfrac{3600}{T_{wet}} \cdot P_{wet} \\
&= \dfrac{3600}{7.07} \times 0.0846 \\
&\approx 509.2 \times 0.0846 \\
&\approx 43.1 \, \text{events/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $P_{wet} \approx 0.085$, $T_{wet} \approx 7.07$ s, $N_{wet} \approx 43$ events/hour.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the relative motion between ship and waves is computed, and how it enters the deck wetness formulation. Discuss the role of bow flare angle and forward speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A naval vessel has effective freeboard $F = 3.5 \, m$ at the forecastle. During a coastal operation, the relative motion spectrum is broad-banded with $m_{0, z_r} = 0.25 \, m^2$ and $m_{2, z_r} = 0.16 \, m^2/s^2$. Determine (i) $P_{wet}$, (ii) $T_{wet}$, and (iii) $N_{wet}$. If the operational limit per IMO is 30 events/hour, decide whether the vessel can operate in this sea state. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

1. (2 marks) Define relative motion: $z_r(t) = \xi_{ship}(t) - \eta(t)$, where $\xi$ is ship vertical displacement (heave + pitch contribution at deck location) and $\eta$ is wave elevation.

2. (2 marks) Sketch: free surface $\eta$ plus ship hull motion → composite $z_r$; deck is wet when $z_r \geq F$ (mention the critical threshold at the bow).

3. (2 marks) Bow flare angle and speed: higher flare reduces the RAO at high frequencies (less green water shipping) by **deflecting water outward**; higher forward speed increases encounter frequency and amplitude of bow motion in head seas, generally increasing wetness.

4. (1 mark) Conclude that wetness is governed by the relative motion spectrum $S_{z_r}(\omega)$ and the freeboard $F$.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) **Probability of wetness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wet} &= \exp\left(-\dfrac{(3.5)^{2}}{2 \times 0.25}\right) \\
&= \exp\left(-\dfrac{12.25}{0.50}\right) \\
&= \exp(-24.5) \\
&\approx 2.18 \times 10^{-11}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) **Mean period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wet} &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{0.25}{0.16}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{1.5625} \\
&= 2\pi \times 1.25 \\
&\approx 7.854 \, s
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) **Number of wet events per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{wet} &= \dfrac{3600}{7.854} \times 2.18 \times 10^{-11} \\
&\approx 458.4 \times 2.18 \times 10^{-11} \\
&\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{events/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision (1 mark):** $N_{wet} \approx 10^{-8}$ events/hour is *negligibly small* and **far below** the IMO limit of 30 events/hour. The vessel is **safe to operate** in this sea state.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $m_0$ with $H_s^2$ directly.** $m_0$ is the variance of relative motion, NOT wave elevation. The student's $m_0$ already contains the RAO squared.
> 2. **Units matter.** $m_2$ has units of $m^2/s^2$; the ratio $m_2/m_0$ has units of $1/s^2$, and the square root is $1/s$. Forgetting units will cost you the final mark.
> 3. **Freeboard is to the bulwark top, not the deck plate.** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Lose 1 mark if missed.
> 4. **Sign convention:** $z_r = \xi - \eta$ means upward ship motion relative to the wave. Some textbooks use $z_r = \eta - \xi$; the Ochi formula form remains the same because it uses the squared freeboard.
> 5. **For broad-banded spectra** (as in Q B-part b), the Rayleigh formula is conservative and may underestimate $P_{wet}$. Mention this caveat for full marks in design-oriented questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Deck wetness is the event $z_r(t) \geq F$, where $z_r$ is relative motion and $F$ is freeboard.
- **Ochi's Formula:** $P_{wet} = \exp(-F^2 / (2 m_{0, z_r}))$ — the central equation.
- **Mean Period:** $T_{wet} = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0 / m_2}$ — base crossing period of the relative motion.
- **Events per Hour:** $N_{wet} = (3600 / T_{wet}) \cdot P_{wet}$.
- **Key Assumptions:** Narrow-band Gaussian process, linear ship response, stationary sea state.
- **Spectral Moments:** $m_0$ = variance of relative motion ($m^2$); $m_2$ = curvature of PSD ($m^2/s^2$).
- **Physical Drivers:** Freeboard, significant wave height, ship speed, heading angle, bow flare, hull form.
- **Engineering Impact:** Affects crew safety, cargo integrity, weapon system operability, IMO compliance.
- **Design Levers:** Increasing freeboard is exponentially effective; reducing speed in head seas helps; bow flare deflects water.
- **Typical Numbers:** $P_{wet} = 0.05$ to $0.20$ for cargo ships in head seas with $H_s = 3$–$5$ m.
- **IMO Reference:** Resolution A.749(18) and the IS Code set operational limits for non-convention vessels.
- **Common Mistake:** Using $H_s$ instead of $F$ as the threshold in the formula — always the freeboard.
- **Be Careful With:** Sign convention of $z_r$; units of $m_2$; broad-band vs narrow-band applicability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sea-Sickness
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sea-Sickness

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sea-Sickness (A.K.A. Mal de Mer)** is a specific physiological form of **Motion Sickness** induced by the low-frequency, oscillatory motions of a vessel (primarily **heave**, **roll**, and **pitch**) in a seaway, arising from a **sensory conflict (neural mismatch)** between the vestibular apparatus of the inner ear, the visual system, and the body's proprioceptive signals.

The phenomenon is formally categorized under the human-factors branch of **Naval Architecture and Ship Dynamics** because the magnitude and frequency of ship motions directly determine the percentage of crew and passengers that will become incapacitated. Sea-sickness is not a disease; it is a self-limiting neurological response whose statistical incidence can be mathematically predicted as a function of vessel motion parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ship dynamics studies sea-sickness:** Because ship design (hull form, metacentric height GM, bilge keels, stabilizer fins, location of accommodation decks) directly governs the amplitudes and frequencies of motion that cause the sickness. The naval architect's job is to keep motions *below* the human-tolerance threshold.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a stationary car in a parking lot, but a friend gently rocks the car from side to side. Even though your eyes tell you that the scenery outside is NOT moving, your **inner ear** (the vestibular system) screams: "We are swaying!" This contradiction confuses your brain → nausea → that is **sea-sickness in miniature**.

On a ship in waves, the conflict is much stronger:
- Your **eyes** may be reading a book in a closed cabin → "the world is still."
- Your **inner ear** feels the rolling, pitching, and heaving → "we are falling and rising rapidly!"
- Your **muscles & joints** (proprioception) feel the swaying body → "we are definitely moving."

> The brain's vomiting center (in the medulla) interprets this **3-way disagreement as a neurotoxin hallucination** and triggers a "purge" response. It is essentially a "false-poison" detection mechanism inherited from our evolutionary ancestors.

### 1.3 Standard Physiological Metrics and Constants

The following are the standard reference values used universally (and adopted in KTU-ship-dynamics literature) to characterize human tolerance to ship motion:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Critical vertical acceleration for onset | $a_{z,crit}$ | **0.20 – 0.40 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Sickness-prone frequency band (vertical) | $f$ | **0.10 – 0.50 Hz** | Hz |
| Peak sensitivity frequency (vertical) | $f_{peak}$ | **0.20 – 0.25 Hz** | Hz |
| ISO 2631 motion-sickness factor (vertical) | $W_{f}$ | tabulated | dimensionless |
| Typical roll period threshold | $T_{\phi,crit}$ | **> 10 s** (calm) ; **< 6 s** (rough) | s |
| Acceptable heave acceleration | $a_{z,lim}$ | **0.315 m/s² (RMS)** for 2 hr | m/s² |
| Vertical motion sickness dose limit (ISO) | $MSDV_{z,lim}$ | **0.315 m·s⁻¹·⁵** | m·s⁻¹·⁵ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The frequency band **0.10 – 0.50 Hz** corresponds to wave periods of **2 s to 10 s**, which are the dominant energy-carrying sea waves (wind waves, mild swell). This is why ordinary beam-sea or head-sea conditions make people sick while long-period ocean swell is more tolerable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sensory Inputs and Their Conflict

Sea-sickness originates in a **tri-input conflict** between three biological sensors. Each contributes a different type of information about the body's state:

1. **Vestibular System (Inner Ear) – Spatial Orientation**
   - Located in the semicircular canals (angular acceleration) and otolith organs (linear acceleration + gravity vector).
   - Detects true motion of the head in 3D space.
   - Frequency response: 0.1 Hz → 10 Hz (peak ~0.5 Hz).
   - This is the **dominant trigger** of sea-sickness.

2. **Visual System (Eyes) – Exteroceptive Reference**
   - Provides external frame of reference (horizon, deck, waves).
   - If the eyes see a fixed cabin wall while the body feels motion → **sensory conflict**.

3. **Somatosensory System (Skin, Joints, Muscles)**
   - Pressure cues on the seat, feet on the deck, proprioception.
   - Confirms or contradicts vestibular input.

> **The "Sensory Conflict Theory" (Reason & Brand, 1975)** states: *Nausea occurs when the pattern of sensory inputs received by the brain does not match any pattern stored in long-term memory from previous motion experience.*

### 2.2 Primary Ship Motions that Cause Sea-Sickness

Although ships have 6 degrees of freedom, only three are quantitatively significant for sea-sickness:

#### A. Roll ($\phi$) — Rotation about the longitudinal (x) axis
- **Most common cause** of sea-sickness.
- Period $T_{\phi}$ depends on metacentric height **GM**, beam **B**, and displacement **Δ**:
$$T_{\phi} = 2\pi \, k \sqrt{\dfrac{B^{2}}{12 \, g \, GM}}$$
where $k$ is the radius of gyration coefficient (typically 0.35–0.45).
- Roll amplitudes up to **20°–30°** in beam seas are common in unstabilized vessels.
- Roll frequency is the **single largest determinant** of sickness incidence on small-to-medium ships.

#### B. Pitch ($\theta$) — Rotation about the transverse (y) axis
- Dominant in head seas.
- Amplitude increases with wavelength, decreases with speed.
- Longitudinal metacentric height **GML** controls pitch period.

#### C. Heave ($z$) — Vertical translation
- Vertical oscillation of the centre of gravity.
- Peak sensitivity at **0.20 Hz** (≈ 5 s period).
- Heave amplitude in irregular seas: $z_a = H_{s}/2$ approx., where $H_s$ is significant wave height.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Mechanism of Sea-Sickness

1. **Wave excitation** produces oscillatory roll, pitch, heave.
2. The vestibular otoliths register **specific-force variations** (true acceleration + gravity).
3. If the person is below decks, the visual system reports "no motion."
4. The brain's comparator detects a mismatch.
5. The mismatch activates the **vomiting centre** via the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ) and the nucleus tractus solitarius.
6. Symptoms emerge: pallor, cold sweat, salivation, nausea, vomiting, apathy.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every exam cycle in Module 2 questions on sea-sickness and ship motions.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Roll period | $T_{\phi} = 2\pi \, k \sqrt{\dfrac{B^{2}}{12 \, g \, GM}}$ | Natural rolling period; sickness rises as $T_{\phi}$ enters 5–10 s band. |
| 2 | Maximum roll angle (linear theory) | $\phi_{max} = \dfrac{a_{z}}{g \sin\omega t}$ | Approximate peak roll from forcing acceleration $a_z$. |
| 3 | Vertical specific-force at deck | $f_{z} = g - \ddot{z}$ | Body feels effective weight; sea-sickness correlates with $f_{z}$ RMS. |
| 4 | ISO 2631-1 weighted RMS vertical acceleration | $a_{wz} = W_{f} \cdot a_{z,RMS}$ | Frequency-weighted acceleration used in motion-sickness standards. |
| 5 | Motion Sickness Dose Value (vertical) | $MSDV_{z} = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{wz}^{2}(t) \, dt\right]^{1/2}$ | Cumulative dose; ISO recommends $MSDV_z \le 0.315 \, m \cdot s^{-1.5}$ for 2-h exposure. |
| 6 | Motion Sickness Incidence (ISO estimate) | $MSI = K \cdot MSDV_{z}^{\,n}$ | Approx. $K = 29.0,\; n = 1.41$ for vertical motion. |
| 7 | MSI – McCauley & Royal (1972) form | $MSI = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-m\,t}\right]\,(\%)$ | Fraction of population sick by time $t$. |
| 8 | Sickness-rate parameter $m$ (vertical) | $m = 0.5 f^{2} \quad$ for $0.1 \le f \le 0.5$ Hz | Frequency-squared dependence; explains the 0.2–0.25 Hz peak. |
| 9 | Sickness-rate parameter $m$ (roll) | $m_{roll} = 0.066\,(f)^{1.5}$ | Lower sickness rate for pure roll vs. pure heave. |
| 10 | Heave natural period (slender ship) | $T_{z} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta \, d}{g \, A_{wp}}}$ | Determines heave resonance; sickness peaks near resonance. |
| 11 | Acceptable heave accel. (2-h limit) | $a_{z,RMS,lim} = 0.315 \, m/s^{2}$ | Threshold of 7% MSI in 2 h. |
| 12 | Critical roll acceleration (ship design) | $a_{\phi} = 4\pi^{2} \phi_{a} f^{2}$ | Tangential acceleration at deck edge from roll. |

> **Notation** — $B$: ship beam (m); $GM$: metacentric height (m); $k$: roll-radius-of-gyration coefficient; $A_{wp}$: waterplane area; $d$: draft; $\Delta$: displacement; $f$: forcing frequency (Hz); $t$: exposure time (s); $\phi_a$: roll amplitude (rad).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Controlling sea-sickness has measurable commercial and military value:

- **Cruise-line industry:** 1% reduction in MSI on a 3000-passenger ship = 30 fewer sick guests per voyage → improved revenue & reviews.
- **Warship design:** "Sea-state 5 operability" of a frigate is rated by the **MSI of the crew**; modern combat-information centres are placed amidships at the **pitch centre of rotation** to minimize vertical motion.
- **Offshore platforms:** ISO 2631 motion-sickness limits are now legal requirements in some classifications (DNV, Lloyd's) for accommodation modules.
- **Yacht design:** Owners pay huge premiums for **fin stabilizers** specifically to suppress the 0.1–0.5 Hz roll band that causes sickness.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive derivation policy** — every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Roll Period $T_{\phi}$ in a Beam Sea

The rolling motion of a ship in still water is governed by the **uncoupled linear roll equation**:

$$
I_{xx} \, \ddot{\phi} + \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \phi = 0
$$

**Step 1** — Identify the moment of inertia. For a rectangular ship model (or near-bilge approximation):

$$
I_{xx} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta}{g}\left(k B\right)^{2}
$$

where $k$ is the roll-radius-of-gyration coefficient (dimensionless).

**Step 2** — Substitute $I_{xx}$ into the roll equation:

$$
\left[\dfrac{\Delta}{g}(kB)^{2}\right]\ddot{\phi} + \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \phi = 0
$$

**Step 3** — Cancel $\Delta$ from every term:

$$
\dfrac{(kB)^{2}}{g}\,\ddot{\phi} + g \cdot GM \cdot \phi = 0
$$

**Step 4** — Divide throughout by $\dfrac{(kB)^{2}}{g}$ to obtain the standard form $\ddot{\phi} + \omega_{n}^{2}\phi = 0$:

$$
\ddot{\phi} + \left[\dfrac{g^{2} \cdot GM}{(kB)^{2}}\right]\phi = 0
$$

**Step 5** — Identify the natural circular frequency:

$$
\omega_{n}^{2} = \dfrac{g^{2}\,GM}{(kB)^{2}}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\omega_{n} = \dfrac{g\,\sqrt{GM}}{kB}
$$

**Step 6** — Convert $\omega_{n}$ to the natural period $T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_{n}}$:

$$
T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi\, k B}{g\,\sqrt{GM}}
$$

**Step 7** — Square the RHS to obtain the KTU textbook form:

$$
T_{\phi} = 2\pi \, k \sqrt{\dfrac{B^{2}}{12 \, g \, GM}} \quad \text{(only if }k^{2}=1/12\text{; otherwise use Step 6 directly)}
$$

For an arbitrary ship, **the KTU reference form used in problems is**:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi\, k B}{g\,\sqrt{GM}}\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motion Sickness Incidence (McCauley & Royal Model)

The number of people expected to vomit (or be incapacitated) follows an **exponential-decay survivor function**. Let:

- $N(t)$ = number of people *not yet* sick at time $t$.
- $m$ = sickness-rate parameter (depends on motion frequency & amplitude).
- $N_0$ = initial population (assumed susceptible).

**Step 1** — Rate of becoming sick is proportional to the number of *not-yet-sick* people:

$$
-\dfrac{dN}{dt} = m \, N(t)
$$

**Step 2** — Separate variables:

$$
\dfrac{dN}{N} = -m\,dt
$$

**Step 3** — Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -m \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 4** — Evaluate:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = -m t
$$

**Step 5** — Exponentiate both sides:

$$
N(t) = N_0\, e^{-mt}
$$

**Step 6** — The number who **are** sick at time $t$:

$$
N_{sick}(t) = N_0 - N(t) = N_0\left(1 - e^{-mt}\right)
$$

**Step 7** — Express as a **percentage** of the population:

$$
\boxed{\,MSI = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-m t}\right] \;\;(\%)\,}
$$

**Step 8** — For pure **vertical (heave) motion** in the sickness-prone band $0.1 \le f \le 0.5$ Hz:

$$
m \;\approx\; 0.5\, f^{2}
$$

Substitute into the boxed equation to obtain a *frequency–time* prediction of sea-sickness:

$$
MSI_{heave} = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-0.5 f^{2} t}\right]
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — ISO 2631-1 Motion Sickness Dose Value (MSDV)

**Step 1** — Start with the time-history of vertical acceleration $a_{z}(t)$ measured at the body (or seat).

**Step 2** — Apply the ISO 2631-1 frequency-weighting $W_{f}(f)$ (tabulated; peaks at 0.25 Hz):

$$
a_{wz}(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{W_{f}(f)\,\mathcal{F}\{a_{z}(t)\}\right\}
$$

In words: Fourier-transform $a_{z}(t)$, multiply by the weighting, inverse-transform.

**Step 3** — Form the cumulative quadratic integral (variance-like quantity):

$$
\int_{0}^{T} a_{wz}^{2}(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 4** — Take the square root to obtain the **Motion Sickness Dose Value**:

$$
\boxed{\,MSDV_{z} = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_{wz}^{2}(t) \, dt\right]^{1/2} \quad [m \cdot s^{-1.5}]\,}
$$

The unusual unit arises because the integral has units $(m/s^{2})^{2} \cdot s = m^{2} s^{-3}$, and the square root gives $m \cdot s^{-1.5}$.

**Step 5** — Empirically, $MSI$ correlates with $MSDV_{z}$ as a power law:

$$
MSI = K \cdot MSDV_{z}^{\,n}
$$

with $K \approx 29.0$ and $n \approx 1.41$ (vertical motion, ISO-derived constants).

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 5000-t displacement ship has beam $B = 16$ m, $GM = 0.6$ m, and roll-radius-of-gyration coefficient $k = 0.4$. Estimate the **natural roll period** and the **MSI after 4 hours** in beam seas where the dominant roll frequency equals the natural roll frequency. Take $m = 0.5 f^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Roll natural period:**

$$
T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi \, k B}{g \sqrt{GM}}
$$

Substituting $k=0.4$, $B=16$ m, $g=9.81$ m/s², $GM=0.6$ m:

$$
T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi (0.4)(16)}{9.81 \sqrt{0.6}}
$$

Compute denominator: $9.81 \times \sqrt{0.6} = 9.81 \times 0.7746 = 7.5989$

Compute numerator: $2\pi \times 6.4 = 40.212$

$$
T_{\phi} = \dfrac{40.212}{7.5989} \approx 5.29 \;\; \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Roll natural frequency:**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{T_{\phi}} = \dfrac{1}{5.29} \approx 0.189 \;\; \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Sickness-rate parameter $m$:**

$$
m = 0.5 f^{2} = 0.5 \times (0.189)^{2} = 0.5 \times 0.03572 = 0.01786 \;\; s^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Exposure time in seconds:**

$$
t = 4 \text{ h} \times 3600 = 14\,400 \;\; \text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Motion Sickness Incidence:**

$$
MSI = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-m t}\right] = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-(0.01786)(14400)}\right]
$$

Compute the exponent: $-(0.01786)(14400) = -257.2$

$$
e^{-257.2} \approx 0 \quad (\text{essentially zero})
$$

$$
\boxed{\,MSI \approx 100\%\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** In beam seas at the natural roll frequency for 4 hours, *every susceptible person* would be sick. This is why **active fin stabilizers** (which raise the effective roll damping) are mandatory on cruise ships, yachts, and warships.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Heave vs. Roll vs. Pitch

| Property | Heave | Roll | Pitch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Axis of motion | Vertical $z$ | Longitudinal $x$ | Transverse $y$ |
| Restoring force | Buoyancy (waterplane) | Gravity–buoyancy couple | Gravity–buoyancy couple |
| Natural period (typical) | 4 – 8 s | 6 – 18 s | 4 – 10 s |
| Peak-sickness frequency | **0.20 – 0.25 Hz** | 0.10 – 0.20 Hz | 0.15 – 0.25 Hz |
| Sickness-rate coefficient $m$ (per ISO/McCauley) | $0.5 f^{2}$ | $0.066 f^{1.5}$ | $0.13 f^{1.5}$ |
| Dominant wave direction | All directions | Beam seas | Head/Following seas |
| Stabilization methods | Flume / anti-heave tanks | Bilge keels, fins, paravanes | Trim, after-body form |
| Relative sickness potential (per unit amplitude) | **HIGHEST** | High | Moderate |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define sea-sickness. Mention the two ship motions that contribute most significantly to its occurrence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sea-sickness is a form of motion sickness occurring on ships due to **sensory conflict** between the vestibular, visual, and proprioceptive systems, triggered by oscillatory vessel motions. The two most significant motions are:
1. **Heave** (vertical translation) — peak sickness frequency 0.20–0.25 Hz.
2. **Roll** (rotation about longitudinal axis) — dominant in beam seas, $T_\phi = 5$–10 s.
*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark heave, 1 mark roll.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the "Sensory Conflict Theory" of motion sickness. Why is the conflict maximum below decks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Sensory Conflict Theory (Reason & Brand, 1975) states that **nausea is induced when the pattern of motion-signals from the vestibular, visual and proprioceptive systems does not match any pattern stored in long-term memory.** Below decks, the **visual input reports a static scene** (no relative motion of cabin walls), while the **vestibular system registers strong oscillation** — this contradiction is maximum, hence sickness is maximum in closed cabins. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — `Module 2 Choice Option A`

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Sensory Conflict Theory** of sea-sickness in detail. List the three sensory systems involved and describe their roles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger vessel has the following particulars:
- Beam $B = 18$ m
- Metacentric height $GM = 0.8$ m
- Roll-radius-of-gyration coefficient $k = 0.42$
- Dominant roll frequency in beam seas $f = 0.18$ Hz

Calculate the **natural roll period** $T_\phi$ and the **Motion Sickness Incidence (MSI)** after 2 hours, assuming the McCauley & Royal model with $m = 0.5 f^2$ for vertical motion. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🔹 Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Sensory Conflict Theory (proposed by Reason & Brand, 1975) is the most widely accepted neuro-physiological explanation of motion sickness. It posits that **sea-sickness occurs when the brain receives mismatched signals from different sensory channels**, which it interprets as evidence of neurotoxin ingestion.

**2. The three sensory systems (3 marks):**

| Sensory System | Detector | Information Conveyed |
|---|---|---|
| **Vestibular** (Inner ear) | Semicircular canals + otoliths | True 3-D angular & linear acceleration of the head |
| **Visual** (Eyes) | Retina + visual cortex | Position/motion of external objects (horizon, walls) |
| **Somatosensory / Proprioceptive** | Skin, joint receptors, Golgi tendon organs | Body's position, pressure cues, gravitational reference |

**3. Why the conflict arises (2 marks):**
On a ship in waves, the **vestibular system** detects oscillating roll/heave/pitch; the **proprioceptors** confirm body sway through seat pressure. However, the **visual system** — especially in a closed cabin with no windows — reports "world is stationary." The mismatch is maximal **below decks**, in calm weather when waves are barely visible, and **at the peak-sickness frequency band (0.10–0.50 Hz)**.

**4. Brain's response (1 mark):**
The brain's **central comparator** in the cerebellum and brainstem detects the inconsistency, fails to match it with any stored memory pattern, and activates the **chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ)** and **vomiting centre** in the medulla, producing pallor, salivation, nausea, and vomiting.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Natural roll period $T_\phi$ (3 marks):**

$$
T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi\, k B}{g\,\sqrt{GM}}
$$

Substitute the data: $k = 0.42$, $B = 18$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $GM = 0.8$ m.

$$
T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi (0.42)(18)}{9.81 \sqrt{0.8}}
$$

Numerator: $2\pi \times 0.42 \times 18 = 47.49$

Denominator: $9.81 \times \sqrt{0.8} = 9.81 \times 0.8944 = 8.774$

$$
T_{\phi} = \dfrac{47.49}{8.774} = 5.41 \;\; \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Sickness-rate parameter $m$ (1 mark):**

$$
m = 0.5 f^{2} = 0.5 \times (0.18)^{2} = 0.5 \times 0.0324 = 0.0162 \;\; s^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Exposure time in seconds (1 mark):**

$$
t = 2 \text{ h} \times 3600 = 7200 \;\; \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Motion Sickness Incidence (2 marks):**

$$
MSI = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-m t}\right] = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-(0.0162)(7200)}\right]
$$

Exponent: $-(0.0162)(7200) = -116.64$

$$
e^{-116.64} \approx 1.78 \times 10^{-51} \approx 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,MSI \approx 100\%\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** In 2 hours, essentially 100% of the susceptible population will be sick. This demonstrates why **passenger ships need fin stabilizers** to reduce the effective roll amplitude and keep $MSI$ below 7–10%.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — `Module 2 Choice Option B` (Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the role of **roll, pitch and heave** in causing sea-sickness. Which has the highest sickness-rate coefficient and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The vertical acceleration measured on a ship's bridge is $a_{z,RMS} = 0.45$ m/s². The dominant frequency is 0.20 Hz. Using the **ISO 2631-1 weighting** $W_f \approx 1.0$ at 0.20 Hz, compute the **Motion Sickness Dose Value (MSDV)** for a 2-hour exposure, and check whether the design satisfies the ISO comfort threshold. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🔹 Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**1. Roll — 2 marks:**
Rotation about the longitudinal (x) axis; $\phi$ amplitude up to 20–30° in beam seas. Sickness rate $m_{roll} = 0.066 f^{1.5}$. Most common cause on passenger ships.

**2. Pitch — 2 marks:**
Rotation about the transverse (y) axis; $\theta$ amplitude dominant in head seas. Sickness rate $m_{pitch} = 0.13 f^{1.5}$.

**3. Heave — 2 marks:**
Vertical translation $z$ of CG. **Highest sickness-rate coefficient** $m_{heave} = 0.5 f^{2}$ — about 4× greater than roll at the same frequency — because the otolith organs are most sensitive to linear vertical acceleration. Peak sensitivity at **0.20 – 0.25 Hz**.

**4. Comparative statement (1 mark):**
Heave > Pitch > Roll (in sickness-inducing potential per unit amplitude). This is why ISO 2631-1 weights vertical acceleration most heavily.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Weighted vertical acceleration (1 mark):**

$$
a_{wz} = W_{f} \cdot a_{z,RMS} = 1.0 \times 0.45 = 0.45 \;\; m/s^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Exposure duration in seconds (1 mark):**

$$
T = 2 \text{ h} = 7200 \;\; s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute MSDV (3 marks):**

Assuming the acceleration is approximately stationary, the integral reduces to:

$$
MSDV_{z} = a_{wz}\,\sqrt{T}
$$

$$
MSDV_{z} = 0.45 \times \sqrt{7200}
$$

$$
\sqrt{7200} = 84.85
$$

$$
MSDV_{z} = 0.45 \times 84.85 = 38.18 \;\; m \cdot s^{-1.5}
$$

**Step 4 — ISO threshold comparison (2 marks):**

ISO 2631-1 recommends $MSDV_z \le 0.315 \, m \cdot s^{-1.5}$ for 2-h exposure (corresponding to 7% MSI).

$$
\dfrac{38.18}{0.315} \approx 121
$$

The measured value is **~121 times the limit** → **Design Fails ISO requirement.**

**Conclusion:** The ship would cause ~100% sea-sickness incidence. Remedial actions: install **flume/anti-heave tanks**, **fin stabilizers**, or relocate the bridge to a position with lower vertical motion (e.g., near the pitch centre).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert hours → seconds before computing $mt$ in the McCauley formula. Many students write $t=2$ and get $e^{-0.03}$ — an error of 3 orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Mixing $k$ with $k^2$:** The roll-period formula has $k$ (linear), not $k^2$. Do not "square" the radius of gyration.
> 3. **Forgetting ISO weighting:** $a_{z,RMS}$ ≠ $a_{wz}$ unless $W_f = 1$. At low frequencies (<0.1 Hz) and high frequencies (>0.5 Hz), $W_f < 1$.
> 4. **Wrong $m$ parameter:** Use $0.5 f^2$ for **heave only**. For roll use $0.066 f^{1.5}$. Confusing these gives a 5–10× error.
> 5. **Confusing "vertigo" with "sea-sickness":** Vertigo = spinning sensation from vestibular pathology. Sea-sickness = nausea from sensory conflict. They are *not* the same.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for sea-sickness (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Sea-sickness = sensory-conflict-induced nausea in a seaway. Caused by **heave + roll + pitch**.
- **Three sensory inputs:** Vestibular (dominant), Visual, Proprioceptive. Conflict = nausea.
- **Peak-sickness frequency band:** **0.10 – 0.50 Hz**, with maximum sensitivity at **0.20 – 0.25 Hz** (heave).
- **Sickness-rate coefficients (McCauley & Royal):**
  - Heave → $m = 0.5 f^{2}$
  - Pitch → $m = 0.13 f^{1.5}$
  - Roll → $m = 0.066 f^{1.5}$
- **Master formula 1:** $MSI = 100\!\left[1 - e^{-m t}\right]$ (%)
- **Master formula 2:** $T_{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi\, k B}{g\,\sqrt{GM}}$ (s)
- **Master formula 3:** $MSDV_z = \left[\int_0^{T} a_{wz}^{2}\,dt\right]^{1/2}$ with ISO limit **0.315 m·s⁻¹·⁵** (2 h).
- **Order of sickness potential:** Heave > Pitch > Roll.
- **Below-deck effect:** Maximum sickness in **closed cabins** (no visual reference). Open decks with horizon-view reduce incidence.
- **Stabilization methods:** **Bilge keels** (passive damping), **fin stabilizers** (active, most effective), **anti-roll tanks**, **trim/flap control**, hull-form optimization.
- **Design philosophy:** Move accommodation to amidships (near pitch centre) to minimize **heave** and angular accelerations.
- **Individual variability:** Susceptibility varies 1000-fold across population; age, gender, habituation, fatigue, alcohol all modulate.
- **Habituation:** Reduces MSI by 50–80% over 2–7 days at sea.
- **Gravitational constant:** $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Calculate $T_\phi$ and MSI for given $B, GM, k$." Memorize Step 1–4 of the worked example.

---

**End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Sea-Sickness | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCNST501**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 | Topic: Added Resistance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Added Resistance ($R_A$):** It is the mean increase in the calm-water resistance of a ship when it is propelled at a constant speed $U$ through regular waves of significant height $H_s$ (or amplitude $\zeta_a$), compared to its resistance in **calm water** at the same speed and displacement.

Mathematically, the total resistance in waves $R_W$ is composed as:

$$R_W = R_{\text{calm}} + R_A$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{calm}}$ = Calm-water resistance at speed $U$
- $R_A$ = Added resistance in waves (time-averaged, mean value)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "Swimming Against a Washing Machine"**
>
> Imagine walking on a still treadmill versus walking on a treadmill placed inside a swimming pool with surface waves. The wavy treadmill does **not** change your body weight, but every wave bump pushes you back, and you must burn extra energy to maintain the same walking speed. That **extra effort** the waves demand — even though your average speed is unchanged — is exactly **Added Resistance** in ship dynamics.
>
> - In **calm water**, the hull only has to push the still water aside (frictional + wave-making resistance).
> - In **waves**, the oscillating water surface adds fluctuating pressures on the hull, generating a **net retarding force** averaged over a wave cycle, even though forward speed $U$ is steady.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> Standard physical constants used throughout this topic:
>
> | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (SI) |
> |---|---|---|
> | $\rho$ | Sea-water density | **1025 kg/m³** |
> | $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
> | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight of sea water | **10054 N/m³** |
> | $L_{pp}$ | Length between perpendiculars | ship-specific |
> | $B$ | Ship breadth (moulded) | ship-specific |
> | $T$ | Mean design draft | ship-specific |
> | $C_B$ | Block coefficient | 0.55 – 0.85 |
> | $\mu$ | Wave heading angle (relative to ship bow) | 0° (following) – 180° (head) |

### 1.4 Why Added Resistance Matters in Naval Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to (i) define added resistance, (ii) identify dominant contributors (wave reflections, eddy shedding, wave-breaking), (iii) compute $R_A$ for head and oblique seas using **Havelock's slender-ship theory** and the **Gerritsma–Beukelman empirical method**, and (iv) explain the influence of ship form ($C_B$, slenderness) and wave steepness $H/\lambda$.

Real-world importance:
- **Powering & fuel-budgeting:** For a container ship on a North Atlantic voyage, $R_A$ can rise **100%–300%** above calm-water resistance in heavy seas.
- **Speed-drop / course-keeping:** Engineers must pre-compute added resistance to select correct engine RPM.
- **Route optimization:** Avoidance of head-sea conditions uses $R_A$ vs. heading charts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Added Resistance

When a ship advances at constant forward velocity $U$ through an incident regular wave of amplitude $\zeta_a$, frequency $\omega$, and heading $\mu$, the local fluid velocity at the hull surface is no longer steady. Three physical mechanisms contribute to the **mean added resistance** $R_A$:

1. **Wave-reflection component ($R_{AR}$):** Radiation of scattered/diffracted waves by the hull; momentum transfer from ship to fluid.
2. **Eddy-making component ($R_{AE}$):** Vortex shedding at sharp bilges, especially in stern-quartering seas.
3. **Wave-breaking component ($R_{AB}$):** Onset of bow-wave breaking in steep head seas at low Froude numbers (slender hulls).

For most displacement-type merchant ships in moderate seas:

$$R_A \approx R_{AR} + R_{AE} \quad ; \quad R_{AB} \text{ becomes significant only when } \frac{H}{\lambda} > \frac{1}{12}$$

## 2.2 Encounter Frequency and Wave Number

In a stationary frame, a wave of absolute frequency $\omega$ is **encountered** at a different frequency $\omega_e$ by a ship moving with velocity $U$ at a heading angle $\mu$:

$$\omega_e = \omega - kU \cos\mu$$

where $k$ is the wave number. In deep water, the dispersion relation gives:

$$\omega^2 = gk \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{\omega^2}{g} \; , \; \lambda = \frac{g}{\omega^2}\cdot 2\pi$$

The non-dimensional **encounter frequency parameter** (also called the Havelock parameter) is:

$$\tau = \frac{\omega_e U}{g} = \frac{U\omega}{g} - \frac{U^2 \omega^2}{g^2}\cos\mu = kU\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{U\cos\mu}{\omega}\right)\frac{\omega}{U}$$

For **head seas ($\mu = 180°$, $\cos\mu = -1$)**:

$$\tau_{\text{head}} = \frac{U\omega}{g} + \frac{U^2\omega^2}{g^2} = \frac{U\omega}{g}\left(1 + \frac{U\omega}{g}\right)$$

## 2.3 Slenderness and Form Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Slenderness parameter** $\sigma = \frac{\omega U}{g}\cdot\frac{L}{2} = \frac{kL}{2}\cdot\frac{U}{\omega}\cdot\omega = \frac{kL}{2}$
>
> A ship behaves as **slender** when $\sigma < 1$ (i.e., the wave is long compared to the hull). Slender-ship theory (Havelock) is most accurate in this regime.

**Form effect** is captured through the **block coefficient $C_B$** and the **prismatic coefficient $C_P$**:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp}\, B\, T} \quad ; \quad C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \, L_{pp}}$$

where $\nabla$ is the displaced volume and $A_M$ is the midship area.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations before attempting any numerical — these are the equations KTU examiners expect you to reproduce.**

### Table 2.4.1 — Core Added Resistance Equations

| # | Equation | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_W = R_{\text{calm}} + R_A$ | Total resistance in waves | $R_W$ [N] |
| 2 | $\omega_e = \omega - kU\cos\mu$ | Encounter frequency | $\omega_e,\omega$ [rad/s] |
| 3 | $k = \omega^2 / g$ | Deep-water wave number | $k$ [1/m] |
| 4 | $\lambda = 2\pi g / \omega^2$ | Wave length | $\lambda$ [m] |
| 5 | $H = 2\zeta_a$ | Wave height from amplitude | $\zeta_a$ [m] |
| 6 | $R_A = \dfrac{\rho g^2 \zeta_a^2 B^2}{L}\, K_H(\tau)$ | **Havelock's slender-ship form** (head waves) | $K_H$ dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{R_A}{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L} = f(\tau, C_B)$ | Non-dimensional $R_A$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_A / \rho g B^2 \zeta_a^2 / L = 0.65 \,(2\zeta_a/B)^{0.6}\cdot F(\tau, C_B)$ | **Gerritsma–Beukelman** empirical | $F$ tabulated |
| 9 | $P_{AE} = R_A \cdot U$ | Added power in waves | $P_{AE}$ [W] |
| 10 | $\dfrac{R_A}{R_{\text{calm}}} \propto \left(\dfrac{H}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Scaling with wave steepness | Dimensionless ratio |

### Table 2.4.2 — Havelock Coefficient $K_H(\tau)$ — Key Behaviour

| Range of $\tau$ | Physical Regime | $K_H$ Trend |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau < 0.4$ | Long waves (low frequency) | Small, slowly rising |
| $0.4 < \tau < 0.8$ | Near resonance with hull length | **Peaks** here (max $R_A$) |
| $0.8 < \tau < 1.2$ | Short waves, ship's bow lifts out | Decreasing |
| $\tau > 1.2$ | Very short chop | Asymptotically small |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Equations

| Domain | How $R_A$ is used |
|---|---|
| **Propulsion design** | Engine selection & fuel-tank sizing must include the worst-case $R_A \cdot U$ for service waves. |
| **Sea-trial acceptance** | Contractual speed guarantees (e.g., 22 knots in calm water) demand corrections for trial-wave conditions. |
| **Weather routing** | Algorithms compute $R_A(\mu)$ for every heading and pick the lowest-fuel route. |
| **Course stability** | Added resistance couples with directional wave forces to determine involuntary yaw. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Havelock's Added Resistance Formula (Head Seas, Slender Ship)

### Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system

We place the origin at the still waterline, $x$-axis along the ship's longitudinal axis, positive forward. A regular deep-water incident wave of amplitude $\zeta_a$, frequency $\omega$, propagates in the negative-$x$ direction (head sea).

### Step 2 — Velocity potential of the incident wave

For a regular deep-water Airy wave, the velocity potential is:

$$\Phi_I(x,y,z,t) = \frac{g\zeta_a}{\omega} e^{kz}\sin\!\big(kx + \omega t\big)$$

The surface elevation is recovered from $\zeta = -\dfrac{1}{g}\dfrac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t}\bigg|_{z=0}$:

$$\zeta(x,t) = \zeta_a \cos(kx + \omega t)$$

### Step 3 — Ship-fixed frame transformation

A fixed point on the hull moves with speed $U$ in the negative-$x$ direction relative to the wave. We move to a **frame moving with the ship**, where the time-dependence becomes the *encounter* frequency $\omega_e$:

$$\omega_e = \omega + kU \quad (\text{head seas}, \cos\mu = -1)$$

In this frame, the incident potential becomes (suppressing $y,z$ for brevity):

$$\Phi_I' = \frac{g\zeta_a}{\omega_e} e^{kz}\sin(kx + \omega_e t)$$

### Step 4 — Slender-body assumption

For a slender ship (length $L \gg$ beam $B$), the section at any $x$ interacts with the local wave almost independently. The added resistance is obtained by **integrating the longitudinal pressure-drag component** over the immersed hull:

$$R_A = -\iint_{S_{\text{hull}}} p\, n_x \, dS \bigg|_{\text{time-averaged}}$$

### Step 5 — Pressure from linearized Bernoulli

$$p = -\rho\,\frac{\partial \Phi_{\text{total}}}{\partial t}\bigg|_{z=0}$$

where $\Phi_{\text{total}} = \Phi_I' + \Phi_S$ (incident + scattered). The time average of $\sin^2$ over a cycle gives a factor $\frac{1}{2}$, and the second-order interaction (because $R_A$ is a **second-order quantity** in wave steepness) yields a $zeta_a^2$ dependence:

$$R_A \propto \rho\, g\, \zeta_a^2 \, B^2 / L$$

### Step 6 — Final Havelock formula (slender ship, head seas)

After non-dimensionalization, the **classical Havelock (1922) result** is:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{R_A}{\rho\, g\, \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L} = \dfrac{4\pi\, K_H(\tau)}{(kL)^2}\;}$$

The factor $K_H(\tau)$ is a known function of the non-dimensional encounter parameter $\tau = \omega U / g$. For a prolate spheroid of major axis $L$ and minor axis $B$, $K_H$ has a closed-form expression involving Bessel functions; for general ship forms it is computed numerically or read from the **Havelock-form charts** in your course textbook.

### Step 7 — Identifying the resonance

Maximum added resistance occurs when the half-wavelength of the incident wave equals the ship length:

$$\frac{\lambda}{2} \approx L \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \frac{kL}{2} \approx \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \omega^2 \approx \frac{\pi g}{L}$$

This is the so-called **Havelock resonance**, and it is precisely the regime where slender-body theory is *least* accurate (the wave is no longer long compared to the hull). Practical corrections apply the **Boese (1970)** or **ITTC 1978** factors.

---

## 3.2 Gerritsma & Beukelman Empirical Method (1969, ITTC recommended)

### Step 1 — Non-dimensionalisation

Gerritsma and Beukelman proposed a model based on tests at MARIN (Wageningen), correlating $R_A$ with three parameters:
- $\tau = \omega_e U / g$
- $C_B$ (block coefficient)
- $H/L$ (relative wave height)

### Step 2 — Empirical formula

$$\boxed{\;R_A = \frac{\rho g B^2 H^2}{L}\, G(\tau, C_B)\;}$$

Equivalently, in terms of wave amplitude $\zeta_a = H/2$:

$$R_A = \frac{4 \rho g B^2 \zeta_a^2}{L}\, G(\tau, C_B)$$

The function $G(\tau, C_B)$ is read from a published chart (Figure 3.2 in course textbook). It peaks near $\tau \approx 0.4$–$0.7$ depending on $C_B$.

### Step 3 — Worked Derivation of Non-dimensional Encounter Speed

Starting from the encounter frequency:

$$\omega_e = \omega - kU\cos\mu$$

For head seas $\cos\mu = -1$:

$$\omega_e = \omega + kU$$

Substituting $k = \omega^2/g$:

$$\omega_e = \omega\left(1 + \frac{\omega U}{g}\right)$$

Dividing both sides by $g/U$:

$$\tau \equiv \frac{\omega_e U}{g} = \frac{\omega U}{g} + \left(\frac{\omega U}{g}\right)^2$$

Letting $\chi = \omega U / g$, we obtain a quadratic:

$$\tau = \chi + \chi^2$$

Solving for the design $\chi$ that gives a chosen $\tau$:

$$\chi = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4\tau}}{2}$$

This is the **inverse relation** used when a designer specifies a target wave period and wants the matching ship speed, or vice-versa.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Given:** A displacement vessel with $L_{pp} = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $C_B = 0.65$, operating in head seas with significant wave height $H_s = 4$ m and wave period $T_w = 9$ s. Ship speed $U = 8$ m/s (≈ 15.55 knots). Water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Step A — Compute wave frequency and number:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T_w} = \frac{2\pi}{9} = 0.6981 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$k = \frac{\omega^2}{g} = \frac{(0.6981)^2}{9.81} = 0.0497 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k} = \frac{2\pi}{0.0497} = 126.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step B — Encounter frequency (head seas, $\cos\mu = -1$):**

$$\omega_e = \omega + kU = 0.6981 + (0.0497)(8) = 0.6981 + 0.3976 = 1.0957 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step C — Non-dimensional Havelock parameter:**

$$\tau = \frac{\omega_e U}{g} = \frac{(1.0957)(8)}{9.81} = 0.8935$$

**Step D — Read Havelock coefficient $K_H(\tau=0.89)$ from textbook chart (slender ship, $C_B = 0.65$):**

$$K_H \approx 0.42$$

**Step E — Compute non-dimensional $R_A$:**

$$\frac{R_A}{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L} = \frac{4\pi K_H}{(kL)^2}$$

Compute denominator:

$$(kL)^2 = (0.0497 \times 150)^2 = (7.455)^2 = 55.58$$

Compute numerator and the ratio:

$$\frac{R_A}{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L} = \frac{4\pi \times 0.42}{55.58} = \frac{5.278}{55.58} = 0.0950$$

**Step F — Compute reference resistance term:**

$$\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L = (1025)(9.81)\left(\frac{4}{2}\right)^2 \frac{(22)^2}{150}$$

$$= 10054.25 \times 4 \times \frac{484}{150} = 10054.25 \times 12.907 = 129{,}776 \text{ N (unit reference)}$$

**Step G — Compute $R_A$:**

$$R_A = 0.0950 \times 129{,}776 = 12{,}329 \text{ N} \approx 12.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step H — Added power:**

$$P_{AE} = R_A \times U = 12{,}329 \times 8 = 98{,}632 \text{ W} \approx 98.6 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** At $\tau = 0.89$, we are **past the resonance peak** ($\tau_{\text{peak}} \approx 0.5$ for slender ships). The dimensionless ratio $0.095$ is reasonable. If the ship were in $\tau \approx 0.55$ (resonance) with the same wave height, the result would be 3–4× higher.

---

## 3.4 Validation Against Physical Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Why does $R_A$ peak at the resonance $\tau \approx 0.5$?**
>
> At resonance, the half-wavelength $\lambda/2 \approx L$. The bow rides the crest while the stern is in the trough, generating a **first-order vertical moment** that, when coupled with phase-lag heave, produces a **net horizontal force opposing motion**. This is also why the ship "pitches heavily" in such seas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
