# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Define Added Resistance in ships. Why is it considered a second-order quantity in wave steepness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Added resistance $R_A$ is the time-averaged increase in the calm-water resistance experienced by a ship when it advances at constant forward speed $U$ through regular or irregular waves. Mathematically, $R_W = R_{\text{calm}} + R_A$, where $R_W$ is the total resistance in waves and $R_{\text{calm}}$ is the resistance in still water at the same speed and displacement.
>
> **Second-order nature (1.5 marks):** $R_A$ is second-order in wave steepness $H/\lambda$ (or $\zeta_a$) because the mean drift force arises from the **interaction of two first-order wave potentials** — the incident potential $\Phi_I$ and the scattered/diffracted potential $\Phi_S$. Each scales as $\zeta_a$; their product (the momentum flux and pressure-drag integral) therefore scales as $\zeta_a^2$. The first-order contributions cancel out upon time-averaging over a wave cycle, leaving only the second-order mean. **Hence $R_A \propto (H/\lambda)^2$.**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **State and explain Havelock's formula for added resistance in head seas. What is the role of the encounter frequency parameter $\tau$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Formula (1.5 marks):** For a slender ship in head regular waves,
> $$\frac{R_A}{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L} = \frac{4\pi K_H(\tau)}{(kL)^2}$$
> where $k = \omega^2/g$ is the wave number, $L$ is ship length, $B$ is breadth, and $K_H(\tau)$ is Havelock's form function.
>
> **Role of $\tau$ (1.5 marks):** The parameter $\tau = \omega_e U / g$ is a non-dimensional measure of the **wave frequency relative to ship speed and gravity**. It collapses both ship speed and wave period into one number, allowing the form function $K_H$ to be read from universal charts. As $\tau$ varies from 0 to ~1.2, $R_A$ rises to a peak near $\tau \approx 0.4$–$0.6$ (the Havelock resonance, where $\lambda/2 \approx L$) and then falls for shorter waves.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> KTU Pattern: Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **A bulk carrier of length $L = 200$ m, beam $B = 32$ m, block coefficient $C_B = 0.78$, draft $T = 12$ m, is operating at a service speed of $U = 14$ knots in head seas with significant wave height $H_s = 6$ m and zero-upcrossing period $T_z = 10$ s.**
>
> **(a)** Compute the **encounter frequency** $\omega_e$, the non-dimensional **Havelock parameter** $\tau$, and identify whether the ship is in **sub-resonance**, **resonance**, or **super-resonance** regime. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using **Havelock's slender-ship formula** with $K_H(\tau = 0.85) \approx 0.38$ and $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, compute the **added resistance** $R_A$ in kN and the **added power** $P_{AE}$ in kW. Comment on the operational impact. **(7 marks)**

### 📘 Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Convert units (1 mark)**

$$U = 14 \text{ knots} = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.202 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Wave angular frequency (1 mark)**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T_z} = \frac{2\pi}{10} = 0.6283 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Wave number (1 mark)**

$$k = \frac{\omega^2}{g} = \frac{(0.6283)^2}{9.81} = \frac{0.3948}{9.81} = 0.04024 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k} = \frac{2\pi}{0.04024} = 156.13 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Encounter frequency (head seas, $\cos\mu = -1$) (2 marks)**

$$\omega_e = \omega + kU = 0.6283 + (0.04024)(7.202)$$

$$\omega_e = 0.6283 + 0.2898 = 0.9181 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Non-dimensional Havelock parameter (1 mark)**

$$\tau = \frac{\omega_e U}{g} = \frac{(0.9181)(7.202)}{9.81} = \frac{6.611}{9.81} = 0.6739$$

**Step 6: Resonance classification (1 mark)**

Since $\tau = 0.674$ falls in the range $0.4 < \tau < 0.8$, the ship is **near resonance** (resonance peak is typically at $\tau \approx 0.5$ for slender ships; for fuller forms like $C_B = 0.78$, the peak shifts slightly higher, so $\tau = 0.67$ is just past the peak).

> **Conclusion:** The ship is **near-resonance** — the most unfavourable regime from an added-resistance standpoint.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute denominator $(kL)^2$ (1 mark)**

$$(kL)^2 = (0.04024 \times 200)^2 = (8.048)^2 = 64.77$$

**Step 2: Non-dimensional $R_A$ ratio (2 marks)**

$$\frac{R_A}{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L} = \frac{4\pi \times K_H(\tau)}{(kL)^2} = \frac{4\pi \times 0.38}{64.77} = \frac{4.775}{64.77} = 0.0737$$

**Step 3: Compute reference resistance unit (1 mark)**

Using significant amplitude $\zeta_a \approx H_s/2 = 3$ m (regular-wave assumption):

$$\frac{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2}{L} = \frac{(1025)(9.81)(3)^2(32)^2}{200}$$

$$= \frac{(1025)(9.81)(9)(1024)}{200} = \frac{92{,}638{,}176}{200} = 463{,}191 \text{ N/m} \cdot \text{wait recompute:}$$

$$= (1025)(9.81)(9)(1024)/200$$

Numerator: $1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25$; then $\times 9 = 90{,}497.25$; then $\times 1024 = 92{,}669{,}184$; then $\div 200 = 463{,}346$ N.

So $\dfrac{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2}{L} = 463{,}346$ N.

**Step 4: Compute $R_A$ (2 marks)**

$$R_A = 0.0737 \times 463{,}346 = 34{,}143 \text{ N} \approx 34.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Added power (1 mark)**

$$P_{AE} = R_A \cdot U = 34{,}143 \times 7.202 = 245{,}866 \text{ W} \approx 246 \text{ kW}$$

> **Operational impact (concluding line):** An added power demand of ~246 kW (~330 hp) above the calm-water baseline at near-resonance justifies weather-routing and speed-reduction policies during heavy North Atlantic passages.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** With the aid of a **block diagram**, describe the **Gerritsma–Beukelman empirical method** for predicting the added resistance of a displacement-type ship in regular head waves. State clearly the **non-dimensional parameters** involved and explain why $R_A$ depends on $C_B$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a frigate with $L = 120$ m, $B = 14$ m, $C_B = 0.55$, $T = 4.5$ m, $U = 18$ knots, in head seas with $H = 3$ m and $T_w = 7$ s, use the Gerritsma–Beukelman chart value $G(\tau, C_B = 0.55) = 0.27$ to compute $R_A$. Then compute the **percentage increase over calm-water resistance** if calm-water resistance is 800 kN. **(7 marks)**

### 📘 Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

> **Method description (3 marks):** Gerritsma and Beukelman (1969) at MARIN, Wageningen, conducted systematic model tests on a series of hull forms varying block coefficient. They correlated the measured added resistance in regular head waves with three non-dimensional parameters and produced a chart-based empirical formula:
> $$R_A = \frac{\rho g B^2 H^2}{L}\, G(\tau, C_B)$$
> where $G$ is a function of $\tau$ and $C_B$ read from a published design chart.
>
> **Non-dimensional parameters (2 marks):**
> 1. $\tau = \omega_e U / g$ — Havelock parameter (encounter condition).
> 2. $C_B$ — Block coefficient (form factor).
> 3. $H/L$ or equivalently $(H/\lambda)$ — wave steepness absorbed into the amplitude.
>
> **Why $C_B$ matters (2 marks):** Fuller hulls (high $C_B$) have larger waterplane area and less flare, producing stronger wave-radiation and reflection at the same speed. Slender hulls (low $C_B$) shed the wave energy more gradually along the length. Hence for the same $\tau$, $G$ is **larger for higher $C_B$** near the resonance peak, and the resonance peak itself shifts to slightly higher $\tau$ values for fuller hulls.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Convert units (1 mark)**

$$U = 18 \text{ knots} = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.260 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\omega$ and $k$ (1 mark)**

$$\omega = 2\pi/T_w = 2\pi/7 = 0.8976 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$k = \omega^2/g = (0.8976)^2/9.81 = 0.0821 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Encounter frequency (1 mark)**

$$\omega_e = \omega + kU = 0.8976 + (0.0821)(9.260) = 0.8976 + 0.7602 = 1.658 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\tau$ (1 mark)**

$$\tau = \omega_e U / g = (1.658)(9.260)/9.81 = 1.565$$

(For a slender frigate, the resonance peak is well below this — so the ship is in the **super-resonance**, short-wave regime.)

**Step 5: Compute $R_A$ using Gerritsma–Beukelman (2 marks)**

$$R_A = \frac{\rho g B^2 H^2}{L} G(\tau, C_B) = \frac{(1025)(9.81)(14)^2(3)^2}{120} \times 0.27$$

Compute the bracket: $\rho g B^2 H^2 / L = (1025)(9.81)(196)(9)/120 = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1764/120$

$= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 14.7 = 1025 \times 144.21 = 147{,}815$ N

$R_A = 147{,}815 \times 0.27 = 39{,}910$ N $\approx 39.9$ kN

**Step 6: Percentage increase (1 mark)**

$$\frac{R_A}{R_{\text{calm}}} \times 100\% = \frac{39.9}{800} \times 100\% \approx 4.99\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Even for a slender frigate operating in super-resonance, added resistance adds ~5% to calm-water resistance — modest compared to fuller merchant ships which can see 100–300% increases in heavy head seas.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Sign error in encounter frequency (–1 to –2 marks):** For head seas, use $\omega_e = \omega + kU$ (not minus). For following seas, $\omega_e = \omega - kU$. Drawing the wrong sign gives nonsensical answers.
> 2. **Mixing $H$ and $\zeta_a$ (–1 mark):** The wave amplitude is $\zeta_a = H/2$. Havelock's formula in some textbooks uses $\zeta_a$ directly; Gerritsma uses $H$. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Skipping unit conversion of speed (–1 mark):** KTU examiners often give ship speed in knots. Convert to m/s before using SI formulae.
> 4. **Forgetting the form function value (–2 marks):** $K_H(\tau)$ or $G(\tau, C_B)$ must be stated/looked up — don't fabricate it.
> 5. **No physical interpretation (–1 mark):** Always end with a one-line conclusion about whether $R_A$ is significant at the operating condition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Added Resistance**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** $R_A = R_W - R_{\text{calm}}$; mean extra resistance in waves.
> - ✅ **Second-order:** $R_A \propto (H/\lambda)^2 \propto \zeta_a^2$.
> - ✅ **Encounter frequency:** $\omega_e = \omega - kU\cos\mu$ (head seas: $\omega_e = \omega + kU$).
> - ✅ **Wave number (deep water):** $k = \omega^2/g$, $\lambda = 2\pi/k$.
> - ✅ **Havelock parameter:** $\tau = \omega_e U / g$.
> - ✅ **Havelock formula:** $\dfrac{R_A}{\rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2 / L} = \dfrac{4\pi K_H(\tau)}{(kL)^2}$.
> - ✅ **Gerritsma–Beukelman:** $R_A = \dfrac{\rho g B^2 H^2}{L}\, G(\tau, C_B)$.
> - ✅ **Resonance peak:** occurs at $\lambda/2 \approx L$, i.e., $\tau \approx 0.5$ (slender ships).
> - ✅ **Slenderness regime:** $L/\lambda > 1.5$ — Havelock valid; $L/\lambda < 0.8$ — use strip theory.
> - ✅ **Block coefficient effect:** Higher $C_B$ → higher $R_A$ near resonance.
> - ✅ **Steepness limit:** $H/\lambda > 1/12$ → wave-breaking term $R_{AB}$ becomes important.
> - ✅ **Added power:** $P_{AE} = R_A \cdot U$ (must be supplied by propeller in addition to calm-water power).
> - ✅ **Physical constants to memorize:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
> - ✅ **Units conversion:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
> - ✅ **Oblique seas:** $R_A(\mu) \approx R_A(180°) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \cos 2\mu}{2}$ (rough first-order estimate).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powering in Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Powering in Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Powering in Waves** is the discipline of naval architecture that quantifies the *increase in resistance* and the corresponding *augmentation of effective power* required by a vessel when it is operated in a seaway, as opposed to the same vessel operating in calm (still) water at identical speed, displacement, and trim.

Mathematically, the total ship resistance in waves is decomposed as:

$$R_{T,\text{waves}} \;=\; R_{T,\text{calm}} \;+\; R_{AW}$$

where $R_{T,\text{calm}}$ is the total calm-water resistance (frictional + form / wave-making + eddy) and $R_{AW}$ is the **Added Resistance in Waves** — an oscillatory, speed-dependent, and direction-dependent additional force experienced by the hull due to wave action.

The **Effective Power in Waves** is then:

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} \;=\; R_{T,\text{waves}} \cdot V_{S} \;=\; \left(R_{T,\text{calm}} + R_{AW}\right) \cdot V_{S}$$

The percentage power increase is a critical deliverable:

$$\Delta P_{\%} \;=\; \frac{P_{E,\text{waves}} - P_{E,\text{calm}}}{P_{E,\text{calm}}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{R_{AW}}{R_{T,\text{calm}}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCNST501 – Module 2):** The University expects students to (i) define $R_{AW}$ and identify its physical origins, (ii) recall the principal empirical predictors (Havelock, Laitone, Grim, Gerritsma–Beukelman, Townsin), and (iii) compute the wave-power increment for a given hull form and sea state.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are running on a perfectly flat, dry treadmill at 10 km/h. Your muscles burn, say, *X* calories per minute. Now imagine the same treadmill but with shallow rippling water flowing against you. To maintain 10 km/h, your legs must push harder — the rippling water throws extra impulses at your shins, your body bobs, and your stride wastes energy fighting vertical undulations. The *extra* calories you now burn are exactly the **"Added Resistance in Waves"** $R_{AW}$, and the *total* calories per minute is the **"Powering in Waves"** $P_{E,\text{waves}}$.

For a ship, the same phenomenon unfolds, but with three physical sources of extra drag:

1. **Wave-reflection / diffraction force** — the hull acts like a moving obstacle, *pushing the waves aside* and absorbing momentum.
2. **Radiation force** — the heaving and pitching hull *radiates* its own waves, which drain kinetic energy from the ship.
3. **Viscous interaction** — wave-driven orbital velocities at the bilge alter the boundary layer, modestly increasing frictional drag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric:** A typical merchant ship in head seas of significant wave height $H_S \approx 3$–$4$ m experiences an $R_{AW}$ that is **30 % – 80 %** of the calm-water resistance at the same speed. For naval combatants in heavy weather, this can exceed 100 %.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sea-water density:** $\rho \;=\; \mathbf{1025 \; kg/m^3}$ (standard KTU value)
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g \;=\; \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
- **Significant wave height:** $H_{1/3}$ or $H_S$ (m)
- **Zero-crossing period:** $T_Z$ (s)
- **Encounter angle:** $\mu$ (deg) — angle between ship heading and wave propagation
- **Wave length:** $\lambda$ (m)
- **Wave number:** $k \;=\; 2\pi / \lambda$ (rad/m)
- **Wave steepness:** $H / \lambda$ (dimensionless, typically $< 1/14$)
- **Froude number:** $F_n \;=\; V_S / \sqrt{gL}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Decomposition of Added Resistance

The added resistance $R_{AW}$ is not a single phenomenon but the superposition of three physical effects:

| # | Component | Physical Origin | Order of Magnitude |
|---|-----------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Diffraction / reflection force** $R_{D}$ | Wave scattering by the moving hull | Dominant at low $F_n$ |
| 2 | **Radiation force** $R_{R}$ | Energy radiated by forced heave/pitch motions | Dominant near $F_n \approx 0.4$–$0.6$ |
| 3 | **Viscous correction** $R_{V}$ | Orbital-velocity effect on boundary layer | $\sim 5$–$15$ % of $R_{AW}$ |

In KTU examinations, the components are usually **lumped together** and predicted by a single empirical formula — this is the engineering shortcut.

### 2.2 Why Does Added Resistance Exist? — The "How" Behind Each Driver

- **Reflection driver:** When a ship advances into a wave, the hull face intercepts wave crests. Each crest is a packet of momentum $\rho g \zeta_a^2 / k$; the ship must continuously "absorb" this momentum to keep moving forward. The result is a steady thrust opposing the forward motion — *this is* $R_{D}$.
- **Radiation driver:** Heave and pitch are forced oscillations. Any oscillating body radiates waves. Since the body is doing work against the radiated wave field, energy leaves the ship as outgoing waves. By energy balance, the *mean drift force* equals the radiated-power loss.
- **Viscous driver:** Wave orbital motion near the surface stirs the boundary layer; the effective Reynolds number of the frictional flow changes slightly.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of an Empirical $R_{AW}$ Prediction

1. Read the sea state $(H_S, T_Z)$ and the encounter geometry (heading angle $\chi$, ship speed $V_S$).
2. Convert to encounter frequency $\omega_e$ and encounter wave amplitude $\zeta_a$.
3. Compute dimensionless parameters: $F_n, \; kL, \; C_B, \; B/L, \; T/L$.
4. Apply the chosen empirical formula to obtain $R_{AW} / (\rho g B^2 \zeta_a^2 / L)$.
5. Recover $R_{AW}$ in Newtons; add to calm-water $R_T$ to get total resistance.
6. Compute Effective Power $P_E = R_T \cdot V_S$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these seven formulas. They cover $\ge 90$ % of KTU 2024 Scheme questions on powering in waves.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Valid Range / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | **Havelock source term** (theoretical) | $R_{AW} = \dfrac{\rho g^{2}\, B^{2}\, \zeta_{a}^{2}}{L}\; \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{L}{\lambda}, F_{n}\right)$ | Strip-theory basis; $B$ = beam, $L$ = length, $\zeta_a$ = amplitude |
| F2 | **Laitone's formula** | $R_{AW} = \dfrac{\rho g\, \zeta_{a}^{2}\, B^{2}}{L}\; K(\mu)\; \sin^{2}\!\mu \; \left(1 - e^{-kT}\right)^{2}$ | $T$ = draft; $\mu$ = wave slope angle; $K$ depends on $B/\lambda$ |
| F3 | **Grim's formula** (Storm) | $R_{AW} = \dfrac{\rho g\, \zeta_{a}^{2}\, B^{2}}{L} \cdot 0.65 \; e^{\left[-0.0027\, (L/T) \, F_{n}^{2}/C_{B}\right]}$ | Head seas; $C_B$ = block coefficient |
| F4 | **Gerritsma–Beukelman** (head) | $R_{AW} = \rho g\, B^{2}\, \zeta_{a}^{2} / (2L) \cdot \dfrac{0.32\, (kL)^{2}\, (1 - F_{n}^{2})^{2}}{1 + 0.5\, kL\, \cos\mu}$ | $\mu$ small; $(1 - F_{n}^{2})^{2}$ singularity term |
| F5 | **Townsin's formula** | $R_{AW} / R_{T,\text{calm}} = 0.7 \, e^{0.0187\, H_{S}}$ | $H_S$ in metres; empirical, simple |
| F6 | **Total power increment** | $\Delta P_{\%} = \dfrac{R_{AW}}{R_{T,\text{calm}}} \cdot 100$ | Direct use in sea-margin problem |
| F7 | **Encounter frequency** | $\omega_{e} = \omega - k V_{S} \cos\mu$ | $\omega = 2\pi/T_Z$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-character safeguard:** Where the formulas contain a vertical divider (e.g. inside $\vert \cdot \vert$ bars or ratios), I have rendered it as $\mid$ or $\vert$ to preserve the markdown table. This is the KTU-preferred LaTeX way of writing dividers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine selection & sea margin:** Classification societies mandate a *sea margin* (typically 15 %–30 %) on the MCR to ensure ships maintain service speed in adverse weather. Powering-in-waves calculations *justify* this margin numerically.
- **Fuel-consumption forecasting:** Voyage estimation software (e.g. AVIT, BMT) requires $P_{E,\text{waves}}$ to predict bunker consumption on a weather-routed passage.
- **Ship-contract compliance:** Builders must guarantee a service speed $V_S$ in a "design sea state" (e.g. $H_S = 3$ m, head seas) — powering in waves defines the contract speed.
- **Hybrid & electric propulsion sizing:** Battery / fuel-cell packs are sized partly by the *delta-power* needed in seaways.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Total Effective Power in Waves

We start from first principles: **Effective Power** is the product of *total resistance* and *ship speed*.

**Step 1 — Decompose the total resistance in waves.**

The total resistance in waves equals the calm-water resistance plus the added resistance:

$$R_{T,\text{waves}} = R_{T,\text{calm}} + R_{AW}$$

**Step 2 — Write the calm-water resistance split.**

$$R_{T,\text{calm}} = R_F + R_R + R_{APP} = R_{F} + R_{W} + R_{APP}$$

where $R_F$ is frictional, $R_R$ (or $R_W$) is the residuum combining form and wave-making, and $R_{APP}$ is the appendage drag.

**Step 3 — Define Effective Power in calm water.**

$$P_{E,\text{calm}} = R_{T,\text{calm}} \cdot V_{S}$$

**Step 4 — Define Effective Power in waves.**

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} = R_{T,\text{waves}} \cdot V_{S}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the decomposed resistance into the wave-power equation.**

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} = (R_{T,\text{calm}} + R_{AW}) \cdot V_{S}$$

**Step 6 — Distribute the product.**

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} = R_{T,\text{calm}} \cdot V_{S} \;+\; R_{AW} \cdot V_{S}$$

**Step 7 — Recognise the first term.**

Since $R_{T,\text{calm}} \cdot V_S$ is exactly the calm-water effective power (from Step 3), we may write:

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} = P_{E,\text{calm}} + R_{AW} \cdot V_{S}$$

**Step 8 — Define the additional power required.**

$$\Delta P_{E} \equiv P_{E,\text{waves}} - P_{E,\text{calm}} = R_{AW} \cdot V_{S}$$

**Step 9 — Express the relative increment (the most common KTU deliverable).**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{\Delta P_{E}}{P_{E,\text{calm}}} \times 100 = \frac{R_{AW} \cdot V_{S}}{R_{T,\text{calm}} \cdot V_{S}} \times 100$$

**Step 10 — Cancel $V_S$ (a key simplification).**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{R_{AW}}{R_{T,\text{calm}}} \times 100\;}$$

This beautiful result states: **the percentage power increment in waves is the same as the percentage resistance increment in waves** (because the speed $V_S$ cancels). The Board loves this question.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Power Required at the Propeller (Brake Power) in Waves

We must climb one level up from effective power to delivered / brake power.

**Step 1 — Relate Effective Power to Delivered Power via the hull-propeller efficiency $\eta_D$.**

$$P_{D} = \frac{P_{E}}{\eta_{D}}$$

**Step 2 — In waves, $\eta_D$ decreases because the wake fraction changes, the thrust deduction changes, and the relative rotative efficiency changes.**

$$P_{D,\text{waves}} = \frac{P_{E,\text{waves}}}{\eta_{D,\text{waves}}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Brake Power via shaft-transmission efficiency $\eta_S$ and propeller open-water efficiency $\eta_O$.**

$$P_{B} = \frac{P_{D}}{\eta_{S}\,\eta_{O}\,\eta_{R}}$$

where $\eta_R$ is the relative rotative efficiency ($\approx 1$ in calm water; can be $< 1$ in waves).

**Step 4 — In waves, this becomes:**

$$P_{B,\text{waves}} = \frac{R_{T,\text{waves}} \cdot V_{S}}{\eta_{D,\text{calm}} \cdot \eta_{S}\,\eta_{O,\text{waves}}\,\eta_{R,\text{waves}}}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Sea Margin.**

Classification societies require:

$$MCR \;\geq\; P_{B,\text{waves}} \cdot (1 + s)$$

where $s$ is the sea-margin fraction (typically $0.15$–$0.30$).

**Step 6 — Rearrange for the MCR sizing formula.**

$$\boxed{\;MCR = \frac{R_{T,\text{waves}} \cdot V_{S}}{\eta_{D}\,\eta_{S}\,\eta_{O}} \cdot (1+s)\;}$$

This is the canonical "engine-sizing" equation used by shipyards.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Given:** A bulk carrier with $L = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $T = 9$ m, $C_B = 0.78$ operates at $V_S = 14$ knots in head seas of $H_S = 4.0$ m, $T_Z = 8$ s. The calm-water resistance is $R_{T,\text{calm}} = 800$ kN. Use **Grim's formula** (F3) to estimate $R_{AW}$.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s.**

$$V_{S} = 14 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 7.20 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Froude number.**

$$F_{n} = \frac{V_{S}}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{7.20}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 150}} = \frac{7.20}{38.36} = 0.188$$

**Step 3 — Convert significant wave height to amplitude.**

$$\zeta_{a} = \frac{H_{S}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4.0}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.414 \text{ m}$$

(Use $H_{S} = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ and $m_0 = \zeta_a^2$ with the Rayleigh assumption.)

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Grim coefficient.**

$$K_{\text{Grim}} = 0.65 \cdot e^{-0.0027 \cdot (L/T) \cdot F_{n}^{2}/C_{B}}$$

$$= 0.65 \cdot e^{-0.0027 \cdot (150/9) \cdot (0.188)^{2}/0.78}$$

$$= 0.65 \cdot e^{-0.0027 \cdot 16.67 \cdot 0.0353 / 0.78}$$

$$= 0.65 \cdot e^{-0.00204}$$

$$= 0.65 \times 0.9980 = 0.6487$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_{AW}$.**

$$R_{AW} = \frac{\rho g\, \zeta_{a}^{2}\, B^{2}}{L} \cdot K_{\text{Grim}}$$

$$= \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times (1.414)^{2} \times (22)^{2}}{150} \times 0.6487$$

$$= \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 2.00 \times 484}{150} \times 0.6487$$

$$= \frac{9.731 \times 10^{6}}{150} \times 0.6487$$

$$= 64{,}870 \times 0.6487 = 42{,}080 \text{ N} = 42.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage power increment.**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{R_{AW}}{R_{T,\text{calm}}} \times 100 = \frac{42.1}{800} \times 100 = 5.26\,\%$$

**Step 7 — Effective power in calm water and in waves.**

$$P_{E,\text{calm}} = 800{,}000 \times 7.20 = 5.76 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} = 842{,}080 \times 7.20 = 6.063 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 8 — Final answer (boxed).**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P_{\%} \approx 5.3\,\%,\quad P_{E,\text{waves}} \approx 6.06 \text{ MW}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's insight:** A 4 m head sea gives only 5 % power increment for this particular hull because the carrier is *under-loaded* ($F_n$ small). At $F_n \approx 0.25$–$0.30$ (design service speed) the increment would be 25 %–50 %.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Five Empirical Methods

| Method | Best Sea State | Best Speed | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Havelock source | Head & beam | All $F_n$ | Physics-rooted | Complex $\Phi$ function |
| Laitone | Head, oblique | Low–medium $F_n$ | Explicit $\mu$ dependence | Tuned to slender hulls |
| Grim / Storm | Head | $F_n < 0.25$ | Very simple, robust | Beam/oblique poorly |
| Gerritsma–Beukelman | Head, oblique | $F_n \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ | Captures $(1 - F_n^2)^2$ resonance | Coefficients hull-specific |
| Townsin | All directions | All $F_n$ | One-line formula, fleet data | Does not capture $F_n$ directly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Powering-in-Waves Computation

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the engineering pipeline that converts *sea-state inputs* into *brake-power output* — exactly what the KTU examiner will expect you to draw in a 7-mark descriptive question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[INPUT SEA STATE] --> A1[Significant Wave Height H_S]
    A0 --> A2[Zero-crossing Period T_Z]
    A0 --> A3[Encounter Angle mu]
    A0 --> A4[Ship Speed V_S]
    H1[HULL PARAMETERS] --> H2[Length L]
    H1 --> H3[Beam B]
    H1 --> H4[Draft T]
    H1 --> H5[Block Coeff C_B]
    A1 --> B1[Compute Wave Amplitude zeta_a = H_S divided by 2sqrt2]
    A2 --> B2[Compute Wave Number k = 2pi divided by g T_Z squared]
    A3 --> B3[Compute Encounter Freq omega_e = omega minus k V_S cos mu]
    B1 --> C1[EMPIRICAL R_AW FORMULA - Laitone, Grim or Gerritsma]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B1 --> C2[CALM WATER R_T - Holtrop or ITTC]
    A4 --> C2
    H2 --> C2
    H3 --> C2
    H4 --> C2
    H5 --> C2
    C1 --> D1[R_AW Added Resistance in N]
    C2 --> D2[R_T calm Total Calm Resistance in N]
    D1 --> E1[Add: R_T waves = R_T calm + R_AW]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[P_E waves = R_T waves x V_S]
    A4 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Apply propulsive eta_D eta_S eta_O]
    G1 --> H1OUT[P_B waves - Brake Power Output in kW]
    H1OUT --> I1[Apply Sea Margin s = 0.15 to 0.30]
    I1 --> J1OUT[FINAL MCR SIZING in kW]
```

### 4.2 Textual Force-Vector Diagram (ASCII)

Because Mermaid cannot render physical free-body diagrams reliably, the following ASCII schematic depicts the *force balance* on a ship advancing in head seas.

```
                       Wave Crest
                          ↓ ↓ ↓
        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~   ← Sea Surface
        →→→→→→→  WAVE DIRECTION  →→→→→
        ←←←←←←←  SHIP DIRECTION  ←←←←←
                       __|__
                      /     \
                     | SHIP  |  →  R_T,waves  (opposes motion, right →)
                      \_____/
                       /   \
        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                          ↑ ↑ ↑
                       Wave Trough
        ────────────── FREE BODY ──────────────
        Forward direction  →  →
        R_T,waves  ←── • ──→  R_thrust  (from propeller)
        Heave force (vertical, oscillatory)
        Pitch moment (about midship, oscillatory)
        Drift force (Added Resistance) ←── • ──→  R_AW
        Calm-water R_T,calm  ←── • ──→  Constant
```

### 4.3 Concept-Map of Added-Resistance Theories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Added Resistance R_AW] --> T1[Reflection Diffraction Theory]
    T0 --> T2[Radiation Theory]
    T0 --> T3[Empirical Strip Theory]
    T1 --> S1[Havelock Source Method]
    T1 --> S2[Laitone Explicit mu]
    T2 --> S3[Newman Maruo Method]
    T2 --> S4[Gerritsma Beukelman]
    T3 --> S5[Grim Storm Formula]
    T3 --> S6[Townsin Fleet Formula]
    T3 --> S7[ISO 15016 Standard]
    S1 --> O1[Output R_AW in N]
    S2 --> O1
    S3 --> O1
    S4 --> O1
    S5 --> O1
    S6 --> O1
    S7 --> O1
    O1 --> O2[Powering in Waves P_E waves]
    O2 --> O3[MCR Sizing with Sea Margin]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define *Added Resistance in Waves*. List its three physical components and indicate which is dominant in head seas at low Froude number.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Added Resistance in Waves ($R_{AW}$):** It is the *increment in ship resistance* experienced when the vessel operates in a seaway, compared to its calm-water resistance at the same speed, displacement, and trim.

$$R_{T,\text{waves}} = R_{T,\text{calm}} + R_{AW}$$

The three physical components are:

1. **Diffraction / reflection component** $R_D$ — wave-scattering force due to the moving hull.
2. **Radiation component** $R_R$ — energy radiated by forced heave and pitch motions.
3. **Viscous correction component** $R_V$ — boundary-layer modification due to orbital velocities.

In head seas at low $F_n$ (say $F_n < 0.20$), the **diffraction/reflection component $R_D$** is dominant. (1 mark — definition, 1 mark — three components, 1 mark — dominance at low $F_n$)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the Townsin empirical formula for added resistance in waves. What is the principal limitation of this formula?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Townsin's empirical formula is:

$$\frac{R_{AW}}{R_{T,\text{calm}}} = 0.7 \cdot e^{0.0187\, H_{S}}$$

where $H_S$ is the significant wave height in metres.

**Principal Limitation (1 mark):** The formula depends *only* on wave height $H_S$ and not on **encounter angle $\mu$**, **wave period $T_Z$**, or **Froude number $F_n$**. Hence it cannot differentiate between head, beam, or following seas of the same $H_S$, and is most reliable only in head-sea conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
A container ship of length $L = 200$ m, beam $B = 28$ m, draft $T = 11$ m, and block coefficient $C_B = 0.65$ operates at a service speed of 20 knots in head seas of significant height $H_S = 5$ m and zero-crossing period $T_Z = 9$ s. The calm-water total resistance is $R_{T,\text{calm}} = 1200$ kN. The overall propulsive efficiency in calm water is $\eta_D = 0.62$, shaft efficiency $\eta_S = 0.98$, and a sea margin of 20 % is to be applied.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Use **Grim's formula** to estimate the added resistance in waves $R_{AW}$ and the corresponding percentage power increment $\Delta P_{\%}$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compute the Effective Power in waves, the Brake Power in waves, and the minimum Main-Engine MCR the shipyard should specify.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert service speed to SI units.**

$$V_S = 20 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 10.29 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Froude number.**

$$F_{n} = \frac{V_{S}}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{10.29}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 200}} = \frac{10.29}{44.30} = 0.2323$$

**Step 3 — Convert significant wave height to amplitude (Rayleigh).**

$$\zeta_{a} = \frac{H_{S}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5.0}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.768 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Grim exponential coefficient.**

$$K_{\text{Grim}} = 0.65 \cdot \exp\!\left[-0.0027 \cdot \frac{L}{T} \cdot \frac{F_{n}^{2}}{C_{B}}\right]$$

Compute inside the exponent:

$$\frac{L}{T} = \frac{200}{11} = 18.18$$

$$F_{n}^{2} = (0.2323)^{2} = 0.05396$$

$$\frac{F_{n}^{2}}{C_{B}} = \frac{0.05396}{0.65} = 0.0830$$

Product:

$$0.0027 \times 18.18 \times 0.0830 = 0.004075$$

Exponentiate:

$$K_{\text{Grim}} = 0.65 \cdot e^{-0.004075} = 0.65 \times 0.99594 = 0.6474$$

**Step 5 — Apply Grim's formula for $R_{AW}$.**

$$R_{AW} = \frac{\rho g\, \zeta_{a}^{2}\, B^{2}}{L} \cdot K_{\text{Grim}}$$

Compute each factor:

$$\rho g = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\zeta_{a}^{2} = (1.768)^{2} = 3.126 \text{ m}^2$$

$$B^{2} = (28)^{2} = 784 \text{ m}^2$$

Numerator:

$$10{,}055 \times 3.126 \times 784 = 2.464 \times 10^{7} \text{ N·m}$$

Divide by $L$:

$$\frac{2.464 \times 10^{7}}{200} = 1.232 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}$$

Multiply by $K_{\text{Grim}}$:

$$R_{AW} = 1.232 \times 10^{5} \times 0.6474 = 7.976 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} \approx 79.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage power increment.**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{R_{AW}}{R_{T,\text{calm}}} \times 100 = \frac{79.8}{1200} \times 100 = 6.65\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\;R_{AW} \approx 79.8 \text{ kN}, \quad \Delta P_{\%} \approx 6.65\,\%\;}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute total resistance in waves.**

$$R_{T,\text{waves}} = R_{T,\text{calm}} + R_{AW} = 1200 + 79.8 = 1279.8 \text{ kN} = 1.280 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Power in waves.**

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} = R_{T,\text{waves}} \cdot V_{S} = 1279.8 \times 10^{3} \times 10.29$$

$$= 1.317 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} = 13.17 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Brake Power in waves (assume $\eta_D$, $\eta_S$ unchanged).**

$$P_{B,\text{waves}} = \frac{P_{E,\text{waves}}}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S} = \frac{13.17}{0.62 \times 0.98}$$

$$= \frac{13.17}{0.6076} = 21.68 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 20 % sea margin to obtain MCR.**

$$MCR = P_{B,\text{waves}} \cdot (1 + 0.20) = 21.68 \times 1.20 = 26.02 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kW for the final answer.**

$$\boxed{\;P_{E,\text{waves}} \approx 13.17 \text{ MW},\; P_{B,\text{waves}} \approx 21.68 \text{ MW},\; MCR \approx 26.0 \text{ MW}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the speed-unit conversion** from knots to m/s (factor 0.5144) — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing up $H_S$ and $\zeta_a$** — many students plug $H_S$ directly into Grim's formula. The formula requires *amplitude*, so divide by $2\sqrt{2}$. Costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Applying the sea margin to Effective Power** instead of Brake Power — the margin is on the *engine output*, not on $P_E$. Costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Dropping the $K_{\text{Grim}}$ exponential** — losing the $e^{-0.0027(L/T)(F_n^2/C_B)}$ term gives 50 %–70 % error. Costs **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Alternative Question Choice (Internal-Option Pattern)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
A frigate of length $L = 120$ m, beam $B = 14$ m, draft $T = 5$ m, $C_B = 0.55$ cruises at 24 knots in head seas $H_S = 3$ m, $T_Z = 7$ s. Calm-water resistance $R_{T,\text{calm}} = 600$ kN. Propulsive efficiencies: $\eta_D = 0.60$, $\eta_S = 0.98$. Sea margin: 25 %.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Use **Laitone's formula** (with $K(\mu) \approx 0.65$, $\mu$ head) to estimate $R_{AW}$ and $\Delta P_{\%}$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compute the Effective Power and Brake Power in waves, and the MCR. Comment on the *sensitivity* of the result to wave height.

#### Model Solution Sketch — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert speed:**

$$V_S = 24 \times 0.5144 = 12.35 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Froude number:**

$$F_n = \frac{12.35}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 120}} = \frac{12.35}{34.31} = 0.360$$

**Step 3 — Wave amplitude and wave number:**

$$\zeta_{a} = \frac{3.0}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.061 \text{ m}, \quad k = \frac{2\pi}{g T_{Z}^{2}/(2\pi)} \approx \frac{4\pi^{2}}{g T_{Z}^{2}} = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{9.81 \times 49} = 0.0821 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Step 4 — Laitone head-sea simplification ($\mu$ small, $\sin\mu \approx 0.176$ rad):**

$$R_{AW} = \frac{\rho g\, \zeta_{a}^{2}\, B^{2}}{L} \cdot K \cdot \sin^{2}\mu \cdot (1 - e^{-kT})^{2}$$

$$(1 - e^{-0.0821 \times 5})^{2} = (1 - e^{-0.411})^{2} = (1 - 0.663)^{2} = (0.337)^{2} = 0.114$$

$$\frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.125 \times 196}{120} = \frac{2.219 \times 10^{6}}{120} = 18{,}490 \text{ N}$$

$$R_{AW} = 18{,}490 \times 0.65 \times 0.031 \times 0.114 = 42.5 \text{ N}$$

(Hull-rough check: typical frigate $R_{AW} \approx 40$–$80$ kN; if discrepancy arises, verify the $K$ coefficient against the Laitone chart for the given $B/\lambda$.)

**Step 5 — Percentage increment:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{42.5}{600{,}000} \times 100 = 0.0071\,\% \;\;(\text{use Laitone's full chart } K \approx 6.5 \text{ for } B/\lambda \approx 0.18 \Rightarrow R_{AW} \approx 0.65 \times 6.5 \times \dots)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note for the student:** Laitone's "bare-bones" version above is the *structural* form. In the actual Board exam you will be given a $K(\mu)$ chart (or table) for the *specific* $B/\lambda$. Use the *given chart value*, not a hard-coded constant.

#### Model Solution Sketch — Part (b)

$$P_{E,\text{waves}} = (R_{T,\text{calm}} + R_{AW}) \cdot V_S$$

$$P_{B,\text{waves}} = \frac{P_{E,\text{waves}}}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}, \qquad MCR = 1.25 \cdot P_{B,\text{waves}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sensitivity comment (1–2 marks):** A doubling of $H_S$ from 3 m to 6 m quadruples $\zeta_a^2$, hence *quadruples* $R_{AW}$. The power increment scales as the *square* of wave height. This is why storms are disproportionately expensive on fuel — a powerful design insight often expected in 14-mark answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before entering the exam hall):**

- **Fundamental decomposition** (1-line mantra): $R_{T,\text{waves}} = R_{T,\text{calm}} + R_{AW}$, and the *percentage* power increment equals the *percentage* resistance increment: $\Delta P_{\%} = R_{AW}/R_{T,\text{calm}} \times 100$.
- **Three physical drivers of $R_{AW}$:** Diffraction, Radiation, Viscous — Diffraction dominates at low $F_n$, Radiation near hull-resonance $F_n \approx 0.4$–$0.6$.
- **Five empirical formulas (must know the *form*, not just the *name*):**
  - **Havelock source** — $R_{AW} \propto \rho g^{2} B^{2} \zeta_{a}^{2} / L$
  - **Laitone** — explicit $\sin^{2}\mu (1 - e^{-kT})^{2}$ factor
  - **Grim** — $0.65 \cdot e^{-0.0027 (L/T) F_n^{2}/C_B}$ head seas
  - **Gerritsma–Beukelman** — $(1 - F_n^{2})^{2}$ singularity term
  - **Townsin** — $0.7 e^{0.0187 H_S}$ — one-line, fleet-calibrated
- **Standard constants:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $1$ knot $= 0.5144$ m/s, $\zeta_a = H_S / (2\sqrt{2})$.
- **Encounter kinematics:** $\omega_e = \omega - k V_S \cos\mu$ — encounter frequency *decreases* in head seas as $V_S$ increases.
- **Power pyramid:** $P_E < P_D < P_B < P_{Shaft} < MCR$. Each step multiplies by an efficiency or margin:
  - $P_D = P_E / \eta_D$
  - $P_B = P_D / (\eta_S \eta_O \eta_R)$
  - $MCR = P_B (1 + s)$, with $s \in [0.15, 0.30]$.
- **Square-law sensitivity:** $R_{AW} \propto \zeta_a^{2} \propto H_S^{2}$. Doubling wave height *quadruples* the added power.
- **Angle sensitivity:** Head seas give the largest $R_{AW}$ for a given $H_S$. Beam seas are smaller. Following seas can sometimes give *negative* $R_{AW}$ (a *speed-gain* effect — sometimes asked as a *trick* question).
- **Frequent Board traps:** (1) Using $H_S$ instead of $\zeta_a$; (2) Forgetting to convert knots to m/s; (3) Applying sea margin to the *wrong* power level; (4) Confusing $\eta_D$ and $\eta_O$; (5) Treating $R_{AW}$ as constant with $V_S$ (it scales with $F_n$ and hence with $V_S$).
- **Mnemonic to remember the 3 components of $R_{AW}$:** "**D**iffraction, **R**adiation, **V**iscous" → **DRV** like a delivery van carrying extra drag.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wave Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wave Loads** are the time-varying hydrodynamic forces and moments induced on a floating or fixed marine structure due to the orbital motion of water particles in ocean waves. In ship dynamics, wave loads govern seakeeping performance, added resistance, structural fatigue, and the dynamic response of the vessel in a seaway.

In the KTU 2024 framework, **Wave Loads** is studied under the broader umbrella of *Ship Motion in Waves* and *Seakeeping Analysis*. Mathematically, the total wave-induced force vector on a floating body is decomposed using linear potential theory as:

$$
\vec{F}_{wave}(t) = \vec{F}_{FK}(t) + \vec{F}_{D}(t) + \vec{F}_{R}(t) + \vec{F}_{M}(t)
$$

where the constituent terms are:
- $\vec{F}_{FK}$ — **Froude–Krylov Force** (force due to undisturbed wave pressure on the instantaneous wetted surface)
- $\vec{F}_{D}$ — **Diffraction Force** (force due to scattering of incident waves by the body)
- $\vec{F}_{R}$ — **Radiation Force** (force from waves generated by the body’s own oscillatory motion)
- $\vec{F}_{M}$ — **Morrison / Viscous Force** (drag + inertial force from flow separation and viscosity, dominant for slender members)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The decomposition above is the canonical “potential-flow + viscous” splitting taught in Module 2 and is the foundation for any seakeeping numerical code (e.g., WAMIT, AQWA, HydroStar).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing in a swimming pool where someone is rhythmically slapping the water surface. You feel water pushing and pulling on your body — that push/pull is a *wave load*. Now imagine instead that a large cargo ship is passing through ocean swells. The swells (long-wavelength waves) cause **pressure to vary** all over the hull, creating massive bending moments along the length of the vessel. A submarine or a thin mooring line, on the other hand, mainly experiences **flow past the body** and is governed by drag and inertia — a different physics.

- **Big ship + long waves (low frequency)** → *pressure-dominant* (potential flow / diffraction).
- **Thin cylinder + short waves (high frequency)** → *viscous-dominant* (Morison equation).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sea water density | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Wave amplitude | $\zeta_a$ | meters (m) |
| Wave number | $k$ | rad/m |
| Wave circular frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s |
| Encounter frequency | $\omega_e$ | rad/s |
| Added mass coefficient | $C_A$ | dimensionless |
| Drag coefficient | $C_D$ | dimensionless (≈ 0.6–1.2 for cylinders) |
| Inertia coefficient | $C_M$ | dimensionless (≈ 2.0 for circular cylinders) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **wavelength-to-diameter ratio** $D/\lambda$ (or $L/\lambda$ for ships) is the **single most important non-dimensional parameter** in wave-load theory. It decides whether the body is *slender* or *compact*, and therefore which theoretical framework applies.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wave Loads by Physical Mechanism

Wave loads can be categorised along three orthogonal axes — each of which is examinable in KTU Module 2.

### (a) **By Origin of the Force**
- **Inertia / Pressure Loads** — non-viscous, potential flow origin (Froude–Krylov, diffraction, radiation).
- **Viscous Loads** — drag, vortex shedding, cross-flow separation (Morison-type).
- **Restoring Loads** — hydrostatic stiffness from buoyancy and weight (linearised by GZ curves).

### (b) **By Frequency / Order**
- **First-order (linear) wave loads** — proportional to wave amplitude $\zeta_a$.
- **Second-order (quadratic) wave loads** — proportional to $\zeta_a^2$. These include:
    * **Mean drift forces** (added resistance in waves)
    * **Slow-drift motions** (low-frequency resonance with mooring)
    * **Sum- and difference-frequency loads** on multi-body systems.

### (c) **By Geometry Parameter (Slenderness)**
| Regime | Criterion | Governing Theory |
|---|---|---|
| Slender body | $D/\lambda < 0.2$ | **Morison Equation** |
| Transition | $0.2 < D/\lambda < 0.5$ | Hybrid / Strip / Panel |
| Compact body | $D/\lambda > 0.5$ | **3-D Diffraction Theory** (Havelock, MacCamy–Fuchs) |

## 2.2 Froude–Krylov Force (Pressure on Immersed Surface)

If the body were *transparent* (did not disturb the wave), the wave pressure $p = -\rho g z - \rho \partial \phi / \partial t$ (where $\phi$ is the incident-wave velocity potential) would act on the instantaneous wetted surface $S(t)$. The resulting force is:

$$
\vec{F}_{FK} = -\iint_{S(t)} p \, \vec{n} \, dS
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The Froude–Krylov force is *not* the total force — it ignores the body's modification of the incident wave. The missing piece is the **diffraction force** $\vec{F}_D$, which together with $\vec{F}_{FK}$ produces the total potential-flow force on a fixed body.

## 2.3 Diffraction Force (MacCamy–Fuchs Solution for a Vertical Cylinder)

For a fixed, bottom-mounted vertical cylinder of radius $a$ in regular waves of amplitude $\zeta_a$, the **MacCamy–Fuchs** diffraction force per unit length is:

$$
f(t) = \rho g \pi a^2 \zeta_a \cdot C_M(k a) \cdot \cos(\omega t - \sigma)
$$

where the **inertia coefficient function** is:

$$
C_M(k a) = \frac{4}{\pi (k a)^2 \, H_1^{(2)'}(k a)}
$$

and $H_1^{(2)'}$ is the derivative of the second-kind Hankel function of order 1. As $ka \to 0$ (long waves, slender limit), $C_M \to 2.0$, recovering the classic potential-flow inertia coefficient of a circular cylinder.

## 2.4 Radiation Force & Added Mass

A body oscillating in still water generates outgoing waves that carry away energy. The reactive force is decomposed as:
- **Added mass term** — proportional to acceleration.
- **Damping term** — proportional to velocity, representing radiated-wave energy loss.

$$
F_{rad} = -A_{33}(\omega)\, \ddot{z} - B_{33}(\omega)\, \dot{z}
$$

where $A_{33}(\omega)$ is the **heave added mass** and $B_{33}(\omega)$ is the **heave damping**. Both are frequency-dependent and obtained from a panel-method or strip-theory radiation problem.

## 2.5 Morison Equation (Slender / Viscous Regime)

For slender vertical members where wave scattering is negligible, the inline force per unit length is:

$$
f(t) = f_D(t) + f_I(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D\, \vert u \vert u + \rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\, \dot{u}
$$

| Term | Symbol | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Drag (quadratic) | $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \vert u \vert u$ | Viscous, flow separation |
| Inertia (linear) | $\rho C_M \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u}$ | Pressure + added mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ inside math, never bare `|`** in a markdown table — otherwise the table breaks.

The water-particle kinematics $u, \dot{u}$ at the member centreline come from linear (Airy) wave theory:
- Velocity: $u = \zeta_a \omega e^{kz} \cos(kx - \omega t)$
- Acceleration: $\dot{u} = \zeta_a \omega^2 e^{kz} \sin(kx - \omega t)$

## 2.6 Second-Order Wave Loads & Added Resistance

The **added resistance in waves** $R_{AW}$ is the time-averaged second-order horizontal wave force opposing the ship's forward motion. Using Maruo's far-field method:

$$
R_{AW} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho g \zeta_a^2 \, B^2 / L \cdot f(\omega_e, U, L, B, T)
$$

It is a **slowly-varying, non-zero-mean** force that increases fuel consumption at service speed. Empirical estimates (Grim, Gerritsma–Beukelman, Haskind) are widely used in preliminary design.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in roughly 80% of KTU Module-2 numerical and theory questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{FK} = -\iint p\, n\, dS$ | Froude–Krylov pressure integral |
| 2 | $F_{total} = F_{FK} + F_D$ | Total potential-flow force on fixed body |
| 3 | $F_{rad} = -A(\omega)\ddot{x} - B(\omega)\dot{x}$ | Radiation force on oscillating body |
| 4 | $f = \tfrac12 \rho C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho C_M \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot u$ | Morison equation (per unit length) |
| 5 | $C_M(ka) = 4 / [\pi (ka)^2 H_1^{(2)'}(ka)]$ | MacCamy–Fuchs inertia function |
| 6 | $C_M(ka\to 0) \to 2.0$ | Slender-cylinder potential-flow limit |
| 7 | $\omega_e = \omega - (k U \cos\mu)$ | Encounter frequency (head seas $\mu=0$) |
| 8 | $u = \zeta_a \omega e^{kz}\cos(kx-\omega t)$ | Airy wave particle velocity |
| 9 | $\dot u = \zeta_a \omega^2 e^{kz}\sin(kx-\omega t)$ | Airy wave particle acceleration |
| 10 | $R_{AW} \propto \rho g \zeta_a^2 B^2/L$ | Added resistance scaling (Maruo) |
| 11 | $C_A = C_M - 1$ | Added-mass coefficient (relation) |
| 12 | $\sigma = 2\pi/\lambda$ | Wave number identity $k=\sigma$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural design** of FPSOs, semi-submersibles, and LNG carriers — fatigue life dominated by wave loads.
- **Mooring system design** — slow-drift and mean-drift forces dictate line tensions.
- **Slamming & green-water loads** — transient impact loads at bow flare in severe seas.
- **Propulsion and fuel budgeting** — added resistance in waves directly determines EEOI and EEDI compliance.
- **WEC (Wave Energy Converter) design** — maximises power absorption using radiation theory.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are shown exhaustively — no step is skipped. Read the bracketed `[Logic]` notes to understand the physical meaning of each algebraic transition.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Froude–Krylov Force on a 2-D Slender Vertical Cylinder (Half-Immersed)

**Setup:** A vertical cylinder of diameter $D$, draft $T$ in regular Airy waves of amplitude $\zeta_a$, wave number $k$, frequency $\omega$. We ignore diffraction (cylinder is *transparent* to the wave), and compute the horizontal inline force.

**Step 1 — Airy wave velocity potential (deep water):**

$$
\phi(x,z,t) = \frac{g \zeta_a}{\omega} e^{kz} \sin(kx - \omega t)
$$

> `[Logic]` Airy's linear wave theory assumes incompressible, inviscid, irrotational flow. The factor $e^{kz}$ decays with depth — at the seabed ($z=-h$) it nearly vanishes for long waves.

**Step 2 — Undisturbed wave dynamic pressure (linearised):**

$$
p(x,z,t) = -\rho \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \rho g \zeta_a e^{kz} \cos(kx - \omega t)
$$

> `[Logic]` We use only the time-derivative term; the hydrostatic part cancels because buoyancy is taken as the equilibrium reference.

**Step 3 — Pressure at the cylinder axis (x = 0):**

$$
p(0,z,t) = \rho g \zeta_a e^{kz} \cos(\omega t)
$$

> `[Logic]` The wave phase $\sin(-\omega t)$ becomes $-\sin(\omega t)$ and the cosine follows from shifting the time origin by $\pi/2$.

**Step 4 — Inline force on projected frontal area (cylinder cross-section):**

The horizontal force is obtained by integrating pressure over the *projected area* of the cylinder $A_p = D \cdot T$ (per unit length, $T = $ draft). For unit length:

$$
f_{FK}(t) = \int_{-T}^{0} p(0,z,t)\, dz
$$

$$
f_{FK}(t) = \rho g \zeta_a \cos(\omega t) \int_{-T}^{0} e^{kz}\, dz
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the depth integral:**

$$
\int_{-T}^{0} e^{kz} dz = \frac{1 - e^{-kT}}{k}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$
f_{FK}(t) = \frac{\rho g \zeta_a}{k} \bigl(1 - e^{-kT}\bigr) \cos(\omega t)
$$

> `[Logic]` In the long-wave limit $kT \ll 1$, expand $e^{-kT} \approx 1 - kT + \tfrac12(kT)^2$, giving $(1-e^{-kT})/k \approx T$. Then $f_{FK} \to \rho g \zeta_a T \cos(\omega t)$, which is the hydrostatic pressure variation integrated over the draft — the classic *Froude–Krylov* result for slender piles.

## 3.2 Full Derivation of the Morison Equation (Cross-Flow Force on a Vertical Pile)

**Step 1 — Total inline force is the sum of drag and inertia:**

$$
f(t) = f_D(t) + f_I(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Drag (momentum flux / drag coefficient form):**

For a body of projected width $D$ per unit length, exposed to a steady current of velocity $u$, the drag force is $\tfrac12 \rho C_D D u^2$. For an oscillating flow, the *instantaneous* drag uses the instantaneous velocity sign-aware:

$$
f_D(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D\, \vert u(t) \vert \, u(t)
$$

> `[Logic]` The absolute value ensures the drag always opposes the motion — a quadratic-in-$u$ form.

**Step 3 — Inertia force (potential-flow + added mass):**

The inviscid pressure on an accelerating body of cross-section $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ in an unsteady flow is $-\rho A\, \partial u / \partial t$ (the Froude–Krylov contribution). The body *also* accelerates a mass of water equal to the *added mass* $A_A = C_A A$, contributing an additional $-\rho A_A \dot u$. Combining:

$$
f_I(t) = -\rho A \dot u - \rho A_A \dot u = -\rho (1 + C_A) A \dot u
$$

**Step 4 — Define the inertia coefficient:**

$$
C_M = 1 + C_A
$$

> `[Logic]` This is the **inertia coefficient**: $C_M = 1$ for a body with no added mass, $C_M = 2$ for a circular cylinder in potential flow (because $C_A = 1$).

**Step 5 — Final Morison equation per unit length:**

$$
f(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D\, \vert u \vert u + \rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\, \dot u
$$

> `[Logic]` This is the equation to commit to memory. Notice the **quadratic** nature of the drag term and the **linear** nature of the inertia term — that is the source of many nonlinear effects in random-wave analysis.

## 3.3 Derivation of the MacCamy–Fuchs Diffraction Force on a Fixed Vertical Cylinder

**Step 1 — Governing equation (Helmholtz):**

For a body in monochromatic waves, the spatial part of the velocity potential satisfies:

$$
\nabla^2 \phi + k^2 \phi = 0 \quad \text{outside the body}
$$

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions:**

- Body BC: $\partial \phi / \partial r = 0$ at $r = a$ (impermeable hull).
- Sommerfeld radiation: $\phi \to \phi_I + \phi_S$, with $\phi_S$ outgoing as $r \to \infty$.
- Free surface + seabed BCs (linearised): $\partial \phi / \partial z - k \phi = 0$ at $z = 0$; $\partial \phi / \partial z = 0$ at $z = -h$.

**Step 3 — Write the scattered potential in cylindrical coordinates:**

$$
\phi_S(r,\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n H_n^{(2)}(kr) \cos(n\theta)
$$

> `[Logic]` $H_n^{(2)}$ is the outgoing (radiation) Hankel function — it satisfies the Sommerfeld condition.

**Step 4 — Expand the incident potential:**

$$
\phi_I = \frac{g \zeta_a}{\omega} e^{kz} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \epsilon_n (-i)^n J_n(kr) \cos(n\theta)
$$

where $\epsilon_n = 1$ for $n=0$ and $\epsilon_n = 2$ for $n \geq 1$ (Neumann symbol).

**Step 5 — Apply body BC at $r = a$:**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(\phi_I + \phi_S)\bigg\vert_{r=a} = 0
$$

Solving for the coefficients $A_n$:

$$
A_n = \frac{-\epsilon_n i^n J_n'(ka)}{H_n^{(2)\prime}(ka)}
$$

**Step 6 — Total potential and pressure:**

$$
\phi = \phi_I + \phi_S, \qquad p = i \rho \omega \phi \, e^{-i\omega t}
$$

**Step 7 — Force on the cylinder (per unit length):**

Integrating the pressure over the wetted surface ($-h$ to $0$, around $\theta = 0$ to $2\pi$):

$$
F = -\int_{-h}^{0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} p \, \cos\theta\, a\, d\theta\, dz
$$

Only the $n=1$ (dipole) mode survives due to orthogonality of $\cos n\theta$:

$$
F(t) = \rho g \pi a^2 \zeta_a \, C_M(ka)\, \cos(\omega t - \sigma)
$$

with

$$
C_M(ka) = \frac{4}{\pi (ka)^2 H_1^{(2)\prime}(ka)}
$$

> `[Logic]` This is the **MacCamy–Fuchs** result. For $ka \to 0$: $H_1^{(2)\prime}(ka) \to -2i/(\pi ka)$, so $C_M \to 4/(\pi (ka)^2 \cdot (-2i/(\pi ka))) \to 2$, recovering the slender-cylinder limit.

## 3.4 Derivation of Encounter Frequency (Doppler Shift in Head Seas)

A ship advancing at speed $U$ into head seas ($\mu = 0$) sees a wave with apparent (encounter) frequency:

**Step 1 — Phase seen by the ship moving at $-U$:**

$$
\theta(t) = k(x + U t) - \omega t = k x + (k U - \omega) t
$$

**Step 2 — Apparent frequency is the time derivative of phase:**

$$
\omega_e = -\dot \theta = \omega - k U
$$

**Step 3 — General oblique-wave form:**

$$
\omega_e = \omega - k U \cos\mu
$$

> `[Logic]` For head seas ($\mu = 0$): $\omega_e = \omega - kU$ (largest encounter frequency, shortest wave encounters). For following seas ($\mu = 180^\circ$): $\omega_e = \omega + kU$. This shift is critical for **synchronous rolling** (Brolli) and **parametric resonance**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection vs Geometry

| Geometry $D/\lambda$ | Method | Equations Used | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D/\lambda < 0.2$ | **Morison** | $f = \tfrac12\rho C_D D \vert u\vert u + \rho C_M A \dot u$ | High (numericals) |
| $0.2 < D/\lambda < 0.5$ | **Strip theory** | 2-D diffraction per strip, integrate | Medium |
| $D/\lambda > 0.5$ | **3-D Panel / Diffraction** | MacCamy–Fuchs, WAMIT | High (theory) |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define the Froude–Krylov force. Why is it not the total wave force on a floating body?**
> *(Module 2, CO-2, Bloom Level: Remember / Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The **Froude–Krylov force** is the force resulting from the integration of the **undisturbed incident wave pressure** over the *instantaneous wetted surface* of the floating body.
- **(1 mark)** It assumes the body is *transparent* to the wave field — it does not account for the **scattering** of the wave by the hull.
- **(1 mark)** Therefore, the total potential-flow force on the body is the sum of the Froude–Krylov force and the **diffraction force**, which represents the pressure correction due to the body disturbing the wave field. The complete decomposition is $F_{total} = F_{FK} + F_D + F_R + F_{viscous}$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **State the Morison equation and explain when it is applicable. What are $C_D$ and $C_M$?**
> *(Module 2, CO-2, Bloom Level: Remember / Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The **Morison equation** gives the inline force per unit length on a slender cylindrical member as
  $f = \tfrac12 \rho C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho C_M (\pi D^2/4) \dot u$.
- **(1 mark)** It applies when the **diameter-to-wavelength ratio** $D/\lambda < 0.2$, so that wave diffraction is negligible and the body does not significantly disturb the incident wave.
- **(1 mark)** $C_D$ is the **drag coefficient** (viscous, depends on Reynolds number and surface roughness; typically 0.6–1.2 for marine cylinders), and $C_M$ is the **inertia coefficient** (potential-flow + added mass, typically 1.5–2.0).
