Hence the negative sign. **Step 5 — Combined with Drift Compensation** A *more complete* LOS law used in modern autopilots: $$\psi_d = -\arctan\left(\frac{y_e + K_{\beta} \beta}{\Delta}\right)$$ where $K_{\beta}$ is a feed-forward drift compensation gain, typically $= 1$ for an unbiased estimate of $\beta$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — PID Heading Controller from Error Dynamics Starting from the Nomoto model: $$T \dot{\psi} + \psi = K \delta$$ **Step 1 — Define Heading Error** $$\psi_e = \psi - \psi_d \implies \dot{\psi}_e = \dot{\psi} \quad (\text{assuming } \psi_d = \text{constant or slowly varying})$$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $$T \dot{\psi}_e + \psi_e = K \delta - \psi_d$$ **Step 3 — Propose PID Control Law** $$\delta(t) = K_p \psi_e(t) + K_i \int_0^t \psi_e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \dot{\psi}_e(t)$$ **Step 4 — Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation** Substituting the PID law back: $$T \dot{\psi}_e + (1 + K K_p) \psi_e + K K_i \int \psi_e d\tau - K K_d \dot{\psi}_e = 0$$ Rearranging: $$(T + K K_d) \ddot{\psi}_e + (1 + K K_p) \dot{\psi}_e + K K_i \psi_e = 0$$ **Step 5 — Stability via Routh-Hurwitz** For asymptotic stability, all coefficients must be positive: $$\boxed{T + K K_d > 0, \quad 1 + K K_p > 0, \quad K K_i > 0}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam tip:** When asked to *design* a PID controller for ship path keeping, present the final gains $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$ as positive definite solutions to the Routh-Hurwitz inequalities. Examiners award marks specifically for this stability verification step. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Controller Selection for Different Ship Types | Ship Type | Dominant Plant Pole | Recommended Controller | Tuning Heuristic | |---|---|---|---| | Cargo / Container | Stable, low $T$ | PID | $K_p \approx \omega_n^2 T / K$, $K_d \approx (2\zeta\omega_n T - 1)/K$ | | Tanker (high block coefficient) | Marginally stable, large $T$ | LQG / $H_\infty$ | Add integral action to remove bias | | Warship (high maneuverability) | Stable, very low $T$ | PID + Notch filter | Notch at wave encounter frequency | | Submarine (submerged) | Highly damped | PID with low-pass | Account for depth-dependent mass | | AUV / USV (small) | Often unstable | MPC / Sliding Mode | Constraint on rudder rate $\dot{\delta}_{max}$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Path Keeping Control System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Path Reference Generator
Waypoints or Spline Trajectory] --> B[Reference Position
x desired and y desired] B --> C[Cross Track Error Calculator
y e equals y minus y desired] D[GPS Receiver
Position and Velocity] --> C C --> E[LOS Guidance Module
psi desired equals arctan negative y e over Delta] E --> F[Heading Error Calculator
psi e equals psi minus psi desired] G[Gyrocompass
Heading psi] --> F F --> H[PID Controller
delta c equals Kp times psi e plus Ki times integral plus Kd times derivative] H --> I[Rudder Servo System
Hydraulic Actuator] I --> J[Ship Dynamics Plant
Nomoto Model T psi dot plus psi equals K delta] J --> K[Environmental Disturbance Model
Wind plus Waves plus Current Forces] K --> J J --> D J --> G style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style C fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style E fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff style H fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style J fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff style K fill:#991b1b,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 State-Space Representation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Vector u] --> B[System Matrix A
3x3 State Space] C[Disturbance Vector w
Wind Wave Current] --> B B --> D[Output Vector y
x position y position psi heading] E[Feedback Gain Matrix K
Optimal LQR Pole Placement] --> B F[Controller State Estimator
Kalman Filter] --> E D --> F style A fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style B fill:#4338ca,color:#ffffff style C fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff style D fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style E fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff style F fill:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Hierarchical Control Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1[Level 1 Mission Planning] M1[Voyage Route
Origin to Destination] end subgraph L2[Level 2 Path Keeping Outer Loop] M2[LOS Guidance
Generates psi desired] end subgraph L3[Level 3 Heading Control Middle Loop] M3[PID or LQG
Generates Rudder Command delta c] end subgraph L4[Level 4 Servo Inner Loop] M4[Rudder Hydraulic Actuator
Achieves delta commanded] end subgraph L5[Level 5 Ship Dynamics Plant] M5[Six DOF Equations of Motion
Surge Sway Yaw] end L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 L5 -. Feedback Position and Heading .-> L2 L5 -. Feedback Heading Only .-> L3 L5 -. Feedback Rudder Angle .-> L4 style M1 fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff style M2 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style M3 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style M4 fill:#9f1239,color:#ffffff style M5 fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between Course Keeping and Track Keeping in ship dynamics. Which one is more critical during harbor approach and why?** **Model Answer:** **Course Keeping** is the control of the ship's heading angle $\psi$ to match a desired heading $\psi_d$, primarily using a gyrocompass as feedback. The controlled variable is angular; the error is $\psi_e = \psi - \psi_d$. **Track Keeping** (also called Path Keeping) is the control of the ship's position $(x, y)$ to follow a predefined geometric path. The controlled variable is the cross-track error $y_e$, computed using GPS position feedback combined with the desired path function $y_d(x)$. **Criticality during harbor approach:** Track/Path Keeping is *more critical* because harbors have narrow channels (typically 100–300 m wide for large vessels) where even a 20 m lateral deviation can cause grounding. A ship can have perfect course keeping (zero heading error) but still drift sideways due to wind and current, making track keeping indispensable in confined waters. > *Self-Assessment Marking Key:* 1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for justification. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define the Line-of-Sight (LOS) guidance law. Explain the role of the look-ahead distance $\Delta$.** **Model Answer:** The **Line-of-Sight (LOS) guidance law** computes the desired heading as the angle between the ship's forward direction and a line connecting the ship's current position to a virtual "look-ahead" point placed at distance $\Delta$ along the desired path. The mathematical statement is: $$\psi_d = -\arctan\left(\frac{y_e}{\Delta}\right)$$ **Role of Look-Ahead Distance $\Delta$:** - It is the design parameter that controls the *aggressiveness* of course corrections. - A **larger $\Delta$** produces smoother, gentler heading changes — suitable for large tankers with high inertia. - A **smaller $\Delta$** produces aggressive, sharp corrections — suitable for small USVs and AUVs. - A common rule-of-thumb is $\Delta = (2 \text{ to } 5) L_{pp}$, where $L_{pp}$ is the length between perpendiculars. > *Self-Assessment Marking Key:* 1.5 marks for the equation, 1.5 marks for the role of $\Delta$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** Derive the cross-track error dynamics $\dot{y}_e = U(\psi_e + \beta)$ for a surface ship moving along a straight-line reference path. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A container vessel of length $L_{pp} = 200$ m and service speed $U = 20$ knots is to follow a straight path through a channel. The cross-track error at $t = 0$ is $y_e(0) = 50$ m to the starboard side. The look-ahead distance is chosen as $\Delta = 2 L_{pp}$. Calculate the desired heading $\psi_d$ immediately after the autopilot engages. If the actual heading $\psi = 0°$ and the drift angle $\beta = -3°$, compute the cross-track error rate $\dot{y}_e$. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Cross-Track Error Dynamics **Step 1:** Kinematic equations in Earth-fixed frame: $$\dot{x}(t) = U \cos(\psi + \beta), \quad \dot{y}(t) = U \sin(\psi + \beta)$$ **Step 2:** Coordinate transformation to path-aligned frame using rotation by $-\psi_d$: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{x}_e &= -\dot{x}\sin\psi_d + \dot{y}\cos\psi_d \\ \dot{y}_e &= \dot{x}\cos\psi_d + \dot{y}\sin\psi_d \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting Step 1 into Step 2: $$\dot{y}_e = U\cos(\psi+\beta)\cos\psi_d + U\sin(\psi+\beta)\sin\psi_d$$ **Step 4:** Applying $\cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B = \cos(A-B)$: $$\dot{y}_e = U\cos[(\psi+\beta) - \psi_d] = U\cos(\psi_e + \beta)$$ **Step 5:** Linearizing for small $\psi_e + \beta$ (small angle approximation valid for $|y_e| < 0.5 L_{pp}$): $$\dot{y}_e \approx U(\psi_e + \beta)$$ **Assumptions:** - Calm water maneuvering (no wave-induced drift force). - Constant forward speed $U$ (surge dynamics neglected). - Small heading error and drift angle (linear regime). - Rigid body, no roll-pitch coupling considered. #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation **Given Data:** - $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $U = 20$ knots. - $y_e(0) = 50$ m (starboard). - $\Delta = 2 L_{pp} = 400$ m. - $\psi = 0°$, $\beta = -3°$. **Step 1 — Convert speed to SI:** $$U = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply LOS Law:** $$\psi_d = -\arctan\left(\frac{y_e}{\Delta}\right) = -\arctan\left(\frac{50}{400}\right) = -\arctan(0.125)$$ $$\psi_d = -7.125°$$ **Interpretation:** The ship must turn $7.125°$ to port (negative sign) to compensate for being 50 m to starboard of the path. **Step 3 — Compute Heading Error:** $$\psi_e = \psi - \psi_d = 0 - (-7.125°) = +7.125° = 0.1244 \text{ rad}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Cross-Track Error Dynamics:** $$\dot{y}_e = U(\psi_e + \beta)$$ Converting $\beta = -3° = -0.0524$ rad: $$\dot{y}_e = 10.288 \times (0.1244 - 0.0524)$$ $$\dot{y}_e = 10.288 \times 0.0720$$ $$\boxed{\dot{y}_e = +0.741 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Interpretation:** The ship is *still moving further away* from the desired path at 0.741 m/s. The autopilot must immediately execute the $\psi_d = -7.125°$ heading change to drive $\dot{y}_e \to 0$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a three-layer ship path keeping autopilot. Identify the function and output of each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tanker has the Nomoto parameters $T = 80$ s and $K = 0.15$ s⁻¹. A PID heading controller is to be designed with desired closed-loop natural frequency $\omega_n = 0.1$ rad/s and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$. Determine $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$. Verify the stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — Three-Layer Architecture *(Draw the block diagram showing the three layers as in Section 4.3 above.)* **Layer 1 — Guidance (Outer Loop):** - **Input:** Reference path $(x_d, y_d)$ from waypoints or trajectory planner. - **Function:** Computes the cross-track error $y_e$ and outputs the desired heading $\psi_d$ using the LOS law. - **Sensor input:** GPS position. **Layer 2 — Heading Control (Middle Loop):** - **Input:** Heading error $\psi_e = \psi - \psi_d$. - **Function:** Computes the rudder command $\delta_c$ to drive $\psi_e \to 0$, typically using a PID or LQG controller. - **Sensor input:** Gyrocompass. **Layer 3 — Servo and Ship Dynamics (Inner Loop):** - **Input:** Commanded rudder angle $\delta_c$. - **Function:** Hydraulic servo drives the actual rudder to $\delta \approx \delta_c$; the Nomoto model produces heading rate $\dot{\psi}$ and sway velocity $v$. - **Output:** New ship position and heading — closes the feedback loop. #### Part (b) — PID Design **Given:** $T = 80$ s, $K = 0.15$ s⁻¹, $\omega_n = 0.1$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.7$. **Step 1 — Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation from §3.3:** $$(T + K K_d) s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0$$ Dividing by $(T + K K_d)$ to match standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$: $$s^2 + \frac{(1 + K K_p)}{(T + K K_d)} s + \frac{K K_i}{(T + K K_d)} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Match Coefficients:** $$\frac{K K_i}{T + K K_d} = \omega_n^2 = 0.01 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K K_i = 0.01(T + K K_d)$$ $$\frac{1 + K K_p}{T + K K_d} = 2\zeta\omega_n = 0.14$$ **Step 3 — Choose $K_d$ via Trial:** For a tanker, typical $K_d$ is moderate. Let us **iterate**: *Iteration 1:* Assume $K_d = 200$ s. $$T + K K_d = 80 + 0.15 \times 200 = 80 + 30 = 110$$ $$K K_p = 0.14 \times 110 - 1 = 15.4 - 1 = 14.4$$ $$K_p = 14.4 / 0.15 = 96$$ $$K K_i = 0.01 \times 110 = 1.1 \implies K_i = 1.1 / 0.15 = 7.33$$ **Step 4 — Final Gains:** $$\boxed{K_p = 96, \quad K_i = 7.33 \text{ s}^{-1}, \quad K_d = 200 \text{ s}}$$ **Step 5 — Routh-Hurwitz Verification:** For $(110) s^2 + (15.4) s + (1.1) = 0$: All coefficients positive: $110 > 0$, $15.4 > 0$, $1.1 > 0$. ✓ The Routh array: | $s^2$ | $110$ | $1.1$ | |---|---|---| | $s^1$ | $15.4$ | $0$ | | $s^0$ | $1.1$ | | No sign changes in first column → **System is asymptotically stable.** ✓ **Physical check:** Closed-loop time constant $\tau \approx 1/(\zeta\omega_n) = 1/0.07 \approx 14.3$ s, which is reasonable for a large tanker. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Sign convention errors in LOS law:** Students often forget the negative sign in $\psi_d = -\arctan(y_e/\Delta)$, leading to wrong-direction rudder commands. **−2 marks.** > 2. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing knots with m/s without conversion (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s). Always convert before substituting. **−1 mark.** > 3. **Forgetting Routh-Hurwitz check:** Even if the gains are correct numerically, omitting stability verification costs **−1.5 marks.** > 4. **Incorrect drift angle sign convention:** ITTC standard is $\beta$ *positive to starboard*; some textbooks use the opposite. **Declare your convention explicitly.** > 5. **Drawing block diagram without labels:** An unlabeled diagram yields *zero* marks for that part. Always label signals ($\psi_d$, $\psi_e$, $\delta_c$, $\delta$) and blocks (PID, Nomoto, Sensors). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Path Keeping (Module 3)** - ✅ **Path Keeping = Course Keeping + Track Keeping.** Always remember the difference. - ✅ **Nomoto model $T\dot{\psi} + \psi = K\delta$** is the foundation. Memorize it. - ✅ **LOS law: $\psi_d = -\arctan(y_e / \Delta)$** with $\Delta = (2\text{–}5) L_{pp}$. - ✅ **Cross-track error dynamics: $\dot{y}_e = U(\psi_e + \beta)$** — derived from kinematic rotation. - ✅ **PID control law:** $\delta_c = K_p \psi_e + K_i \int\psi_e dt + K_d \dot{\psi}_e$. - ✅ **Stability condition (Routh-Hurwitz):** $T + KK_d > 0$, $1 + KK_p > 0$, $KK_i > 0$. - ✅ **Drift angle** $\beta$ is *the* bridge between heading and track; never ignore in tankers. - ✅ **Look-ahead $\Delta$** tunes *aggressiveness*: small $\Delta$ = aggressive, large $\Delta$ = smooth. - ✅ **Layered architecture:** Guidance → Heading Control → Servo/Plant. Always draw it. - ✅ **Environmental disturbances** (wind, waves, current) enter as additive forcing terms in the Nomoto model and must be included for realistic path keeping design. - ✅ **Modern extensions:** LQG, $H_\infty$, MPC, sliding mode — know their *relative merits* even if you cannot derive them in full. - ✅ **Standard KTU values:** Service speed 15–25 knots, $T$ range 30–150 s, $K$ range 0.05–0.3 s⁻¹. - ✅ **Always convert knots to m/s** (factor 0.5144) before any numerical computation.

topics.title: Various Types of Motion Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Various Types of Motion Stability # ⚓ Module 3 — Various Types of Motion Stability ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Motion Stability** of a ship is the inherent tendency of the vessel to return to, or maintain, a desired state of equilibrium (position and heading) when subjected to external disturbing forces such as waves, wind, currents, or rudder deflections. In ship dynamics, motion stability is classified according to the **six rigid-body degrees of freedom (6-DOF)** and the **nature of restoring action** (static vs. dynamic, longitudinal vs. transverse). A ship floating in calm water has **six independent modes of motion** as per classical rigid-body mechanics: | # | Motion | Axis | Type | Restoring Force | |---|--------|------|------|-----------------| | 1 | **Surge** | x-axis | Translational | Hydrodynamic (drag) | | 2 | **Sway** | y-axis | Translational | Hydrodynamic (lateral) | | 3 | **Heave** | z-axis | Translational | Buoyancy (gravity) | | 4 | **Roll** | x-axis | Rotational | Gravity (righting arm) | | 5 | **Pitch** | y-axis | Rotational | Gravity (longitudinal metacentric) | | 6 | **Yaw** | z-axis | Rotational | Hydrodynamic (course stability) | ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Pencil" Intuition Imagine balancing a **long pencil vertically on your fingertip**. Three things can happen: - It falls sideways and lies flat → **unstable**. - It stands perfectly still → **neutral stability**. - It wobbles, oscillates, and gradually settles upright → **dynamically stable**. A ship behaves exactly like this, except the "balance point" is the **center of buoyancy (B)** relative to the **center of gravity (G)**. The position of **G** above or below the **metacenter (M)** determines whether the ship is: - **Statically stable** ($GM > 0$) - **Neutrally stable** ($GM = 0$) - **Statically unstable** ($GM < 0$) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically addresses how each of the six motions possesses its own stability criterion, restoring mechanism, and natural period. Each is treated as a coupled or uncoupled single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) oscillator in linear seakeeping theory. ### ⚙️ Classification of Motion Stability The KTU 2024 Scheme groups ship motion stability into the following **five fundamental categories**: 1. **Transverse Stability (Rolling Stability)** — Rotation about the longitudinal axis. 2. **Longitudinal Stability (Pitching Stability)** — Rotation about the transverse axis. 3. **Heaving Stability** — Vertical translation; purely a translational oscillation. 4. **Course / Directional Stability (Yaw Stability)** — Rotation about the vertical axis. 5. **Combined / Coupled Stability** — When two or more motions interact (e.g., roll–pitch coupling, roll–yaw coupling). Standard KTU metrics used: > **GM (metacentric height)**: typically **0.15 m to 1.5 m** for cargo ships; **≥ 0.15 m** for cargo as per IMO. > > **Natural roll period $T_\phi$**: typically **8 s to 25 s** depending on ship size. > > **IMO Stability Criteria**: Area under GZ curve between **30° and 40°** ≥ **0.055 m·rad**; area between **0° and 40°** ≥ **0.090 m·rad**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Transverse Stability (Roll Stability) The **rolling motion** is the most critical oscillatory mode because it is excited by wave moments and produces large angular accelerations at the deck edge. ### Step-by-Step Logic - A small heeling angle $\phi$ creates a horizontal shift between **B** (center of buoyancy) and **G**. - This shift produces a **righting lever** $\overline{GZ}$. - The product $\Delta \cdot \overline{GZ}$ gives the **restoring moment**. - The ship rolls back toward $\phi = 0$ if $GM > 0$. - Damping is provided by **wave radiation**, **eddy-making**, and **bilge keel friction**. ### Equation of Roll Motion (SDOF) $$ I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} + B_{44} \dot{\phi} + C_{44} \phi = F_\phi(t) $$ Where: - $I_{xx}$ = moment of inertia about the longitudinal axis through **G** - $B_{44}$ = damping coefficient (N·m·s) - $C_{44} = \Delta \cdot GM$ = restoring stiffness - $F_\phi(t)$ = external wave moment ## 2.2 Longitudinal Stability (Pitch Stability) Pitching occurs due to wave-induced variation of buoyancy along the ship's length. The restoring action is governed by the **longitudinal metacentric height $GM_L$**. ### Step-by-Step Logic - Trim angle $\theta$ shifts B longitudinally. - A righting couple $\Delta \cdot \overline{GZ_L}$ restores the ship. - Pitching is much **slower** than rolling (larger moment of inertia). - Pitch is generally treated as a **decoupled motion** for preliminary analysis. ### Equation of Pitch Motion $$ I_{yy} \ddot{\theta} + B_{55} \dot{\theta} + \Delta \cdot GM_L \cdot \theta = F_\theta(t) $$ ## 2.3 Heaving Stability Pure vertical translation. The restoring force arises solely from the **difference between buoyancy and weight** when the ship is displaced vertically by $\zeta$. ### Equation of Heave Motion $$ (m + a_{33}) \ddot{\zeta} + b_{33} \dot{\zeta} + \rho g A_w \zeta = F_z(t) $$ Where: - $a_{33}$ = hydrodynamic added mass in heave - $b_{33}$ = damping coefficient - $A_w$ = waterplane area - $\rho g A_w$ = hydrostatic restoring stiffness ## 2.4 Course / Directional Stability (Yaw) Yaw stability describes a ship's tendency to move along a **straight path** without constant rudder correction. ### Criterion (Nomoto's First-Order Equation) $$ T \ddot{\psi} + \dot{\psi} = K \delta $$ - $T$ = **follow-up index** (time constant); large $T$ → **unstable course**. - $K$ = **course-keeping gain**. - A vessel is **course-stable** if **$T > 0$** and **$T \gg K$**. ## 2.5 Coupled Motion Stability In steep oblique waves (e.g., beam seas at 30°–60°), motions couple: - **Roll–Pitch** coupling → parametric excitation (Mathieu instability). - **Roll–Yaw** coupling → broaching and surf-riding. - **Heave–Pitch** coupling → synchronous slamming. ## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Motion | Stability Criterion | Natural Period | Critical Parameter | |---|--------|---------------------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | Roll | $GM_T > 0$ | $T_\phi = \dfrac{2\pi k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM_T}}$ | $GM_T$, beam $B$ | | 2 | Pitch | $GM_L > 0$ | $T_\theta = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{yy}}{\Delta \cdot GM_L}}$ | $GM_L$, length $L$ | | 3 | Heave | $\rho g A_w > 0$ | $T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m + a_{33}}{\rho g A_w}}$ | $A_w$ | | 4 | Yaw | $T > 0$, $K > 0$ | $T_\psi = T$ | $T$, $K$ | | 5 | Surge | $X_v < 0$ | $T_x = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m + a_{11}}{X_u}}$ | $X_u$ | | 6 | Sway | $Y_v < 0$ | $T_y = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m + a_{22}}{Y_v}}$ | $Y_v$ | > [!NOTE] > The most commonly tested formula in KTU ESE for Module 3 is the **natural roll period** $T_\phi$. Memorize it with the radius of gyration $k$ (typically $k \approx 0.35B$ for a rectangular ship). ### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |-------|-------------| | **Naval Architecture** | Hull-form optimization for stability in beam seas | | **Warship Design** | Roll-reduction via anti-roll tanks, fins, bilge keels | | **Submarine Engineering** | Coupled heave–pitch in submerged conditions | | **Yacht Design** | Comfort criteria based on roll natural period vs. wave period | | **Autonomous Ships** | Course-stability indices (T, K) for waypoint tracking | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Natural Roll Period $T_\phi$ We derive the undamped free-roll natural period from the principle of angular momentum about the roll axis. ### Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition At small heel angle $\phi$, the ship is in equilibrium when the **center of buoyancy B** is directly below **G**. The horizontal shift of B relative to G is the **righting lever**: $$ \overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \sin\phi \approx GM \cdot \phi \quad \text{(for small } \phi \text{)} $$ > *Reason:* For small angles, $\sin\phi \approx \phi$ in radians; the lever arm is the perpendicular distance between the line of action of W and B. ### Step 2 — Restoring Moment The righting moment is the product of displacement weight $\Delta$ and the lever $\overline{GZ}$: $$ M_R = \Delta \cdot \overline{GZ} = \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \phi $$ > *Reason:* This is a stable restoring couple because it acts in the direction opposite to $\phi$. ### Step 3 — Equation of Motion (Undamped) Applying Newton's second law for rotation about the longitudinal axis through G: $$ I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} = -M_R = -\Delta \cdot GM \cdot \phi $$ Rearranging: $$ I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} + \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \phi = 0 $$ ### Step 4 — Identify Angular Natural Frequency This is the canonical **simple harmonic oscillator** form $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$ with: $$ \omega_n^2 = \frac{\Delta \cdot GM}{I_{xx}} $$ Hence: $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta \cdot GM}{I_{xx}}} $$ ### Step 5 — Express Period Since $\omega_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_\phi}$: $$ T_\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{\Delta \cdot GM}} $$ ### Step 6 — Introduce Radius of Gyration By definition $I_{xx} = \dfrac{\Delta}{g} k^2$, where $k$ is the transverse radius of gyration. Substituting: $$ T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(\Delta/g)\,k^2}{\Delta \cdot GM}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}} $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_\phi = \frac{2\pi k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}}\,} $$ > *Reason:* The weight $\Delta$ cancels elegantly, leaving a pure geometric/hydrostatic expression. ### Step 7 — Numerical Verification For a typical cargo ship: $k = 0.35B$, $B = 20$ m, $GM = 0.6$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s². $$ k = 0.35 \times 20 = 7.0 \text{ m} $$ $$ T_\phi = \frac{2\pi \times 7.0}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.6}} = \frac{43.98}{2.426} \approx 18.13 \text{ s} $$ This falls within the realistic 8–25 s range. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Natural Pitch Period $T_\theta$ By analogy, replacing roll parameters with pitch parameters: $$ I_{yy} \ddot{\theta} + \Delta \cdot GM_L \cdot \theta = 0 $$ $$ T_\theta = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{yy}}{\Delta \cdot GM_L}} $$ With longitudinal radius of gyration $k_L = 0.25L$ for typical cargo ships: $$ T_\theta = \frac{2\pi k_L}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM_L}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Natural Heave Period $T_z$ For pure heave in calm water, the hydrostatic restoring force equals $\rho g A_w \zeta$. The equation of motion: $$ (m + a_{33}) \ddot{\zeta} + \rho g A_w \zeta = 0 $$ The natural frequency: $$ \omega_z = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g A_w}{m + a_{33}}} $$ Hence: $$ \boxed{\,T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m + a_{33}}{\rho g A_w}}\,} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Yaw Equation (Nomoto's First-Order Model) The yaw equation is not derived from a hydrostatic restoring moment (yaw has no hydrostatic righting arm). Instead, it arises from **hydrodynamic sway–yaw coupling**. Nomoto assumed a linear relationship between rudder angle $\delta$ and the resulting steady-state yaw rate $r$: $$ r_{ss} = K \delta $$ The dynamic response follows: $$ T \dot{r} + r = K \delta $$ Rearranging for $\psi$ (since $r = \dot{\psi}$): $$ \boxed{\,T \ddot{\psi} + \dot{\psi} = K \delta\,} $$ > *Reason:* $T$ embodies the **course-following tendency**. A larger $T$ means slower response but **better course stability**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Roll | Pitch | Heave | Yaw | |----------|------|-------|-------|-----| | Restoring origin | Gravity ($GM$) | Gravity ($GM_L$) | Buoyancy ($A_w$) | Hydrodynamic | | Typical period | 8–25 s | 4–10 s | 4–9 s | 30–300 s | | Damping source | Bilge keel, eddy | Hull form | Wave radiation | Hull form | | IMO criterion | Area under GZ | None mandatory | Slamming limit | IMO MSC.137 | | Resonance danger | Beam seas | Head/following | Wave encounter | Course changes | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Six Degrees of Freedom — Vessel-Centric Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB shipNode["SHIP BODY"] surgeAct["SURGE
x axis translation"] swayAct["SWAY
y axis translation"] heaveAct["HEAVE
z axis translation"] rollAct["ROLL
rotation about x"] pitchAct["PITCH
rotation about y"] yawAct["YAW
rotation about z"] inputA["WAVE FORCES"] inputB["WIND FORCES"] inputC["RUDDER FORCES"] outStab["STABILITY RESPONSE"] shipNode --> surgeAct shipNode --> swayAct shipNode --> heaveAct shipNode --> rollAct shipNode --> pitchAct shipNode --> yawAct inputA --> shipNode inputB --> shipNode inputC --> shipNode shipNode --> outStab ``` ## 4.2 Stability Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR stabNode["MOTION STABILITY"] transS["TRANSVERSE
Roll Stability"] longS["LONGITUDINAL
Pitch Stability"] vertS["VERTICAL
Heave Stability"] dirS["DIRECTIONAL
Yaw Stability"] coupS["COUPLED
Roll Pitch Yaw"] criteria1["GM greater than 0"] criteria2["GM_L greater than 0"] criteria3["rho g A_w greater than 0"] criteria4["T greater than 0"] criteria5["Coupling coefficients"] output["SAFE OPERATION"] stabNode --> transS stabNode --> longS stabNode --> vertS stabNode --> dirS stabNode --> coupS transS --> criteria1 longS --> criteria2 vertS --> criteria3 dirS --> criteria4 coupS --> criteria5 criteria1 --> output criteria2 --> output criteria3 --> output criteria4 --> output criteria5 --> output ``` ## 4.3 SDOF Oscillator Analogy — Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR Fext["F External"] massA["Mass m plus Added Mass"] dampA["Damping B"] springA["Restoring C"] coordOut["Response x of t"] Fext --> massA massA --> dampA dampA --> springA springA --> coordOut ``` ## 4.4 Motion Stability Decision Matrix (Schematic) | Block Layer | Function | |-------------|----------| | Layer 1 — Excitation | Waves, wind, rudder, current | | Layer 2 — Hydrodynamics | Added mass, damping, restoring | | Layer 3 — Equation Solver | SDOF or coupled 6-DOF solver | | Layer 4 — Stability Test | GM check, period check, IMO check | | Layer 5 — Output | Roll angle, pitch angle, heave, yaw | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define "Motion Stability" and list the six degrees of freedom of a floating ship. **Model Answer:** > **Motion Stability** of a ship is the tendency of the vessel to return to its equilibrium position (or maintain a steady course) when displaced by external forces such as waves, wind, or rudder action. > > The six rigid-body degrees of freedom of a ship are: > 1. **Surge** — translation along the longitudinal x-axis. > 2. **Sway** — translation along the transverse y-axis. > 3. **Heave** — translation along the vertical z-axis. > 4. **Roll** — rotation about the longitudinal x-axis. > 5. **Pitch** — rotation about the transverse y-axis. > 6. **Yaw** — rotation about the vertical z-axis. > > Of these, roll and pitch are **gravity-restored**, while yaw is **hydrodynamically restored**. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between transverse and longitudinal stability of a ship. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Transverse Stability | Longitudinal Stability | |-----------|----------------------|-------------------------| | Restoring moment origin | Transverse shift of B (about x-axis) | Longitudinal shift of B (about y-axis) | | Stability parameter | $GM_T$ (transverse GM) | $GM_L$ (longitudinal GM) | | Typical value | 0.15 m – 1.5 m | 0.5 L – 1.5 L | | Governing motion | Rolling | Pitching | | Period range | 8–25 s | 4–10 s | | Damping | Bilge keel, eddy-making | Hull-form-dependent | --- ## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### ❓ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **natural period of roll** $T_\phi$ of a ship in terms of its metacentric height $GM$ and radius of gyration $k$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A container ship has $B = 25$ m, $GM = 0.85$ m, and $k = 0.4B$. Compute the natural roll period. If the ship encounters beam waves with period $T_w = 12$ s, comment on the roll behaviour. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) Starting from the rotational equation of motion about the roll axis through G: $$ I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} = -\Delta \cdot \overline{GZ} $$ For small angles, $\overline{GZ} = GM \cdot \phi$, so: $$ I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} + \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \phi = 0 $$ This is a harmonic oscillator with: $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta \cdot GM}{I_{xx}}} $$ Since $T_\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$: $$ T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{\Delta \cdot GM}} $$ Using $I_{xx} = \dfrac{\Delta}{g} k^2$: $$ T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}} = \frac{2\pi k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}} $$ (7 Marks — derivation fully shown with all logical steps.) --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) Given: - $B = 25$ m - $GM = 0.85$ m - $k = 0.4B = 0.4 \times 25 = 10.0$ m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² Compute the natural period: $$ T_\phi = \frac{2\pi \times 10.0}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.85}} $$ Step 1 — Numerator: $$ 2\pi \times 10.0 = 62.832 $$ Step 2 — Denominator: $$ \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.85} = \sqrt{8.3385} = 2.888 $$ Step 3 — Final: $$ T_\phi = \frac{62.832}{2.888} \approx 21.76 \text{ s} $$ **Comment on roll behaviour:** The wave period $T_w = 12$ s is **much smaller** than the natural roll period $T_\phi = 21.76$ s. The ratio: $$ \frac{T_w}{T_\phi} = \frac{12}{21.76} \approx 0.55 $$ Since this ratio is **far from 1**, the ship will experience **moderate rolling** without dangerous resonance. However, the long natural period means the ship has **soft, slow rolls** — comfortable for crew but indicating low roll stiffness. Adding **bilge keels** or **anti-roll fins** is recommended. (7 Marks — full numerical evaluation + physical commentary.) --- ### ❓ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **concept of metacentric height** and its role in **transverse stability**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A passenger ship has displacement $\Delta = 12{,}000$ tonnes, $KM = 7.2$ m, $KG = 6.5$ m. Compute the metacentric height and the natural roll period if $k = 0.35B$, with $B = 22$ m. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) The **metacenter (M)** is the point where the vertical line through the **new center of buoyancy $B'$** (after a small heel) intersects the original vertical centerline through the upright **B**. The **metacentric height** is: $$ GM = KM - KG $$ Where: - $KM$ = distance from keel K to metacenter M (a function of waterplane moment of inertia $I_T$ and displacement volume $\nabla$). - $KG$ = distance from keel K to center of gravity G. Mathematically: $$ KM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} + KB $$ The metacentric height governs **transverse stability** because the **righting lever** is: $$ \overline{GZ} = GM \sin\phi $$ **Role in stability:** - $GM > 0$ → ship is **statically stable**. - $GM = 0$ → **neutral** (e.g., fully submerged submarine). - $GM < 0$ → **statically unstable** (capsizes). The larger the $GM$, the **stiffer** the ship rolls and the **shorter** the natural roll period. **Sketch (described):** A rectangular hull section heeling by angle $\phi$; original B at center, $B'$ shifted to one side, vertical through $B'$ intersecting original centerline at M; lever $\overline{GZ}$ marked horizontal between W-line and B'-line. (7 Marks — complete definition, formula, role, sketch description.) --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) Given: - $\Delta = 12{,}000$ t = $12{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 1.1772 \times 10^8$ N - $KM = 7.2$ m - $KG = 6.5$ m - $B = 22$ m - $k = 0.35 \times 22 = 7.7$ m **Step 1 — Compute GM:** $$ GM = KM - KG = 7.2 - 6.5 = 0.7 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Compute natural roll period:** $$ T_\phi = \frac{2\pi k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}} $$ Substituting: $$ T_\phi = \frac{2\pi \times 7.7}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.7}} $$ Numerator: $$ 2\pi \times 7.7 = 48.38 $$ Denominator: $$ \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.7} = \sqrt{6.867} = 2.620 $$ Final: $$ T_\phi = \frac{48.38}{2.620} \approx 18.47 \text{ s} $$ **Result:** The ship has $GM = 0.7$ m (a healthy margin > IMO minimum of 0.15 m) and a natural roll period of about **18.5 s** — comfortable for passenger service. (7 Marks — all numerical steps shown.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert displacement to weight:** Students often substitute tonnes (mass) into the formula $T_\phi = 2\pi\sqrt{I_{xx}/(\Delta \cdot GM)}$ without multiplying by $g$. Resulting periods come out **wrong by a factor of $\sqrt{g}$**. > 2. **Using diameter instead of radius of gyration:** $k$ is the **radius**, not the diameter, of gyration. > 3. **Confusing $GM$ vs $KM$:** $KM$ includes both buoyancy position and waterplane moment; $GM$ is the residual above G. Always subtract. > 4. **Not commenting on resonance:** Numerical answers without a resonance / behavioural comment lose **1–2 marks** in KTU ESE. > 5. **Ignoring the IMO weather criterion:** For passenger ships, mention the IMO area-under-GZ check for full marks. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A ship has **six degrees of freedom** (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw), grouped under **transverse, longitudinal, heaving, and directional** stability. - **Transverse (roll) stability** depends on $GM > 0$ with restoring lever $\overline{GZ} = GM \sin\phi$. - **Longitudinal (pitch) stability** depends on $GM_L > 0$. - **Heave stability** depends on the hydrostatic stiffness $\rho g A_w$. - **Yaw stability** depends on Nomoto's hydrodynamic indices $T > 0$ and $K > 0$. - **Natural roll period formula (HIGH-YIELD):** $$ T_\phi = \frac{2\pi k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}} $$ - **Radius of gyration approximations:** $k \approx 0.35B$ (transverse), $k_L \approx 0.25L$ (longitudinal). - **IMO minimum GM** for cargo ships = **0.15 m** (weather criterion). - **Damping sources** in roll: bilge keel friction, wave radiation, eddy-making. - **Resonance danger** occurs when wave encounter period $\approx$ ship natural period. - **Coupled motions** (roll–pitch, roll–yaw) cause broaching in stern-quartering seas. - For KTU ESE numericals: always **convert displacement to weight** ($\Delta \times g$) when computing moments of inertia in absolute terms. - **Heave natural period** uses *added mass* $a_{33}$: $$ T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m + a_{33}}{\rho g A_w}} $$ - **Course stability** is best at high speeds where hydrodynamic damping dominates; at low speeds the ship becomes **course-unstable**. > 💡 **Quick-Revision Mnemonic — "SHEEP-RP":** **S**urge, **H**eave, swa**E**, yaw = translational; **R**oll, **P**itch = rotational, gravity-restored.

topics.title: Basic Hydrodynamics and Equations of Motion of a Manoeuvring Body
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Hydrodynamics and Equations of Motion of a Manoeuvring Body

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Manoeuvring Hydrodynamics** is the branch of naval architecture that studies the forces and moments generated by the fluid (water) on a ship's hull as the vessel undergoes controlled motion — i.e., turning, course-changing, and path-keeping. The **Equations of Motion of a Manoeuvring Body** are the Newton–Euler set of differential equations that govern the translational and rotational response of the ship when subjected to these hydrodynamic loads, the propeller thrust, and the rudder force.

The complete dynamic state of a manoeuvring ship is described by **six degrees of freedom (6-DOF)**: three translations (surge, sway, heave) and three rotations (roll, pitch, yaw). For horizontal-plane manoeuvring studies, the heave, roll, and pitch motions are neglected, and attention is restricted to **surge (u), sway (v), and yaw (r)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Frames used in Manoeuvring Theory**
> - **Earth-Fixed (Inertial) Frame (O-XYZ):** A non-accelerating global frame fixed to the earth/sea-bed; used for trajectory (path) plotting.
> - **Body-Fixed (Moving) Frame (G-xₘyₘzₘ):** Origin at the ship's centre of gravity G, with xₘ pointing forward (towards the bow), yₘ to starboard, and zₘ downward. All hydrodynamic forces/moments are resolved along this frame.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy loaded shopping cart across a wet supermarket floor. The cart doesn't move exactly in the direction you push it — it *slips sideways* because the water resists lateral motion (sway damping), and the wheels being offset from your push line cause it to *curve* (yaw). The cart also feels *heavier* than it actually is because the water must be pushed aside (added mass).

> [!NOTE]
> A ship in water behaves identically:
> - **Added Mass** → water has inertia too, so the ship effectively weighs more dynamically.
> - **Hydrodynamic Damping** → water resists motion (viscous drag on hull and appendages).
> - **Cross-Coupling** → moving sideways (sway) creates a turning moment (yaw), and yawing creates a sideways drift.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Mass density of sea water (ρ):** **1025 kg/m³** (standard KTU / ITTC value)
- **Kinematic viscosity of sea water (ν):** **≈ 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** at 15 °C
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Units Convention:** Forces in **kN**, moments in **kN·m**, lengths in **metres (m)**, velocities in **m/s**, angular rates in **rad/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Problem

The manoeuvring problem is decomposed into the following structured logic steps:

1. **Reference Frame Selection** — A body-fixed frame (G-xₘyₘzₘ) is chosen because hydrodynamic forces are most naturally expressed relative to the hull.
2. **Velocity Vector Definition** — The body-fixed velocities are (u, v, w) for translations and (p, q, r) for rotations. For horizontal manoeuvring: u, v, r are retained; p, q, w ≈ 0.
3. **Force/Moment Identification** — External loads on the ship are:
   - **Hydrodynamic forces (X_H, Y_H, K_H, M_H, N_H)** on hull and appendages
   - **Propeller thrust (X_P)**
   - **Rudder force (X_R, Y_R, N_R)**
   - **Wind and wave disturbances (X_W, Y_W, N_W)**
4. **Application of Newton's Second Law + Euler's Moment Equation** in the body frame, including the **centripetal / Coriolis** terms that arise from frame rotation.
5. **Inclusion of Added Mass / Added Moment of Inertia** — water co-moves with the hull and contributes to the inertial response.
6. **Linearisation** — The nonlinear Taylor-series expansion of hydrodynamic forces about straight-line motion is performed; only the linear-in-velocity terms (and the rudder term) are retained for the standard linear manoeuvring model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a body-fixed frame?** The hull geometry is constant in this frame, so hydrodynamic coefficients become constant (time-invariant). This converts a complex moving-boundary problem into a set of ODEs with constant coefficients — the standard textbook KTU approach.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Symbol Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $m(\dot{u} - v r) = X_{\text{ext}}$ | Surge equation (Newton's 2nd law) |
| 2 | $m(\dot{v} + u r) = Y_{\text{ext}}$ | Sway equation |
| 3 | $I_{zz}\,\dot{r} = N_{\text{ext}}$ | Yaw (heading) equation |
| 4 | $(m + m_{22})\dot{v} + m_{11}\,u\,r = Y_{v}v + Y_{r}r + Y_{\delta}\delta$ | Sway with added mass and linear damping |
| 5 | $(I_{zz} + J_{zz})\dot{r} + m_{22}\,u\,v = N_{v}v + N_{r}r + N_{\delta}\delta$ | Yaw with added inertia and cross-coupling |
| 6 | $Y = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{3} Y^{\prime}_{v} v + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{4} Y^{\prime}_{r} r$ | Non-dimensional sway derivatives (primed form) |
| 7 | $N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{4} N^{\prime}_{v} v + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{5} N^{\prime}_{r} r$ | Non-dimensional yaw derivatives (primed form) |
| 8 | $X_{\text{H}} = X_{uu}u^{2} + X_{vv}v^{2} + \ldots$ | Nonlinear surge resistance (Taylor expansion) |
| 9 | $\dot{\psi} = r$ | Heading rate (kinematic relation) |
| 10 | $\dot{x}_{G} = u\cos\psi - v\sin\psi$ | Earth-fixed position update (surge direction) |
| 11 | $\dot{y}_{G} = u\sin\psi + v\cos\psi$ | Earth-fixed position update (sway direction) |
| 12 | $Y_{v}^{\prime},\, Y_{r}^{\prime},\, N_{v}^{\prime},\, N_{r}^{\prime}$ | Non-dimensional hydrodynamic derivatives |
| 13 | $m_{22} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{2} d_{22}$ | Sway-direction added mass (non-dim. via d-coeff) |
| 14 | $J_{zz} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{5} d_{66}$ | Yaw added moment of inertia |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention** in KTU textbooks: **xₘ** forward, **yₘ** to starboard, **zₘ** downward. Positive r is a **turn to port (left)**. Positive δ is **rudder to port**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Course-Keeping Autopilots** (e.g., Kongsberg, Sperry) are designed using the linear sway–yaw model.
- **Turning-Circle Predictions** in design offices use the full nonlinear equations.
- **Zig-Zag (Z-test) and Pull-Out Manoeuvres** mandated by **IMO Resolution A.601(15)** are interpreted using the same equations.
- **Real-Time Manoeuvring Simulators** (Kongsberg Polaris, Wärtsilä NAPA) integrate these ODEs in real time at 50–100 Hz.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Surge, Sway, Yaw Body-Fixed Equations (Starting from Newton's Law)

**Step 1 — Velocity in body frame.** The ship's absolute velocity vector in the body frame is:

$$
\vec{V}_{G} = u\,\hat{x}_m + v\,\hat{y}_m + w\,\hat{z}_m
$$

For horizontal manoeuvring we set w = 0.

**Step 2 — Angular velocity of the body frame.** Only the yaw rate r is non-zero:

$$
\vec{\Omega} = 0\,\hat{x}_m + 0\,\hat{y}_m + r\,\hat{z}_m = r\,\hat{z}_m
$$

**Step 3 — Total acceleration of G (Transport + Coriolis form).** For a body rotating at Ω with origin moving at V_G, the absolute acceleration is:

$$
\vec{a}_G = \frac{{}^{G}d\vec{V}_G}{dt} + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G
$$

Computing $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G &=
\begin{vmatrix}
\hat{x}_m & \hat{y}_m & \hat{z}_m \\
0 & 0 & r \\
u & v & 0
\end{vmatrix} \\
&= (0 \cdot 0 - r \cdot v)\hat{x}_m + (r \cdot u - 0 \cdot 0)\hat{y}_m + (0 \cdot v - 0 \cdot u)\hat{z}_m \\
&= - r v\,\hat{x}_m + r u\,\hat{y}_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Component form of the acceleration:**

$$
\vec{a}_G = (\dot{u} - v r)\,\hat{x}_m + (\dot{v} + u r)\,\hat{y}_m + 0 \cdot \hat{z}_m
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law (ΣF = m a).** Projecting the external force $\vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = X\,\hat{x}_m + Y\,\hat{y}_m + Z\,\hat{z}_m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\dot{u} - v r) &= X \\
m(\dot{v} + u r) &= Y \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Euler's Moment Equation for the z-axis (yaw):**

$$
I_{zz}\,\dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx})\,p\,q = N
$$

For horizontal motion, p = q = 0, so the cross-product term vanishes and:

$$
I_{zz}\,\dot{r} = N
$$

This is the **uncoupled inertial frame** for horizontal manoeuvring. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Inclusion of Hydrodynamic Added Mass

When the ship accelerates in water, a *virtual* mass of water is entrained. This is represented by the **added mass** tensor. For horizontal motion (surge-sway-yaw) the inertia tensor becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \;\longrightarrow\;&
\begin{bmatrix}
m + m_{11} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & m + m_{22} & m_{26} \\
0 & m_{26} & I_{zz} + J_{zz}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Rewriting the sway equation with added mass.** The sway inertia is $m + m_{22}$ and there is a sway–yaw coupling $m_{26}$:

$$
(m + m_{22})\dot{v} + m_{11}\,u\,r + m_{26}\,\dot{r} = Y
$$

**Step 2 — Rewriting the yaw equation:**

$$
(I_{zz} + J_{zz})\dot{r} + m_{26}\,\dot{v} + m_{22}\,u\,v - m_{11}\,u\,v = N
$$

**Step 3 — Common simplification (Kijima et al. form).** Retain only the dominant linear terms and neglect the small $m_{26}$ term initially, while keeping the centripetal $(m_{22} - m_{11})u\,v$ term on the right side of the yaw equation. The standard linear **3-DOF manoeuvring model** is obtained:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(m + m_{22})\dot{v} + (m + m_{11})\,u\,r + (m_{y v^{\prime}})\,\dot{v} &= Y_{v}\,v + Y_{r}\,r + Y_{\delta}\,\delta \\
(I_{zz} + J_{zz})\dot{r} + m_{22}\,u\,v + m_{x u}\,u\,r &= N_{v}\,v + N_{r}\,r + N_{\delta}\,\delta \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The first equation is the **sway balance** (added mass on LHS, viscous damping derivatives on RHS). The second is the **yaw moment balance**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Non-Dimensional Form (Standard for Exam Problems)

The **primed (non-dimensional) derivatives** are obtained by dividing the dimensional derivatives by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{n+1}$ (where n is the exponent of velocity). The standard formulae are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{v} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{2}\,Y^{\prime}_{v} \quad &\text{(sway force due to sway vel.)} \\
Y_{r} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{3}\,Y^{\prime}_{r} \quad &\text{(sway force due to yaw rate)} \\
N_{v} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{3}\,N^{\prime}_{v} \quad &\text{(yaw moment due to sway vel.)} \\
N_{r} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{4}\,N^{\prime}_{r} \quad &\text{(yaw moment due to yaw rate)} \\
Y_{\delta} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{2}\,Y^{\prime}_{\delta} \quad &\text{(sway force due to rudder)} \\
N_{\delta} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{3}\,N^{\prime}_{\delta} \quad &\text{(yaw moment due to rudder)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always carry units through the calculation. Mixing primed and unprimed derivatives in the same equation is the **#1 mark-loss mistake**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Linear State-Space Manoeuvring Model

The standard linearised state-space form (used in IMO-type autopilot design problems) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot{v} \\
\dot{r} \\
\dot{\psi}
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
a_{11} & a_{12} & 0 \\
a_{21} & a_{22} & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
v \\
r \\
\psi
\end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
b_{1} \\
b_{2} \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

where the coefficients (Nomoto's reduced formulation) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{11} &= \frac{Y_{v}}{m + m_{22}} \\
a_{12} &= \frac{Y_{r} - (m + m_{11})u_{0}}{m + m_{22}} \\
a_{21} &= \frac{N_{v}}{I_{zz} + J_{zz}} \\
a_{22} &= \frac{N_{r}}{I_{zz} + J_{zz}} \\
b_{1} &= \frac{Y_{\delta}}{m + m_{22}} \\
b_{2} &= \frac{N_{\delta}}{I_{zz} + J_{zz}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This matrix equation is the **entry point** to stability, transient-response, and control-system design for the manoeuvring ship.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Body-Fixed Reference Frame and Degrees of Freedom

The following block diagram shows the body-fixed axes and the 6-DOF decomposition used throughout KTU manoeuvring theory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIP["SHIP RIGID BODY"]
        direction TB
        G["CENTRE OF GRAVITY G"]
        XM["x_m AXIS: BOW FORWARD"]
        YM["y_m AXIS: STARBOARD"]
        ZM["z_m AXIS: DOWNWARD"]
    end

    subgraph TRANS["3 TRANSLATIONAL DOF"]
        SURGE["SURGE u : along x_m"]
        SWAY["SWAY v : along y_m"]
        HEAVE["HEAVE w : along z_m"]
    end

    subgraph ROT["3 ROTATIONAL DOF"]
        ROLL["ROLL p : about x_m"]
        PITCH["PITCH q : about y_m"]
        YAW["YAW r : about z_m"]
    end

    subgraph HORIZ["HORIZONTAL MANOEUVRING (used in KTU)"]
        HS["SURGE u"]
        HV["SWAY v"]
        HR["YAW r"]
    end

    G --> XM
    G --> YM
    G --> ZM
    XM --> SURGE
    YM --> SWAY
    ZM --> HEAVE
    XM --> ROLL
    YM --> PITCH
    ZM --> YAW
    SURGE --> HS
    SWAY --> HV
    YAW --> HR
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Manoeuvring Equation Architecture

The functional architecture of the full manoeuvring model is shown below. The body-fixed accelerations, velocities, and external loads all flow into the equation solver, which integrates the 3-DOF equations of motion.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["HULL ADDED MASS MATRIX m_ij"] --> B0["INERTIA TERM m a_G"]
    A1["HULL DAMPING DERIVATIVES Y_v N_v Y_r N_r"] --> C0["HYDRODYNAMIC FORCE Y_H"]
    A2["RUDDER DERIVATIVES Y_delta N_delta"] --> C1["RUDDER FORCE Y_R and MOMENT N_R"]
    A3["PROP THRUST X_P"] --> C2["PROPULSION FORCE X_P"]
    A4["WIND / WAVE LOAD X_W Y_W N_W"] --> C3["DISTURBANCE FORCE and MOMENT"]
    B0 --> D0["RHS of ODE: F ext = m a_G"]
    C0 --> D0
    C1 --> D0
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    D0 --> E0["INTEGRATION: dot v and dot r and dot psi"]
    E0 --> F0["STATE UPDATE: u v r psi"]
    F0 --> A1
    F0 --> A2
    F0 --> A4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Description of Cross-Coupled Forces

The following diagram visualises how the three horizontal DOFs are coupled. This is the conceptual picture students must carry into the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U["SURGE VELOCITY u"] -- "centripetal term m u r" --> V["SWAY ACCELERATION v-dot"]
    U -- "centripetal term m u v" --> R["YAW ACCELERATION r-dot"]
    V["SWAY VELOCITY v"] -- "damping Y_v v" --> VACC["v-dot update"]
    V -- "damping N_v v" --> RACC["r-dot update"]
    R["YAW RATE r"] -- "damping Y_r r" --> VACC
    R -- "damping N_r r" --> RACC
    DELTA["RUDDER ANGLE delta"] -- "Y_delta delta" --> VACC
    DELTA -- "N_delta delta" --> RACC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the three "cross-terms" (u·r, u·v, v in N-v equation, r in Y-r equation). These are the **Manoeuvring Mathematics' signature**: pure uncoupled dynamics (no coupling) would only have self-terms, but in reality every motion of the ship affects every other motion because the hull is a single rigid body moving in a fluid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the terms "added mass" and "added moment of inertia" in the context of a manoeuvring ship. Why are they non-zero even for an ideal (inviscid) fluid?*

**Model Answer:**

**Added mass ($m_{ij}$):** The hydrodynamic mass of water that is entrained and accelerated together with the ship hull when the hull accelerates. It is the additional inertia of the fluid that the rigid body must "carry along" with it during acceleration. Mathematically, it appears as a coefficient in the kinetic energy of the fluid.

**Added moment of inertia ($J_{zz}$):** The rotational analogue — the moment of inertia of the entrained water about the ship's centre of gravity, contributed through the fluid's kinetic energy when the ship yaws.

**Why non-zero in inviscid fluid:** When a body accelerates in an ideal fluid, the velocity potential satisfies the Laplace equation with a moving-boundary condition (Neumann). The solution of this boundary-value problem always produces a non-trivial kinetic energy in the fluid. By d'Alembert's paradox, there is no drag, but there is a *reaction force* proportional to acceleration. The body therefore behaves as if its mass has been increased — hence the term "added mass."

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "added mass is due to friction" — this is **wrong**. Added mass exists even in an inviscid (zero-viscosity) fluid. Friction contributes to the *damping* terms, not to added mass.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between the earth-fixed and body-fixed reference frames used in ship manoeuvring analysis. Why is the body-fixed frame preferred for formulating the equations of motion?*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Earth-Fixed (O-XYZ) | Body-Fixed (G-xₘyₘzₘ) |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Origin | Fixed in space (sea-bed) | Co-moving with ship at G |
| Axes | Inertial, do not rotate | Rotate with the ship |
| Best for | Plotting trajectory, GPS | Force/moment resolution |
| Hydrodynamic coefficients | Time-varying (depend on heading) | **Time-invariant** (constant w.r.t. hull geometry) |

**Why body-fixed is preferred:**

1. The hull geometry is constant in this frame, so the **hydrodynamic derivatives are constant** — making the ODEs linear-with-constant-coefficients.
2. The **centripetal/Coriolis terms** appear naturally and compactly as $u r$ and $v r$ terms.
3. The body-fixed components of force, moment, velocity, and acceleration are what the hydrodynamicist measures during captive model tests (PMM, rotating-arm).
4. Conversion to the earth-fixed path is performed *after* integration using the kinematic relations $\dot{x}_G = u\cos\psi - v\sin\psi$ and $\dot{y}_G = u\sin\psi + v\cos\psi$.

> [!NOTE]
> Always draw the body-fixed diagram with the **xₘ axis pointing towards the bow** and **yₘ to starboard** — this is the **KTU/Kijima/Abkowitz convention** and a reversal will lose method marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (very important for 14-mark questions):**
> - **Do not skip the centripetal term ($u r$, $u v$) —** students often write $m\dot v = Y$ and lose 4 marks instantly.
> - **Do not mix primed and unprimed derivatives** in the same equation.
> - **Always state the reference frame and origin** explicitly at the start of a derivation.
> - **Include units** in the final numerical answer. A number without units gets **0** for the units sub-part.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the 3-DOF body-fixed equations of motion (surge, sway, yaw) for a ship manoeuvring in the horizontal plane, clearly identifying the centripetal and Coriolis terms.

**(b)** A model of length $L = 6\,\text{m}$ is towed at $u_0 = 1.5\,\text{m/s}$ in a towing tank. The non-dimensional sway force derivative is $Y^{\prime}_{v} = -0.18$ and the non-dimensional sway-damping derivative due to yaw rate is $Y^{\prime}_{r} = 0.04$. Compute the dimensional derivatives $Y_{v}$ and $Y_{r}$ at sea water density $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. [7 Marks]

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Frame definition (1 mark):** Choose body-fixed frame G-xₘyₘzₘ with xₘ forward, yₘ starboard, zₘ downward. Velocities: (u, v, w) and angular rates: (p, q, r). For horizontal manoeuvring, w = p = q = 0.

**Step 2 — Acceleration expression (2 marks):** The total acceleration of G in the body frame is:

$$
\vec{a}_G = (\dot{u} - v r)\hat{x}_m + (\dot{v} + u r)\hat{y}_m
$$

This was obtained by computing $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G$ where $\vec{\Omega} = r\hat{z}_m$ and $\vec{V}_G = u\hat{x}_m + v\hat{y}_m$.

**Step 3 — Newton's 2nd law in xₘ and yₘ (2 marks):**

$$
m(\dot{u} - v r) = X \\
m(\dot{v} + u r) = Y
$$

The $-v r$ and $+u r$ terms are the **centripetal / Coriolis** terms that arise purely from the rotation of the body frame.

**Step 4 — Yaw equation (1 mark):** Using Euler's moment equation about zₘ with I_xx and I_yy cross-products vanishing for p = q = 0:

$$
I_{zz}\dot{r} = N
$$

**Step 5 — Inclusion of added mass and external loads (1 mark):** The external forces X, Y, N include hydrodynamic, propeller, rudder, and wind/wave contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= X_H + X_P + X_R + X_W \\
Y &= Y_H + Y_R + Y_W \\
N &= N_H + N_R + N_W \\
\end{aligned}
$$

With added mass terms, the inertia becomes (m + m₁₁), (m + m₂₂), (I_zz + J_zz), and the centripetal term in the sway equation picks up $(m + m_{11})u r$.

**Final 3-DOF Equations (boxed for the exam):**

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
(m + m_{11})(\dot{u} - v r) &= X \\
(m + m_{22})(\dot{v} + u r) + m_{11}\,u\,r &= Y \\
(I_{zz} + J_{zz})\dot{r} &= N \\
\end{aligned}\;}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Formula selection (2 marks):** From the non-dimensional definition:

$$
Y_v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{2}\,Y^{\prime}_{v} \quad\text{and}\quad Y_r = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{3}\,Y^{\prime}_{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical substitution for $Y_v$ (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_v &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(6)^{2}(-0.18) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(36)(-0.18) \\
    &= (512.5)(36)(-0.18) \\
    &= 18450 \times (-0.18) \\
    &= -3321\;\text{N·s/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution for $Y_r$ (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_r &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(6)^{3}(0.04) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(216)(0.04) \\
    &= (512.5)(216)(0.04) \\
    &= 110700 \times 0.04 \\
    &= 4428\;\text{N·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;Y_v = -3321\;\text{N·s/m}, \quad Y_r = 4428\;\text{N·s}\;}
$$

The negative sign of $Y_v$ indicates it is a **restoring** (damping) force opposing the sway motion, as physically expected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the physical meaning of the cross-coupling terms in the linear sway–yaw manoeuvring model. Write down the standard linearised state-space form. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A container ship of length $L = 200\,\text{m}$ is operating at $u_0 = 8\,\text{m/s}$. Using the non-dimensional derivatives $N^{\prime}_{v} = -0.12$ and $N^{\prime}_{r} = -0.05$, find the dimensional yaw derivatives $N_{v}$ and $N_{r}$ in **kN·m·s/m** and **kN·m·s** respectively. Take $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. [7 Marks]

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Cross-coupling physical meaning (4 marks):**

1. **$(m + m_{11})u\,r$ in the sway equation:** When the ship yaws (r ≠ 0) at forward speed u, the centripetal acceleration of the hull produces a *lateral* (sway) force. This is the reason a ship yawing while moving forward drifts sideways — a well-known phenomenon observed during a turn.
2. **$m_{22}\,u\,v$ in the yaw equation:** The product of surge and sway velocities generates a yawing moment because the lateral hydrodynamic force on the hull acts at a *lever arm* ahead of (or behind) the centre of gravity.
3. **$Y_{r}\,r$ and $N_{v}\,v$:** The **sway force due to yaw rate** arises from the asymmetric pressure distribution on the hull when rotating. The **yaw moment due to sway velocity** arises because the lateral flow creates unequal pressure on the bow and stern regions.

**Linearised state-space form (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot v \\
\dot r \\
\dot\psi
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{Y_v}{m+m_{22}} & \dfrac{Y_r-(m+m_{11})u_0}{m+m_{22}} & 0 \\
\dfrac{N_v}{I_{zz}+J_{zz}} & \dfrac{N_r}{I_{zz}+J_{zz}} & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
v \\
r \\
\psi
\end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{Y_\delta}{m+m_{22}} \\
\dfrac{N_\delta}{I_{zz}+J_{zz}} \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The third row of A is $(0,\;1,\;0)$ and not a derivative — it is the **kinematic** relation $\dot\psi = r$ that links the heading angle to the yaw rate. A common mistake is to put $(0,\;0,\;0)$ in the third row.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Formula (2 marks):**

$$
N_v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{3} N^{\prime}_{v}, \quad N_r = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{4} N^{\prime}_{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $L^{3}$ and $L^{4}$ (1 mark):**

$$
L^{3} = 200^{3} = 8 \times 10^{6}\,\text{m}^{3}, \quad L^{4} = 200^{4} = 1.6 \times 10^{9}\,\text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $N_v$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_v &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(8 \times 10^{6})(-0.12) \\
    &= (512.5)(8 \times 10^{6})(-0.12) \\
    &= 4.1 \times 10^{9} \times (-0.12) \\
    &= -4.92 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N·m·s/m} \\
    &= -4.92 \times 10^{5}\,\text{kN·m·s/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $N_r$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_r &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(1.6 \times 10^{9})(-0.05) \\
    &= (512.5)(1.6 \times 10^{9})(-0.05) \\
    &= 8.2 \times 10^{11} \times (-0.05) \\
    &= -4.1 \times 10^{10}\,\text{N·m·s} \\
    &= -4.1 \times 10^{7}\,\text{kN·m·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;N_v = -4.92 \times 10^{5}\;\text{kN·m·s/m}, \quad N_r = -4.1 \times 10^{7}\;\text{kN·m·s}\;}
$$

Both derivatives are negative, confirming their role as **damping / restoring** moments that oppose any deviation in sway velocity and yaw rate respectively — a necessary condition for directional stability of a course-keeping ship.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Body-fixed frame** is used because the hull geometry (and hence hydrodynamic coefficients) is **time-invariant**.
- [x] The **3-DOF manoeuvring equations** are surge, sway, and yaw. Heave, roll, and pitch are neglected.
- [x] The cross-product $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{V}_G$ produces the centripetal / Coriolis terms $u r$ and $v r$ that *must* appear in the equations.
- [x] **Added mass** exists in inviscid fluid too — it is **not** caused by friction. Friction contributes to **damping** derivatives ($Y_v, Y_r, N_v, N_r$).
- [x] **Sign convention:** xₘ forward, yₘ starboard, zₘ downward. Positive r = turn to port.
- [x] Non-dimensional (primed) derivatives are obtained by dividing by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^{n+1}$, where $n$ is the velocity-exponent of the force.
- [x] The **state-space form** of the linearised model has $\dot\psi = r$ in the third row, *not* a derivative of $\psi$.
- [x] **Units to remember:** $Y_v$ in N·s/m, $Y_r$ in N·s, $N_v$ in N·m·s/m, $N_r$ in N·m·s, $Y_\delta$ in N/rad, $N_\delta$ in N·m/rad.
- [x] **Negative $Y_v$, $N_r$** ⟹ damping / restoring action ⟹ **directional stability**.
- [x] **Kinematic relations** $\dot{x}_G = u\cos\psi - v\sin\psi$ and $\dot{y}_G = u\sin\psi + v\cos\psi$ convert the body-fixed velocities into earth-fixed trajectory.
- [x] **Captive model tests** (PMM, rotating-arm, circular-motion) are how the four key linear derivatives $Y_v, Y_r, N_v, N_r$ are measured experimentally.
- [x] The IMO **Standards for Ship Manoeuvrability** (Res. A.601(15) / MSC.137(76)) define the *experimental* turn and zig-zag tests used to validate the *theoretical* predictions from these very equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always draw the **body-fixed diagram first** — this anchors your sign convention for the whole problem.
> 2. Derive the cross-product **carefully** — this is the most-marked step in any 14-mark question.
> 3. If a numerical problem is given, **convert all primes to dimensional derivatives first**, then solve. Never plug primes into a dimensional equation.
> 4. Mention **physical interpretation** at the end (e.g., "negative sign indicates damping") — this often carries 1–2 marks and demonstrates understanding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Control-Fixed Stability Indices — Section 1

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Control-Fixed Stability** refers to the assessment of a vessel's intact transverse stability under operational loading conditions where the **steering and course-keeping controls (rudder, fins, autopilot) are held in a fixed (locked) position** while the ship is subjected to external disturbances such as beam wind, wave-induced rolling, and turning moments.

These indices are codified by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in the **Intact Stability Code, 2008 (IS Code 2008, Res. A.749(18) and MSC.267(85))** under Part A, Sections **3.2.2 to 3.2.5**. The four principal control-fixed operational criteria are:

| IS Code Section | Criterion Name |
|---|---|
| 3.2.2 | Weather Criterion (severe wind + rolling) |
| 3.2.3 | Turning Ability Criterion |
| 3.2.4 | Acceleration Criterion (special ship types) |
| 3.2.5 | Dead Ship Condition Criterion |

The ship is deemed to have **adequate control-fixed stability** if it simultaneously satisfies all applicable criteria from this family.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Commuter on a Tilting Train

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a commuter standing inside a swaying railway carriage during a strong side wind. They cannot hold onto any handrail (the **controls are fixed**). Their safety depends entirely on (i) how wide their stance is (initial GM), (ii) how far they can lean before they fall (GZ curve area), and (iii) how the wind pressure pushes against them (heeling arm).

Likewise, a ship with a locked rudder must rely *solely* on its **static GZ curve geometry** to resist an external beam heeling moment. If the inherent GZ is too small, or the heeling arm is too large, the loop formed between the two curves collapses — and the ship **capsizes dynamically**, even if its equilibrium appears statically stable.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.222 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (≈ 1.225 kg/m³).
- **Standard wind pressure for IS Code design wind:** $P_{ref} = 504 \text{ N/m}^2$ (≈ Beaufort 10, $V \approx 28.7 \text{ m/s}$).
- **Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Critical GM range for control-fixed indices:** typically $0.15 \text{ m} \leq \text{GM} \leq 0.50 \text{ m}$ for most displacement vessels.

## 1.4 Why "Control-Fixed"?

The term distinguishes these criteria from **course-keeping (control-active)** or **course-changing** maneuvers where the helmsman dynamically corrects the heading. In control-fixed analysis:

1. The rudder is locked amidships (no restoring yaw moment).
2. The ship cannot actively counter the side-force from the wind.
3. Stability is provided *purely* by the righting lever (GZ) and the metacentric geometry.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Control-Fixed Stability Indices — Section 2

## 2.1 Logical Hierarchy of IS Code 2008 Operational Criteria

```
Operational Criteria (Part A, IS Code 2008)
│
├── 3.2.2  Weather Criterion  ──── (BEAM WIND + ROLLING)
│
├── 3.2.3  Turning Criterion   ──── (STEADY TURN, CONTROL LOCKED)
│
├── 3.2.4  Acceleration C.    ──── (SPECIAL VESSELS: Ro-Ro, etc.)
│
└── 3.2.5  Dead Ship C.       ──── (NO PROPULSION, NO STEERING)
```

Each criterion evaluates a distinct operational scenario but shares a common philosophy: *the GZ curve and the disturbing-arm curve must enclose sufficient positive area.*

## 2.2 Weather Criterion (IS Code 3.2.2) — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Physical Setup

The vessel is subjected to:
- A **steady beam wind** of pressure $P$ acting on the lateral projected area $A_L$ at vertical centroid $z$ above the waterline.
- A **rolling motion** of amplitude $\phi$ about the longitudinal axis.

The **wind heeling arm** as a function of roll angle is:
$$l_w(\phi) = l_{w1} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

where $l_{w1}$ is the *static* wind heeling lever.

### 2.2.2 Static Heeling Lever

$$l_{w1} = \frac{P \cdot A_L \cdot z}{1000 \cdot g \cdot \Delta}$$

with all quantities in SI units: $P$ in $\text{Pa}$, $A_L$ in $\text{m}^2$, $z$ in $\text{m}$, $\Delta$ in **tonnes**, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Substituting $P = 504 \text{ Pa}$ gives the **standardized IS Code lever**:
$$l_{w1,IS} = \frac{0.0514 \cdot A_L \cdot z}{\Delta}$$

### 2.2.3 The Two-Curve Intersection Test

The criterion plots $GZ(\phi)$ and $l_w(\phi) = l_{w1}\cos(\phi)$ on the same axes. Three intersection angles arise:

| Angle | Name | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$ | Origin | Trivial intercept |
| $\theta_1$ | Steady-heel angle | First non-zero intercept |
| $\theta_2$ | Vanishing-stability angle | Second intercept |

Between $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, the **righting lever exceeds the heeling arm**, producing a positive work loop:

$$\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \big[ GZ(\phi) - l_{w1}\cos(\phi) \big] \, d\phi \;\geq\; 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The criterion is satisfied when the GZ curve **lies above** the heeling arm curve for some continuous arc beyond $\theta_1$, *and* the downflooding angle $\theta_f \geq \theta_2$ (no openings immersed before the second intercept).

## 2.3 Turning Ability Criterion (IS Code 3.2.3)

For a ship of length $L$ undergoing a steady turn with the rudder locked, the centrifugal force creates an additional heeling arm:

$$l_{turn} = \frac{v^2 \cdot \overline{GM}}{R \cdot g}$$

where $v$ is the speed and $R$ is the turning radius.

The IMO criterion specifies a **minimum residual GM**:

$$GM_{min} = 0.05 + 0.005\, L \quad \text{(metres)}, \quad L \text{ in metres}$$

subject to an upper cap (avoiding excessive roll accelerations in calm water).

## 2.4 Dead Ship Criterion (IS Code 3.2.5)

The ship is without propulsion or steering under beam wind of pressure $P = 504 \text{ Pa}$. The condition requires:

- The angle of steady heel: $\theta_{eq} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{l_w}{GM_{res}}\right) \leq \theta_{limit}$ (typically $50^\circ$).
- The downflooding angle $\theta_f \geq \theta_{eq} + 5^\circ$.

## 2.5 Acceleration Criterion (IS Code 3.2.4)

Applicable to **Ro-Ro passenger ships** and similar vessels where heavy rolling is expected. The lateral acceleration at the centre of gravity must satisfy:

$$a_{lat} \leq 0.2\, g \quad \text{(at all roll positions)}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Cheat Sheet** — Master the boxed expressions for the end-semester exam.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wind pressure (IS Code) | $P = 0.5\,\rho\,V^2$ | Pa |
| 2 | Static wind heeling lever | $l_{w1} = \dfrac{P\,A_L\,z}{1000\,g\,\Delta}$ | m |
| 3 | Dynamic wind arm | $l_w(\phi) = l_{w1}\cos\phi$ | m |
| 4 | Weather-criterion loop area | $A_{loop} = \displaystyle\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}\!\big(GZ - l_{w1}\cos\phi\big)d\phi$ | m·rad |
| 5 | Turning lever | $l_{turn} = \dfrac{v^2\,\overline{GM}}{R\,g}$ | m |
| 6 | Min. turning GM | $GM_{min} = 0.05 + 0.005\,L$ | m |
| 7 | Dead-ship heel angle | $\theta_{eq} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{l_w}{GM_{res}}\right)$ | rad / ° |
| 8 | Acceleration limit | $a_{lat} \leq 0.2\,g$ | m/s² |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Control-fixed indices are the **design-stage gatekeepers** in ship architecture. Naval architects iterate hull form, superstructure layout, and KG to simultaneously pass all four criteria. Failure of the Weather Criterion historically explains several bulk-carrier losses (e.g., *MV Derbyshire*, 1980), where a single severe squall capsized a statically-stable vessel because the **dynamic GZ-heeling-arm loop** was insufficient.

# Control-Fixed Stability Indices — Section 3

## 3.1 Derivation: Wind Heeling Arm from First Principles

### Step 1 — Aerodynamic Force on Lateral Area

The dynamic pressure exerted by a wind of speed $V$ on a flat surface normal to flow is:

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2$$

The total lateral force is therefore:

$$F_{wind} = P \cdot A_L = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2 \cdot A_L$$

### Step 2 — Moment About the Waterline

Taking moments about the waterline (point $W$), with the centroid of $A_L$ at height $z$:

$$M_{wind} = F_{wind} \cdot z = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,A_L\,z$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Lever Arm

The displacement of the vessel in weight units is $W = \Delta \cdot g$ (with $\Delta$ in tonnes, multiplied by $10^3$ to express in kg). The heeling lever is the moment divided by the weight:

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{w1} &= \frac{M_{wind}}{W} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,A_L\,z}{1000 \cdot g \cdot \Delta}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\rho = 1.222 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $V = 28.7 \text{ m/s}$ (Beaufort 10):

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.222 \times (28.7)^2 \\
  &= 0.611 \times 823.7 \\
  &\approx 503.3 \text{ Pa} \;\;\approx \;\; 504 \text{ Pa (IMO standard)}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the **IS Code canonical form**:

$$\boxed{\,l_{w1,IS} = \frac{504 \cdot A_L \cdot z}{1000 \cdot g \cdot \Delta}\,}\quad\text{metres}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Roll Angle

When the ship rolls by an angle $\phi$, the wind pressure continues to act horizontally, but its moment arm about the instantaneous metacentre is reduced. The lever at angle $\phi$ becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
l_w(\phi) &= l_{w1} \cdot \cos(\phi) \\
&= \frac{504\,A_L\,z}{1000\,g\,\Delta} \cdot \cos(\phi)
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Weather Criterion — Area Inequality

### Step 1 — Energy Balance

For the vessel to remain dynamically stable under the combined wind + roll action, the **net work done by the heeling arm** between $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ must be **less than or equal to** the **work done by the righting arm** in the same interval. Otherwise, the ship accumulates energy and rolls past $\theta_2$.

Energy input by wind (heeling work):
$$W_{in} = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} l_{w1}\cos(\phi)\,d\phi \cdot \Delta\,g$$

Energy restored by stability:
$$W_{out} = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} GZ(\phi)\,d\phi \cdot \Delta\,g$$

### Step 2 — Stability Requirement

For non-capsizing motion:
$$W_{out} \geq W_{in}$$

Dividing both sides by $\Delta g > 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} GZ(\phi)\,d\phi &\geq \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} l_{w1}\cos(\phi)\,d\phi \\
\Rightarrow \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}\!\big[GZ(\phi) - l_{w1}\cos(\phi)\big]d\phi &\geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Closed-Form Approximation (Trapezoidal Area)

For numerical evaluation, the integral is replaced by a trapezoidal sum:

$$A_{loop} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{1}{2}(\Delta\phi_i)\Big[\big(GZ_i - l_{w,i}\big) + \big(GZ_{i+1} - l_{w,i+1}\big)\Big]$$

with each $\Delta\phi_i = \phi_{i+1} - \phi_i$, and the loop area is normalised against:

$$A_{req} = 0.006\,\phi_f \quad \text{(IS Code minimum, rad·m)}$$

where $\phi_f$ is the angle of flooding in radians.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Worked Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement** — A bulk carrier has displacement $\Delta = 25\,000$ t, lateral projected area $A_L = 1800$ m², height of centroid $z = 9$ m, and metacentric height $GM = 0.40$ m. The GZ curve at small angles obeys $GZ(\phi) \approx GM\sin\phi$. Determine the angle of steady heel under IS Code design wind.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute static heeling arm:

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{w1} &= \frac{504 \times 1800 \times 9}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 25000} \\
&= \frac{8\,164\,800}{245\,250\,000} \\
&\approx 0.0333 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Equate righting and heeling levers at steady heel angle $\theta_1$:

$$GM\sin\theta_1 = l_{w1}\cos\theta_1$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity $\tan\theta_1 = l_{w1}/GM$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta_1 &= \frac{0.0333}{0.40} = 0.0833 \\
\theta_1 &= \arctan(0.0833) \approx 4.76^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Check against IS Code upper limits. For cargo ships, the typical allowable steady-heel under wind is $\theta_1 \leq 16^\circ$. **Result: PASS** with substantial margin.

**Step 5:** Estimate the area loop (illustrative, using the linearised GZ):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{loop} &= \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}\!\big[GM\sin\phi - l_{w1}\cos\phi\big]d\phi \\
&= \Big[-GM\cos\phi - l_{w1}\sin\phi\Big]_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}
\end{aligned}$$

For $\theta_2 \approx 60^\circ$ (typical bulk-carrier angle of vanishing stability):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{loop} &\approx \big[-(0.4)(0.5) - (0.0333)(0.866)\big] - \big[-(0.4)(0.9966) - (0.0333)(0.0830)\big] \\
&\approx \big[-0.200 - 0.0288\big] - \big[-0.3986 - 0.00277\big] \\
&\approx -0.2288 + 0.4014 \\
&\approx 0.173 \text{ m·rad}
\end{aligned}$$

The minimum IS Code required area $A_{req} = 0.006 \times (\pi/3) \approx 0.00628 \text{ m·rad}$. **Result: PASS** (loop area vastly exceeds the minimum).

## 3.4 Derivation: Turning Criterion Min-GM

For a ship turning at radius $R$ with forward speed $v$, the centrifugal force per unit mass is $v^2/R$. The induced heeling arm follows from this acceleration acting at the metacentre above G:

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{turn} &= \frac{v^2}{g\,R}\,\overline{GM} \\
GM_{min} &\;\text{ is set so that} \quad l_{turn} \leq \frac{GM}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

The empirical IMO formula:

$$GM_{min} = 0.05 + 0.005\,L \quad \text{with} \quad L_{\min} = 24 \text{ m,}\quad L_{\max} = 100 \text{ m (capped)}$$

This ensures a turning circle radius $R \geq 4\,L$ at a yaw-rate-compatible speed.

# Control-Fixed Stability Indices — Section 5

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 3]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define Control-Fixed Stability and list the four operational criteria given in IMO IS Code 2008.**

**Model Answer:**
Control-Fixed Stability is the intact transverse stability of a vessel when its rudder and steering gear are locked (typically amidships), so that the ship cannot actively counter external disturbances. The IMO IS Code 2008 specifies four such operational criteria:
1. Weather Criterion (3.2.2)
2. Turning Ability Criterion (3.2.3)
