3. Acceleration Criterion (3.2.4)
4. Dead Ship Condition Criterion (3.2.5)

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correct listing of all four.)*

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Why is the weather criterion expressed as an *area* between the GZ curve and the heeling-arm curve, rather than as a single lever-arm value?**

**Model Answer:**
The weather criterion evaluates **dynamic** stability — i.e., the ship's capacity to absorb the *work* done by wind gusts while rolling. A single lever comparison (e.g., $GZ_{max} \geq l_{w1}$) only addresses *static* equilibrium. The area enclosed between the curves represents the **net energy loop** between the steady-heel angle $\theta_1$ and the vanishing-stability angle $\theta_2$. A positive loop area ensures the ship returns from any roll excursion, while a zero or negative area means the wind accumulates energy faster than the ship can right itself — leading to dynamic capsizing.

*(3 marks: 1 for static-vs-dynamic distinction + 2 for energy/area argument.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model Paper, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the IS Code standardized wind heeling arm $l_{w1}$ from first principles. State the standard design wind pressure and explain the role of lateral projected area and its centroid height.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A general-cargo vessel has displacement $\Delta = 18\,000$ t, lateral projected area $A_L = 1400$ m², and height of lateral-area centroid $z = 8$ m. The GZ curve is approximated by $GZ(\phi) = 0.55\,\sin(\phi)$ m, valid up to $\phi = 70^\circ$. Determine whether the vessel satisfies the IS Code Weather Criterion under standard design wind.

---

**Model Solution — Part B-A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

Starting from the kinetic theory of fluids, the dynamic pressure on a flat surface normal to a uniform stream of speed $V$ and density $\rho$ is:

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$$

The total lateral force on the ship's exposed lateral area $A_L$ is:
$$F_{wind} = P \cdot A_L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_L$$

Taking moments about the waterline $W$, with the centroid of the lateral area at height $z$ above $W$:
$$M_{wind} = F_{wind}\cdot z = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_L\, z$$

The displacement in weight units is $W = \Delta\cdot g$, where $\Delta$ is in tonnes (hence the factor of 1000 in the denominator to convert tonnes → kg). The heeling lever is:

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{w1} &= \frac{M_{wind}}{W} \\
       &= \frac{\dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2\, A_L\, z}{1000\,g\,\Delta}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting IMO reference values $\rho = 1.222$ kg/m³ and $V = 28.7$ m/s (Beaufort 10):

$$P = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.222)(28.7)^2 \approx 504 \text{ Pa}$$

Therefore the **IS Code standard form** is:

$$\boxed{\,l_{w1} = \frac{504\,A_L\,z}{1000\,g\,\Delta}\,}\quad\text{(metres)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** $A_L$ captures the *frontal exposure* to the wind, while $z$ amplifies the moment arm — hence a tall superstructure is far more destabilising than a low-profile hull with the same lateral area.

*(Marks: 2 for pressure derivation, 2 for moment/length conversion, 2 for IS standardisation, 1 for physical insight.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $l_{w1}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{w1} &= \frac{504 \times 1400 \times 8}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 18000} \\
       &= \frac{5\,644\,800}{1\,765\,800\,000} \\
       &\approx 0.03197 \text{ m} \;\;\approx\;\; 0.032 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the equality $GZ(\theta_1) = l_{w1}\cos(\theta_1)$:**

$$0.55\sin\theta_1 = 0.032\cos\theta_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \tan\theta_1 = \frac{0.032}{0.55} = 0.0582$$

$$\theta_1 \approx \arctan(0.0582) \approx 3.33^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Find $\theta_2$ (second intercept):**

$$0.55\sin\theta_2 = 0.032\cos\theta_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \tan\theta_2 = 0.0582$$

Wait — the trigonometric identity yields the same equation, so we must use the *general* solution. Since $l_{w1}\cos\phi$ is monotonically decreasing and $GZ(\phi) = 0.55\sin\phi$ increases monotonically up to $90^\circ$, the second intercept occurs at the angle where the GZ curve again meets the heeling curve. Using the supplementary identity:

$$\sin\theta_2 = \frac{l_{w1}}{GM_{eff}} \cos\theta_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_2 \approx 90^\circ - 3.33^\circ = 86.67^\circ$$

(More rigorously, $\tan\theta_2 = l_{w1}/GM_{eff}$ has solution in Q1 *and* Q2; the Q2 solution gives $\theta_2 = 180^\circ - 3.33^\circ = 176.67^\circ$, but the practical physical limit is flooding, taken here as $\theta_f = 70^\circ$.)

**Step 4 — Evaluate the loop area from $\theta_1 = 3.33^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 70^\circ$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{loop} &= \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_f}\!\big[GZ - l_{w1}\cos\phi\big]\,d\phi \\
        &= \int_{3.33^\circ}^{70^\circ}\!\big[0.55\sin\phi - 0.032\cos\phi\big]\,d\phi \\
        &= \Big[-0.55\cos\phi - 0.032\sin\phi\Big]_{3.33^\circ}^{70^\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

Evaluating (converting to radians):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Big[\cdot\Big]_{70^\circ} &= -0.55\cos 70^\circ - 0.032\sin 70^\circ \\
&= -0.55(0.342) - 0.032(0.940) \\
&= -0.1881 - 0.0301 = -0.2182
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Big[\cdot\Big]_{3.33^\circ} &= -0.55\cos 3.33^\circ - 0.032\sin 3.33^\circ \\
&= -0.55(0.9983) - 0.032(0.0581) \\
&= -0.5491 - 0.00186 = -0.5509
\end{aligned}$$

$$A_{loop} = -0.2182 - (-0.5509) = +0.3327 \text{ m·rad}$$

**Step 5 — IS Code minimum required area:**

$$A_{req} = 0.006 \times \theta_f(\text{rad}) = 0.006 \times \frac{70\pi}{180} \approx 0.00733 \text{ m·rad}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

$$A_{loop} = 0.333 \;\gg\; A_{req} = 0.0073$$

The Weather Criterion is **satisfied by a wide margin**. The vessel is dynamically stable under IS Code design wind.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for $l_{w1}$, 1 for $\theta_1$, 1 for $\theta_2$, 2 for integration, 1 for comparison, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **Turning Ability Criterion (IS Code 3.2.3)** and the **Dead Ship Criterion (IS Code 3.2.5)**. State the relevant formulas and the limiting values.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A 120 m container ship is to be evaluated against the Turning Ability Criterion. The design service speed is $v = 18$ knots and the desired turning radius is $R = 600$ m. The metacentric height is $GM = 0.65$ m. Calculate the turning heeling arm and the IMO-required minimum GM. Comment on the compliance.

---

**Model Solution — Part B-B:**

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation [7 Marks]

**Turning Ability Criterion (3.2.3):**
This criterion ensures the ship can execute a controlled turn of approximately $60^\circ$ in azimuth without losing way excessively. The centrifugal acceleration acting at the metacentre above G produces a heeling arm:

$$l_{turn} = \frac{v^2}{g\,R}\,\overline{GM}$$

The IMO empirical rule for the *minimum* metacentric height (residual, after free-surface corrections) is:

$$GM_{min} = 0.05 + 0.005\,L \quad \text{(metres, for } 24 \text{ m} \leq L \leq 100 \text{ m)}$$

Beyond $L = 100$ m, the criterion is generally considered satisfied by ships meeting intact-stability requirements.

**Dead Ship Criterion (3.2.5):**
A "dead ship" has no propulsion and no steering. Under beam wind of pressure $P = 504$ Pa, the residual heeling arm is:

$$l_{w,res} = \frac{P\,A_L\,z}{1000\,g\,\Delta}$$

The equilibrium heel angle is:

$$\theta_{eq} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{l_{w,res}}{GM_{res}}\right)$$

The criterion requires:
- $\theta_{eq} \leq 50^\circ$ (in practice, typically $\leq 25^\circ$ for cargo vessels)
- $\theta_f \geq \theta_{eq} + 5^\circ$ (no downflooding within $5^\circ$ of equilibrium)

The *residual* GM accounts for free-surface corrections, fuel consumptions, and partially filled tanks.

*(Marks: 3.5 for turning, 3.5 for dead ship.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Verification [7 Marks]

**Given:** $L = 120$ m, $v = 18$ knots, $R = 600$ m, $GM = 0.65$ m.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s:**

$$v = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.26 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute turning heeling arm:**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_{turn} &= \frac{v^2}{g\,R}\,GM \\
        &= \frac{(9.26)^2}{9.81 \times 600} \times 0.65 \\
        &= \frac{85.75}{5886} \times 0.65 \\
        &= 0.01457 \times 0.65 \\
        &\approx 0.00947 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute IMO minimum GM:**

$$GM_{min} = 0.05 + 0.005 \times L = 0.05 + 0.005 \times 120 = 0.65 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$GM_{actual} = 0.65 \text{ m} = GM_{min} = 0.65 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The vessel **just satisfies** the Turning Ability Criterion at the threshold value. In practice, a safety margin of 5–10% is desirable, so a slightly higher GM (≈ 0.70–0.72 m) would be recommended for production design. The turning heeling arm $l_{turn} \approx 0.0095$ m is very small (≈ 1 cm), indicating the turn is dynamically benign — provided the rudder remains effective.

*(Marks: 1.5 for unit conversion, 2 for $l_{turn}$, 1 for $GM_{min}$, 1 for comparison, 1.5 for engineering conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in $l_{w1}$:** Forgetting the factor of **1000** when $\Delta$ is in tonnes instead of kilograms. This single error can swing a numerical answer by three orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Confusing $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$:** $\theta_1$ is the *first* non-zero intercept (steady heel); $\theta_2$ is the *second* intercept (vanishing stability). Reversing these breaks the loop-area integral.
> 3. **Ignoring the Cosine Distribution:** Using a constant $l_{w1}$ instead of $l_{w1}\cos\phi$ for the heeling arm at a roll angle $\phi$ is a **frequently repeated mistake**.
> 4. **Forgetting the Downflooding Cap:** The Weather Criterion is meaningless if openings immerse before $\theta_2$; the criterion must always be cross-checked against the smallest angle of downflooding $\theta_f$.
> 5. **Treating "control-fixed" as "intact":** Control-fixed criteria are *operational* and *complement* (not replace) the basic intact-stability criteria of Section 2 of the IS Code.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (One-Glance Summary)**

- **Control-Fixed Stability** = ship stability assessed with rudder locked; covered under IS Code 2008 Part A, Sections 3.2.2–3.2.5.
- **Four Criteria:** Weather, Turning, Acceleration, Dead Ship.
- **Standard IS Code Wind Pressure:** $P = 504$ Pa (≈ Beaufort 10, $V \approx 28.7$ m/s).
- **Heeling Lever Formula:** $l_{w1} = \dfrac{504\,A_L\,z}{1000\,g\,\Delta}$, with $\Delta$ in tonnes.
- **Heeling Curve at Roll:** $l_w(\phi) = l_{w1}\cos\phi$.
- **Weather Criterion Core Test:** $\displaystyle\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}\!\big[GZ(\phi) - l_{w1}\cos\phi\big]\,d\phi \geq 0$.
- **Turning Criterion:** $GM_{min} = 0.05 + 0.005L$ for $24 \text{ m} \leq L \leq 100$ m.
- **Dead Ship Equilibrium:** $\theta_{eq} = \arctan(l_w/GM_{res})$, with $\theta_f \geq \theta_{eq} + 5^\circ$.
- **Acceleration Criterion:** Applies to Ro-Ro passenger ships; $a_{lat} \leq 0.2g$.
- **Typical Allowable Steady-Heel under Wind:** $\theta_1 \leq 16^\circ$ for passenger ships; $\leq 25^\circ$ for cargo vessels.
- **Two-Curve Intersection:** Always plot $GZ$ *and* $l_{w1}\cos\phi$ together; loop area tells the dynamic story.
- **Units Vigilance:** $\Delta$ in tonnes → multiply denominator by 1000; $P$ in Pa; areas in m²; $z$ in m.
- **Real-World Case:** *MV Derbyshire* (1980) — bulk-carrier loss attributed to dynamic capsize despite static stability compliance — underscores why the *area* test (and not just the lever test) is non-negotiable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Hull Configuration on Controls-Fixed Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Hull Configuration on Controls-Fixed Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Controls-Fixed Stability (Bare-Hull Stability)**
> In naval architecture, *controls-fixed stability* refers to the restoring capability of a vessel (submarine or surface ship) when all its control surfaces — rudders, diving planes (hydroplanes), fairwater planes, and stern planes — are mechanically locked in their amidships/neutral position. Under this condition, the vessel behaves purely as a buoyant body whose restoring moments arise solely from the geometry, mass distribution, and underwater volume of the **hull configuration** itself.

Mathematically, the controls-fixed metacentric height is expressed as:

$$
GM_T^{CF} = KB + BM_T - KG
$$

where the **Transverse BM (BM_T)** is a *direct function of the waterplane geometry*, hence a direct function of the **hull configuration**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Log" Intuition

Imagine dropping a long, thin wooden log into a swimming pool. Now imagine dropping a short, fat disc-shaped piece of wood. Both float, but:

- The **long thin log** rolls (pitches) easily along its long axis because its waterplane is a very narrow strip — it has very little transverse *I_T*.
- The **short fat disc** is much stiffer against rolling because its waterplane is a wide circle — it has very high *I_T*.

This is *exactly* how hull configuration controls stability. A ship with a **wide beam-to-draft ratio** has high transverse GM. A ship with a **long, slender, fine-lined hull** has high longitudinal GM but low transverse GM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In Module 3 (Controls-Fixed and Controls-Free Stability), the examiner will *systematically* test how variations in hull length, beam, draft, prismatic coefficient, hull form, and appendages shift the **GMT**, **GML**, the **righting lever GZ**, and hence the **area under the GZ curve (dynamic stability)**. Students must be able to *qualitatively* and *quantitatively* predict the direction of change.

## 1.3 Key Hull Configuration Parameters (Standard Metrics)

The following **six (6) hull parameters** dictate controls-fixed stability:

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Length-to-beam ratio | **L/B** | Slenderness in plan view |
| Length-to-depth ratio | **L/D** | Slenderness in elevation (submarines) |
| Beam-to-draft ratio | **B/T** | Hull fullness vertically |
| Block coefficient | **C_B** | Volume fullness |
| Prismatic coefficient | **C_P** | Longitudinal taper of hull |
| Midship coefficient | **C_M** | Cross-sectional fullness |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stability "Levers" of Hull Configuration

The metacentric height **GM** is governed by **three independent levers**, all of which are pure hull-geometry functions when controls are fixed:

### Lever 1 — KB (Center of Buoyancy Position)
- **Hull Configurations that raise KB**: Deep-V hulls, fine entries, slender forebodies.
- **Hull Configurations that lower KB**: Full-form hulls (U-shaped), blocky cross-sections, increased draft.
- **Why?** *KB* depends on the longitudinal and vertical distribution of the underwater volume. A hull that displaces water higher up shifts *B* upward.

### Lever 2 — BM (Metacentric Radius — pure hull geometry function)
$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} \quad \text{where} \quad I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} \text{ (rectangular approx.)}
$$

- **Hull Configurations that raise BM_T**: Wider beam (*B³ effect is cubic!*), fuller waterplane, parallel middle body.
- **Hull Configurations that lower BM_T**: Fine ends, hollow waterplane, tumblehome, cruiser stern.

### Lever 3 — KG (Center of Gravity Position)
- **Hull Configurations that raise KG**: Heavy superstructures, tall deckhouses, deck-mounted machinery, high freeboard.
- **Hull Configurations that lower KG**: Deep double bottoms, heavy machinery in lower levels, low-profile hull forms.

## 2.2 Submarine-Specific Hull Configuration Effects (L/D Dominance)

For a submarine, the controlling parameter is the **Length-to-Diameter ratio (L/D)**:

$$
GM_T^{submarine} \propto \frac{1}{(L/D)^2}
$$

- **Low L/D (< 6)** — *Tuna-shape*: high transverse stability, sluggish diving response, poor seakeeping.
- **Medium L/D (6–9)** — *Optimum*: balanced stability, good diving characteristics, this is the modern SSK/SSN design sweet spot.
- **High L/D (> 10)** — *Needle-shape*: very low transverse stability, fast diving, prone to broaching, requires active controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:**
> Increasing the **L/D** of a submarine *always reduces GM_T* (transverse) and *always increases GML* (longitudinal) when controls are fixed. This is the central trade-off in submarine hull design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$ | Transverse metacentric height (controls fixed) | m |
| 2 | $GM_L = KB + BM_L - KG$ | Longitudinal metacentric height | m |
| 3 | $BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Transverse BM, where $\nabla$ = displaced volume | m |
| 4 | $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{\nabla}$ | Longitudinal BM, where $I_L = LB^3/12$ (approx.) | m |
| 5 | $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Rectangular waterplane transverse 2nd moment | m⁴ |
| 6 | $GZ = GM_T \cdot \sin\phi$ (small $\phi$) | Righting lever at heel $\phi$ | m |
| 7 | $GZ = KB \cdot \sin\phi - KG \cdot \sin(\phi - \theta)$ | Exact GZ (Attwood / exact method) | m |
| 8 | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ | Displacement = weight | N |
| 9 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Prismatic coefficient | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship section coefficient | dimensionless |
| 12 | $Dynamic \; Stability = \int_{0}^{\phi} GZ \, d\phi$ | Area under GZ curve | m·rad |
| 13 | $Range = \angle (GZ_{max})$ | Stability range of positive righting levers | deg |
| 14 | $Angle \; of \; Vanishing \; Stability (\phi_v)$ | Where GZ = 0 at large heel | deg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in every stage of ship design — from the *concept design* (parametric studies) to the *stability booklet* generation that must accompany every commercial vessel per IMO/SOLAS regulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Hull Configuration Enters GM (from First Principles)

We start from Archimedes' principle. Consider a vessel of weight *W* floating at draft *T*. When heeled by a small angle *dφ*, the center of buoyancy *B* shifts to a new position *B₁*. The line of action of buoyancy now passes through a point *M* — the **metacenter** — which is fixed for small angles.

**Step 1 — Define the righting moment.**
The moment arm between *G* (center of gravity) and the new buoyancy line is *GZ*. The righting moment is:

$$
M_R = W \cdot GZ
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose GZ using geometry.**
For a small heel angle *dφ*, the metacenter M is essentially fixed. From the triangle G-M-B:

$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi
$$

**Step 3 — Express GM using the three hull-configuration levers.**
By definition of metacenter, *BM* is the distance from *B* to *M*, which equals the *curvature radius* of the buoyancy locus:

$$
BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}
$$

This is the **key hull-geometry term** — it depends entirely on the second moment of area of the waterplane and the displaced volume. Substituting into the basic stability equation:

$$
GM_T = KB + \frac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the rectangular waterplane approximation.**
For a rectangular waterplane of length *L* and beam *B*:

$$
I_T = \int_A y^2 \, dA = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \int_{-B/2}^{B/2} y^2 \, dy \, dx
$$

Expanding the inner integral:

$$
\int_{-B/2}^{B/2} y^2 \, dy = \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{-B/2}^{B/2} = \frac{B^3}{24} - \left(-\frac{B^3}{24}\right) = \frac{B^3}{12}
$$

Multiplying by length *L*:

$$
I_T = L \cdot \frac{B^3}{12} = \frac{LB^3}{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the GZ at finite angle (Attwood formula).**
The Attwood formula is derived by assuming the underwater volume pivots about some convenient axis. The exact lever is:

$$
GZ = KB \cdot \sin\phi - KG \cdot \sin(\phi - \theta)
$$

where *θ* is the trim angle. For a non-trimming vessel (*θ = 0*):

$$
GZ = KB \cdot \sin\phi - KG \cdot \sin\phi = (KB - KG)\sin\phi = -GK \cdot \sin\phi
$$

For small angles, *sin φ ≈ φ*, and the linearized expression emerges. The full stability curve requires numerical integration of the exact Attwood expression at discrete heel angles (15°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 75°, 90°).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Hull Configuration Effect

> **Problem (KTU-typical):**
> A twin-screw frigate has the following particulars: *L* = 120 m, *B* = 14 m, *T* = 4.5 m, *C_B* = 0.55, *KG* = 5.8 m, *KB* = 2.4 m. The ship is then reconfigured with a **wider hull** (*B* = 16 m) at the **same draft and displacement**. Determine:
> (i) The change in *GM_T*.
> (ii) The new *GZ* at *φ* = 30° (using Attwood).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the original BM_T.**
We need *∇* first:

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = 0.55 \times 120 \times 14 \times 4.5
$$

Performing the multiplication step-by-step:

$$
120 \times 14 = 1680
$$

$$
1680 \times 4.5 = 7560
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.55 \times 7560 = 4158 \; m^3
$$

Original waterplane moment of inertia:

$$
I_T^{orig} = \frac{120 \times 14^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 2744}{12} = \frac{329280}{12} = 27440 \; m^4
$$

Original *BM_T*:

$$
BM_T^{orig} = \frac{27440}{4158} = 6.599 \; m
$$

Original *GM_T*:

$$
GM_T^{orig} = 2.4 + 6.599 - 5.8 = 3.199 \; m
$$

**Step 2 — Reconfigure the hull with new beam B = 16 m.**
The displacement is preserved, but the *waterplane shape* changes. The new waterplane moment of inertia:

$$
I_T^{new} = \frac{120 \times 16^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 4096}{12} = \frac{491520}{12} = 40960 \; m^4
$$

*Note: Since displacement is preserved, the displaced volume is unchanged, and the prismatic distribution is assumed preserved. The KB value will be slightly re-evaluated using a new block coefficient.*

New block coefficient (for same *∇* but new *B*):

$$
C_B^{new} = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B_{new} \cdot T} = \frac{4158}{120 \times 16 \times 4.5} = \frac{4158}{8640} = 0.4813
$$

*For a slightly finer block form, KB is approximately:*

$$
KB^{new} \approx \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{(1 + 4C_B^{new}/3)}{1 + 2C_B^{new}/3}
$$

Computing:

$$
\frac{2 C_B^{new}}{3} = \frac{2 \times 0.4813}{3} = 0.3209
$$

$$
1 + 0.3209 = 1.3209
$$

$$
\frac{4 C_B^{new}}{3} = 0.6417
$$

$$
1 + 0.6417 = 1.6417
$$

$$
\frac{1.6417}{1.3209} = 1.2429
$$

$$
KB^{new} = \frac{4.5}{2} \times 1.2429 = 2.25 \times 1.2429 = 2.797 \; m
$$

New *BM_T*:

$$
BM_T^{new} = \frac{40960}{4158} = 9.851 \; m
$$

New *GM_T*:

$$
GM_T^{new} = 2.797 + 9.851 - 5.8 = 6.848 \; m
$$

**Step 3 — Change in GM_T:**

$$
\Delta GM_T = 6.848 - 3.199 = +3.649 \; m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Widening the beam from 14 m to 16 m *more than doubled* the GM_T. The *B³* dependency in the moment of inertia makes beam the single most powerful hull-configuration lever for transverse stability.

**Step 4 — GZ at 30° using Attwood formula.**
At *φ* = 30° (≈ 0.5236 rad), *sin 30°* = 0.5. Assume *θ* = 0 (no trim). For a small *φ*:

$$
GZ \approx GM_T \cdot \sin\phi = 6.848 \times 0.5 = 3.424 \; m
$$

For the *exact* Attwood at *φ* = 30° (with sin 30° = 0.5):

$$
GZ = (KB - KG) \sin\phi + BM \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi
$$

Wait — this needs a more careful derivation. The **exact Attwood form** is:

$$
GZ = KB \sin\phi + BM \sin\phi \cos\phi - KG \sin\phi
$$

But the standard compact form (when *θ* = 0) is:

$$
GZ = (KB - KG) \sin\phi + BM \sin\phi \cos\phi
$$

Plugging in:

$$
GZ = (2.797 - 5.8)(0.5) + 9.851 \times 0.5 \times 0.8660
$$

$$
= (-3.003)(0.5) + 9.851 \times 0.4330
$$

$$
= -1.5015 + 4.265
$$

$$
GZ = 2.764 \; m
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hull Configuration → Stability Flow Architecture

The following block diagram captures the **cause-effect architecture** of how each hull-configuration variable feeds into the three stability levers, and ultimately into the righting moment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Hull Configuration Inputs] --> B1[Length L]
    A0 --> B2[Beam B]
    A0 --> B3[Draft T]
    A0 --> B4[Block Coeff Cb]
    A0 --> B5[Prismatic Coeff Cp]
    A0 --> B6[Appendages Sail Planes]
    A0 --> B7[Free Flooding Spaces]
    
    B1 --> C1[KB Position]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    
    B1 --> C2[BM via Waterplane IT]
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
    B5 --> C2
    
    B1 --> C3[KG Vertical Mass Dist]
    B2 --> C3
    B3 --> C3
    B6 --> C3
    B7 --> C3
    
    C1 --> D1[GMT Formulation]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    
    C1 --> D2[GML Formulation]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    
    D1 --> E1[Righting Lever GZ Phi]
    D2 --> E1
    
    E1 --> F1[Static Stability Area]
    E1 --> F2[Dynamic Stability Integral]
    E1 --> F3[Range Angle of Vanishing]
    
    F1 --> G1[IMO SOLAS Compliance]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Submarine L/D Ratio Effect on Stability — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB[Submarine Hull Form]
        SF1[Low L D less than 6 Tuna]
        SF2[Medium L D 6 to 9 Optimum]
        SF3[High L D greater than 10 Needle]
    end
    
    SF1 --> SA1[High GMT Robust]
    SF2 --> SA2[Balanced GMT Maneuverable]
    SF3 --> SA3[Low GMT Active Controls Needed]
    
    SA1 --> SB1[Slow Dive Sluggish]
    SA2 --> SB2[Fast Dive Stable]
    SA3 --> SB3[Very Fast Dive Unstable CF]
    
    SA1 --> SC1[Course Stable Poor Turn]
    SA2 --> SC2[Course Stable Good Turn]
    SA3 --> SC3[Course Unstable Excellent Turn]
```

## 4.3 GZ Curve Anatomy — Hull Configuration Impact

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[GZ Curve Critical Points] --> B1[Origin O at Phi equals 0]
    A0 --> B2[Maximum GZ Point]
    A0 --> B3[Angle of Maximum GZ Phi Max]
    A0 --> B4[Angle of Vanishing Stability Phi V]
    A0 --> B5[Angle of Deck Edge Immersion]
    
    C1[Full Hull Form] --> D1[Phi V high Greater than 90]
    C1 --> D2[Large GZ Max]
    C1 --> D3[Area Under Curve Large]
    
    C2[Fine Form Hull] --> D4[Phi V low 60 to 70]
    C2 --> D5[Smaller GZ Max]
    C2 --> D6[Area Under Curve Smaller]
    
    C3[Wide Beam Hull] --> D7[GM T Large Initial Slope Steep]
    C3 --> D8[Dynamic Stability High]
    
    C4[Tumblehome Hull] --> D9[GM T Reduced]
    C4 --> D10[Phi V Reduced Significantly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** These mermaid flow charts show the *causal chain* from raw hull-geometry inputs → hydrostatic levers (KB, BM, KG) → metacentric heights (GM_T, GM_L) → righting lever curve (GZ) → IMO compliance check. For physical hull-line drawings, students are advised to use **Maxsurf**, **Rhino**, or **Freeship** software in their design studios.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define the term "controls-fixed stability" and list the three hull-geometry parameters that have the strongest influence on it.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Controls-fixed stability** is the inherent restoring capability of a vessel when all its control surfaces (rudder, diving planes, hydroplanes) are locked in their amidships position. Under this condition, the vessel behaves as a pure buoyant body.

**The three dominant hull-geometry parameters are:**

1. **Length-to-beam ratio (L/B)** — controls the *plan-form slenderness* and the ratio of *BM_T to BM_L*.
2. **Beam-to-draft ratio (B/T)** — directly scales *BM_T* (via the *B³* effect on waterplane inertia).
3. **Prismatic / block coefficient (*C_P, C_B*)** — controls the longitudinal and transverse distribution of buoyancy, hence *KB*.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**State the Attwood formula for the righting lever *GZ* at any angle of heel. Why is the exact form preferred over the small-angle approximation *GZ = GM·sin φ* for hull-configuration studies?**

**Model Answer:**

The exact Attwood formula (no trim) is:

$$
GZ = (KB - KG)\sin\phi + BM \sin\phi \cos\phi
$$

**Why exact form is preferred:**

- The linearized form $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$ is only valid for $\phi < 10°$–15°.
- For **hull-configuration studies** (e.g., comparing a fine-entry vs. a full-form hull), the *shape* of the GZ curve between 30°–90° — where deck-edge immersion and bilge geometry dominate — must be evaluated.
- Different hull forms change the **angle of maximum GZ ($\phi_{max}$)** and the **angle of vanishing stability ($\phi_v$)**, both of which only appear in the exact form.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 3 Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Hull Reconfiguration Study (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3]**

A bulk carrier has the following original particulars: *L* = 150 m, *B* = 22 m, *T* = 9.0 m, *C_B* = 0.78, *KG* = 8.5 m.

**(a)** Compute the original *GM_T* and the righting lever *GZ* at 20° heel (assume no trim). (7 marks)

**(b)** The hull is **reconfigured with a finer entrance** (reducing *C_P* from 0.82 to 0.75) at **constant displacement**. Assuming *KB* reduces by 4% and *BM_T* reduces by 7% due to the more hollow waterplane, compute the **new *GM_T*** and comment on the implications for IMO weather-criterion stability. (7 marks)

---

### **Question A — Complete Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Original Configuration

**Step 1 — Displaced volume:**

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = 0.78 \times 150 \times 22 \times 9.0
$$

Computing stepwise:

$$
150 \times 22 = 3300
$$

$$
3300 \times 9.0 = 29700
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.78 \times 29700 = 23166 \; m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Waterplane moment of inertia (rectangular approx.):**

$$
I_T = \frac{LB^3}{12} = \frac{150 \times 22^3}{12}
$$

$$
22^3 = 10648
$$

$$
150 \times 10648 = 1597200
$$

$$
I_T = \frac{1597200}{12} = 133100 \; m^4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute *BM_T*:**

$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{133100}{23166} = 5.745 \; m
$$

**Step 4 — Compute *KB* (using empirical Biles formula for block coefficient form):**

$$
KB \approx \frac{T}{3}\left(1 + \frac{1 - C_B}{2 C_B}\right) \cdot \text{(refined form)}
$$

Or use a simpler estimate: $KB \approx 0.53 \cdot T$ for a full-form hull:

$$
KB \approx 0.53 \times 9.0 = 4.77 \; m
$$

**Step 5 — Compute *GM_T*:**

$$
GM_T = 4.77 + 5.745 - 8.5 = 2.015 \; m
$$

**Step 6 — GZ at 20° using exact Attwood:**

$$
GZ = (KB - KG) \sin\phi + BM \sin\phi \cos\phi
$$

With *φ* = 20°, *sin 20°* = 0.3420, *cos 20°* = 0.9397:

$$
GZ = (4.77 - 8.5)(0.3420) + 5.745 \times 0.3420 \times 0.9397
$$

$$
= (-3.73)(0.3420) + 5.745 \times 0.3214
$$

$$
= -1.276 + 1.846
$$

$$
GZ = 0.570 \; m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (a) — Final Answer:** $GM_T = 2.015$ m; $GZ_{20°} = 0.570$ m. This is barely positive, suggesting the vessel is close to its intact-stability limit.

#### Part (b) — Reconfigured Hull

**Step 1 — Apply the given perturbations:**

$$
KB_{new} = KB \times (1 - 0.04) = 4.77 \times 0.96 = 4.579 \; m
$$

$$
BM_{new} = BM \times (1 - 0.07) = 5.745 \times 0.93 = 5.343 \; m
$$

*KG* is assumed unchanged (no mass redistribution): *KG* = 8.5 m.

**Step 2 — New *GM_T*:**

$$
GM_T^{new} = 4.579 + 5.343 - 8.5 = 1.422 \; m
$$

**Step 3 — Change in *GM_T*:**

$$
\Delta GM_T = 1.422 - 2.015 = -0.593 \; m
$$

This is a **29.4% reduction** in metacentric height.

**Step 4 — Implications for IMO Weather Criterion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commentary:**
> - The **29% drop in GM_T** moves the vessel into a marginal stability regime. For a bulk carrier, IMO Resolution A.749(18) requires the area under the GZ curve up to 30° to be ≥ 0.055 m·rad, and up to 40° (or *φ_f*, whichever is smaller) to be ≥ 0.090 m·rad.
> - With *GM_T* reduced from 2.015 m to 1.422 m, the GZ at 30° will drop proportionally, and the area under the GZ curve will shrink by approximately the same factor.
> - **Conclusion:** The finer-entry reconfiguration **fails IMO weather-criterion** unless compensated by (a) lowering KG through rearrangement of cargo, or (b) fitting passive stability-boosting appendages such as bilge keels.

---

### **Question B — Submarine L/D Effect (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Internal Choice]**

A modern attack submarine has the following particulars: *L* = 75 m, *D* (hull diameter) = 9.5 m, *KG* (at submerged condition) = at the centerline (0 m relative to hull axis), *KB* (submerged, fully flooded) = 0 m (by symmetry).

**(a)** Compute the controls-fixed transverse metacentric height *GM_T* for this submarine, treating the waterplane as the circular cross-section at the upper hull limit. (7 marks)

**(b)** The submarine is reconfigured with a *tapered hull* (Hammerhead form) such that the effective waterplane *I_T* reduces to **55% of the cylindrical value**, but the *L/D* ratio increases to **9.5**. Compute the new *GM_T* and discuss whether the vessel can be safely operated *with controls fixed* at periscope depth. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B — Complete Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Cylindrical Hull

**Step 1 — Submerged volume (cylindrical approximation):**

$$
\nabla = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} (9.5)^2 \times 75
$$

Computing:

$$
9.5^2 = 90.25
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} \times 90.25 = 0.7854 \times 90.25 = 70.882
$$

$$
\nabla = 70.882 \times 75 = 5316.15 \; m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Waterplane moment of inertia (circular cross-section):**

For a circle of diameter *D*:

$$
I_T = \frac{\pi D^4}{64}
$$

Computing:

$$
D^4 = 9.5^4 = 8145.0625
$$

$$
I_T = \frac{\pi \times 8145.0625}{64} = \frac{25588.5}{64} = 399.82 \; m^4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute *BM_T*:**

$$
BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{399.82}{5316.15} = 0.0752 \; m
$$

**Step 4 — Compute *GM_T*:**
For fully submerged condition with *KG = KB = 0* (centered mass on the axis):

$$
GM_T = 0 + 0.0752 - 0 = 0.0752 \; m \; (\approx 7.5 \; cm)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (a) — Final Answer:** $GM_T \approx 0.075$ m. This is an *extremely small* metacentric height, illustrating the well-known fact that submarines are **inherently unstable in pure controls-fixed submerged condition**. Active depth control via planes is mandatory.

#### Part (b) — Tapered (Hammerhead) Configuration

**Step 1 — New *I_T* (55% of original):**

$$
I_T^{new} = 0.55 \times 399.82 = 219.90 \; m^4
$$

**Step 2 — Submerged volume (tapered — assume a 5% reduction due to taper):**

$$
\nabla^{new} = 0.95 \times 5316.15 = 5050.34 \; m^3
$$

**Step 3 — New *BM_T*:**

$$
BM_T^{new} = \frac{219.90}{5050.34} = 0.04354 \; m
$$

**Step 4 — *KG* and *KB* adjustment:**
With the tapered (Hammerhead) form, the center of buoyancy shifts *slightly forward* and *slightly upward* relative to the geometric center. The new *KB* offset (from the centerline reference) is *approximately zero* (submerged, so no waterplane — the "KB" is essentially the centroid of the displaced volume). For a tapered hull symmetric fore-aft, *KB* ≈ 0.

*KG* also shifts because the heavier tapered ends have more mass distributed away from the center. However, by trim-and-balance design, *KG* is maintained ≈ 0 (this is one of the design constraints of a submarine).

**Step 5 — New *GM_T*:**

$$
GM_T^{new} = 0 + 0.04354 - 0 = 0.0435 \; m
$$

**Step 6 — Discussion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Discussion:**
> - The new *GM_T* = **0.0435 m** is *less than 60%* of the original. This is **below the threshold** for safe controls-fixed operation at periscope depth (typical safe minimum: 0.05 m).
> - **Stability Margins Lost:** $0.0752 \to 0.0435$ represents a 42% reduction in transverse metacentric height.
> - **Conclusion:** The vessel *cannot be safely operated with controls fixed*. The reduction in *I_T* (because of the tapered waterplane area) and the marginal change in volume both conspire to drop *GM_T* below the safety threshold.
> - **Mitigation:** The vessel must rely on **active depth-control planes** (fairwater planes + stern planes) at all times at periscope depth. Submerged transit must be at **safe speed** with the planes engaged.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the *B³* effect:** When asked how a hull-config change affects *BM_T*, students often write "linear" or "square" dependence. **It is CUBIC in beam.** Missing this loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing *GMT* and *GML*:** Width of the hull *raises GMT* but *lowers GML*. Students often get this reversed.
> 3. **Sign errors in *Attwood*:** The term $(KB - KG)$ is **negative** for a high-KG vessel. Watch the algebra.
> 4. **Submarine trap:** Submerged *KB* is *not* the half-draft. For a fully submerged body, *KB* is the centroid of the displaced volume, which for a symmetric hull is the geometric center. Do **not** apply surface-ship *KB = T/2* logic.
> 5. **Apparent vs. real *GZ*:** The small-angle formula $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ is **not** valid beyond ~15°. Examiners love to test this at 30°, 45°, 60°.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block:**

- **Controls-fixed stability** = restoring capability with control surfaces locked. Depends *only* on **hull geometry + mass distribution**.
- **Master Equation:** $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$. All three terms are hull-configuration functions.
- **Beam is the king of transverse stability** — *BM_T ∝ B³*. A 10% increase in beam yields ~33% increase in *BM_T*.
- **Length is the king of longitudinal stability** — *BM_L ∝ L*. A 10% increase in length yields ~10% increase in *BM_L*.
- **Draft (T)** raises *KB* and *BM_T* both — net effect on *GM_T* is *positive* for a high-KG vessel.
- **Block coefficient *C_B*** lowers *KB* when *C_B* increases (full-form hulls have low *KB*).
- **Prismatic coefficient *C_P*** affects the *longitudinal distribution* of buoyancy, hence trim and *GZ* at large angles.
- **Submarines: L/D ratio** — high L/D = low *GM_T* and high *GML* (controls fixed).
- **Tumblehome / cruiser stern / fin form** *reduce* *BM_T* by hollowing the waterplane.
- **Bilge keels, fins, skegs** are **appendages** — they affect *BM_T* by adding virtual waterplane area.
- **Free-flooding spaces (ballast tanks, free-flood voids)** are *excluded* from buoyancy calculations; they are *included* in mass for *KG* — so they reduce *GM_T*.
- **IMO SOLAS intact-stability criteria** are checked using the *area under the GZ curve*, *range of positive stability*, and *GZ_max* — all of which are pure hull-configuration outputs.
- **Attwood's exact formula** must be used beyond ~15° heel. Linearized $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ is for *small angles only*.
- **Righting moment** = $W \cdot GZ$. Heeling moment from wind/wave must be less for stability.
- **Dynamic stability** = $\int_0^\phi GZ \, d\phi$ — this is the *energy* the ship can absorb before capsizing.
- **Angle of vanishing stability $\phi_v$** is the *upper bound* of positive righting levers — beyond this, the ship *capsizes*.
- **Design rule of thumb:** A well-designed surface ship has $\phi_v > 90°$ (i.e., GZ remains positive even when the ship is on its beam-ends).
- **For a submarine:** $\phi_v \to 180°$ because there is no deck-edge immersion in submerged condition — the *submarine cannot capsize* (it can only roll indefinitely without divergence), but it is *unstable* for *small* perturbations without active controls.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turning Circle Manoeuvre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Turning Circle Manoeuvre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Turning Circle Manoeuvre** (also called the **Turning Trial** or **Over-the-Ground Turning Test**) is a standardised sea-trial procedure in which a vessel, proceeding at a steady trial speed and trimmed to its normal seagoing condition, is given a hard, constant rudder angle (conventionally **35° to one side**) and held on that rudder until the ship completes a full 360° turn and reaches a steady turning condition.

The path traced by the ship's centre of gravity (or pivot point) on the surface of the earth, relative to its original course line, is plotted and the characteristic geometric quantities — **Advance**, **Transfer**, **Tactical Diameter**, **Steady Turning Diameter**, and **Steady Turning Period** — are extracted. These parameters quantitatively describe the ship's **course-keeping and course-changing ability** and form the international benchmark for manoeuvrability under **IMO Resolution MSC.137(76)** (Standards for Ship Manoeuvrability, 2002).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag (PCNST501 / Module 3):**
> *"Turning circle, advance, transfer, tactical diameter, steady turning diameter, spiral test, IMO manoeuvrability criteria."*
> The turning circle is the single most heavily-tested topic in Module 3 of Ship Dynamics.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a Maruti car versus a loaded Volvo truck around a roundabout. You give each the **same hard steering lock**. The car obediently curls into a *tight* circle almost immediately. The truck, because of its **momentum, length, and the time needed for its front wheels to "drag" the rear axle around**, traces a *much bigger* circle and takes a *much longer* time to complete a full revolution.

A ship behaves exactly like that truck — except on a colossal scale. A 200-metre tanker cannot turn "instantly". The rudder deflects the water, the water pushes the stern, the stern swings, and the bow follows. The whole ship slowly curves onto a circular path. The *size* of that circle and the *time* taken to complete it are the **Turning Circle Parameters**, and they decide whether a ship can safely navigate canals, harbours, and crowded shipping lanes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (Memorise):**
> For a typical, well-designed single-screw merchant ship, the **Tactical Diameter ≈ 4 to 5 × Lpp**, where Lpp is the length between perpendiculars. This is the "rule of thumb" examiners love to test.

## Standard KTU Sea-Trial Conditions

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Rudder angle (δ) | **35°** |
| Trial speed | Service / design speed, **deep water** |
| Loading condition | **Full load (ballast to freeboard)** |
| Water depth | **Deep water** (depth > 3 × draught) |
| Wind / current | Negligible (or corrected) |
| Recording method | Radar plotting, GPS track, DGPS |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of the Turning Circle

The complete turning-circle motion is divided into three distinct phases. Each phase has its own physics.

### Phase I — Approach Phase (0° to ~90° heading change)
- The rudder is put to **35°** and held.
- A **transverse force** is created on the stern (opposite to rudder side).
- A **yaw moment** starts rotating the ship.
- A **drift angle (β)** builds up at the ship's centre of gravity.
- The ship begins to **lose forward speed** (typically drops to 50–70 % of trial speed).
- The trajectory is a **transitioning spiral** — not yet a circle.

> **Why this matters:** This is the *most dangerous* phase, especially for a VLCC or a container ship, because the ship is still moving forward into waters ahead of it while the bow is swinging. The bridge team must anticipate this.

### Phase II — Steady Turning Phase (after ~180° heading change)
- The drift angle **β** and the **rudder angle δ** reach equilibrium.
- The centripetal force (provided by rudder + hull lateral force) balances the centrifugal demand.
- The ship now follows a **near-circular path** of constant radius.
- The **steady turning diameter (D)** and the **steady turning period (T)** become measurable.

### Phase III — Completion (360° back to original heading)
- The original heading is crossed, but the ship is now displaced sideways from its starting point by a distance called the **Reach**.
- Engine telegraph is returned; rudder is centralised; the ship straightens out.

## 2.2 The Five Canonical Turning-Circle Parameters

1. **Advance (A)** — Distance travelled along the **original course direction**, measured from the point where the rudder was ordered to the point where the ship's heading has changed by **90°**.

2. **Transfer (T)** — Distance travelled **perpendicular to the original course**, measured at the same instant (i.e., when heading has changed by 90°). Sometimes called the *90° transfer*.

3. **Tactical Diameter (D_T)** — Distance **perpendicular to the original course** measured at the point where the ship has changed heading by **180°**. This is the *most quoted* parameter.

4. **Steady Turning Diameter (D)** — Diameter of the *final, steady-state* circle the ship follows after the transient dies out. Numerically: D = 2 × R, where R is the steady-state radius.

5. **Steady Turning Period (T_p)** — Time taken to complete one revolution in steady turning. Typical values: 60–90 s for a frigate, 4–6 minutes for a VLCC.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula set** for KTU Module 3. Memorise units, not just the symbols. Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all answers to avoid markdown rendering errors.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Range (Merchant) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed reduction during turn | V / V₀ | 0.5 – 0.7 | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Drift angle (steady) | β | $ \beta \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{v}{u}\right) $ | 3° – 10° |
| 3 | Advance | A | $ A \;\approx\; (1.0 \text{ to } 2.0)\,L_{pp} $ | metres |
| 4 | Transfer (at 90°) | Tr | $ Tr \;\approx\; (0.8 \text{ to } 1.2)\,L_{pp} $ | metres |
| 5 | Tactical Diameter | D_T | $ D_{T} \;\approx\; (4.0 \text{ to } 5.0)\,L_{pp} $ | metres |
| 6 | Steady Turning Diameter | D | $ D \;\approx\; 0.9 \cdot D_{T} $ | metres |
| 7 | Steady turning radius | R | $ R \;=\; \dfrac{D}{2} $ | metres |
| 8 | Centripetal accel. (steady) | a_n | $ a_{n} \;=\; \dfrac{V^{2}}{R} $ | m/s² |
| 9 | Speed (steady turn) | V | $ V \;\approx\; 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8\,V_{0} $ | m/s |
| 10 | Steering ratio (rudder) | — | $ \delta \;=\; 35^{\circ} $ | degrees |
| 11 | IMO Tactical-Diameter limit | D_T | $ D_{T} \;\le\; 5\,L_{pp} $ | Regulation |
| 12 | IMO Advance limit | A | $ A \;\le\; 4.5\,L_{pp} $ | Regulation |
| 13 | IMO Speed-loss in turn | V / V₀ | $ \ge\; 0.6 $ (cargo), $ \ge\; 0.7 $ (passenger) | Regulation |

**Where:**
- $L_{pp}$ = Length between perpendiculars (m)
- $u$ = surge velocity, $v$ = sway velocity (m/s)
- $V_0$ = initial straight-running speed

## 2.4 Spiral Test — The Companion Test

The spiral test is run **before** the turning-circle test and provides the *initial conditions* (rudder angle) for it. With a small starting rudder, the heading is slowly increased to 35° and then decreased, while recording the equilibrium heading angle for each rudder. The plot of *rudder angle (δ) vs steady-state heading rate (r)* is the **Spiral Curve** and reveals:
- **Stable steering** (curve crosses origin in one place — desirable)
- **Instability** (curve does not pass through origin — dangerous; corrective fin/stabiliser may be needed)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a question says "the ship tends to yaw on a straight course and is hard to steer on a fixed heading", the answer lies in the **spiral test**, not the turning circle. The two tests are related but answer different questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Harbour design:** Channel width and bend radius are designed using the worst-case **tactical diameter** of the largest ship expected.
- **Pilotage:** Maritime pilots memorise the *advance* of each vessel class so they know how much "head reach" the ship needs before the stern follows.
- **COLREGS compliance:** Rule 8 of the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea explicitly requires that *the turning ability of the ship* be considered when manoeuvring to avoid collision. Turning-circle data is the proof.
- **Course-Keeping Autopilot Tuning:** PID gains of the steering autopilot are calibrated using spiral-test data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tactical Diameter from Geometric Projection

Consider a ship at point **O** sailing due East at speed V₀. At time t = 0, the helm is put **35° to starboard** and held. The ship's centre of gravity traces the curve **O → P → Q → R → ...** (as shown in the diagram in §4). Let the original heading line be the **X-axis** and the perpendicular through O be the **Y-axis**.

**Step 1 — Coordinate setup.**
At any time t, the ship's position is $(x(t),\,y(t))$ in the fixed Earth frame, and its heading is $\psi(t)$ measured positive clockwise from the X-axis.

**Step 2 — Velocity components in the Earth frame.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) \;&=\; u(t)\,\cos\psi(t) \;-\; v(t)\,\sin\psi(t) \\
\dot{y}(t) \;&=\; u(t)\,\sin\psi(t) \;+\; v(t)\,\cos\psi(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reason:** This is the standard 2-D coordinate transformation from the ship-fixed frame (u, v) to the Earth-fixed frame using heading angle ψ.

**Step 3 — Definition of Advance.**
Advance A is the *x-coordinate* when the heading has changed by exactly 90°, i.e. when $\psi(t^{\star}) = 90^{\circ}$.

$$
A \;\equiv\; x\!\left(t^{\star}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \psi\!\left(t^{\star}\right) = 90^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Definition of Transfer.**
Transfer Tr is the corresponding *y-coordinate* at the same instant.

$$
Tr \;\equiv\; y\!\left(t^{\star}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \psi\!\left(t^{\star}\right) = 90^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Definition of Tactical Diameter.**
Tactical diameter is the *y-coordinate* when the heading has changed by 180°, i.e. $\psi(t^{\star\star}) = 180^{\circ}$.

$$
D_{T} \;\equiv\; y\!\left(t^{\star\star}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \psi\!\left(t^{\star\star}\right) = 180^{\circ}
$$

**Step 6 — Practical extraction (for sea-trial plots).**
The values of A, Tr, and D_T are read directly off the **radar / GPS track plot** using a parallel rule or a digital equivalent. No closed-form formula exists for an arbitrary hull form; empirical ratios are used.

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{D_{T}}{L_{pp}} \;\in\; [4,\; 5] \qquad \dfrac{A}{L_{pp}} \;\in\; [1.0,\; 2.0] \qquad \dfrac{Tr}{L_{pp}} \;\in\; [0.8,\; 1.2]\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady Turning Period and Centripetal Acceleration

In the **steady turning phase**, the ship has constant speed V, constant drift β, and constant yaw rate r = dψ/dt. The path is a circle of radius R.

**Step 1 — Kinematic relation between yaw rate and speed.**

$$
r \;=\; \dfrac{d\psi}{dt} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{R} \quad \text{(radians per second)}
$$

**Reason:** For circular motion, arc length = R × angle, and arc length per second = V. Hence V/R = angular rate.

**Step 2 — Steady turning period.**

The ship traverses 2π radians in one full revolution, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{p} \;&=\; \dfrac{2\pi}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi R}{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Centripetal acceleration experienced by the ship.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{n} \;&=\; \dfrac{V^{2}}{R} \;=\; V \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-favourite).**
A frigate has Lpp = 120 m, V₀ = 15 knots, and the trial produced R = 250 m.
*(i) Find the steady turning period. (ii) Find the centripetal acceleration.*

Convert speed: V = 15 × 0.5144 = **7.716 m/s**.

**(i) Turning period:**

$$
T_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 250}{7.716} \;=\; \dfrac{1570.8}{7.716} \;\approx\; \boxed{203.6\ \text{seconds} \;\approx\; 3\text{ min } 24\text{ s}}
$$

**(ii) Centripetal acceleration:**

$$
a_{n} \;=\; \dfrac{(7.716)^{2}}{250} \;=\; \dfrac{59.54}{250} \;\approx\; \boxed{0.238\ \text{m/s}^{2} \;\approx\; 0.024\,g}
$$

**Interpretation:** A centripetal acceleration of ~0.024 g is mild; for a VLCC, however, R can be 800 m at V = 8 m/s, giving a_n ≈ 0.08 m/s² (still small), but the *reach* is so long that bridge crews have to plan evasive manoeuvres several minutes in advance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Approximate Tactical Diameter from the MMG (Manoeuvring Modelling Group) Equations

For derivations beyond the empirical range, the **MMG model** is used. The lateral equation of motion in the ship-fixed frame is:

$$
m\,\left(\dot{v} \;+\; u\,r\right) \;=\; Y_{\beta}\,\beta v \;+\; Y_{r}\,r \;+\; Y_{\delta}\,\delta
$$

where the right-hand side terms are the **sway-force derivatives** due to drift, yaw rate, and rudder respectively. In steady turning, $\dot{v} = 0$ and the equations reduce to two algebraic equations in β and r, solved for a given δ = 35°:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \;&=\; Y_{\beta}\,\beta \,V \;+\; \left(Y_{r} - m\,u\right)\,r \;+\; Y_{\delta}\,\delta \\[4pt]
N_{\beta}\,\beta \,V \;&+\; \left(N_{r} - m\,x_{G}\,u\right)\,r \;+\; N_{\delta}\,\delta \;\approx\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
\boxed{\;r \;\approx\; \dfrac{-\,N_{\beta}\,Y_{\delta} \;+\; N_{\delta}\,Y_{\beta}}{N_{r}\,Y_{\beta} \;-\; Y_{r}\,N_{\beta}}\;\delta \quad \text{and} \quad R \;\approx\; \dfrac{V}{r}\;}
$$

**Reason:** This is the standard 2-DOF linearised turning solution. KTU students are not expected to derive this from scratch, but **should be able to state it, identify the terms, and use it to compute R for a given δ**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Turning Circle vs. Spiral Test)

| Aspect | Turning Circle Test | Spiral Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Measure turning trajectory parameters | Identify stable/unstable steering behaviour |
| **Rudder input** | Constant 35° | Slowly increasing then decreasing δ |
| **Output quantity** | A, Tr, D_T, D, T_p | δ vs. r equilibrium curve |
| **Steady-state result** | Stable circular motion | May diverge (unstable) or converge (stable) |
| **Time to complete** | ~10 – 30 min | ~45 – 60 min |
| **KTU question type** | "Compute D_T for Lpp = 200 m at 35°" | "What does the spiral test reveal about a yawing ship?" |
| **IMO regulation?** | Yes (MSC.137(76)) | No (research/development only) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem (14-Mark Style):**
A merchant ship has Lpp = 180 m, B = 28 m, draught T = 10.5 m, and trial speed V₀ = 18 knots. During a turning-circle trial with δ = 35° to starboard, the following observations are made:
- Distance travelled along original course at 90° heading change: **295 m**
- Distance perpendicular at 90° heading change: **195 m**
- Distance perpendicular at 180° heading change: **810 m**
- Steady turning diameter: **760 m**

**(a) Calculate the Advance, Transfer, Tactical Diameter, and Steady Turning Period. Compare the results with IMO Resolution MSC.137(76) criteria. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the ship is proceeding in the Arabian Sea at the same speed and encounters a fishing boat 600 m ahead on its course, the Master decides an emergency turn of 35° to port. Calculate the time available before the bow begins swinging, and the lateral clearance achieved by the time the bow has swung 60°. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Read off the standard parameters directly.**

$$
A \;=\; 295\ \text{m}, \qquad Tr \;=\; 195\ \text{m}, \qquad D_{T} \;=\; 810\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute non-dimensional ratios.**

$$
\dfrac{A}{L_{pp}} \;=\; \dfrac{295}{180} \;\approx\; 1.64
$$

$$
\dfrac{D_{T}}{L_{pp}} \;=\; \dfrac{810}{180} \;=\; 4.5
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with IMO Standards (MSC.137(76)).**

- IMO Advance criterion: $A \le 4.5\,L_{pp} = 810$ m. Our value **295 m < 810 m → PASSES**. ✓
- IMO Tactical-Diameter criterion: $D_{T} \le 5\,L_{pp} = 900$ m. Our value **810 m < 900 m → PASSES**. ✓
- IMO Speed-loss criterion (≥ 0.6 of V₀ for cargo ships): Need separate measurement. Assume it is met.

**Step 4 — Compute Steady Turning Period.**

Convert V₀: $V = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.26$ m/s. Steady turning radius R = 760/2 = 380 m.

$$
T_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi R}{V} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 380}{9.26} \;\approx\; \boxed{257.8\ \text{s} \;\approx\; 4\ \text{min } 18\ \text{s}}
$$

### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Time available before the bow swings.**

In a 35° turn, the *bow begins swinging the moment the rudder is applied*. There is **no delay** for the rudder action itself. However, there is a *drift-buildup* period of roughly 10–15 s before measurable swing. Conservative answer: **rudder applied immediately, swing begins at t ≈ 0 s**.

**Step 2 — Lateral clearance at 60° heading change.**

Empirical rule: at 60° heading change, the lateral offset ≈ 0.7 × Transfer (Tr).

$$
y_{60^{\circ}} \;\approx\; 0.7 \times 195 \;\approx\; 136.5\ \text{m}
$$

(For a precise answer, integrate the trajectory using the MMG equations; for KTU, the empirical rule is accepted.)

**Step 3 — Time to swing 60°.**

For a 35°-turn at 18 knots, empirical: angular rate ≈ 0.7°/s during the approach phase. So:

$$
t_{60^{\circ}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{60^{\circ}}{0.7^{\circ}/\text{s}} \;\approx\; 86\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

The ship will have yawed by 60° (bow now pointing 60° off original) in ~86 s, with a lateral offset of ~137 m. The Master must ensure the *bow arc* (radius ≈ 137 m from the original track) does not intersect the fishing boat. Since the boat is **600 m ahead** (along the original course) and the ship swings left, the bow will *miss* the boat laterally as long as the lateral offset at the time the bow passes the boat's latitude is less than the safe passing distance. For KTU, the qualitative conclusion is: **the turn is feasible** because the ship's tactical diameter (810 m) is comparable to the available sea-room.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Turning Circle Trajectory — Nodal Schematic

The diagram below uses a **block-architecture** representation. Physical hand-drawing of the spiral trajectory is replaced by a labelled node-flow mapping the **Advance (A)**, **Transfer (Tr)**, **Tactical Diameter (D_T)**, and **Steady Turning Diameter (D)** onto the geometry, with all node IDs purely alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid safeguard rule.

```mermaid
graph TD
    O["ORIGIN O: HELM ORDERED 35 deg"] --> A1["POINT P1: HEADING 45 deg"]
    A1 --> A2["POINT P2: HEADING 90 deg - ADVANCE AND TRANSFER READ"]
    A2 --> A3["POINT P3: HEADING 135 deg"]
    A3 --> A4["POINT P4: HEADING 180 deg - TACTICAL DIAMETER READ"]
    A4 --> A5["POINT P5: HEADING 270 deg"]
    A5 --> A6["POINT P6: STEADY TURNING CYCLE COMPLETE"]
    A6 --> A7["POINT P7: HELM MIDLINE - SHIP STEADY ON NEW COURSE"]

    O -.->|x-axis: original course| X["ADVANCE A measured along X"]
    O -.->|y-axis: perpendicular| Y["TRANSFER Tr measured along Y at 90 deg"]
    O -.->|y at 180 deg| YT["TACTICAL DIAMETER D_T measured along Y at 180 deg"]
    A6 -.->|final loop diameter| D["STEADY TURNING DIAMETER D = 2R"]

    style O fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#B8E0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A4 fill:#FFB8B8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A7 fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** The five coloured nodes correspond to the five *measurement stations* of a real turning-circle plot. **Yellow = start**, **Blue = 90° point (A & Tr)**, **Red = 180° point (D_T)**, **Green = completion of one steady revolution**. The dotted lines show *which length is read from where*.

## 4.2 The Three Phases as a Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PHASE1["PHASE I: APPROACH 0 to 90 deg"]
        P1A["Rudder 35 deg applied"]
        P1B["Transverse force on stern"]
        P1C["Drift angle beta builds"]
        P1D["Yaw rate r increases"]
        P1A --> P1B --> P1C --> P1D
    end
    subgraph PHASE2["PHASE II: STEADY TURNING beyond 180 deg"]
        P2A["beta and r reach steady values"]
        P2B["Ship traces circular path"]
        P2C["D and T_p measured"]
        P2A --> P2B --> P2C
    end
    subgraph PHASE3["PHASE III: COMPLETION 360 deg"]
        P3A["Helm returned to midship"]
        P3B["Ship straightens on new course"]
        P3C["Reach measured at 360 deg"]
        P3A --> P3B --> P3C
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2 --> PHASE3

    style PHASE1 fill:#FFE7B0
    style PHASE2 fill:#B0E0FF
    style PHASE3 fill:#C8F0C8
```

## 4.3 IMO Manoeuvrability Compliance Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Sea trial conducted"] --> Q1{"Is A <= 4.5 Lpp?"}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{"Is D_T <= 5 Lpp?"}
    Q1 -->|NO| FAIL1["FAIL: Advance exceeds IMO limit"]
    Q2 -->|YES| Q3{"Is V / V0 >= 0.6?"}
    Q2 -->|NO| FAIL2["FAIL: Tactical diameter exceeds IMO limit"]
    Q3 -->|YES| PASS["PASS: Ship meets MSC.137(76)"]
    Q3 -->|NO| FAIL3["FAIL: Excessive speed loss in turn"]

    style PASS fill:#90EE90
    style FAIL1 fill:#FFB0B0
    style FAIL2 fill:#FFB0B0
    style FAIL3 fill:#FFB0B0
```

> [!TIP]
> This decision block is the **single most useful chart** for answering KTU "compare and comment on manoeuvrability" type 14-mark questions. Draw it in the answer script for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *Tactical Diameter* of a ship. State the IMO limit for tactical diameter as per Resolution MSC.137(76).

**Model Answer:**
The Tactical Diameter (D_T) of a ship is the distance, measured perpendicular to the original course line, between the track of the ship's centre of gravity at the moment the ship's heading has changed by **180°** and the original course line. It is the most representative single measure of a ship's turning ability. As per **IMO Resolution MSC.137(76)**, the tactical diameter of a ship of length Lpp shall not exceed **5 × Lpp** for ships above 100 m in length. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** What is the *spiral test*? How does it differ from the turning-circle test?

**Model Answer:**
The spiral test is a sea-trial procedure in which the rudder is put to a small angle, and then **slowly and continuously increased to 35° and then back to zero**, while recording the equilibrium heading (yaw rate) for each rudder angle. The resulting plot of rudder angle vs steady-state yaw rate is called the spiral curve. **Difference from turning-circle test:** the turning-circle test uses a *constant* 35° rudder and measures geometric trajectory parameters (Advance, Transfer, Tactical Diameter); the spiral test uses a *continuously varying* rudder and measures *steering stability*. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Turning Circle Manoeuvre** procedure. Define the terms *Advance*, *Transfer*, *Tactical Diameter*, and *Steady Turning Diameter*. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A bulk carrier of **Lpp = 175 m** performs a turning-circle trial at **V₀ = 16 knots** with **35° rudder**. The trial results are:
> - Advance = 320 m
> - Transfer = 210 m
> - Tactical Diameter = 780 m
> - Steady turning diameter = 740 m
> Calculate (i) the steady turning period in seconds, (ii) the centripetal acceleration in m/s², and (iii) comment on IMO compliance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

The Turning Circle Manoeuvre is a standard sea trial in which the ship is run at a steady trial speed on a straight course in deep, calm water. A constant rudder angle of **35°** is ordered to one side and held throughout. The path of the ship's pivot point is recorded using GPS or radar.

*Sketch:* (Draw a turning-circle diagram with O = origin, A = advance, Tr = transfer, D_T = tactical diameter, and the steady turning circle of diameter D.)

- **Advance (A):** Distance travelled along the original course line, measured at the instant the heading has changed by **90°**.
- **Transfer (Tr):** Distance travelled perpendicular to the original course line, measured at the same instant (90° heading change).
- **Tactical Diameter (D_T):** Distance perpendicular to the original course at the point where the heading has changed by **180°**.
- **Steady Turning Diameter (D):** Diameter of the final steady circular path after all transients have died out, approximately 0.9 × D_T. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b)

**(i) Steady Turning Period:**
Convert speed: V = 16 × 0.5144 = 8.230 m/s. Steady radius R = 740/2 = 370 m.

$$
T_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi R}{V} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 370}{8.230} \;\approx\; \boxed{282.5\ \text{seconds} \;\approx\; 4\ \text{min } 43\ \text{s}}
$$

**(ii) Centripetal acceleration:**

$$
a_{n} \;=\; \dfrac{V^{2}}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{(8.230)^{2}}{370} \;=\; \dfrac{67.73}{370} \;\approx\; \boxed{0.183\ \text{m/s}^{2} \;\approx\; 0.019\,g}
$$

**(iii) IMO Compliance Check (MSC.137(76)):**

- Advance ratio: $A/L_{pp} = 320/175 = 1.83$ ≤ 4.5 → **PASS** ✓
- Tactical Diameter ratio: $D_{T}/L_{pp} = 780/175 = 4.46$ ≤ 5.0 → **PASS** ✓

**Conclusion:** The bulk carrier satisfies both IMO Advance and Tactical-Diameter criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the **three phases** of a turning-circle manoeuvre with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A naval frigate of **Lpp = 130 m** and trial speed **27 knots** performs a turning-circle trial with **35° rudder**. The measured **Tactical Diameter is 590 m** and the **Steady Turning Diameter is 540 m**. Calculate (i) the time taken for the ship to complete one full steady turn, (ii) the speed of the ship in m/s, and (iii) the centripetal force on a 25-tonne helicopter parked on deck during steady turning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Phase I — Approach Phase (0° to 90°):**
On applying 35° rudder, a transverse hydrodynamic force develops on the stern opposite to the rudder side. This force generates a **yaw moment** about the ship's centre of gravity. The ship begins to swing; a **drift angle β** builds up between the ship's centreline and its direction of motion. Forward speed drops to ~50–70 % of trial speed. The trajectory is a transitional spiral.

**Phase II — Steady Turning Phase (180° onward):**
The drift angle β and the rudder deflection reach a dynamic equilibrium. The centripetal force (provided by the rudder and the asymmetric lateral hull force) exactly balances the centrifugal demand. The ship now follows a **near-perfect circular path** of constant radius R. This is where the *Steady Turning Diameter* is measured.

**Phase III — Completion (360°):**
The ship completes a full revolution. When the heading returns to 360° (i.e., the original direction), the ship is laterally offset from the starting point by a distance called the *Reach*. The helm is then brought to midship, and the ship stabilises on the new course. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b)

**(i) Time for one full steady turn:**
V = 27 × 0.5144 = 13.889 m/s. Steady radius R = 540/2 = 270 m.

$$
T_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi R}{V} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 270}{13.889} \;\approx\; \boxed{122.1\ \text{seconds} \;\approx\; 2\ \text{min } 2\ \text{s}}
$$

**(ii) Speed in m/s:** 27 knots × 0.5144 = **13.89 m/s** (already computed).

**(iii) Centripetal force on 25-tonne helicopter:**

Mass m = 25,000 kg.

$$
F_{c} \;=\; m \cdot a_{n} \;=\; 25000 \times \dfrac{(13.889)^{2}}{270} \;=\; 25000 \times 0.714
$$

$$
F_{c} \;\approx\; \boxed{17{,}860\ \text{N} \;\approx\; 17.9\ \text{kN}}
$$

This is the *additional* lateral force the helicopter tie-down system must resist during the steady turn. Naval architects must ensure the lashing capacity exceeds this value with a safety factor of at least **1.5 ×**. **(7 marks)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on Turning-Circle questions (compiled from KTU valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion from knots to m/s** when computing T_p or a_n. KTU examiners deduct a full mark for each missing conversion.
> 2. **Confusing Tactical Diameter with Steady Turning Diameter.** Tactical Diameter is at **180° heading change**; Steady Turning Diameter is the *final* circle. They are NOT the same.
> 3. **Stating "Tactical Diameter = 4 Lpp"** without units. Always write "Tactical Diameter ≈ 4 to 5 × Lpp, where Lpp is in metres → D_T in metres".
> 4. **Skipping the IMO comparison.** A 14-mark question that asks "comment on manoeuvrability" without quoting MSC.137(76) loses **at least 2 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up Advance and Transfer.** Advance is *along* the original course; Transfer is *perpendicular*. Drawing the diagram is the only way to avoid this mistake.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Turning Circle Manoeuvre**

- The **standard rudder angle** for the turning-circle test is **35°**.
- The **five canonical parameters** are: **Advance (A)**, **Transfer (Tr)**, **Tactical Diameter (D_T)**, **Steady Turning Diameter (D)**, and **Steady Turning Period (T_p)**.
- **Advance** is measured *along* the original course at **90° heading change**.
- **Transfer** is measured *perpendicular* to the original course at **90° heading change**.
- **Tactical Diameter (D_T)** is measured *perpendicular* to the original course at **180° heading change**.
- **Steady Turning Diameter (D) ≈ 0.9 × D_T** is the *final* circle.
- **Rule of thumb:** $D_{T} \approx 4 \text{ to } 5\,L_{pp}$, $A \approx 1 \text{ to } 2\,L_{pp}$, $Tr \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.2\,L_{pp}$.
- **IMO MSC.137(76) limits:** $A \le 4.5\,L_{pp}$ and $D_{T} \le 5\,L_{pp}$ for ships above 100 m.
- **Steady turning period** formula: $T_{p} = \dfrac{2\pi R}{V}$, where R = D/2.
- **Centripetal acceleration:** $a_{n} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$.
- **Speed loss** during turn: typically 30–50 %; IMO requires at least **60 % of V₀** for cargo ships and **70 %** for passenger ships.
- **Drift angle** β is the angle between the ship's centreline and its velocity vector; in steady turn, β ≈ 3° to 10°.
- **Spiral test** is the *companion* test, used to identify steering stability (stable vs. unstable), NOT turning-circle parameters.
- The **MMG model** is the standard mathematical framework for analytical prediction of the turning circle.
- **Course-keeping vs. course-changing**: spiral test is for *course-keeping*; turning circle is for *course-changing*.
- **Reach** is the lateral offset at 360° heading change.
- **T_p units** are always in **seconds** (or minutes); examiners are strict.
- **Lateral force on deck cargo** during steady turn: $F_{c} = m \cdot V^{2} / R$; remember the **1.5× safety factor** in design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heel and Speed Loss During Turn
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heel and Speed Loss During Turn

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Heel During a Turn?

When a ship is steered away from a straight course using her rudder, she does not simply pivot in the horizontal plane. Because she possesses mass distributed vertically above the waterline (chiefly in the superstructure, deckhouses, masts, and cargo), the centrifugal force generated by the circular motion acts horizontally at the **Center of Gravity (G)**. This horizontal force, applied above the keel, creates a heeling moment that causes the ship to **list outward** (away from the center of the turning circle) in a steady, constant-radius turn.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Heel During Turn):**
> The transverse inclination of a ship from the vertical, caused by the centrifugal force acting at the ship's center of gravity during a steady turning maneuver, balanced by the righting lever developed through the shift of the buoyancy center of gravity (B) toward the new center of buoyancy (B′).

### 1.2 What is Speed Loss During Turn?

When a ship deviates from a straight course, she experiences **increased hydrodynamic resistance** arising from:
- **Increased hull form resistance** (yaw and sway motions)
- **Drift angle** (angle between ship's centerline and her path of travel)
- **Rudder drag** (the rudder carries a side force to maintain the turn)
- **Wave-making resistance changes** due to altered hull attitude

As a result, the vessel's sustained speed in a turn is always less than her open-water, straight-running speed. This reduction is termed **Speed Loss During Turn**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** For a typical merchant ship executing a turn with a moderate rudder angle (15°–20°), the speed loss is empirically between **10% and 20%** of the straight-line speed. For hard turns at full rudder (35°), speed loss can rise to **30%–40%**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle on a Banked Track

Imagine a cyclist riding in a flat circular path. To avoid falling outward, the cyclist must **lean inward** into the turn so that the centripetal reaction through the tires and the gravitational force align with the body's tilted axis. A ship does the *opposite* — she **leans outward**, because her "balance point" is the Center of Gravity G, and the centrifugal pull above the keel cannot be balanced by shifting her weight inward (she has no legs to lean with!). Instead, her underwater hull shifts the buoyancy center outward to counterbalance the moment. The equilibrium tilt you observe is the **heel angle (φ)**.

Speed loss is analogous to driving a car uphill versus on flat road — extra effort (drag) is needed to overcome the new forces resisting motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constant Used Throughout This Topic:**
> - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity)

### 1.4 The Two Phases of Heel — Transient vs. Steady

Heel during turn is NOT a single constant value. There are two distinct phases:

| Phase | Description | Heel Direction |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Phase 1: Transient (Inward Heel)** | At the instant the rudder is put over, the ship begins to yaw. The hydrodynamic forces on the hull generate a moment that initially heels the ship **inward** toward the center of turn. | Inward |
| **Phase 2: Steady (Outward Heel)** | After the steady circular motion is established, centrifugal force dominates and the ship settles to a constant outward heel angle determined by the equilibrium of moments. | Outward |

The KTU 2024 syllabus Module 3 focuses primarily on **steady-state heel**, which is the analytically tractable case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Free-Body Picture of a Ship in Steady Turn

In a steady circular turn of radius **R** at forward speed **V**, the following forces act on the ship:

1. **Weight (W)** — acting vertically downward through G.
2. **Buoyancy (B = W)** — acting vertically upward through the centroid of the submerged volume.
3. **Centrifugal Force (F_c)** — acting horizontally outward through G.
4. **Propulsive Force (T)** — acting forward along the ship's centerline.
5. **Hydrodynamic Side Force (F_y)** — acting laterally (mostly via the rudder and asymmetric hull pressures).

The **heel angle φ** arises from the rotational equilibrium of the couple formed by:
- The **Centrifugal Force F_c** applied at G (horizontal, outward)
- The **Reaction Buoyancy** passing through the *new* Center of Buoyancy B′

For equilibrium, the line of action of the resultant buoyancy must pass through the intersection of W (vertical at G) and F_c (horizontal at G). This intersection point lies *outboard* of the ship's centerline at a height greater than G when the ship heels outward.

### 2.2 Why Does Speed Drop in a Turn? — The "Why" Behind Each Cause

- **Yaw Angle (Drift):** In a turn, the ship's heading is not aligned with her velocity vector. The drift angle β means the flow meets the hull at an angle, increasing pressure drag.
- **Rudder Side Force:** The rudder, held at angle δ, must generate a lateral force F_y to curve the ship's path. This force has a stern component that **robs forward thrust**.
- **Wave Pattern Change:** Moving obliquely, the ship's bow wave interferes with the ship's own flow, increasing wave-making resistance.
- **Squat Effect Reversal:** Some loss of dynamic support near the stern in a hard turn.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all formulas required for solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Centrifugal Force | $F_c = \dfrac{W \cdot V^2}{g \cdot R}$ | kN or N | Acts at G horizontally outward |
| 2 | Steady Heel Angle (turn) | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{F_c \cdot KG}{W \cdot GM} = \dfrac{V^2 \cdot KG}{g \cdot R \cdot GM}$ | radians | The primary KTU equation |
| 3 | Alternative Heel Form | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{V^2}{g \cdot R} \cdot \dfrac{KG}{GM}$ | radians | Same equation, reordered |
| 4 | Speed Ratio (empirical) | $\dfrac{V_{turn}}{V_{straight}} \approx 1 - k \cdot \sin\delta$ | dimensionless | $k \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ depending on hull form |
| 5 | Tactical Diameter → Radius | $R \approx \dfrac{Tactical\,Diameter}{2}$ | m | Used in maneuverability tables |
| 6 | Heeling Moment (Turn) | $M_{heel} = F_c \cdot KG = \dfrac{W V^2 KG}{gR}$ | kN·m | Used for moment diagrams |
| 7 | Righting Moment (Turn) | $M_{right} = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\phi$ | kN·m | At equilibrium = M_heel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary / Validity Conditions:**
> 1. The heel formula applies to **steady-state** circular motion, NOT transient start of turn.
> 2. **V** is the *sustained speed during the turn*, NOT the straight-line speed.
> 3. **GM** here is the *transverse* metacentric height (stability in roll), not longitudinal GM.
> 4. Formula assumes small angles (φ < 10°); for large angles use the cosine form or numerical integration.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Routing Software:** Modern ECDIS systems predict heel in turns to warn of cargo-shift risks for grain carriers and container ships.
- **Submarine Design:** Submarines are especially sensitive to heel during turn because they operate at low GM; designers must keep radius of turn large enough that $\tan\phi$ remains within safe bounds.
- **Passenger Comfort:** Cruise ships limit rudder deflection in heavy weather to minimize transient inward heel that alarms passengers.
- **Warship Design:** High-speed destroyers require **sufficient GM** to prevent dangerous heel during high-speed evasive maneuvers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Steady-Turn Heel Angle

We begin with the free-body diagram of a ship in steady circular motion. The derivation proceeds in numbered, logically-justified steps.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Acceleration and Inertial Force**
