# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 marks each)

### Q1. Define "aspect ratio" of a rudder and state its typical range for ship rudders. Why does a finite aspect ratio reduce the lift-curve slope below the ideal 2-D value of $2\pi$? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Aspect ratio $\Lambda = b^2 / A_R = b/c$, ratio of squared span to projected area (or span-to-chord for rectangular rudder).
- **Typical range (1 mark):** $\Lambda = 1.5$ to $2.0$ for merchant ship rudders; can be lower (~1.0) for high-speed vessels.
- **Why reduced (1 mark):** At finite span, high-pressure fluid below the foil leaks around the **wingtips** to the low-pressure side, producing **trailing vortices** and **induced downwash**. The downwash tilts the local flow, reducing the effective angle of attack and hence the lift. The strength of this effect is inversely related to $\Lambda$.

### Q2. With a neat sketch, distinguish between a **balanced** and an **unbalanced** rudder. What is the key advantage of a balanced rudder? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Sketch (1 mark):** Unbalanced — stock at the leading edge (entire chord aft of stock). Balanced — stock placed at ~20–25% of the chord from LE, with part of the rudder area forward of the stock.
- **Distinction (1 mark):** In the unbalanced case, the entire fluid force creates a turning moment about the stock. In the balanced case, the forward area's pressure partially cancels this moment.
- **Advantage (1 mark):** **Reduced torque on the rudder stock**, allowing a smaller, lighter, and cheaper steering gear. At the *design angle* $\alpha_d$, the moment can be brought to nearly zero.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 marks each, internal choice)

> **Module 4 carries 14 marks (typically two 7-mark sub-parts).**

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **lift curve slope $C_{L\alpha}$** of a rectangular rudder of aspect ratio $\Lambda$, starting from the 2-D lift-curve slope $a_0 = 2\pi$ per radian. Use the lifting-line induced-drag correction. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A single-screw ship travels at 15 knots. The rudder is rectangular with $c = 1.8$ m, $b = 2.7$ m. Wake fraction $w = 0.25$, propeller diameter $D_p = 3.0$ m, rpm $N = 100$. If the rudder is set at $\delta = 25^\circ$, compute:
  1. The effective velocity $V_R$ at the rudder.
  2. The lateral (normal) force $N_R$.
  3. The torque on the stock, assuming an **unbalanced** rudder.

Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation of $C_{L\alpha}$ \[7 marks\]**

The 2-D lift curve slope of an ideal hydrofoil is $a_0 = 2\pi$ per rad. For a finite span $b$ and area $A_R$, define aspect ratio $\Lambda = b^2/A_R$. According to **Prandtl's lifting-line theory**, the 3-D lift curve slope is reduced by induced effects:

$$
C_{L\alpha} \;=\; \frac{a_0}{1 \;+\; \dfrac{a_0}{\pi\,\Lambda}}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $a_0 = 2\pi$:**

$$
C_{L\alpha} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{1 \;+\; \dfrac{2\pi}{\pi\Lambda}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{1 \;+\; \dfrac{2}{\Lambda}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\Lambda}{\Lambda + 2}
$$

**Step 2 — Refined form (Weihs):** replacing the elliptic approximation with the exact induced angle gives:

$$
\boxed{\;C_{L\alpha} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\Lambda}{2\pi \;+\; \sqrt{\Lambda^{2} + 4\pi^{2}}}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Limiting cases:**
- As $\Lambda \to \infty$ (infinite span): $C_{L\alpha} \to 2\pi$ ✓
- As $\Lambda \to 0$ (very short span): $C_{L\alpha} \to 0$ ✓

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation (1 mark):** Finite span allows **tip vortices** that induce downwash $w_i$, tilting the local flow and reducing the effective angle of attack:

$$
\alpha_{eff} = \alpha - w_i/V_R, \quad C_L = C_{L\alpha}\alpha_{eff} < 2\pi\alpha
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution \[7 marks\]**

**Step 1 — Ship speed in SI:** $V_S = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Effective inflow speed (sub-part 1, 2 marks):**

$$
V_{axial} = 7.716(1 - 0.25) = 5.787 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{tan} = \pi \times 3.0 \times (100/60) = \pi \times 3.0 \times 1.6667 = 15.708 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_R = \sqrt{5.787^2 + 15.708^2} = \sqrt{33.49 + 246.74} = \sqrt{280.23} = 16.74 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Geometry and $C_{L\alpha}$ (sub-part 2, 2 marks):**

$$
A_R = 1.8 \times 2.7 = 4.86 \text{ m}^2, \quad \Lambda = 2.7/1.8 = 1.5
$$

$$
C_{L\alpha} = \frac{2\pi(1.5)}{2\pi + \sqrt{1.5^2 + 4\pi^2}} = \frac{9.4248}{12.7337} = 0.7402 \text{ per rad}
$$

$$
\delta = 25^\circ = 0.4363 \text{ rad}
$$

Lift: $L_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 16.74^2 \times 4.86 \times 0.7402 \times 0.4363$

$$
= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 280.23 \times 4.86 \times 0.7402 \times 0.4363
$$

$$
= 512.5 \times 280.23 \times 4.86 \times 0.7402 \times 0.4363
$$

- $512.5 \times 280.23 = 143{,}618$
- $143{,}618 \times 4.86 = 697{,}984$
- $697{,}984 \times 0.7402 = 516{,}569$
- $516{,}569 \times 0.4363 = 225{,}355$ N

$$
L_R \approx 225.4 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
N_R = L_R \sin 25^\circ = 225.4 \times 0.4226 = 95.3 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Torque on stock (sub-part 3, 3 marks):**

$$
\frac{x_{cp}}{c} = \frac{0.25 + 1.5/6}{1 + 1.5/3} = \frac{0.25 + 0.25}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{0.50}{1.50} = 0.333
$$

Unbalanced → $l_{cp} = 0.333 \times 1.8 = 0.600$ m.

$$
M_{stock} = N_R \times l_{cp} = 95.3 \times 0.600 = 57.2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Final Answers (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{V_R = 16.74 \text{ m/s}, \quad N_R = 95.3 \text{ kN}, \quad M_{stock} = 57.2 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain, with a free-body sketch, the decomposition of the rudder's hydrodynamic force into **lift, drag, normal, and tangential** components. State the standard sign convention and identify which component produces the **yawing moment** of the ship. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A twin-screw ship (no slipstream effect on the rudder — use $V_R = V_S$) is fitted with a balanced rudder. Ship speed $V_S = 12$ knots, rudder area $A_R = 5.5$ m², $C_{L\alpha} = 0.95$ per rad. The rudder is set at $\delta = 30^\circ$.
  1. Compute the lateral force $N_R$ (use full $\sin\delta$ form, **not** small-angle).
  2. If the balance ratio is $\eta = 0.22$ and $x_{cp}/c = 0.34$, find the lever arm $l_{cp}$ and the stock torque.
  3. If the rudder is positioned 50 m aft of the CG, calculate the yawing moment on the hull. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Force decomposition \[7 marks\]**

**Free-body sketch description (3 marks):**

A rudder at angle $\delta$ in flow $V_R$ experiences a **resultant** fluid force $F_R$. This is decomposed as:

```
                  Lift L_R  ↑
                              \
                               \   V_R
                                \ ↗
                                 \/--------→  Drag D_R
                              ↗
                             /|
                  T_R      / |   N_R (lateral)
                  ↖       /  ↓
                    \    /   (toward ship centerline)
        Ship's       \  /
        axis ─────────\*────────→  Ship heading
                      Stock
```

**Component definitions (2 marks):**

- $L_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_R^2 A_R C_L$ — perpendicular to the free stream $V_R$ (aerodynamic lift).
- $D_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_R^2 A_R C_D$ — along the free stream (aerodynamic drag).
- $N_R = L_R \sin\delta - D_R \cos\delta$ — **lateral force** perpendicular to ship centerplane.
- $T_R = L_R \cos\delta + D_R \sin\delta$ — tangential force along ship centerplane (opposes motion).

**Yaw-producing component (1 mark):**
The **normal force $N_R$** is the only component that produces a moment about the ship's vertical axis (the yaw axis). The tangential force $T_R$ adds to the ship's resistance but does not yaw the vessel.

**Sign convention (1 mark):** Positive $N_R$ is conventionally taken **to port** (or starboard, depending on standard), positive rudder angle $\delta$ turns the ship's heading in the same direction. KTU papers use the **right-hand rule** with the rudder stock as the vertical axis.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution \[7 marks\]**

**Given:** $V_S = 12$ knots $= 6.173$ m/s, $A_R = 5.5$ m², $C_{L\alpha} = 0.95$ per rad, $\delta = 30^\circ = \pi/6 = 0.5236$ rad, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $x_R = 50$ m, $\eta = 0.22$, $x_{cp}/c = 0.34$.

**Step 1 — Lateral force (2 marks):**

$$
L_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 6.173^2 \times 5.5 \times 0.95 \times 0.5236
$$

- $0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$
- $6.173^2 = 38.11$
- $512.5 \times 38.11 = 19{,}531$
- $19{,}531 \times 5.5 = 107{,}420$
- $107{,}420 \times 0.95 = 102{,}049$
- $102{,}049 \times 0.5236 = 53{,}425$ N

$$
L_R = 53.43 \text{ kN}
$$

Full $\sin\delta$ form for normal force (using $D_R \ll L_R$):

$$
N_R = L_R \sin 30^\circ = 53.43 \times 0.5 = 26.71 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Lever arm & stock torque (3 marks):**

Balance ratio $\eta = 0.22$ means the stock is at $0.22c$ from LE. The center of pressure is at $0.34c$ from LE. Therefore:

$$
l_{cp} = (0.34 - 0.22)\,c = 0.12\,c
$$

But we are not given $c$ directly. We need to express $l_{cp}$ in terms of $A_R$ and $\Lambda$. If we assume the rudder is **square** (Λ = 1, $b = c$), then $A_R = c^2$, $c = \sqrt{5.5} = 2.345$ m:

$$
l_{cp} = 0.12 \times 2.345 = 0.281 \text{ m}
$$

$$
M_{stock} = N_R \cdot l_{cp} = 26.71 \times 0.281 = 7.51 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Yaw moment on hull (2 marks):**

$$
N_{yaw} = N_R \cdot x_R = 26.71 \times 50 = 1335.5 \text{ kN·m} \;\approx\; 1.34 \text{ MN·m}
$$

**Final Answers (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{N_R = 26.7 \text{ kN}, \quad M_{stock} = 7.51 \text{ kN·m}, \quad N_{yaw} = 1.34 \text{ MN·m}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Degrees vs Radians:** Using $\alpha$ in degrees in $C_{L\alpha}\alpha$ instead of radians → error by factor of ~57. **Always convert to radians.**
> 2. **Forgetting the slipstream:** Computing $V_R = V_S$ for a single-screw ship *behind* a working propeller gives only **40–60%** of the correct $V_R$. The propeller slipstream is the **dominant** effect.
> 3. **Using 2π for $C_{L\alpha}$:** Real rudders are finite-span; the answer is typically **0.6–1.0 per rad**, not 6.28.
> 4. **Confusing stock position with CP:** For a balanced rudder, $l_{cp}$ is measured from the **stock**, not from the LE.
> 5. **Skipping units in the answer:** KTU examiners deduct marks for naked numbers. Always write **kN** and **kN·m**.
> 6. **Ignoring the linearisation warning:** A "find $N_R$" question may demand either the small-angle form (linear) or the full $\sin\delta$ form — **read the question carefully**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rudder Forces & Moments — Must-Know Summary**

- ☐ **Rudder is a finite-span vertical hydrofoil** at the stern — behaves like a wing, not a flat plate.
- ☐ **Aspect ratio** $\Lambda = b^2 / A_R = b/c$; typical $\Lambda = 1.5$–$2.0$.
- ☐ **Lift curve slope** $C_{L\alpha} = 2\pi\Lambda / (2\pi + \sqrt{\Lambda^2 + 4\pi^2})$ — **always less than $2\pi$** for finite span.
- ☐ **Local inflow speed** is enhanced by the propeller: $V_R = \sqrt{(V_S(1-w))^2 + (\pi D_p n)^2}$. For twin-screw or no-propeller, $V_R \approx V_S(1-w)$.
- ☐ **Wash ratio** $K = V_R/V_S$ is typically **1.2–2.5** — the propeller can **double** the effective rudder speed.
- ☐ **Force decomposition:** $L_R, D_R$ (along/perpendicular to $V_R$) → $N_R, T_R$ (lateral/longitudinal in ship frame).
- ☐ **Small-angle form:** $N_R \approx L_R \sin\delta$; $T_R \approx L_R \cos\delta$.
- ☐ **Center of pressure** for rectangular rudder: $x_{cp}/c = (0.25 + \Lambda/6)/(1 + \Lambda/3)$.
- ☐ **Stock torque** $M_{stock} = N_R \cdot l_{cp}$ — the **steering gear rating** is set by this.
- ☐ **Balanced rudder** places the stock at ~20–25% chord, **reducing** $M_{stock}$ by **50–70%**.
- ☐ **Yaw moment on hull** $N_{yaw} = N_R \cdot x_R$ where $x_R$ is the rudder's distance aft of the CG.
- ☐ **Form drag** $C_{D_0} \approx 0.01$–$0.02$ for streamlined rudders; neglected in small-angle linear analysis.
- ☐ **Units:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, angles in **radians** when multiplying with $C_{L\alpha}$.
- ☐ **Engineering outputs:** torque on stock (steering-gear sizing), lateral force (course change), tangential force (added resistance).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Around Rudder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flow Around Rudder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — FLOW AROUND RUDDER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rudder:** A rudder is a hydrodynamically shaped, vertically oriented surface fitted at the **stern** of a ship, usually abaft the propeller, whose purpose is to generate a **lateral side force** when inclined at an angle to the flow of water. This side force produces a **yawing moment** (turning moment) about the ship's center of gravity, enabling course control and maneuverability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Around Rudder:** The study of the hydrodynamic behaviour of water as it flows over the inclined rudder surface, resulting in the generation of pressure distribution, lift (side force), drag, and the associated turning moment. This is conceptually analogous to **thin-aerofoil theory** applied to a low-speed, high-density fluid medium (water) instead of air.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive View)

Imagine a small oar blade dipped into flowing river water and held at an angle. The water striking the inclined face of the oar exerts a perpendicular push — this is exactly what happens to a ship's rudder. When the helmsman turns the rudder by an angle **δ**, water streaming past the hull strikes the rudder surface obliquely. The deflection of this stream creates:

- A **side force (F_S)** perpendicular to the forward motion of the ship,
- A small **drag component** along the flow direction,
- And consequently, a **turning moment** that rotates the ship.

The rudder essentially behaves like an **underwater aircraft wing (hydrofoil)** working in the propeller's slipstream, but mounted on a hinge so its angle can be controlled.

### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters

The following standard naval-architecture metrics govern flow around the rudder:

- **Rudder Angle (δ):** Angle between the rudder's chord plane and the ship's centerline, measured in degrees (typical range **±35°**).
- **Angle of Attack (α):** Angle between the rudder chord and the **relative water flow direction** (which is influenced by the ship's forward speed, wake, and propeller-induced velocities).
- **Rudder Chord Length (c):** Streamwise length of the rudder profile.
- **Rudder Span (b):** Vertical height of the rudder.
- **Aspect Ratio (Λ):** Defined as **Λ = b² / A** where **A** is the lateral (planform) area of the rudder.
- **Rudder Area (A_R):** Projected lateral area of the rudder.
- **Wake Fraction (w):** Fraction of ship's speed lost as wake, typically **0.20 – 0.40**.
- **Thrust Deduction (t):** Fraction of propeller thrust lost due to hull interaction, typically **0.10 – 0.25**.

> [!NOTE]
> These parameters are universal KTU Board-exam constants; students are expected to memorize the typical numerical ranges for Part A short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Flow Description

When a ship moves forward with velocity **V_S** through still water:

1. Water flows past the hull at a velocity reduced by the wake — the **effective inflow velocity** at the rudder position is:
$$V_R = V_S \, (1 - w)$$

2. The propeller accelerates this flow further by adding a **race velocity (u_P)** in the propeller slipstream, producing the **total effective rudder inflow velocity**:
$$V = V_S (1 - w) + u_P$$

3. When the rudder is deflected by an angle **δ**, the effective angle of attack on the rudder profile becomes:
$$\alpha = \delta - \beta$$
where **β** is the **drift angle** at the stern (small, often neglected for straight-line motion).

4. The flow separates smoothly from the trailing edge (Kutta condition), creating **circulation (Γ)** around the rudder, which by **Kutta–Joukowski's theorem** produces lift.

## 2.2 Force and Moment Components

The **total hydrodynamic force (R)** on the rudder can be resolved into:

- **Normal Force (F_N):** Perpendicular to the rudder chord.
- **Tangential Force (F_T):** Along the rudder chord (drag-like component).

In the **ship-fixed coordinate system**, these are converted to:

- **Side Force (F_S):** Lateral force turning the ship.
- **Drag Force (F_D'):** Additional resistance.

Transformation relations:
$$F_S = F_N \cos\delta - F_T \sin\delta$$

$$F_D' = F_N \sin\delta + F_T \cos\delta$$

## 2.3 Lift and Drag Coefficients

Lift coefficient (slender-body / thin-aerofoil approximation):
$$C_L = \frac{2\pi \, \alpha}{1 + \dfrac{2}{\Lambda}}$$

For **high aspect ratio** rudders (Λ → ∞), this reduces to:
$$C_L \approx 2\pi \, \alpha \quad (\text{radians})$$

The **normal force coefficient** is closely related to the lift coefficient for thin profiles:
$$C_N \approx C_L \quad \text{(for small } \delta\text{)}$$

The **drag coefficient** combines profile drag and induced drag:
$$C_D = C_{D0} + \frac{C_L^{\,2}}{\pi \, \Lambda \, e}$$

where **e** is the **Oswald efficiency factor** (typically 0.85 – 0.95 for streamlined rudders).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU student must master for the ESE on Flow Around Rudder.

| **S.No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Standard Units** | **Remarks** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Effective Rudder Velocity | $V_R = V_S (1 - w)$ | m/s | Wake-corrected inflow |
| 2 | Total Rudder Inflow Velocity | $V = V_S (1 - w) + u_P$ | m/s | Includes propeller race |
| 3 | Angle of Attack | $\alpha = \delta - \beta$ | rad or deg | Drift angle often small |
| 4 | Aspect Ratio | $\Lambda = b^2 / A$ | dimensionless | $b$ = span, $A$ = area |
| 5 | Lift Coefficient (thin profile) | $C_L = 2\pi\alpha / (1 + 2/\Lambda)$ | dimensionless | From lifting-line theory |
| 6 | High-AR Approximation | $C_L \approx 2\pi\alpha$ | dimensionless | $\alpha$ in radians |
| 7 | Normal Force on Rudder | $F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_N$ | N | Primary steering force |
| 8 | Side Force | $F_S = F_N \cos\delta - F_T \sin\delta$ | N | Acts laterally on ship |
| 9 | Lift Force | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_L$ | N | Perpendicular to V |
| 10 | Drag Force | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_D$ | N | Along V direction |
| 11 | Kutta–Joukowski Lift | $L' = \rho V \Gamma$ | N/m | Per unit span |
| 12 | Turning Moment (about midship) | $M_T = F_S \cdot x_R$ | N·m | $x_R$ = rudder moment arm |
| 13 | Hull-Rudder Force Ratio (Kerwin) | $\dfrac{F_R}{F_H} \approx \dfrac{A_R}{A_H} \cdot \dfrac{\Lambda}{\Lambda + 2}$ | dimensionless | Approximate empirical |
| 14 | Rudder Normal Force (approx) | $F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_R V^2 \, f(\alpha)$ | N | $f(\alpha)$ from charts |

> **Note on symbols:** $\rho$ = **density of sea water** ≈ **1025 kg/m³**; $V$ = velocity; $A$ = area; $\Gamma$ = circulation.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The flow-around-rudder analysis is the foundation of:

- **Course-keeping autopilot design** (PID controllers use rudder force models).
- **Z-manoeuvre and turning circle prediction** (IMO manoeuvring standards).
- **IMO MSC.137** standardized manoeuvring predictions.
- **Propeller-rudder interaction studies** (ducted rudders, high-lift rudders like **Schilling rudder**, **Pleuger rudder**).
- **Fuel-efficient course control** through optimal rudder usage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lift on a Rudder from Kutta–Joukowski Theorem

We begin with the steady-state Kutta–Joukowski theorem for an infinite-span hydrofoil (the rudder, treating it as a 2D aerofoil in water):

$$L' = \rho V \Gamma$$

where $L'$ is the **lift per unit span**, $V$ is the **free-stream velocity**, and $\Gamma$ is the **bound circulation** around the rudder profile.

**Step 1 — Circulation via Thin-Aerofoil Theory:**

For a flat-plate approximation (which represents a streamlined rudder at small angles), the bound circulation is:

$$\Gamma = \pi c V \alpha$$

Here, $c$ is the rudder chord, and $\alpha$ is the geometric angle of attack in radians.

**Step 2 — Substituting into Kutta–Joukowski:**

$$L' = \rho V (\pi c V \alpha) = \pi \rho c V^2 \alpha$$

**Step 3 — Converting to Total Lift:**

The total lift (over span $b$) is:

$$L = L' \cdot b = \pi \rho c b V^2 \alpha$$

Since the planform area $A = c \cdot b$:

$$L = \pi \rho A V^2 \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Defining the Lift Coefficient:**

The lift coefficient is defined as:

$$C_L = \frac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A}$$

Substituting:

$$C_L = \frac{\pi \rho A V^2 \alpha}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A} = 2\pi \alpha$$

This is the classical result $\boxed{C_L = 2\pi \alpha}$ for an infinite-span flat plate in inviscid flow.

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion logic at each step is: (i) apply physical theorem → (ii) substitute geometric relation → (iii) normalise by dynamic pressure × area to obtain a dimensionless coefficient.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aspect-Ratio Correction (Finite-Span Rudder)

Real ship rudders have **finite span**, so the infinite-span assumption over-predicts lift. We must apply the **finite-wing correction** from Prandtl's lifting-line theory.

**Step 1 — Three-Dimensional Lift Slope:**

The 3D lift-curve slope for a finite aspect-ratio wing is given by:

$$\frac{dC_L}{d\alpha} = \frac{2\pi}{1 + \dfrac{2}{\Lambda}}$$

This is derived by adding a **downwash velocity** $w = \tfrac{L'}{2\pi\rho V b}$ behind the finite wing, which reduces the effective angle of attack.

**Step 2 — Re-substitute into Lift:**

The effective angle of attack becomes:

$$\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \alpha - \alpha_i = \frac{\alpha}{1 + \dfrac{2}{\Lambda}}$$

where $\alpha_i$ is the **induced angle of attack** due to downwash.

**Step 3 — Final Lift Expression:**

$$C_L = \frac{2\pi \alpha}{1 + \dfrac{2}{\Lambda}}$$

For a typical ship rudder with $\Lambda = 1.5$:

$$C_L = \frac{2\pi \alpha}{1 + 2/1.5} = \frac{2\pi \alpha}{1 + 1.333} = \frac{2\pi \alpha}{2.333} \approx 2.69\,\alpha$$

This is a **~15% reduction** from the infinite-span value, illustrating why finite-span corrections matter in ship-handling predictions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Normal Force and Side Force Transformation

**Step 1 — Define the Forces:**

The hydrodynamic force $R$ on the rudder acts at the **center of pressure**, located roughly at the **quarter-chord point** (c/4) from the leading edge for a flat plate at small $\alpha$.

We resolve $R$ into:

$$F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_N \quad \text{(normal to chord)}$$

$$F_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_T \quad \text{(tangential to chord)}$$

**Step 2 — Rotate from Rudder Coordinates to Ship Coordinates:**

The chord of the rudder is inclined at angle $\delta$ to the ship centerline. Rotating $(F_N, F_T)$ by $\delta$:

$$F_S = F_N \cos\delta - F_T \sin\delta$$

$$F_D' = F_N \sin\delta + F_T \cos\delta$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**

Take $V = 8$ m/s, $A = 12$ m², $\delta = 30°$, $C_N = 1.2$, $C_T = 0.15$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

Compute dynamic pressure:

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(8)^2 = 32{,}800 \text{ Pa}$$

Compute normal and tangential forces:

$$F_N = 32{,}800 \times 12 \times 1.2 = 472{,}320 \text{ N} \approx 472.3 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_T = 32{,}800 \times 12 \times 0.15 = 59{,}040 \text{ N} \approx 59.0 \text{ kN}$$

Side force:

$$F_S = 472{,}320 \cos 30° - 59{,}040 \sin 30°$$

$$F_S = 472{,}320 (0.866) - 59{,}040 (0.500)$$

$$F_S = 409{,}029 - 29{,}520 = 379{,}509 \text{ N} \approx 379.5 \text{ kN}$$

This is the **lateral steering force** delivered by the rudder.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Taylor's Approximate Series Form (Empirical)

> [!NOTE]
> **W.F. Durand** and later workers adopted an approximate series form for $C_N$:

$$C_N = a_1 \sin\delta + a_2 \sin\delta \cos\delta + a_3 \sin\delta \cos^2\delta + \cdots$$

For a flat-plate rudder at moderate angles ($\delta < 30°$), a sufficient approximation is:

$$C_N \approx a_1 \sin\delta \quad \text{where } a_1 \approx 5.0 \text{ to } 6.0 \text{ (depending on profile)}$$

This is the basis of **Strip Theory** rudder-force calculations used in early manoeuvring simulators.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Hydrodynamic Force Generation on a Rudder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Moving Forward with V_S] --> B[Wake at Stern: V_S reduced by w]
    B --> C[Propeller Slipstream Adds Race Velocity u_P]
    C --> D[Total Effective Velocity V at Rudder]
    D --> E[Rudder Deflected by Angle delta]
    E --> F[Effective Angle of Attack alpha equals delta minus beta]
    F --> G[Flow Over Rudder Profile Generates Circulation Gamma]
    G --> H[Kutta-Joukowski Lift L equals rho V Gamma]
    H --> I[Resolve into Normal Force F_N and Tangential Force F_T]
    I --> J[Transform to Side Force F_S and Drag F_D]
    J --> K[Turning Moment M_T equals F_S times Rudder Lever x_R]
    K --> L[Ship Yaws and Changes Heading]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hull-Propeller-Rudder Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ship Hull] --> A2[Generates Wake Fraction w]
    B1[Propeller] --> B2[Produces Thrust T]
    B2 --> B3[Induces Race Velocity u_P at Rudder]
    A2 --> C1[Combined Velocity Field at Rudder Position]
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Effective Inflow Velocity V to Rudder]
    C2 --> C3[Rudder Force F_S and Moment M_T]
    C3 --> D1[Yaw Rate r of Ship]
    D1 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Force Resolution Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Normal Force F_N perpendicular to Rudder Chord] --> S
    T[Tangential Force F_T along Rudder Chord] --> S
    S[Rotation by Rudder Angle delta] --> SF[Side Force F_S lateral]
    S --> DF[Drag Force F_Dprime along ship centerline]
    SF --> TM[Turning Moment M_T about midship]
    DF --> TM
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **alphanumeric node identifiers** (no reserved keywords) and **plain-text labels** to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **"Aspect Ratio"** as applied to a ship rudder, and state its typical range for merchant ship rudders.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Aspect Ratio (Λ) is defined as the ratio of the square of the rudder span to its lateral planform area:
> $$\Lambda = \frac{b^2}{A_R}$$
> where $b$ = vertical span of the rudder and $A_R$ = lateral area.
>
> For **merchant ship rudders**, the aspect ratio typically lies in the range **1.0 to 2.0**, with **single-plate rudders** around **1.0 – 1.5** and **high-lift (vented) rudders** around **1.5 – 2.0**. Aspect ratio governs the lift-curve slope: low Λ reduces $C_L$ but increases induced drag, whereas high Ξ gives stronger steering force per unit area.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the **effective velocity** at the rudder, and how is it related to the ship's speed, wake fraction, and propeller race velocity?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The effective velocity at the rudder position $V$ is the actual speed of water striking the rudder, which differs from the ship's speed $V_S$ because:
>
> 1. The **wake** at the stern reduces the inflow (wake fraction $w$),
> 2. The **propeller slipstream** accelerates the flow locally (race velocity $u_P$).
>
> The combined effective velocity is:
> $$V = V_S (1 - w) + u_P$$
>
> For typical single-screw merchant ships, $w \approx 0.25$ to $0.35$, so the wake-corrected inflow is roughly **65–75% of $V_S$**, while $u_P$ may add another **10–25%**, making $V$ close to $V_S$ at full speed.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **force system acting on a ship rudder** when deflected by an angle $\delta$ in a uniform flow of velocity $V$. Derive expressions for the **normal force** $F_N$, **side force** $F_S$, and **turning moment** $M_T$ about the midship section.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A rectangular ship rudder has the following particulars:

- Span $b = 4.0$ m
- Chord $c = 2.0$ m
- Aspect ratio $\Lambda = 1.5$
- Rudder angle $\delta = 25°$
- Effective inflow velocity $V = 7.5$ m/s
- Sea-water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Normal force coefficient $C_N = 1.0$
- Tangential force coefficient $C_T = 0.10$

Calculate the **side force** $F_S$ and **additional drag** $F_D'$ developed on the rudder. Take the rudder moment arm from midship as $x_R = 75$ m, and determine the **turning moment** $M_T$.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Theory:**

A ship rudder, when deflected by angle $\delta$ from the ship centerline, experiences a resultant hydrodynamic force $R$ at its center of pressure (≈ quarter-chord for thin profiles).

Resolving $R$ in rudder-local axes:
- $F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_N$ — normal to chord,
- $F_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_T$ — along chord.

In ship-fixed axes:
$$F_S = F_N \cos\delta - F_T \sin\delta$$

$$F_D' = F_N \sin\delta + F_T \cos\delta$$

The turning moment about midship:
$$M_T = F_S \cdot x_R$$

where $x_R$ is the longitudinal distance from midship to the rudder center of pressure.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

Step 1 — Planform area:
$$A = b \cdot c = 4.0 \times 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2 — Dynamic pressure:
$$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(7.5)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(56.25)$$

$$q = 28{,}828.125 \text{ Pa}$$

Step 3 — Normal force:
$$F_N = q \cdot A \cdot C_N = 28{,}828.125 \times 8.0 \times 1.0 = 230{,}625 \text{ N} \approx 230.6 \text{ kN}$$

Step 4 — Tangential force:
$$F_T = q \cdot A \cdot C_T = 28{,}828.125 \times 8.0 \times 0.10 = 23{,}062.5 \text{ N} \approx 23.06 \text{ kN}$$

Step 5 — Side force (using $\cos 25° = 0.9063$, $\sin 25° = 0.4226$):
$$F_S = 230{,}625 \times 0.9063 - 23{,}062.5 \times 0.4226$$

$$F_S = 209{,}015 - 9{,}746 = 199{,}269 \text{ N} \approx 199.3 \text{ kN}$$

Step 6 — Additional drag:
$$F_D' = 230{,}625 \times 0.4226 + 23{,}062.5 \times 0.9063$$

$$F_D' = 97{,}462 + 20{,}902 = 118{,}364 \text{ N} \approx 118.4 \text{ kN}$$

Step 7 — Turning moment:
$$M_T = F_S \cdot x_R = 199{,}269 \times 75 = 14{,}945{,}175 \text{ N·m} \approx 14.95 \text{ MN·m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:** $F_S \approx 199.3$ kN, $F_D' \approx 118.4$ kN, $M_T \approx 14.95$ MN·m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** State and explain the **Kutta–Joukowski theorem** as applied to a ship rudder. Show that for an **infinite-span rudder** with flat-plate approximation, the lift coefficient is $C_L = 2\pi\alpha$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A ship of length 150 m operates at 15 knots in sea water. The wake fraction at the rudder position is $w = 0.30$, and the propeller race velocity is $u_P = 1.8$ m/s. The rudder has an area $A_R = 18$ m² and aspect ratio $\Lambda = 1.2$. At a rudder angle of $\delta = 20°$, compute the **lift coefficient**, the **lift force**, and the **induced drag coefficient**. Take $e = 0.90$.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Theory:**

The **Kutta–Joukowski theorem** states that the lift per unit span on a 2D body in steady, inviscid, incompressible flow is:

$$L' = \rho V \Gamma$$

where $\Gamma$ = bound circulation around the body.

For a flat-plate rudder (small angle $\alpha$ in radians), thin-aerofoil theory gives:
$$\Gamma = \pi c V \alpha$$

Therefore:
$$L = L' \cdot b = \rho V (\pi c V \alpha) b = \pi \rho c b V^2 \alpha$$

Since $A = c \cdot b$:
$$L = \pi \rho A V^2 \alpha$$

The lift coefficient is:
$$C_L = \frac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A} = \frac{\pi \rho A V^2 \alpha}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A} = 2\pi \alpha$$

Hence the result $\boxed{C_L = 2\pi\alpha}$ is derived.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

Step 1 — Convert ship speed:
$$V_S = 15 \text{ knots} = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2 — Effective rudder velocity:
$$V = V_S (1 - w) + u_P = 7.716 (0.70) + 1.8 = 5.401 + 1.8 = 7.201 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 3 — Angle of attack (assuming drift angle ≈ 0):
$$\alpha = \delta = 20° = \frac{20\pi}{180} = 0.3491 \text{ rad}$$

Step 4 — Lift coefficient with finite-AR correction:
$$C_L = \frac{2\pi\alpha}{1 + 2/\Lambda} = \frac{2\pi (0.3491)}{1 + 2/1.2}$$

$$C_L = \frac{2.193}{1 + 1.6667} = \frac{2.193}{2.6667} = 0.822$$

Step 5 — Lift force:
$$L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_L = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(7.201)^2 (18)(0.822)$$

$$L = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(51.85)(18)(0.822)$$

$$L = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(767.07) = 393{,}123 \text{ N} \approx 393.1 \text{ kN}$$

Step 6 — Induced drag coefficient:
$$C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^{\,2}}{\pi \Lambda e} = \frac{(0.822)^2}{\pi (1.2)(0.90)}$$

$$C_{D,i} = \frac{0.6757}{3.393} = 0.1992$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:** $C_L \approx 0.822$, $L \approx 393.1$ kN, $C_{D,i} \approx 0.199$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Knots → m/s. Failing this costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using infinite-span $C_L = 2\pi\alpha$ instead of finite-AR form.** When Λ is given, you **must** apply the correction.
> 3. **Sign confusion in $F_S$ and $F_D'$:** $F_S$ is the *lateral steering force* (largest component); $F_D'$ is the *added resistance* (must be reported as a positive magnitude).
> 4. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** when using $2\pi\alpha$.
> 5. **Neglecting propeller race velocity $u_P$:** A common trap — $V$ is **not equal** to $V_S$ in ship problems.
> 6. **Reporting $M_T$ without specifying units** (N·m or kN·m). Always include units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Rudder** is essentially a **vertically mounted hydrofoil** producing lateral force for steering.
- **Lift** on a rudder arises from **circulation** (Kutta–Joukowski theorem): $L' = \rho V \Gamma$.
- **Thin-aerofoil / flat-plate lift slope** is $\boxed{C_L = 2\pi\alpha}$ for infinite span.
- **Finite aspect ratio correction:** $C_L = \dfrac{2\pi\alpha}{1 + 2/\Lambda}$ — **always apply this for real ship rudders**.
- **Effective inflow velocity** at the rudder: $V = V_S (1 - w) + u_P$ — combining wake reduction and propeller race.
- **Forces in ship axes:** $F_S = F_N\cos\delta - F_T\sin\delta$ and $F_D' = F_N\sin\delta + F_T\cos\delta$.
- **Turning moment** about midship: $M_T = F_S \cdot x_R$.
- **Typical ranges to memorise:** $\Lambda \in [1.0,\, 2.0]$, $w \in [0.20,\, 0.40]$, $t \in [0.10,\, 0.25]$, $\rho_{\text{sea}} = 1025$ kg/m³.
- **Typical rudder angles:** service range **±35°**, with maximum steering effectiveness around **30° – 35°**.
- **Aspect ratio governs trade-off:** higher Λ → more lift but **less induced drag penalty**; lower Λ → stronger structural rudder stock but reduced efficiency.
- **Center of pressure** for a flat-plate rudder sits at ≈ **quarter-chord** (c/4 from LE) at small $\alpha$; it shifts toward **mid-chord** as $\alpha$ increases (stall onset).
- **High-lift devices** (Schilling, Pleuger, flap rudders) increase $C_L$ by 2–3× via slot effects — KTU frequently tests this conceptually.
- **Kutta condition** at the trailing edge is what determines the unique circulation value; without it, the thin-aerofoil theory is indeterminate.
- **Stall:** When $\alpha > 15°$–$20°$ on a flat plate, $C_L$ peaks and drops, flow separates — rudder loses effectiveness; this is why steering becomes sluggish at extreme angles.
- **Induced drag** $C_{D,i} = C_L^2 / (\pi \Lambda e)$ — directly proportional to lift squared, inversely to Λ.
- **Hull-propeller-rudder interaction** (wake non-uniformity, race rotation) means real $C_L$ is typically **10–20% lower** than the idealised 2D value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rudder Design Process
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rudder Design Process

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Rudder Design Process** is a systematic engineering procedure used in naval architecture to determine the geometric, structural, and hydrodynamic characteristics of a ship's rudder. It encompasses the calculation of the required **rudder area**, selection of the **rudder type**, estimation of hydrodynamic **rudder forces** and **torque**, and the structural sizing of the **rudder stock** to ensure safe and effective ship maneuverability.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the rudder design process is treated as an **iterative sizing and verification procedure** that ensures the rudder:
1. Produces adequate **turning moment** to meet IMO maneuverability standards.
2. Withstands hydrodynamic loads without structural failure.
3. Operates within the capacity of the steering gear system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The rudder design process integrates **lifting-line theory**, **momentum theory**, and **empirical K-T factors** to predict rudder side force, and it terminates in the **rudder stock diameter calculation** based on combined bending and torsion.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a rudder like the **trailer hitch of a giant truck** sailing through water. Imagine a massive cargo truck driving on a highway — if you want to turn it, you cannot just spin the steering wheel of the front wheels; you need a powerful blade at the back to "push" the rear end sideways. That blade is the rudder.

The rudder is essentially a **vertical underwater wing** pivoted at the stern. When the helmsman angles this wing by ±35°, the oncoming water flowing past the hull strikes the angled surface and generates a **sideways force**. That force, acting at some distance behind the ship's pivot point (the **resistance center**), creates a **yawing moment** that rotates the ship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> - **Hull** = truck body (resists turning due to inertia and water drag).
> - **Rudder** = rear blade that pushes the stern sideways.
> - **Angle of attack (α)** = how much the blade is tilted relative to the flow.
> - **Rudder stock** = the bolt holding the blade; it must be strong enough to take the force.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are essential for KTU-level rudder design calculations:

- **Sea water density (ρ):** **1025 kg/m³** (standard for design)
- **Fresh water density:** **1000 kg/m³**
- **Rudder angle limit (δ_max):** **±35°** (typical operational max)
- **Maximum stock torque (per Classification Societies):** **Torsional yield strength** of steel stock
- **IMO Rudder Area Recommendation:** **A_R ≈ L_pp × T** (a first-cut empirical benchmark)
- **Allowable shear stress (mild steel rudder stock):** **τ_allow ≈ 50–60 MPa**
- **Standard stock material:** **Forged steel** (Grade **ASTM A668** or equivalent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Callout — Rudder Stock:**
> The **rudder stock** is the vertical shaft that connects the rudder blade to the tiller or quadrant in the steering gear compartment. It transmits both **bending moment (from side force)** and **torque (from hydrodynamic twisting)** to the hull.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rudder Design Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The rudder design process follows a structured sequence. Each step has a clear engineering rationale:

### **Step 1: Determine Required Rudder Area**

The rudder area is sized so that the ship can execute a tactical diameter turn within IMO maneuverability limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical baseline (KTU standard):**
> $$A_R = L_{pp} \times T$$
> where $L_{pp}$ = length between perpendiculars and $T$ = draft.

A more refined empirical formula often used:

$$A_R = \frac{L_{pp} \times d}{25}$$

where $d$ = design draft. For very large ships, Classification Societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) provide look-up tables.

### **Step 2: Select Rudder Type**

Choose based on hull form and service:
- **Spade rudder** — most efficient, no skeg support.
- **Balanced rudder** — portion ahead of stock reduces torque.
- **Semi-balanced** — compromise solution.
- **Twin rudders** — for highly slender ships (e.g., large tankers, container ships).

### **Step 3: Compute Effective Flow Velocity**

The velocity at the rudder is less than ship speed because of wake fraction $w$:

$$V_R = V_S \times (1 - w)$$

where $w$ ≈ **0.20–0.40** depending on hull form (typical 0.30 for slender hulls).

### **Step 4: Calculate Rudder Side Force**

Using **lifting-line theory** with K-T (Kuchta-Tachibana) correction:

$$F_R = \frac{1}{2} \times \rho \times V_R^2 \times A_R \times K_T \times \sin(\delta)$$

where:
- $\rho$ = water density = **1025 kg/m³**
- $K_T$ = thrust correction factor (function of aspect ratio λ and rudder geometry) ≈ **0.30–0.55**
- $\delta$ = rudder angle

### **Step 5: Calculate Rudder Torque**

The torque is the side force times the moment arm (rudder chord $c$):

$$Q = F_R \times c \times \cos(\lambda)$$

where $\lambda$ is the **rudder hydrodynamic inflow angle** (≈ 0 for direct approach).

### **Step 6: Compute Bending Moment on Stock**

The bending moment is the side force times the **stock length** (vertical lever):

$$M = F_R \times L_{stock}$$

### **Step 7: Size the Rudder Stock Diameter**

Using combined torsion and bending (von Mises or direct shear):

$$d = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16}{\pi \times \tau_{allow}} \times \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}}$$

### **Step 8: Structural Verification**

- Check against Classification Society minimum stock diameters.
- Check bearing pressure at the rudder carrier.
- Check pintle and gudgeon stress.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Rudder Area (empirical) | $A_R = L_{pp} \times T$ | m² | First-cut baseline |
| 2 | Rudder Area (refined) | $A_R = L_{pp} \times d / 25$ | m² | More accurate for large ships |
| 3 | Effective Flow Velocity | $V_R = V_S (1 - w)$ | m/s | Wake fraction $w$ typically 0.20–0.40 |
| 4 | Rudder Side Force | $F_R = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 A_R K_T \sin\delta$ | N | Lifting-line based |
| 5 | K-T Factor (aspect ratio λ) | $K_T = \frac{2\pi \lambda}{2\pi \lambda + K_{\text{ind}}}$ | – | For finite aspect ratio |
| 6 | Rudder Torque | $Q = F_R \times c \times \cos\lambda$ | N·m | c = mean chord |
| 7 | Bending Moment | $M = F_R \times L_{stock}$ | N·m | L_stock = vertical length |
| 8 | Stock Diameter (combined) | $d = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 \sqrt{M^2+Q^2}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}}$ | m | τ_allow for steel ≈ 55 MPa |
| 9 | Aspect Ratio | $\lambda = h^2 / A_R$ | – | h = rudder span height |
| 10 | Rudder Lift Coefficient | $C_L = \frac{2\pi \sin\alpha}{1 + 2\pi\lambda / \pi \times AR}$ | – | From thin-airfoil theory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The rudder design process is critical in shipyards for both **new-build design** and **retrofit modifications**. It directly affects a vessel's IMO compliance (turn ability, course stability, and stopping ability under SOLAS Reg. II-1).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rudder Side Force from Lifting-Line Theory

### Starting Point

Consider a rudder of finite span immersed in a uniform flow of velocity $V_R$ at angle of attack $\alpha$ (which equals rudder angle $\delta$ for slow maneuvering). The lift on a 2D section of chord $c$ is:

$$L_{2D} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 c \, C_{l,2D}$$

For thin-airfoil theory at small angles:

$$C_{l,2D} = 2\pi \alpha$$

Substituting:

$$L_{2D} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 c \, (2\pi \alpha)$$

### Extension to 3D Finite Span

For a finite aspect-ratio wing, **Prandtl's lifting-line theory** introduces the induced drag factor. The 3D lift coefficient becomes:

$$C_L = \frac{C_{l,2D}}{1 + C_{l,2D}/(\pi \cdot AR)}$$

where $AR = \lambda$ is the aspect ratio.

Substituting $C_{l,2D} = 2\pi \alpha$:

$$C_L = \frac{2\pi \alpha}{1 + 2\pi \alpha /(\pi \lambda)} = \frac{2\pi \alpha}{1 + 2\alpha/\lambda}$$

For typical rudder aspect ratios ($\lambda \approx 1$ to 2), we approximate:

$$K_T = \frac{2\pi \lambda}{2\pi \lambda + K_{\text{ind}}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $K_T$ is a geometric correction factor that captures how 3D wings are less efficient than 2D foils due to **wingtip vortices**. For $\lambda = 1.5$ and $K_{\text{ind}} \approx 4$, $K_T \approx 0.49$.

### Final Side Force

$$F_R = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 A_R K_T \sin\delta$$

This is the canonical form used in KTU exam problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rudder Stock Diameter

### Setup

The rudder stock is subjected to:
- **Bending moment $M$** from $F_R$ acting at the lever arm $L_s$.
- **Torsion $Q$** from the twisting action of $F_R$ about the stock axis.

The equivalent **von Mises torque** combining bending and torsion is:

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}$$

### Solid Circular Shaft Theory

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$, the polar section modulus is:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$$

The maximum shear stress from a torque $T_{eq}$ is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T_{eq}}{Z_p} = \frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3}$$

Setting $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allow}$:

$$\frac{16 \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}}{\pi d^3} \le \tau_{allow}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d^3 \ge \frac{16 \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}$$

$$d \ge \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This formula is the **workhorse of stock design**. The cube root arises because shaft stiffness scales with $d^3$ (the section modulus), so a 50% increase in diameter roughly doubles the load capacity.

## 3.3 Analytical Example Walkthrough (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A ship with $L_{pp} = 150$ m, $T = 8$ m, $V_S = 15$ knots, wake fraction $w = 0.30$, rudder angle $\delta = 35°$, $K_T = 0.50$. Determine rudder area, effective velocity, and maximum side force.

### Step 1: Rudder Area

$$A_R = L_{pp} \times T = 150 \times 8 = 1200 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Effective Velocity

Convert $V_S = 15$ knots to m/s: $V_S = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s

$$V_R = V_S (1 - w) = 7.716 \times 0.70 = 5.401 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3: Side Force

$$F_R = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (5.401)^2 \times 1200 \times 0.50 \times \sin(35°)$$

Compute term-by-term:
- $\frac{1}{2} \times 1025 = 512.5$
- $(5.401)^2 = 29.17$
- $\sin(35°) = 0.5736$

$$F_R = 512.5 \times 29.17 \times 1200 \times 0.50 \times 0.5736$$

$$F_R = 512.5 \times 29.17 = 14{,}949.6$$
$$F_R = 14{,}949.6 \times 1200 = 17{,}939{,}520$$
$$F_R = 17{,}939{,}520 \times 0.50 = 8{,}969{,}760$$
$$F_R = 8{,}969{,}760 \times 0.5736 \approx 5{,}145{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 5.15 \text{ MN}$$

> **Final answer:** $F_R \approx 5.15$ MN (about 525 tonnes of force — an enormous load that justifies the need for the structural stock design).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rudder Design Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Ship Parameters: Lpp, T, Vs, w] --> B[Step 1: Estimate Rudder Area AR]
    B --> C[Step 2: Select Rudder Type]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Effective Velocity VR]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Side Force FR using K-T Factor]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute Torque Q and Bending Moment M]
    F --> G[Step 6: Size Rudder Stock Diameter d]
    G --> H[Step 7: Check Bearing Pressures and Pintle Stress]
    H --> I{Verification OK?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Final Rudder Design Output]
    I -- No --> B
```

## 4.2 Rudder Force Generation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hull Velocity Vs] --> B[Wake Fraction w]
    B --> C[Effective Rudder Velocity VR]
    C --> D[Rudder Angle delta]
    D --> E[Lifting Line Side Force FR]
    E --> F[Yaw Moment on Ship]
    F --> G[Ship Turns]
```

## 4.3 Rudder Stock Load Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrodynamic Side Force FR] --> B[Bending Moment M at Stock]
    A --> C[Torque Q at Stock]
    B --> D[Combined Von Mises Load]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Required Stock Diameter d]
    E --> F[Check vs Allowable Shear Stress]
    F --> G[Final Stock Diameter]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The first flow chart shows the **iterative design loop** — design parameters are refined until verification passes.
> - The second flow chart illustrates the **causal chain from hull motion to ship turn**.
> - The third flow chart maps the **structural design path** from load to final diameter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "rudder area" and state the empirical formula used for its initial estimation in ship design.**

**Model Answer:**
The **rudder area** is the projected lateral area of the rudder (span × chord), and it represents the surface available to generate side force. The empirical formula for first-cut estimation is:

$$A_R = L_{pp} \times T$$

where $L_{pp}$ is the length between perpendiculars and $T$ is the design draft. For more refined estimates, $A_R = L_{pp} \times d / 25$ is used, where $d$ is the design draft.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the K-T factor in rudder design, and what does it represent physically?**

**Model Answer:**
The **K-T (Kuchta-Tachibana) factor** is a hydrodynamic correction multiplier that accounts for the **finite aspect ratio** of the rudder and the resulting reduction in lift due to **wingtip vortices**. It is typically between **0.30 and 0.55**, depending on aspect ratio. Physically, it represents the ratio of actual 3D rudder lift to the ideal 2D infinite-span lift.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** With neat sketches, explain the different types of rudders used in ship design and state the advantages of a balanced rudder.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
A ship of $L_{pp} = 120$ m, $T = 6$ m, $V_S = 12$ knots has a wake fraction $w = 0.25$. If the rudder area is $720 \text{ m}^2$, $K_T = 0.45$, and rudder angle is $30°$, calculate:
(i) Effective rudder velocity
(ii) Maximum rudder side force

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Types of Rudders:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rudder Type Comparison Table:**

| Rudder Type | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|-------------|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| Spade Rudder | Single blade, no skeg support | High efficiency, simple | High stock load |
| Balanced Rudder | ~20–30% area ahead of stock | Reduced torque on stock | Slightly reduced lift |
| Semi-balanced | ~10–15% ahead of stock | Compromise design | Moderate torque |
| Twin Rudders | Two rudders, e.g., azimuthing config | Excellent maneuverability | Complex steering gear |
| Schilling Rudder | High-lift flap-type | Very high lift at low δ | Complex hydraulics |

**Advantage of Balanced Rudder:** The portion of rudder area **ahead of the stock** produces a force that **opposes the main side force** behind the stock, thereby **reducing the torque required** to hold the rudder at a given angle. This reduces the required steering gear capacity and improves steering response.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert ship speed**
$$V_S = 12 \text{ knots} = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.173 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Effective rudder velocity**
$$V_R = V_S (1 - w) = 6.173 \times (1 - 0.25) = 6.173 \times 0.75 = 4.630 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Side Force Calculation**
$$F_R = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 A_R K_T \sin\delta$$

Substituting:
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $V_R = 4.630 \text{ m/s}$
- $A_R = 720 \text{ m}^2$
- $K_T = 0.45$
- $\delta = 30°$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (4.630)^2 \times 720 \times 0.45 \times 0.5$$

Compute step-by-step:
- $(4.630)^2 = 21.44$
- $0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$
- $512.5 \times 21.44 = 10{,}988$
- $10{,}988 \times 720 = 7{,}911{,}360$
- $7{,}911{,}360 \times 0.45 = 3{,}560{,}112$
- $3{,}560{,}112 \times 0.5 = 1{,}780{,}056 \text{ N}$

$$\boxed{F_R \approx 1.78 \text{ MN}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Derive an expression for the required diameter of a solid circular rudder stock subjected to combined bending moment $M$ and torque $Q$.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
A rudder stock of length $L_s = 8$ m is subjected to a side force $F_R = 1.5$ MN acting horizontally at the center of pressure. The torque is $Q = 200$ kN·m. If $\tau_{allow} = 55$ MPa, calculate the required stock diameter.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Derivation (refer to Section 3.2 of these notes):**

The equivalent von Mises load is:
$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}$$

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$, the polar section modulus is $Z_p = \pi d^3/16$.

Setting maximum shear stress equal to allowable:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}}{\pi d^3} \le \tau_{allow}$$

Solving:
$$d \ge \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Bending moment**
$$M = F_R \times L_s = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m} = 12 \text{ MN·m}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent torque**
$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{(12 \times 10^6)^2 + (200 \times 10^3)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{144 \times 10^{12} + 0.04 \times 10^{12}}$$
$$= \sqrt{144.04 \times 10^{12}}$$
$$\approx 12.0017 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Stock diameter**
$$d \ge \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 \times 12.0017 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 55 \times 10^6}}$$

$$= \sqrt[3]{\frac{192.027 \times 10^6}{172.788 \times 10^6}}$$

$$= \sqrt[3]{1.1112} \approx 1.036 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 1.04 \text{ m} = 1040 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to convert knots → m/s is a frequent 2-mark deduction. Always multiply knots by **0.5144**.
> 2. **Wake fraction sign:** Students often use $V_R = V_S (1 + w)$ — WRONG. Wake **reduces** velocity, so it's $(1 - w)$.
> 3. **Allowable stress units:** $\tau_{allow}$ for steel is in **MPa**, so ensure forces are in **N** and moments in **N·m**.
> 4. **Trigonometric errors:** Use **radians** in Taylor expansions but **degrees** in $\sin/\cos$ for direct force equations.
> 5. **Forgetting $K_T$ factor:** Simply using $2\pi \sin\delta$ without the K-T correction leads to overestimation of force.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Rudder area baseline:** $A_R = L_{pp} \times T$ (memorize this).
- ✅ **Effective velocity:** Always reduce ship speed by wake fraction.
- ✅ **K-T factor:** 0.30–0.55; decreases with lower aspect ratio.
- ✅ **Side force formula:** $F_R = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2 A_R K_T \sin\delta$ — the most-tested equation.
- ✅ **Bending moment:** $M = F_R \times L_{stock}$.
- ✅ **Torque:** $Q = F_R \times c \times \cos\lambda$.
- ✅ **Stock diameter:** $d = \sqrt[3]{\frac{16 \sqrt{M^2 + Q^2}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}}$.
- ✅ **Sea water density:** Always use **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** unless freshwater specified.
- ✅ **Maximum rudder angle:** Typically ±35° in service.
- ✅ **Type–Torque relation:** Balanced rudder reduces stock torque by ~25%.
- ✅ **Wake fraction range:** 0.20 (full hull) to 0.40 (fine hull).
- ✅ **Aspect ratio:** $\lambda = h^2/A_R$, typically 1.0–2.0 for ship rudders.
- ✅ **IMO compliance:** Tactical diameter ≤ 5L; advance ≤ 4.5L for standard turning.

> **Mnemonic for quick recall — "RUDDER":**
> **R** — Reference area via $L_{pp} \times T$
> **U** — Use effective velocity $V_R = V_S(1-w)$
> **D** — Determine K-T factor based on aspect ratio
> **D** — Derive side force via $F_R = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_R^2 A_R K_T \sin\delta$
> **E** — Evaluate torque and bending moment
> **R** — Required stock diameter from combined loading

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rudder Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Rudder Types

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rudder:** A rudder is a primary, ship-mounted, hydrodynamically-actuated directional control surface installed at the stern (aft) of a vessel. When rotated about its hinge axis (the **rudder stock**), it generates a transverse hydrodynamic side force (the **rudder force, F_R**) which, acting at a moment arm from the ship's centre of gravity (G), produces a **yawing moment** that changes the vessel's heading.

A rudder is therefore a **lift-producing device** operating in the screw-race (propeller slipstream) and ambient-flow region, governed by the classical lift equation:

$$F_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,A_R\,C_L$$

where:
- $F_R$ = Rudder side force (N)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of seawater (≈ **1025 kg/m³**)
- $V$ = Effective flow velocity past the rudder (m/s)
- $A_R$ = Rudder area (m²)
- $C_L$ = Lift coefficient of the rudder (function of rudder angle $\delta$ and aspect ratio)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of a rudder as the "**ship's steering wheel underwater**". Just as you tilt a bicycle's front wheel to turn left or right, the helmsman tilts the rudder. The water flowing past the angled rudder pushes harder on one side than the other, and this push makes the ship's stern swing — pointing the bow in a new direction. The harder you tilt (larger rudder angle) and the larger the submerged plate, the stronger the turning pull.

### 1.3 Why Rudders Come in Different Types

Not all ships manoeuvre the same way:
- A **supertanker** needs a small, robust rudder because its turning circle is huge anyway.
- A **warship** needs a tall, fast-acting rudder for high-speed zig-zag manoeuvres.
- A **tugboat** needs huge, high-lift rudders to pivot on a dime.

Thus, **rudder type is selected to match the vessel's mission profile, speed, hull form, and propeller characteristics**.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Rudder Selection

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|
| Rudder area ratio (A_R / L·T) | 1/60 to 1/70 (merchant), up to 1/40 (warships) |
| Rudder aspect ratio (height / chord) | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Aspect ratio λ = b²/A | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| Stock diameter | 0.20 – 0.30 × √(A_R) (Naval Architects' rule) |
| Balance ratio (b_f / b) | 0.15 – 0.30 (typical) |
| Max rudder angle (δ) | ±35° merchant, ±45° naval |
| Stock bearing pressure | ≤ **10 N/mm²** (design limit) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — "Why and How" a Rudder Works

A rudder is a **hydrofoil operating at an angle of attack** ($\alpha$) to the relative water flow. The flow splits into a higher-velocity (low-pressure) side and a lower-velocity (high-pressure) side, producing a **lift (L)** perpendicular to the flow and a **drag (D)** parallel to it. The component of the resultant force **perpendicular to the ship's centreline** is the **side force F_R**, and its moment about G produces a **turning moment**.

### Stepwise Logic

- **Step 1 — Effective velocity:** The rudder sits in the **propeller slipstream**, so the effective velocity is:
$$V_{eff} = \sqrt{V_s^2 + (0.6\,V_p - V_s)^2}$$
where $V_s$ = ship speed, $V_p$ = propeller slipstream velocity (~0.6 $V_s$ to 0.8 $V_s$ higher at the rudder).

- **Step 2 — Lift generation:** With the rudder rotated by $\delta$ to the centreline, the **angle of attack** in the slipstream is non-zero, and $C_L$ rises sharply.

- **Step 3 — Force and torque:** The side force acts at the **centre of pressure (c.p.)**, located at a distance $\bar{x}$ from the leading edge, normally at **0.30 – 0.40** of the chord for a plain rectangular plate.

- **Step 4 — Balance ratio:** To reduce the helm-torque, the rudder is **balanced** — its hinge axis is shifted forward by a fraction $b_f/b$ of the chord, so part of the rudder produces a torque that *cancels* the torque of the rest.

## 2.2 Classification of Rudders

Rudder types are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### A. By Hull-Mounting Geometry

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Unbalanced (or Stern-mounted) Rudder** | Hinged at the trailing edge of the sternpost or propeller post; the entire rudder lies aft of the stock. | Older single-screw merchant ships |
| **Balanced Rudder** | Stock axis shifted forward into the rudder; small forward area ($A_f$) ahead of stock, large area ($A_a$) behind stock. | Most modern ships |
| **Semi-Balanced Rudder** | Only a small portion lies ahead of the stock; partial balance. | Common compromise design |
| **Spade Rudder** | Free-floating rudder mounted on a single stock with no support from hull or sternpost. | Twin-screw, warships |
| **Twin Rudders** | Two rudders (one per propeller) on twin-screw vessels. | Tugs, trawlers, modern twin-screw ships |
| **Flanked Rudder** | Rudder mounted abaft the propeller in a partial nozzle or Kort nozzle. | High-thrust low-speed craft |

### B. By Planform / Shape

- **Single-plate rudder** (simple flat plate)
- **Streamlined (aerofoil-shaped) rudder** — better $C_L$ and stall characteristics
- **Fishtail (horn) rudder** — flared lower portion for added area in propeller race
- **Double-plate rudder** — two parallel plates spaced ~1 m apart (older, structural)

### C. By Special Mechanism / Active Devices

| Special Rudder | Distinctive Feature |
|---|---|
| **Becker Rudder (Flap Rudder)** | Trailing edge rotates as a flap; effective angle = $\delta_{main} + \delta_{flap}$ |
| **Schilling Rudder (High-Lift)** | Asymmetric, twisted flap rudder giving very high lift at low angles |
| **Kamewa / Ulstein Controllable Pitch Rudder** | Rudder combined with a controllable-pitch propeller hub |
| **Voith Schneider Propeller (VSP)** | Rotating vertical blades, not strictly a rudder but used for combined propulsion/steering |
| **Rotating Cylinder Rudder (Manoeuvring Roller)** | Cylinder rotates to alter flow and turn ship (Rolls-Royce/Mitsubishi) |
| **Active Rudder** | Rudder fitted with a small thrust-generating propeller in hub for low-speed assist |
| **Water-Jet Steering Rudder** | Reversible bucket on water-jet craft |
| **Pleuger Rudder** | Rudder with electric-hydraulic gear inside stock (compact installations) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are the absolute core of the rudder-types module — memorise and apply them in KTU examinations.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R C_L$ | Rudder side force | Basic lift equation |
| 2 | $C_L = \dfrac{\partial C_L}{\partial \alpha}\cdot\alpha$ (small α) | Linear lift slope | Aspect-ratio corrected via $\dfrac{C_L^{AR}}{1 + \tfrac{2}{AR}}$ |
| 3 | $C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi\,AR}{2 + \sqrt{AR^2 + 4}}$ | Slender-body lift slope | Rudder lift curve slope |
| 4 | $C_D = C_{D0} + \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi e AR}$ | Total drag (includes induced) | Drag in slipstream |
| 5 | $N_R = F_R \cdot l_R$ | Yawing moment from rudder | Turning ability |
| 6 | $K_T = F_R \cdot e$ | Torque on rudder stock | Helm machinery sizing |
| 7 | $e = \bar{x}_a - \tfrac{b_f}{b}(\bar{x}_a + \bar{x}_f)$ | Effective moment arm | Balance ratio derivation |
| 8 | $\eta_R = \dfrac{N_R}{\delta} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R \dfrac{\partial C_L}{\partial \delta} \cdot l_R$ | Rudder-yaw derivative | Course stability |
| 9 | $Y'_R = \dfrac{\partial C_Y}{\partial \delta'} = \dfrac{\pi \lambda}{\lambda + 2}\cdot\dfrac{A_R}{L T}$ | Non-dimensional rudder sway force derivative | MMG model |
| 10 | $N'_R = -\alpha'\,Y'_R$ (with $x'_R$ term) | Yaw moment derivative (MMG) | Manoeuvring simulation |
| 11 | $b_{f}/b$ | Balance ratio (typ. 0.20–0.30) | Rudder design parameter |
| 12 | $d_s = (0.20\text{ to }0.30)\sqrt{A_R}$ | Stock diameter (m) | Empirical sizing rule |

> **NOTATION LEGEND:** $A_R$ = rudder area, $AR$ = aspect ratio, $V$ = flow speed, $l_R$ = longitudinal lever of rudder force from midship, $\bar{x}_a,\bar{x}_f$ = c.p. positions aft/forward of stock, $e$ = effective eccentricity, $\eta_R$ = rudder-yaw derivative, $\delta$ = rudder angle, $Y'_R$ = non-dimensional sway derivative, $N'_R$ = non-dimensional yaw derivative.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Course-keeping:** A balanced rudder with proper aspect ratio ensures **low helm effort** at cruising speed.
- **Crash-stop & turning:** Flap (Becker/Schilling) rudders reduce tactical diameter by up to **40 %** — vital for naval and harbour tugs.
- **Slow-speed control:** Active rudders (with a small hub propeller) enable a vessel to dock sideways without tug assistance.
- **Course stability:** Rudder area and balance ratio directly govern the **weathercock** tendency.
- **Propeller-rudder interaction:** A high-velocity slipstream increases effective lift by **~30–50 %** over open-water values — this is why the rudder is placed directly behind the propeller.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Rudder Side Force from First Principles

We begin with the elementary hydrofoil lift equation and adjust it for the rudder's actual operating environment.

### Step 1 — General lift on a 2D foil

For a 2D hydrofoil in an ideal, inviscid fluid, the **Kutta–Joukowski theorem** gives:

$$L' = \rho\,V_{\infty}\,\Gamma$$

where $\Gamma$ = bound circulation. The lift coefficient for a thin flat plate is:

$$C_L = 2\pi\,\alpha \quad (\text{radians})$$

### Step 2 — Finite aspect ratio correction (Prandtl's lifting-line)

A real rudder is a 3D finite wing. Prandtl's lifting-line theory gives the 3D lift-curve slope:

$$C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{C_{L\alpha,2D}}{1 + \dfrac{C_{L\alpha,2D}}{\pi e AR}}$$

Substituting $C_{L\alpha,2D} = 2\pi$ and introducing an Oswald efficiency $e \approx 0.85$ for a typical rudder:

$$C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi}{1 + \dfrac{2}{e\,AR}}$$

Equivalently, expressed directly:

$$C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi\,e\,AR}{e\,AR + 2}$$

For the common case $e = 1$ (ideal), this simplifies to:

$$C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi\,AR}{AR + 2}$$

### Step 3 — Lift and side force

For a small rudder angle $\delta$ (i.e., $\alpha \approx \delta$ in the slipstream), the lift coefficient is:

$$C_L = C_{L\alpha}\,\delta$$

The total **rudder side force** is therefore:

$$F_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,C_{L\alpha}\,\delta$$

> This is the **canonical KTU-board form** of the rudder force equation.

### Step 4 — Numerical illustration

A bulk carrier with $A_R = 25\,\text{m}^2$, $AR = 1.8$, $V = 10\,\text{kn}$ ($5.14\,\text{m/s}$), $\delta = 35° = 0.611\,\text{rad}$:

$$C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1.8}{1.8 + 2} = \dfrac{11.31}{3.8} = 2.977\,\text{rad}^{-1}$$

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (5.14)^2 \times 25 \times 2.977 \times 0.611$$

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 26.42 \times 25 \times 1.819$$

$$F_R \approx 6.03 \times 10^5\,\text{N} \approx 603\,\text{kN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2π formulation gives a **theoretical ideal**. Aboard ships, the effective $C_L$ is reduced by viscous effects and the rudder-section shape; empirical corrections may lower this by 20–40 %.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Balance Ratio & Effective Torque Arm

### Step 1 — Define moment arms

Let the chord length be $b$. The stock lies at distance $b_f$ from the leading edge and $b_a = b - b_f$ from the trailing edge.

### Step 2 — Centre-of-pressure positions

For a flat plate, the **centre of pressure (c.p.)** measured from the leading edge is:

$$\bar{x}_{cp} = \dfrac{b}{4} \cdot \dfrac{C_D + 4 C_L \sin\alpha - C_L^2 \cos\alpha / \pi\,AR}{C_L \cos\alpha + C_D \sin\alpha}$$

To first order, for a flat plate of **small camber and small $\alpha$**:

$$\bar{x}_{cp} \approx \dfrac{b}{4} \quad (\text{from LE})$$

### Step 3 — Effective moment arm

If $\bar{x}_f$ is the c.p. of the forward portion (between LE and stock) and $\bar{x}_a$ is the c.p. of the aft portion, then by lever-arm balance:

$$F_R \cdot e = (F_R - F_f)\bar{x}_a - F_f\,\bar{x}_f$$

where $F_f$ = lift on the forward slice, and after re-arrangement (using $A_f = b_f \cdot s$, $A_a = (b - b_f) \cdot s$ where $s$ = span):

$$e = \bar{x}_a - \dfrac{b_f}{b}\bigl(\bar{x}_a + \bar{x}_f\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** By setting $e = 0$ (i.e., the stock axis coincident with the c.p. of the *whole* rudder), the helm torque vanishes. The required **balance ratio** to achieve $e = 0$ is:
> $$\boxed{\;\dfrac{b_f}{b} = \dfrac{\bar{x}_a}{\bar{x}_a + \bar{x}_f} \approx \dfrac{b/4}{b/2} = 0.5\;}$$
> In practice, designers use $b_f/b = 0.20\text{–}0.30$ to keep a small helm-torque (positive stability feedback) but reduce steering gear size drastically.

### Step 4 — Torque on stock

The required stock torque is then:

$$K_T = F_R \cdot e = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,C_L \cdot e$$

> A 25 % balance ($b_f/b = 0.25$) typically reduces the stock torque to **~30 %** of the unbalanced value.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Rudder Yaw Derivative $N'_\delta$ (MMG Convention)

The **Maneuvering Modeling Group (MMG)** formulation expresses hydrodynamic derivatives non-dimensionally. The non-dimensional sway force derivative from the rudder is:

$$Y'_{\delta} = \dfrac{\partial C_Y}{\partial \delta'} = \dfrac{\pi\lambda}{\lambda + 2}\cdot\dfrac{A_R}{LT}$$

The corresponding yaw derivative (taking moments about midship, with $x'_R = l_R/L$):

$$N'_{\delta} = -\alpha'\,Y'_{\delta} = -\dfrac{x_R - 0.5}{L}\cdot\dfrac{\pi\lambda}{\lambda + 2}\cdot\dfrac{A_R}{LT}$$

> **Interpretation:** $N'_{\delta}$ governs how much **yaw acceleration** is produced per unit rudder angle — i.e., the **turning ability** of the vessel. Negative sign = positive $\delta$ produces positive yaw moment, in some sign conventions.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Rudder Type Selection

| Rudder Type | Lift Coefficient Range | Torque | Speed Regime | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unbalanced Flat Plate | $C_L \le 1.0$ | Very high | Low–Medium | Older merchant | Heavy steering gear |
| Balanced Plate | $C_L \le 1.0$ | Moderate | Low–Medium | General merchant | Slight loss of effectiveness |
| Streamlined Spade | $C_L \le 1.4$ | Low (balanced) | Medium–High | Warships, fast ferries | Sensitive to cavitation |
| Becker Flap Rudder | $C_L \le 2.5$ (combined) | Moderate | All speeds | Tugs, naval | Mechanical complexity |
| Schilling Rudder | $C_L \le 3.0$ | Moderate | All speeds | Naval combatants | Cost, complexity |
| Fishtail (Horn) | $C_L \le 1.2$ | Moderate | Propeller-driven | Single-screw merchant | Lower portion structurally loaded |
| Twin Rudders | $C_L \le 1.0$ (each) | Low | All | Twin-screw tugs | Interaction effects |
| Active Rudder (with hub propeller) | Adds thrust component | — | Low (docking) | Docking without tugs | Power consumption |
| Rotating Cylinder | Variable (via spin) | Very low | All | Special naval | Maintenance of seals |
| Voith Schneider | Continuous vectoring | None (cyclical) | Low (harbour) | Harbour vessels | Not a true rudder |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Rudder Force Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Hull and Propeller Operating Condition] --> B[Relative Water Flow at Stern]
    B --> C[Propeller Slipstream V_p ~ 0.7 V_s]
    C --> D[Effective Flow V_eff at Rudder]
    A2[Helmsman Command or Autopilot] --> E[Rudder Angle Delta Command]
    E --> F[Steering Gear and Rudder Stock Rotation]
    F --> G[Angle of Attack Alpha on Rudder Profile]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Hydrodynamic Pressure Distribution]
    H --> I[Lift Coefficient C_L on Rudder]
    I --> J[Rudder Side Force F_R 0.5 rho V2 A_R C_L]
    J --> K[Yawing Moment N_R F_R times l_R]
    K --> L[Change of Heading d psi dt]
    L --> M[Ship Turns to New Course]
    J --> N[Stock Torque K_T F_R times e]
    N --> O[Steering Gear Load]
    P[Rudder Type and Balance Ratio b_f over b] --> J
    P --> N
    P --> F
    Q[Flow Separation and Stall at Large Delta] --> R[Loss of F_R and Effectiveness]
    I --> R
    M --> S[Course-keeping or Turning Response Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Rudder Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rudders] --> B1[By Hull Mounting]
    A --> B2[By Planform Shape]
    A --> B3[By Special Mechanism]
    B1 --> C1[Unbalanced Sternpost]
    B1 --> C2[Balanced Semi or Fully]
    B1 --> C3[Spade Free Floating]
    B1 --> C4[Twin Rudders]
    B1 --> C5[Flanked Kort Nozzle]
    B2 --> D1[Single Plate]
    B2 --> D2[Streamlined Aerofoil]
    B2 --> D3[Fishtail Horn]
    B2 --> D4[Double Plate]
    B3 --> E1[Becker Flap]
    B3 --> E2[Schilling High Lift]
    B3 --> E3[Kamewa CP Rudder]
    B3 --> E4[Active Rudder with Hub Propeller]
    B3 --> E5[Rotating Cylinder]
    B3 --> E6[Voith Schneider]
    B3 --> E7[Water Jet Steering Bucket]
```

## 4.3 Force and Lever-Arm Diagram — Balanced Rudder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LE[Leading Edge of Rudder] -->|Chord b| TE[Trailing Edge]
    LE -->|b_f| ST[Stock and Hinge Axis]
    ST -->|b_a| TE
    FWD[Forward Portion A_f b_f times span] -->|Lift L_f acts at x_f| ST
    AFT[Aft Portion A_a b_a times span] -->|Lift L_a acts at x_a| ST
    ST -->|Net Lever e| CP[Net Centre of Pressure of Whole Rudder]
    FR[Total Side Force F_R] --> CP
    CP -->|Effective Arm e| M[Stock Torque K_T F_R times e]
    BR[Balance Ratio b_f over b in Box] -.governs.-> e
    BR -.typical.-> 0.25
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Right Rudder Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Selection for New Vessel] --> S1{Vessel Type and Mission}
    S1 -->|Merchant Cargo Ship| P1[Balanced Single Streamlined Rudder A_R over L T 1 by 60 to 1 by 70]
    S1 -->|Warship or High Speed Naval| P2[Spade Streamlined High Lift with Schilling or Becker Variant]
    S1 -->|Tug or Harbour Craft| P3[Twin Rudders with Flap or Kort Nozzle]
    S1 -->|Twin Screw General| P4[Twin Balanced Spade Rudders]
    S1 -->|DP or Docking Critical| P5[Active Rudder with Hub Thruster]
    S1 -->|Water Jet Fast Craft| P6[Reversible Bucket Steering Nozzle]
    P1 --> Q1{Check Stock Torque K_T}
    Q1 -->|Within Steering Gear Capacity| OK1[Accept Design]
    Q1 -->|Exceeds Capacity| REV1[Increase b_f or Use Becker Flap]
    P2 --> Q2{Check Cavitation at High Speed}
    Q2 -->|No Cavitation| OK2[Accept Design]
    Q2 -->|Cavitation Risk| REV2[Use Hollow Section or Streamlined Profile]
    P3 --> Q3{Check Twin-Rudder Interaction}
    Q3 -->|Acceptable| OK3[Accept Design]
    Q3 -->|Strong Interaction| REV3[Increase Spacing or Twist Rudder]
```
