# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**Define a balanced rudder and state the typical balance ratio used in modern ship design.**

**Model Answer:**
A **balanced rudder** is one in which the hinge axis (rudder stock) is shifted forward from the leading edge so that a small portion of the rudder area lies *ahead* of the stock. The hydrodynamic side force generated by this forward portion produces a torque opposite to that produced by the larger aft portion, thereby **reducing the net torque** that the steering gear must overcome.

The **balance ratio** is defined as:
$$\text{Balance ratio} = \dfrac{b_f}{b} = \dfrac{\text{Chord ahead of stock}}{\text{Total chord}}$$

A typical balance ratio in modern design is **0.20 to 0.30** (i.e., 20–30 % of the rudder area is forward of the stock).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**What is a Becker rudder? Mention its main advantage.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Becker rudder** (also called a **flap rudder**) is a high-lift rudder type in which the trailing edge of the rudder is designed as a separate **flap** that can rotate by an additional angle relative to the main rudder. The effective angle of attack of the combined surface becomes:
$$\delta_{eff} = \delta_{main} + \delta_{flap}$$

**Main advantage:** It produces a **significantly higher lift coefficient** (up to $C_L \approx 2.5$) compared to a plain plate rudder (max $C_L \approx 1.0$), thereby **reducing the tactical diameter of the turning circle** and improving low-speed manoeuvrability — making it especially suited for tugs and naval vessels.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Internal-Choice Format Note:** In the KTU University End-Semester Examination (ESE), the question paper presents a single question with an **OR** choice. Below we provide BOTH the main question (Question A) and the OR-choice (Question B), each worth 14 marks and split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Rudder Force and Balance Analysis* `[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]`

**Q. (a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, explain the construction and working of a **balanced spade rudder**. Discuss the effect of balance ratio on helm torque and rudder effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **balanced spade rudder** is a single plate (or aerofoil-section plate) that is **free-floating** with respect to the hull — it is supported only by a single rudder stock passing through the bottom shell of the ship. It has **no connection to the sternpost or propeller post**. The hinge (stock) axis is shifted forward by a chord-wise distance $b_f$, making the rudder *balanced*.

**Construction (labelled sketch — described):**
- Leading edge (LE) and trailing edge (TE) define a chord length $b$.
- Rudder stock passes vertically at distance $b_f$ from LE and $b_a$ from TE.
- Tiller arm at the top of the stock is rotated by the steering gear.
- Lower bearing and upper bearing support the stock.
- Forward portion (ahead of stock) area $A_f$ and aft portion $A_a$.

**Working:** When the helmsman applies a rudder angle $\delta$:
1. The angled rudder creates a **side force $F_R$** given by:
$$F_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R\,C_L$$
2. $F_R$ acts at the centre of pressure, which for a flat plate is at $b/4$ from LE.
3. The stock torque is:
$$K_T = F_R \cdot e$$
where $e = \bar{x}_a - \tfrac{b_f}{b}(\bar{x}_a + \bar{x}_f)$ is the effective moment arm.

**Effect of balance ratio:**
- **$b_f/b = 0$** (unbalanced): $e = \bar{x}_a \approx b/4$ → maximum helm torque, but maximum effectiveness (the entire force is "useful").
- **$b_f/b = 0.25$** (typical): $e$ is reduced to ~30 % of the unbalanced value → helm torque is much smaller, with only a marginal loss in total $F_R$ because the forward portion's lift contributes to $F_R$ as well.
- **$b_f/b \to 0.5$** (theoretical): $e \to 0$ → no helm torque, but the rudder is "neutrally stable" and can flutter, so this is avoided in practice.

**Conclusion:** Designers select a balance ratio in the range **0.20 to 0.30** to optimise the trade-off between **low helm torque** and **retained turning effectiveness** with positive stability.

---

**Q. (b)** A ship has a rudder of area $A_R = 22\,\text{m}^2$ and aspect ratio $AR = 1.6$. At a service speed of 14 knots, the rudder is put to $30°$ hard-over. Calculate:
1. The rudder side force $F_R$ assuming $C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi\,AR}{AR + 2}$.
2. The torque on the stock if the balance ratio is 0.25 and $\bar{x}_a = 0.30 b$, $\bar{x}_f = 0.20 b$.
3. Comment on how the propeller slipstream modifies these values in practice. **(7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $A_R = 22\,\text{m}^2$, $AR = 1.6$
- $V = 14\,\text{kn} = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.20\,\text{m/s}$
- $\delta = 30° = \pi/6 = 0.5236\,\text{rad}$
- $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $b_f/b = 0.25$, $\bar{x}_a = 0.30b$, $\bar{x}_f = 0.20b$

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{L\alpha}$:**

$$C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1.6}{1.6 + 2} = \dfrac{10.05}{3.6} = 2.792\,\text{rad}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_L$:**

$$C_L = C_{L\alpha}\cdot\delta = 2.792 \times 0.5236 = 1.462$$

**Step 3 — Compute $F_R$:**

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.20)^2 \times 22 \times 1.462$$

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 51.84 \times 22 \times 1.462$$

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 51.84 \times 32.16$$

$$F_R \approx 8.55 \times 10^5\,\text{N} \approx 855\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective moment arm $e$:**

$$e = \bar{x}_a - \dfrac{b_f}{b}\bigl(\bar{x}_a + \bar{x}_f\bigr)$$

$$e = 0.30b - 0.25(0.30b + 0.20b)$$

$$e = 0.30b - 0.25(0.50b) = 0.30b - 0.125b = 0.175b$$

For a typical chord (say $b \approx 2.2$ m from $A_R = 22\,\text{m}^2$ with $AR = 1.6 \Rightarrow$ span $\approx 3.52$ m, $b \approx 22/3.52 \approx 6.25$ m — however, for the **torque** we need the *dimensionless* $e$ and a different formulation, so we proceed symbolically):

$$e/b = 0.175$$

So the effective moment arm is **17.5 % of the chord**.

**Step 5 — Stock torque:**

The actual stock torque for a rudder with span $s$ and chord $b$ where $A_R = b\cdot s$:

- $s = b \cdot AR = 1.6 b$ → $A_R = b \times 1.6b = 1.6 b^2 = 22$ → $b^2 = 13.75$ → $b = 3.71$ m, $s = 5.93$ m.
- Effective lever $e = 0.175 \times 3.71 = 0.649$ m.

$$K_T = F_R \cdot e = 855 \times 10^3 \times 0.649 \approx 5.55 \times 10^5\,\text{N·m} \approx 555\,\text{kN·m}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on slipstream effect:**

In practice the rudder sits in the **propeller slipstream**, whose axial velocity is roughly $0.6 V_s$ higher than the ambient ship speed. Hence:
$$V_{eff} = V_s + 0.6 V_s = 1.6 V_s$$
This **increases $F_R$ by a factor of $1.6^2 \approx 2.56$** — i.e., the rudder force may be **2.5× higher** than the open-water estimate. The stock torque scales similarly. This is why the rudder is most effective at high propeller loads (during crash-stop, crash-astern, or low ship speed with high r.p.m.).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Special Rudder Types* `[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]`

**Q. (a)** Compare the construction and operational features of **Becker, Schilling, and active rudders**. State the typical lift-coefficient ranges and where each type is most commonly employed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Becker Flap Rudder | Schilling Rudder | Active Rudder |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Main rudder with a separate pivoted flap at the trailing edge; flap rotates by $\delta_{flap}$ | Asymmetric (twisted) main rudder + flap with independent control | Conventional rudder with a small **hub propeller** fitted in a streamlined pod |
| **Mechanism** | $\delta_{eff} = \delta_{main} + \delta_{flap}$ | Twist of main foil + flap deflection for high lift at low angles | Thrust vectoring by the rudder; propeller supplies transverse thrust |
| **Max $C_L$** | $\approx 1.8$ – $2.5$ | $\approx 2.0$ – $3.0$ (best at low $\alpha$) | $C_L$ as for normal rudder, **plus** thrust component |
| **Effective speed regime** | All speeds; excellent at low speed | All speeds; designed for low-α high-lift | **Low-speed / zero-speed** (docking) |
| **Helm torque** | Moderate (flap also produces torque) | Moderate | Modest; propeller provides assist |
| **Application** | Tugs, naval combatants, harbour craft | Naval combatants, fast ferries | DP vessels, shuttle tankers, cruise ships (docking) |
| **Limitations** | Mechanical complexity; flap hinge maintenance | Costly; complex controls | Fuel/power penalty; noise/vibration |

**Summary of use:** Becker and Schilling are used when **high lift at moderate speed** is needed; the active rudder is used for **slow-speed lateral control**.

---

**Q. (b)** A ship uses twin rudders, each of area $9\,\text{m}^2$, aspect ratio $1.5$, balance ratio $0.22$. At $V = 12$ knots, both rudders are put to $25°$ in the same direction (overhauled). Calculate the **total yawing moment** $N_R$ if the rudders are placed at $l_R = 60$ m aft of midship. Assume seawater $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and ideal 2π-lift slope corrected for aspect ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $A_R$ (each) = $9\,\text{m}^2$, twin → total $A_R = 18\,\text{m}^2$
- $AR = 1.5$, $b_f/b = 0.22$ (not needed for force)
- $V = 12$ kn $= 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.173\,\text{m/s}$
- $\delta = 25° = 0.4363\,\text{rad}$
- $l_R = 60$ m

**Step 1 — Lift-curve slope:**

$$C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1.5}{1.5 + 2} = \dfrac{9.42}{3.5} = 2.692\,\text{rad}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Lift coefficient:**

$$C_L = 2.692 \times 0.4363 = 1.175$$

**Step 3 — Total rudder side force (both rudders):**

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (6.173)^2 \times 18 \times 1.175$$

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 38.10 \times 18 \times 1.175$$

$$F_R = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 38.10 \times 21.15$$

$$F_R \approx 4.13 \times 10^5\,\text{N} \approx 413\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Yawing moment:**

$$N_R = F_R \cdot l_R = 413 \times 10^3 \times 60 \approx 2.48 \times 10^7\,\text{N·m} \approx 24.8\,\text{MN·m}$$

**Note on interaction:** Because the twin rudders operate close to the centreline, the **effective** $F_R$ on each is reduced by approximately 5–10 % due to hydrodynamic interaction; the actual moment may therefore be **~22 MN·m** in practice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Rudder-Type Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s** — many students keep $V$ in knots in the equation $F_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R C_L$. This is the **#1 cause of arithmetic blunders**. Use $V (\text{m/s}) = V(\text{kn}) \times 0.5144$.
> 2. **Using $\delta$ in degrees** — $\alpha$ and $\delta$ in the linear lift formula MUST be in **radians**. Convert with $\pi/180$.
> 3. **Mixing up balance-ratio sign** — the formula $e = \bar{x}_a - \tfrac{b_f}{b}(\bar{x}_a + \bar{x}_f)$ is the only correct form. Writing $e = \bar{x}_a + \tfrac{b_f}{b}(\bar{x}_a - \bar{x}_f)$ is a common, *expensive* error.
> 4. **Ignoring the propeller slipstream** — open-water $F_R$ is **2–3× lower** than the actual force on a ship; the KTU paper often awards a 2-mark sub-part for this comment, and students skip it.
> 5. **Confusing "spade" with "balanced"** — a spade rudder is *free-floating* (no hull support), and a balanced rudder is defined by the **forward shift of the stock**. The two concepts are orthogonal. A spade rudder may be balanced, semi-balanced, or unbalanced.
> 6. **Forgetting $A_R = $ total of all rudders** — for **twin rudders**, the *total* rudder area is the sum. Some students take $A_R$ of one rudder only and get half the moment.
> 7. **Cavitation oversight** — at high speeds, streamlined rudders cavitate, reducing $C_L$. Naval architecture answers should mention this on warships.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Rudder Types**

- ✅ A **rudder** is a ship-mounted hydrofoil that produces a side force $F_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_R C_L$ and hence a yawing moment $N_R = F_R \cdot l_R$.
- ✅ **Unbalanced rudders** are hinged at the trailing edge of the sternpost; the **whole** rudder is aft of the stock → **high helm torque**.
- ✅ **Balanced rudders** shift the stock forward by a balance ratio $b_f/b = 0.20$ – $0.30$, drastically reducing helm torque.
- ✅ **Spade rudders** are free-floating, mounted on a single stock — common on warships and twin-screw ships.
- ✅ **Twin rudders** appear on twin-screw vessels and tugs; total $A_R$ is the sum of both.
- ✅ **Fishtail (horn) rudder** has an enlarged lower portion in the propeller slipstream for higher force.
- ✅ **Streamlined / aerofoil-section rudders** give $C_L$ up to 1.4 with delayed stall vs. ~1.0 for flat plates.
- ✅ **Becker (flap) rudders** add a pivoted trailing-edge flap with $\delta_{eff} = \delta_{main} + \delta_{flap}$, giving $C_L$ up to ~2.5.
- ✅ **Schilling rudders** are asymmetric, twisted high-lift rudders (naval combat vessels).
- ✅ **Active rudders** integrate a small hub propeller — for low-speed lateral control / dynamic positioning.
- ✅ **Voith Schneider** and **rotating cylinder rudders** are special high-manoeuvrability devices for harbour and special craft.
- ✅ **Stock torque** is $K_T = F_R \cdot e$, with $e = \bar{x}_a - \tfrac{b_f}{b}(\bar{x}_a + \bar{x}_f)$.
- ✅ **Stock diameter** ≈ $0.20$ to $0.30 \times \sqrt{A_R}$ (Naval Architects' empirical rule).
- ✅ **Rudder-area ratio** $A_R/(L T) = 1/60$ to $1/70$ (merchant), up to $1/40$ (warships).
- ✅ **Lift-curve slope** for finite-aspect-ratio rudder: $C_{L\alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi\,AR}{AR + 2}$.
- ✅ The **propeller slipstream** raises effective $V$ by ~60 %, multiplying $F_R$ by ~2.5×.
- ✅ **Max rudder angle**: ±35° (merchant), ±45° (naval).
- ✅ **Cavitation** at high speed can reduce $C_L$ — addressed by hollow sections and aerofoil shapes.
- ✅ **MMG derivatives** $Y'_\delta$ and $N'_\delta$ are the **non-dimensional** rudder sway and yaw derivatives used in manoeuvring simulation.

> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions on rudder types, structure your answer in three parts: **(i) labelled sketch + construction**, **(ii) force/balance analysis with formulae**, and **(iii) a clear comparison table** with a paragraph on the specific application. This pattern is the **highest-scoring layout** identified from past valuation keys.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Location
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Location # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Concept of "Location" in Ship Dynamics In **Ship Dynamics (Module 4)**, the topic of **Location** refers to the determination of the spatial position of the three fundamental vertical points that govern the static and dynamic stability of a floating vessel: 1. The **Center of Buoyancy (B)** – the centroid of the submerged (displaced) volume. 2. The **Center of Gravity (G)** – the centroid of the ship's mass (weight). 3. The **Metacenter (M)** – a geometric point derived from the new position of B when the ship is inclined by a small angle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Location of B, G, and M (and the resulting distances KB, KG, KM, BM, and GM) is the **single most important geometric pre-requisite** for all subsequent modules covering rolling, pitching, dynamic stability, and righting levers. ## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board Standard Terminology) ### Center of Buoyancy (B) > The point through which the **total buoyancy force** (equal to the weight of displaced water) is considered to act. Mathematically, it is the **centroid of the underwater volume** of the ship. ### Center of Gravity (G) > The point through which the **total weight** of the ship (and its contents) is considered to act. It is the **centroid of the ship's mass distribution** above and below the waterline. ### Metacenter (M) > The point of intersection between the **vertical line passing through the new Center of Buoyancy (B')** (in the heeled condition) and the **original vertical centerline** of the ship. For small angles of heel (θ ≤ 10°), M remains effectively fixed. ### Key Vertical Distances (measured from the Keel, K) | Symbol | Meaning | Direction | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **KB** | Height of Center of Buoyancy above Keel | Always Positive | | **KG** | Height of Center of Gravity above Keel | Always Positive | | **KM** | Height of Metacenter above Keel | Always Positive | | **BM** | Metacentric Radius (= KM − KB) | Always Positive | | **GM** | Metacentric Height (= KM − KG) | May be Positive or Negative | > [!NOTE] > The **stiffness** of a ship (i.e., its tendency to return upright) is **directly proportional to GM**. A large positive GM implies a stiff ship; a small or negative GM implies a tender or unstable ship. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "The Floating Pencil" Imagine holding a **pencil vertically** in a glass of water and trying to tilt it slightly: - The **weight** of the pencil (acting at G) tries to tip it over. - The **upthrust** from water (acting at B) tries to push it upright. - If you tilt the pencil a tiny amount, B shifts sideways to a new position B'. - The vertical line drawn upward through B' intersects the pencil's original centerline at a point we call **M**. Now, there are **three possible scenarios**: - If **M lies above G** → the pencil's weight creates a **restoring couple** → it rights itself. (Stable ship, GM > 0) - If **M lies exactly at G** → the pencil is in **neutral equilibrium** → it stays at any angle. (Critical condition, GM = 0) - If **M lies below G** → the weight creates an **overturning couple** → it capsizes. (Unstable ship, GM < 0) > [!TIP] > Think of **GM as a "stability dial"**: positive = safe, zero = critical, negative = capsize. Naval architects spend their careers making sure GM stays positive under all loading conditions. ## 1.4 The Standard KTU Notation Sketch (Memorize This) ``` M (Metacenter) | | | G (Center of Gravity) | | B' (new B in heeled condition) | / | / B / (Center of Buoyancy in upright condition) | / | / θ (heel angle) | / | / ───────────K───────────────────── Keel ``` The three critical points — K, B, G, M — are **collinear in the upright condition** and define the longitudinal stability axis of the ship. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Master Equation of Vertical Location The KTU board expects every student to commit the following **Master Equation** to memory. It is the **single most tested relation** in Module 4 (Location): $$ GM = KM - KG $$ Where: - **KM** = height of metacenter above keel - **KG** = height of center of gravity above keel - **GM** = metacentric height (the stability indicator) And the sub-relation: $$ KM = KB + BM $$ Where: - **KB** = height of center of buoyancy above keel - **BM** = metacentric radius ## 2.2 The Three Foundational Formulas ### Formula 1: Metacentric Radius (Transverse) $$ BM_{T} = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} $$ Where: - $I_{T}$ = **transverse second moment of area** of the waterplane about the centerline (m⁴) - $\nabla$ = **displaced volume** of the ship (m³) - $BM_{T}$ = transverse metacentric radius (m) ### Formula 2: Metacentric Radius (Longitudinal) $$ BM_{L} = \frac{I_{L}}{\nabla} $$ Where: - $I_{L}$ = **longitudinal second moment of area** of the waterplane about the transverse centerline (m⁴) - $BM_{L}$ = longitudinal metacentric radius (m) ### Formula 3: Center of Buoyancy Location $$ KB = \frac{\int_{0}^{T} A(z) \cdot z \, dz}{\nabla} $$ Where: - $A(z)$ = cross-sectional area at depth $z$ below waterline - $T$ = draft - The integral gives the **first moment of underwater volume** about the keel. ## 2.3 Second Moment of Area for Common Waterplanes | Waterplane Shape | Dimensions | $I_{T}$ (Transverse) | $I_{L}$ (Longitudinal) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Rectangle** | Length L, Breadth B | $\dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{L^{3} \cdot B}{12}$ | | **Triangle** (apex forward) | Base B, Height L | $\dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{36}$ | $\dfrac{L^{3} \cdot B}{36}$ | | **Trapezoid** | Parallel sides $a, b$, Length L | $\dfrac{L \cdot (a^{3} + b^{3})}{36}$ (approx.) | $\dfrac{L^{3}(a+b)}{48}$ | | **Ellipse** | Semi-axes a, b | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot a \cdot b^{3}}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot a^{3} \cdot b}{4}$ | | **Circle** | Radius R | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot R^{4}}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot R^{4}}{4}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Trap:** Do NOT confuse $I_{T}$ and $I_{L}$. $I_{T}$ uses $B^{3}$ (breadth cubed) — it controls **rolling**. $I_{L}$ uses $L^{3}$ (length cubed) — it controls **pitching/trim**. ## 2.4 Righting Lever (GZ) — The Stability Yardstick The **righting lever (GZ)** measures the effectiveness of the righting couple at any angle of heel $\theta$: $$ GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta + \text{(BONJOUR correction for large angles)} $$ For small angles (KTU Module 4 standard assumption): $$ GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx GM \cdot \theta \quad (\theta \text{ in radians}) $$ The **Righting Moment (RM)** is: $$ RM = W \cdot GZ = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta $$ Where $W$ is the **displacement** (in tonnes-force or kN). ## 2.5 The Moment to Change Trim (MCT) A **longitudinal** counterpart to GM, used for trim calculations: $$ \text{MCT (per cm)} = \frac{W \cdot GM_{L}}{100 \cdot L} $$ Where: - $W$ = displacement (tonnes) - $L$ = LBP (Length Between Perpendiculars, in meters) - $GM_{L}$ = longitudinal metacentric height (m) - MCT is in **tonnes·m per cm** of trim. The actual trim produced by a moment $M$ is: $$ \text{Trim (cm)} = \frac{M \text{ (tonnes·m)}}{\text{MCT (tonnes·m/cm)}} $$ ## 2.6 Free Surface Effect (FSE) Correction When a partially filled tank has a **free liquid surface**, the effective GM is **reduced**: $$ GM_{\text{effective}} = GM - \frac{i_{T} \cdot \rho_{\text{liquid}}}{\rho_{\text{seawater}} \cdot \nabla} $$ For a rectangular slack tank of length $l$ and breadth $b$: $$ GG' = \frac{l \cdot b^{3}}{12 \cdot \nabla} \quad \text{(virtual rise of G)} $$ > [!NOTE] > The **free surface correction is always subtractive** — it makes the ship **less stable**. This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The location of B, G, and M is the foundation for: - **Loading computer outputs** in modern ships (real-time GM display) - **Damage stability** calculations (IMO SOLAS requirement) - **Longitudinal strength** and trim optimization - **Dynamic stability** in waves (Module 4 onward) - **Parametric rolling** analysis (Module 5 typically) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Metacentric Radius Formula: $BM = I / \nabla$ This is the **derivation the KTU board loves to ask** for full marks. We derive it from **first principles using statics and geometry**. ### Assumptions - Ship is heeled by a **small angle** $\theta$ (typically $\theta \leq 10°$). - The underwater volume $\nabla$ remains **constant** (Archimedes' principle). - The center of gravity G has **not shifted** (a very small angle assumption). ### Geometric Setup Consider a small wedge-shaped volume of water $\delta v$ at a horizontal distance $y$ from the centerline. This wedge is: - **Added** to the down-heeling side (immersion wedge). - **Removed** from the up-heeling side (emersion wedge). The two wedges are **equal in volume** (since displacement is constant). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Define the wedge volume.** The transverse length of the wedge at distance $y$ from the centerline is approximately $y \cdot \theta$ (for small $\theta$). If $b$ is the longitudinal length of the strip, then: $$ \delta v = b \cdot y \cdot \theta \cdot \delta y $$ **Step 2: Locate the centroid of the wedge.** For a triangular wedge, the centroid is at $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of the height from the apex. The centroid of the **immersion wedge** is at: $$ y_{\text{imm}} = -\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{B}{2} = -\frac{B}{3} \quad \text{(negative, to port)} $$ The centroid of the **emersion wedge** is at: $$ y_{\text{em}} = +\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{B}{2} = +\frac{B}{3} \quad \text{(positive, to starboard)} $$ **Step 3: Compute the horizontal shift of the buoyancy center.** The shift in B from the centerline is given by the **first moment of the wedge volumes** about the centerline: $$ \overline{BB'} = \frac{\int y \, dv}{\nabla} $$ Since the wedges are equal in volume $\delta v$ but on opposite sides: $$ \overline{BB'} = \frac{\sum (\delta v \cdot y_{\text{centroid}})}{\nabla} $$ **Step 4: Substitute and simplify.** $$ \overline{BB'} = \frac{1}{\nabla} \int y \cdot b \cdot y \cdot \theta \, dy $$ $$ \overline{BB'} = \frac{\theta}{\nabla} \int b \cdot y^{2} \, dy $$ **Step 5: Recognize the integral as the second moment of area.** The integral $\int b \cdot y^{2} \, dy$ is exactly the **transverse second moment of area** of the waterplane about the centerline: $$ I_{T} = \int b \cdot y^{2} \, dy $$ Therefore: $$ \overline{BB'} = \frac{I_{T} \cdot \theta}{\nabla} $$ **Step 6: Use the small-angle triangle to find BM.** From the geometry of the heeled ship, in triangle $BMB'$: $$ \tan\theta = \frac{\overline{BB'}}{BM} \approx \theta = \frac{\overline{BB'}}{BM} \quad \text{(for small } \theta\text{)} $$ Substituting: $$ \theta = \frac{I_{T} \cdot \theta}{\nabla \cdot BM} $$ **Step 7: Cancel $\theta$ on both sides.** $$ 1 = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla \cdot BM} $$ $$ \boxed{BM = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla}} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Hint:** When asked to "derive the metacentric radius", always begin with the **buoyancy shift = $\theta \cdot$ (moment of wedge volume)** and end by **cancelling $\theta$**. The board examiner will be looking for these two "magic" steps. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing GM for a Rectangular Barge A **box-shaped barge** has the following particulars: - Length $L = 80$ m - Breadth $B = 12$ m - Draft $T = 4$ m$ - Total weight $W = 4000$ tonnes - Center of gravity $KG = 3.0$ m above keel - Seawater density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ **Find:** (i) KB, (ii) BM, (iii) KM, (iv) GM, (v) Righting moment at 5° heel. ### Solution **Step 1: Compute the displaced volume.** $$ \nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T = 80 \cdot 12 \cdot 4 = 3840 \text{ m}^{3} $$ Displacement verification: $$ W = \nabla \cdot \rho = 3840 \cdot 1.025 = 3936 \text{ tonnes} $$ (Note: There is a slight inconsistency with the given $W = 4000$ t. KTU examiners will usually accept the calculated $\nabla$ value.) **Step 2: Compute KB (Center of Buoyancy).** For a rectangular barge, B is at the **centroid of the underwater volume**, which is at half the draft: $$ KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.0 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3: Compute $I_{T}$ and BM.** $$ I_{T} = \frac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \cdot 12^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \cdot 1728}{12} = 11520 \text{ m}^{4} $$ $$ BM = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} = \frac{11520}{3840} = 3.0 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: Compute KM.** $$ KM = KB + BM = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.0 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5: Compute GM.** $$ GM = KM - KG = 5.0 - 3.0 = 2.0 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6: Compute the Righting Moment at $\theta = 5°$.** First, convert 5° to radians: $$ \theta = 5° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.0873 \text{ rad} $$ Then: $$ RM = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = 3936 \cdot 2.0 \cdot \sin(5°) $$ $$ RM = 3936 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.0872 = 686.5 \text{ t·m} $$ ### Final Answer Summary | Quantity | Value | | :--- | :--- | | KB | **2.0 m** | | BM | **3.0 m** | | KM | **5.0 m** | | GM | **2.0 m** | | RM at 5° | **686.5 t·m** | ## 3.3 Complete Derivation: KB by Integration for a Non-Rectangular Hull For a non-rectangular hull, KB is computed by integrating the **first moment of the underwater volume** about the keel. The standard form is: $$ KB = \frac{\int_{0}^{T} A(z) \cdot z \, dz}{\int_{0}^{T} A(z) \, dz} $$ **Numerical Approximation (Simpson's 1/3 Rule):** If the cross-sectional areas are tabulated at $n$ equispaced depths $z_{0}, z_{1}, \ldots, z_{n}$ with spacing $h$: $$ KB = \frac{\dfrac{h}{3} \left[ A_{0} z_{0} + 4 A_{1} z_{1} + 2 A_{2} z_{2} + 4 A_{3} z_{3} + \cdots + A_{n} z_{n} \right]}{\dfrac{h}{3} \left[ A_{0} + 4 A_{1} + 2 A_{2} + 4 A_{3} + \cdots + A_{n} \right]} $$ The $z$-factor in the numerator is the **Simpson weight × depth** product. > [!NOTE] > KTU Module 4 problems often give the offsets table and ask the student to **numerically evaluate KB** using Simpson's rule. Marks are awarded for correctly applying weights: **1, 4, 2, 4, 2, ..., 4, 1**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for KTU Problems | **Given** | **Wanted** | **Procedure** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | L, B, T, W, KG, $\rho$ (rectangular hull) | GM | $KB = T/2$, $I_{T} = LB^{3}/12$, $BM = I_{T}/\nabla$, $KM = KB+BM$, $GM = KM-KG$ | | W, KG, draft, freeboard (irregular hull) | GM | Use Bonjean data → $A(z)$ curve → Simpson's rule for KB and $\nabla$ | | Tank dimensions, fluid density, $\nabla$ | Free surface correction | $GG' = \dfrac{i_{T} \cdot \rho_{f}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla}$ (rectangular tank: $i_{T} = l b^{3}/12$) | | W, shift in G, $GM_{L}$, L | Trim change | $M = W \cdot \Delta GG_{L}$, $\text{Trim (cm)} = M / \text{MCT}$ | | $\nabla$, load, KG curve | Stability at multiple drafts | Plot $GZ$ vs $\theta$ for each condition (KTU Module 4 standard plot) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The "Stability Triangle" and its Components ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ship at Rest
Heel Angle theta = 0] --> B[Identify Displacement
Weight W = Displaced Volume × Density] B --> C[Locate Center of Buoyancy B
KB = Integral of A z dz / Integral A dz] B --> D[Locate Center of Gravity G
KG = Sum Wi z i / Sum Wi] C --> E[Calculate BM
BM Transverse = I Transverse / Volume] C --> F[Calculate BM Longitudinal
BM Longitudinal = I Longitudinal / Volume] E --> G[KM = KB + BM] D --> H[GM = KM minus KG] H --> I{Is GM Greater Than Zero?} I -->|Yes, Stable| J[Stiff Ship
Positive Righting Lever] I -->|Zero, Neutral| K[Critical Condition
Capsize Threshold] I -->|Negative, Unstable| L[Tender Ship
Immediate Capsize Risk] J --> M[Compute GZ = GM sin theta] M --> N[Plot GZ versus theta Curve] N --> O[Determine Maximum GZ
and Angle of Vanishing Stability] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Numerical Computation Pipeline for KB and BM ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hull Form Data
Bonjean Areas] --> B[Step 1: Tabulate A z versus z] B --> C[Step 2: Apply Simpson Weights
1 4 2 4 ... 4 1] C --> D[Step 3: Numerator Integral
Moment of Volume about Keel] C --> E[Step 4: Denominator Integral
Total Displaced Volume] D --> F[KB equals Numerator / Denominator] E --> G[Volume is the Denominator] G --> H[Step 5: Compute I Transverse
using Waterplane Integration] H --> I[Step 6: BM = I Transverse / Volume] F --> J[KM = KB + BM] I --> J K[KG from Loading Manual] --> L[GM = KM minus KG] J --> L ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Effect of Free Surface on Location of G ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Original Ship with KG] --> B[Partially Filled Tank
at Height h] B --> C[Tank Fluid Shifts
on Heel] C --> D[Virtual Rise of G
GG prime = iT × rho fluid / rho sw × Volume] D --> E[Effective KG_new = KG + GG prime] E --> F[Effective GM = KM minus KG_new
Smaller than original GM] F --> G[Reduced Stability
More Tender Ship] ``` ## 4.4 Component Relationship Map (KTU-Approved) ```mermaid flowchart TD K[K - Keel Reference] --> KB[KB] K --> KG[KG] K --> KM[KM] KB --> B[B - Center of Buoyancy] KG --> G[G - Center of Gravity] KM --> M[M - Metacenter] B --> BM[BM = KM minus KB] G --> GM[GM = KM minus KG] BM --> KM KG --> GM KM --> GM GM --> STAB{Stability Check} STAB -->|GM Greater Than 0| S1[Positive Stability] STAB -->|GM Equals 0| S2[Neutral Equilibrium] STAB -->|GM Less Than 0| S3[Negative Stability Capsize] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Visualization Tip:** Always draw the **"stability triangle"** (B, G, M) at the start of any 14-mark problem. It earns you **2 free marks** out of 14 just for being methodical. The examiner sees the framework and knows you know the geometry. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define the following terms with neat sketches:** (a) Center of Buoyancy (b) Center of Gravity (c) Metacenter ### **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Center of Buoyancy (B):** It is the point through which the **total buoyancy force** (equal to the weight of displaced water) is considered to act. Mathematically, it is the **centroid of the underwater (submerged) volume** of the ship. **Center of Gravity (G):** It is the point through which the **total weight** of the ship (including hull, machinery, cargo, and consumables) is considered to act. It is the **centroid of the ship's mass distribution**. **Metacenter (M):** When a ship is heeled by a small angle $\theta$, the Center of Buoyancy shifts from B to B'. The point of intersection of the **vertical line through B'** with the **original vertical centerline** of the ship is called the **Metacenter (M)**. **Sketch (1 Mark):** ``` M | G | B | K ───────── ``` --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **State and explain the relation $BM = I / \nabla$ used in ship stability. What is the significance of this equation?** ### **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **metacentric radius** (transverse) is given by: $$ BM_{T} = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} $$ **Where:** - $I_{T}$ = **transverse second moment of area** of the waterplane about the centerline (m⁴) - $\nabla$ = **displaced volume** of the ship (m³) - $BM_{T}$ = transverse metacentric radius (m) **Significance (2 Marks):** 1. It links the **geometric property** of the waterplane ($I_{T}$) with the **stability** of the ship. 2. A **wider waterplane** (larger $I_{T}$) gives a **larger BM** and hence **greater initial stability**. 3. A **deeper draft** (larger $\nabla$) gives a **smaller BM** and hence **lower initial stability**. 4. It is the **foundation** for the famous GZ curve and the entire IMO damage-stability regulatory framework. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` A ship of **displacement 12000 tonnes** and **KG = 5.5 m** is floating at a draft of **6.0 m**. The waterplane area is **rectangular** with length **120 m** and breadth **20 m**. The underwater volume is also rectangular. Seawater density = **1.025 t/m³**. **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **metacentric height (GM)** of the ship. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A tank of length **20 m** and breadth **8 m** is now partially filled with **oil of relative density 0.85**, having a free surface at mid-height. Calculate the **new effective metacentric height** and comment on the **stability**. ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### **Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Displaced volume.** $$ \nabla = \frac{W}{\rho} = \frac{12000}{1.025} = 11707.3 \text{ m}^{3} $$ **Step 2: Waterplane second moment of area.** $$ I_{T} = \frac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} = \frac{120 \cdot 20^{3}}{12} = \frac{120 \cdot 8000}{12} = 80000 \text{ m}^{4} $$ **Step 3: Transverse metacentric radius.** $$ BM = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} = \frac{80000}{11707.3} = 6.832 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: Height of B (rectangular underwater volume).** $$ KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{6.0}{2} = 3.0 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5: Height of M.** $$ KM = KB + BM = 3.0 + 6.832 = 9.832 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6: Metacentric height.** $$ \boxed{GM = KM - KG = 9.832 - 5.5 = 4.332 \text{ m}} $$ > The ship is **stiff** (GM > 0.15 m, well above the IMO minimum). #### **Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Second moment of the free surface area.** For a rectangular slack tank: $$ i_{T} = \frac{l \cdot b^{3}}{12} = \frac{20 \cdot 8^{3}}{12} = \frac{20 \cdot 512}{12} = 853.33 \text{ m}^{4} $$ **Step 2: Virtual rise of G (free surface correction).** $$ GG' = \frac{i_{T} \cdot \rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{seawater}} \cdot \nabla} = \frac{853.33 \cdot 0.85}{1.025 \cdot 11707.3} $$ $$ GG' = \frac{725.33}{12000} = 0.0604 \text{ m} $$ (Or, more simply, using $W = 12000$ t in denominator: $GG' = i_T \cdot \rho_f / W = 725.33/12000 = 0.0604$ m.) **Step 3: New effective KG.** $$ KG_{\text{new}} = KG + GG' = 5.5 + 0.0604 = 5.5604 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: New effective GM.** $$ \boxed{GM_{\text{eff}} = KM - KG_{\text{new}} = 9.832 - 5.5604 = 4.272 \text{ m}} $$ **Step 5: Stability Comment.** The free surface effect **reduces GM by 0.060 m** (about 1.4%). The ship remains **positively stable** but the margin is reduced. In a real ship, multiple slack tanks can accumulate, reducing GM by **several decimeters** — this is why **slack tanks are forbidden by the IMO Code on Intact Stability (2008 IS Code) for vessels operating in severe weather.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice* A box-shaped barge has **L = 60 m**, **B = 10 m**, and floats at a draft of **T = 3 m**. The center of gravity is at **KG = 2.0 m** above the keel. The density of seawater is **1.025 t/m³**. **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **metacentric height (GM)** and the **righting lever at 8° heel**. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A **point weight of 50 tonnes** is added on the deck at a height of **4 m** above the keel, **off-center** at a distance of **3 m** from the centerline. Find the **new KG** and the **angle of list** (in degrees). ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### **Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Displaced volume.** $$ \nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T = 60 \cdot 10 \cdot 3 = 1800 \text{ m}^{3} $$ **Step 2: Displacement weight.** $$ W = \nabla \cdot \rho = 1800 \cdot 1.025 = 1845 \text{ t} $$ **Step 3: KB (centroid of rectangular volume).** $$ KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: Transverse second moment of area.** $$ I_{T} = \frac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} = \frac{60 \cdot 10^{3}}{12} = 5000 \text{ m}^{4} $$ **Step 5: BM.** $$ BM = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} = \frac{5000}{1800} = 2.778 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6: KM and GM.** $$ KM = KB + BM = 1.5 + 2.778 = 4.278 \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{GM = KM - KG = 4.278 - 2.0 = 2.278 \text{ m}} $$ **Step 7: Righting Lever at $\theta = 8°$.** $$ GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta = 2.278 \cdot \sin(8°) = 2.278 \cdot 0.1392 = 0.317 \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{GZ = 0.317 \text{ m}} $$ #### **Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Vertical shift in G (parallel-axis).** Using the **parallel-axis theorem** for moment of weight: $$ KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{W \cdot KG + w \cdot z}{W + w} $$ Where $w = 50$ t (the added weight) and $z = 4$ m: $$ KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{1845 \cdot 2.0 + 50 \cdot 4}{1845 + 50} = \frac{3690 + 200}{1895} = \frac{3890}{1895} = 2.053 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2: Transverse shift in G (list-causing moment).** $$ GG'_{\text{trans}} = \frac{w \cdot y}{W + w} = \frac{50 \cdot 3}{1895} = \frac{150}{1895} = 0.0792 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3: New GM (assuming KM unchanged — small angle).** $$ KM \text{ is unchanged} = 4.278 \text{ m} $$ $$ GM_{\text{new}} = KM - KG_{\text{new}} = 4.278 - 2.053 = 2.225 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: Angle of list (tan formulation).** For small angles: $$ \tan\theta = \frac{GG'_{\text{trans}}}{GM_{\text{new}}} = \frac{0.0792}{2.225} = 0.0356 $$ $$ \theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0356) = 2.04° $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_{\text{list}} \approx 2.04° \text{ to the side of the added weight}} $$ > The ship will **list** to the side of the added weight by approximately **2 degrees** — a small list, indicating the ship is still in a healthy stability condition. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting units in $I_{T}$:** Many students write $I_{T} = LB^{3}/12$ but substitute $B$ in cm and $L$ in m, leading to a **factor of $10^{6}$** error. Always convert to **meters** first. > 2. **Confusing $I_{T}$ and $I_{L}$:** $I_{T}$ uses $B^{3}$ (controls rolling). $I_{L}$ uses $L^{3}$ (controls pitching/trim). A common 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Using displacement in tonnes-force instead of volume in m³** for $\nabla$: the formula $BM = I/\nabla$ requires **volume, not mass**. Divide $W$ by $\rho$ first. > 4. **Sign error in free surface correction:** It is **always subtractive** on GM, not additive. $GG'$ **raises** G, so it is **added** to KG (effectively **subtracting** from GM). > 5. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** for $GZ$ calculations using small-angle approximation $GZ = GM \cdot \theta$: $\theta$ must be in radians for the linear approximation to hold. > 6. **Not drawing the "stability triangle"**: This single sketch in your answer sheet earns you **2 free marks** out of 14 and gives the examiner confidence that you understand the geometry. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) ### 🔑 The Five Master Formulas - **$BM_{T} = I_{T} / \nabla$** — the heart of initial transverse stability - **$BM_{L} = I_{L} / \nabla$** — for trim/longitudinal stability - **$KM = KB + BM$** — the master equation - **$GM = KM - KG$** — the stability indicator - **$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$** — the righting lever (small angles) ### 📌 Key Definitions (must memorize word-for-word) - **B** = Centroid of displaced water volume - **G** = Centroid of ship mass (including all weights) - **M** = Intersection of new vertical axis (through B') with original centerline - **KB, KG, KM, BM, GM** = All measured from the keel (K) along the vertical centerline ### 📐 Geometry Rules - $I_{T}$ for rectangle = $\dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12}$ (rolling) - $I_{L}$ for rectangle = $\dfrac{L^{3} \cdot B}{12}$ (pitching) - For a rectangular hull, **KB = T/2** ### ⚠️ Critical Stability Criteria - **GM > 0** → Stable (stiff ship) - **GM = 0** → Neutral equilibrium (critical) - **GM < 0** → Capsize imminent - **IMO minimum GM** for passenger ships ≈ **0.15 m** - **Free surface effect** ALWAYS **reduces** effective GM ### 🧮 Numerical Tools - **Simpson's 1/3 Rule weights:** 1, 4, 2, 4, 2, …, 4, 1 - For irregular hulls: use Bonjean data, integrate $A(z) \cdot z \, dz$ for KB - For trim: $\text{Trim (cm)} = \dfrac{M}{\text{MCT}}$, $\text{MCT} = \dfrac{W \cdot GM_{L}}{100L}$ ### 🎯 KTU Board Exam Pattern (Module 4 - Location) - **Part A (3 marks):** Usually asks for **definition of B, G, M** OR **state the formula for BM** - **Part B (14 marks):** A full **GM calculation problem** with (a) basic GM and (b) modification (free surface, added weight, suspended weight, etc.) - **Internal choice:** Always provided — be prepared for **either a rectangular hull** or a **Bonjean-data hull** problem ### 🔗 Real-World Hook The "Location" of B, G, M is the foundation of: - **Loading computer algorithms** in every modern ship - **Damage stability** under IMO SOLAS Chapter II-1 - **Dynamic stability** in beam seas (Module 5) - **Parametric roll** analysis - **Submarine** stability (with the added twist of a compressible G) > **Final Mantra for the KTU Board Exam:** *"Without a properly located G, B, and M, a ship is just a floating box. With them, she becomes a stable, maneuverable, seaworthy vessel."*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — SIZE: PRINCIPAL DIMENSIONS, FORM COEFFICIENTS & SCALING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Size" in Ship Dynamics?

In the context of **Naval Architecture & Ship Dynamics**, the term **"Size"** is a composite descriptor that quantifies a vessel through three intertwined layers:

1. **Geometric Size** — the raw physical dimensions (Length, Beam, Depth, Draft).
2. **Volumetric / Displacement Size** — the volume of water displaced (and hence the mass of the ship) by the hull's underwater form.
3. **Form-Based Size** — dimensionless ratios (form coefficients) that describe how "fat" or "slender" the hull is, capturing the *shape* aspect of size in addition to mere magnitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardised):**
> *"Size of a ship is characterised by her principal geometric dimensions, displacement volume, wetted surface area, and the set of dimensionless form coefficients that uniquely describe the proportions and fullness of the underwater hull."*

Mathematically, the **displacement volume** $\nabla$ (in m³) is the integrated volume of the submerged portion of the hull:

$$\nabla = \int\!\!\int\!\!\int_{V_{sub}} dV \;\;[\text{m}^3]$$

and the corresponding **displacement mass** is:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla \cdot g \;\;[\text{kN or tonnes}]$$

where $\rho$ is the density of water (typically **1025 kg/m³** for seawater and **1000 kg/m³** for freshwater) and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Family of Water Bottles"**
>
> Imagine three water bottles on a desk:
>
> * A **tall, slim 500 mL mineral-water bottle** → long and skinny.
> * A **short, fat 500 mL juice can** → same volume, totally different proportions.
> * A **bigger 1-litre juice can** → larger size, but similar shape to the 500 mL can.
>
> All three "hold" something, but they are **different in size and different in shape**. In ship dynamics, **"Size" means BOTH how big the ship is AND how its volume is distributed along its length**. Two ships of identical length can have wildly different displacement volumes, just as the 500 mL bottle and 500 mL can share a capacity but differ entirely in proportions.
>
> Naval Architects therefore characterise a ship's size using a **family of numbers**: principal dimensions (L, B, T) PLUS form coefficients (Cb, Cp, Cm, Cwp). Together they form a "DNA fingerprint" of the hull.

---

### 1.3 Standard Symbols & Reference Values (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (Merchant) |
|---|---|---|
| $L_{PP}$ | Length between Perpendiculars | 100 – 350 m |
| $L_{OA}$ | Length Overall | $L_{PP} + L_{bow} + L_{stern}$ |
| $B$ | Breadth (Moulded) | 0.10 L – 0.18 L |
| $D$ | Depth (Moulded) | varies |
| $T$ | Draft (Design) | 0.04 L – 0.07 L |
| $\nabla$ | Displacement Volume (moulded) | 10,000 – 500,000 m³ |
| $C_B$ | Block Coefficient | 0.55 (fine) – 0.85 (full) |
| $C_P$ | Prismatic Coefficient | 0.60 – 0.85 |
| $C_M$ | Midship Section Coefficient | 0.90 – 0.99 |
| $C_{WP}$ | Waterplane Area Coefficient | 0.65 – 0.90 |
| $S$ | Wetted Surface Area | — |
| $\Delta$ | Displacement (mass/weight) | — |

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Geometric Dimensions — Step-by-Step Logic

A ship is a **3-D body**. To describe it numerically, naval architects extract **six principal dimensions** (often called the "six degrees of size"):

### 2.1.1 Length Measures
* **Length Overall (LOA or $L_{OA}$):** The maximum length measured parallel to the design waterline, including any projections such as bulbous bow or stern overhang.
* **Length Between Perpendiculars ($L_{PP}$):** The distance between the **forward perpendicular (FP)** — usually where the design waterline meets the stem — and the **aft perpendicular (AP)** — usually the rudder stock centreline (or where the design waterline meets the rudder post on older vessels).
* **Length on Waterline ($L_{WL}$):** The length of the ship at the design waterline; usually $L_{WL} \approx L_{PP} + L_{bow}/2 + L_{stern}/2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** For resistance and powering calculations, **always use $L_{PP}$** unless otherwise stated. For ship-motion and sea-keeping work, $L_{WL}$ is often preferred.

### 2.1.2 Transverse & Vertical Dimensions
* **Breadth $B$ (Moulded):** Maximum inside-shell width measured at the **midship section**, amidships. Excludes shell plating thickness.
* **Breadth Extreme $B_{ext}$:** Overall outside-shell width — slightly larger than moulded breadth.
* **Depth $D$ (Moulded):** Vertical distance from the **moulded base line (keel)** to the **moulded deck line** at the side, measured at midship.
* **Draft $T$ (or $d$):** Vertical distance from the **design waterline** to the **keel** (or sometimes the bottom of the keel plate). It has two components:
  * $T_F$ — Draft at the Forward Perpendicular.
  * $T_A$ — Draft at the Aft Perpendicular.
* **Freeboard $F_B = D - T$:** The visible vertical side of the hull above water — a critical safety metric.

---

## 2.2 Displacement & Volume Concepts

### 2.2.1 Displacement Volume $\nabla$

The **displacement volume** is the submerged volume of the hull up to the waterplane:

$$\nabla = L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B \;\;[\text{m}^3]$$

This is the **most used equation in naval architecture** for quick size estimation.

### 2.2.2 Displacement Weight $\Delta$

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla \;\;[\text{N}]$$

For shipbuilding practice, **metric tonnes** are used:

$$\Delta \text{(tonnes)} = \frac{\nabla \cdot \rho}{1000} \;\; \text{(with } \rho \text{ in kg/m}^3\text{)}$$

For **seawater** ($\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ kg/m³):

$$\Delta \approx 1.025 \cdot \nabla \;\;[\text{tonnes}]$$

### 2.2.3 Deadweight & Lightweight — Quick Distinction

* **Lightweight ($LW$ or $LWT$):** Mass of the empty ship (steel + outfit + machinery). "What the ship weighs with nobody aboard."
* **Deadweight ($DWT$):** Mass of cargo + fuel + freshwater + stores + crew. "What the ship carries."
* **Displacement (loaded):** $\Delta = LW + DWT$.

---

## 2.3 Form Coefficients — The "DNA" of Hull Shape

Form coefficients are **dimensionless ratios** that compress the hull's shape into a few numbers. They are the **single most important set of parameters** for KTU exam questions on "Size".

### 2.3.1 Block Coefficient $C_B$

The **block coefficient** is the ratio of the displaced volume to the volume of a rectangular box having the same $L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** $C_B = 1$ means a perfect rectangular barge (maximally full). $C_B \to 0$ means a needle-like hull (minimally full). Most merchant ships lie in $0.55 < C_B < 0.85$.

### 2.3.2 Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$

The ratio of the displaced volume to the volume of a **prism with the same length and the midship-section area**:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}$$

where $A_M$ is the **midship-section submerged area**.

* $C_P$ reflects the **longitudinal distribution** of volume — how quickly the hull tapers.
* Fine, slender hulls (warships) have **lower $C_P$** (~0.60).
* Full hulls (tankers) have **higher $C_P$** (~0.80–0.85).

### 2.3.3 Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

* Measures the **fullness of the cross-section** amidships.
* Typically very high: $C_M \approx 0.90$ – $0.99$.
* $C_M = 1$ means a perfectly rectangular midship section.

### 2.3.4 The Fundamental Identity (⭐ KTU Favourite)

The three coefficients **multiply to give back the block coefficient**:

$$\boxed{\,C_B = C_P \times C_M\,}$$

This is a **must-know relationship** for KTU exams — it appears in nearly every past paper.

### 2.3.5 Waterplane Area Coefficient $C_{WP}$

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L_{WL} \cdot B}$$

where $A_{WP}$ is the waterplane area.
* $C_{WP}$ affects **initial stability** (GMT) and **added resistance in waves**.

### 2.3.6 Longitudinal & Vertical Prismatic Coefficients

$$C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot A_{WP}} \quad,\quad C_{LP} = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot A_M}$$

Used less frequently but useful for detailed hull-form analysis.

---

## 2.4 Wetted Surface Area $S$

The **wetted surface area** is the total area of the hull in contact with water at the design draft. Empirical formulas (used universally in KTU-level problems):

### 2.4.1 Series-60 / Inui / Taylor Style Formulas

For a typical single-screw merchant hull:

$$S = C \cdot \sqrt{\nabla \cdot L_{PP}}$$

where $C \approx 2.6$ to $2.8$ (Tscherny formula gives $C \approx 2.68$).

A more refined form:

$$S = 1.7 \cdot L_{PP} \cdot T + \nabla / T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why wetted surface matters:** Frictional resistance scales with wetted area: $R_F \propto S$. So *size* indirectly determines resistance through $S$.

---

## 2.5 Size Effect & Reynolds-Number Scaling (Module 4 Linkage)

For model–ship correlation (often a KTU Module 4 question), similarity in friction is enforced by equal **Reynolds number**:

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$

where $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity ($\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s for freshwater, $\approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s for seawater at 20°C).

* A **larger ship** at the same speed has a **much higher $R_n$** → thinner boundary layer → lower frictional coefficient $C_F$ → relatively less frictional resistance per unit displacement.

The **ITTC 1978 friction line** (the KTU-mandated formula for frictional resistance):

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

This shows how size (via $L$) **reduces the unit frictional resistance** of bigger ships.

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The vertical bar `|` inside math expressions is written as `\vert` to protect the markdown table below.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla = L_{PP}\cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ | Displacement volume | m³ |
| 2 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement weight | N (or tonnes) |
| 3 | $C_B = \nabla/(L_{PP}\cdot B \cdot T)$ | Block / fullness coefficient | — |
| 4 | $C_P = \nabla/(A_M \cdot L_{PP})$ | Prismatic coefficient | — |
| 5 | $C_M = A_M/(B\cdot T)$ | Midship coefficient | — |
| 6 | $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ | **Identity** | — |
| 7 | $C_{WP} = A_{WP}/(L_{WL}\cdot B)$ | Waterplane coefficient | — |
| 8 | $S \approx 2.68\cdot\sqrt{\nabla \cdot L_{PP}}$ | Wetted surface area | m² |
| 9 | $R_n = V\cdot L_{PP}/\nu$ | Reynolds number (size effect) | — |
| 10 | $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10}R_n - 2)^2$ | ITTC-78 frictional coefficient | — |
| 11 | $F_n = V/\sqrt{g \cdot L_{PP}}$ | Froude number (wave similarity) | — |
| 12 | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_F$ | Frictional resistance | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The form coefficients $C_B, C_P, C_M, C_{WP}$ are used by:
> 1. **Shipyards** to choose the right hull form for the desired service (tanker vs. container vs. warship).
> 2. **Classification Societies** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) for design approval.
> 3. **Naval Architects** to estimate resistance and powering without expensive CFD.
> 4. **Model basins** to scale model tests to full scale.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Identity $C_B = C_P \times C_M$

This is **the most-tested derivation in KTU Module 4** on Size. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Start with the definitions of each coefficient

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP}\cdot B\cdot T}$$

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}$$

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B\cdot T}$$

### Step 2: Multiply $C_P$ and $C_M$ symbolically

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P \cdot C_M
&= \left[\frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{A_M}{B\cdot T}\right] 
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Cancel $A_M$ in numerator and denominator

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P \cdot C_M 
&= \frac{\nabla \cdot \cancel{A_M}}{L_{PP}\cdot \cancel{A_M}\cdot B\cdot T} 
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to compare with $C_B$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P \cdot C_M 
&= \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP}\cdot B\cdot T} = C_B
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Result:

$$
\boxed{\;C_B \;=\; C_P \cdot C_M\;}
$$

> **Physical Meaning:** The overall fullness of the hull ($C_B$) is the product of *how the volume is distributed along the length* ($C_P$) and *how full the cross-section is amidships* ($C_M$). Two ships can have the same $C_B$ but very different $C_P$–$C_M$ splits.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Displacement Volume from Block Coefficient

### Step 1: Express the "bounding box" volume

The box of dimensions $L_{PP} \times B \times T$ has volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{box} = L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \;\; [\text{m}^3]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the block coefficient

By definition, $C_B$ is the **fraction** of this box that is actually occupied by the submerged hull:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot V_{box} = C_B \cdot L_{PP}\cdot B \cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to displacement weight in seawater

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{sw} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \nabla \;\;[\text{N}] \\
\Delta_{sw}^{tonnes} &= \frac{1025}{1000}\cdot\nabla = 1.025\,\nabla
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Result:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta \;=\; 1.025 \cdot C_B \cdot L_{PP}\cdot B \cdot T \;\;[\text{tonnes}]\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wetted Surface Area (Tscherny / Inui Approximation)

### Step 1: Empirical assumption

For a "normal" merchant hull, wetted area scales with the square root of the product $\nabla\cdot L_{PP}$.

### Step 2: Empirical constant $C$ fitted to many ship forms

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= C \cdot \sqrt{\nabla \cdot L_{PP}}, \quad C \approx 2.68
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Dimensional check

$[\nabla] = \text{m}^3$, $[L_{PP}] = \text{m}$, so $[\nabla \cdot L_{PP}] = \text{m}^4$, $[\sqrt{\cdots}] = \text{m}^2$. ✅ Units match.

### Step 4: Alternative form (Harvald's approximation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = 1.7\,L_{PP}\,T + \frac{\nabla}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dimensional check: $[1.7\,L_{PP}\,T] = \text{m}^2$, $[\nabla/T] = \text{m}^3/\text{m} = \text{m}^2$. ✅

> **Use Tscherny's formula when you need a quick estimate; use Harvald's when you have $T$ already.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reynolds Number and Frictional Coefficient

### Step 1: Define Reynolds number

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Plug in the ITTC-78 form for $C_F$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}R_n - 2)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Frictional resistance

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_F &= \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_F
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU-style)

A 200 m long ship at $V = 15$ knots in seawater ($\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s):

* $V = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s
* $R_n = (7.716 \times 200)/(1.19\times 10^{-6}) = 1.297 \times 10^{9}$
* $\log_{10} R_n = 9.113$
* $C_F = 0.075/(9.113 - 2)^2 = 0.075/50.55 = 1.484 \times 10^{-3}$

$$
\boxed{C_F \approx 1.48 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

A 1:30 model of the same ship at $V_m = 7.716/\sqrt{30} = 1.409$ m/s (Froude-similar):

* $R_{n,m} = (1.409 \times 6.667)/(1.0\times 10^{-6}) = 9.39\times 10^{6}$
* $C_{F,m} = 0.075/(\log_{10}9.39\times 10^6 - 2)^2 = 0.075/(6.973-2)^2 = 0.075/24.74 = 3.03\times 10^{-3}$

The **model has roughly 2× the unit frictional resistance of the ship** — illustrating the *size effect*.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Quick Reference

| Hull Type | $C_B$ | $C_P$ | $C_M$ | $L/B$ | $B/T$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tanker (full) | 0.80–0.85 | 0.80–0.85 | 0.98–0.99 | 5–7 | 2.5–3.5 |
| Bulk Carrier | 0.72–0.82 | 0.78–0.85 | 0.95–0.99 | 6–8 | 2.2–3.0 |
| Container Ship | 0.55–0.70 | 0.62–0.78 | 0.90–0.98 | 6–9 | 2.5–4.0 |
| Warship / Frigate | 0.45–0.55 | 0.55–0.70 | 0.75–0.90 | 8–12 | 2.5–4.0 |
| Fishing Vessel | 0.50–0.65 | 0.60–0.75 | 0.80–0.95 | 5–7 | 2.0–3.0 |
| Submarine | 0.40–0.50 | 0.70–0.85 | 0.60–0.75 | 7–10 | 4.0–7.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the table:** Higher $C_B$ = "boxier" = fuller ship = more cargo capacity but higher resistance. Lower $C_B$ = "sleeker" = faster but less carrying capacity. The **trade-off** between capacity and performance is fundamentally a *size-and-shape* problem.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of "Size" in a Ship

flowchart TD

    A --> C[Volumetric Size]
    A --> D[Form-Based Size]

    B --> B1[Length L_PP, L_OA, L_WL]
    B --> B2[Breadth B]
    B --> B3[Depth D, Draft T, Freeboard FB]

    C --> C1[Displacement Volume Nabla]
    C --> C2[Displacement Mass Delta]

    D --> D1[Block Coefficient C_B]

    D --> D4[Waterplane Coefficient C_WP]

    D1 --> E[Identity C_B = C_P times C_M]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    C1 --> F[Resistance R_F proportional to S times C_F]

    F --> G[Powering P = R times V]
```

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the following terms, stating their physical meaning and typical range for merchant ships: (i) Block coefficient $C_B$, (ii) Prismatic coefficient $C_P$, (iii) Midship section coefficient $C_M$. **[3 Marks]**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

* **(i) Block Coefficient $C_B$:** The ratio of the displaced volume $\nabla$ of a ship to the volume of a rectangular box of length $L_{PP}$, breadth $B$ and draft $T$, i.e. $C_B = \nabla/(L_{PP}\cdot B\cdot T)$. It measures the *overall fullness* of the underwater hull. Typical range for merchant ships: **0.55 to 0.85**.

* **(ii) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$:** The ratio of the displaced volume $\nabla$ to the volume of a prism having the same length $L_{PP}$ and a constant cross-section equal to the midship section area $A_M$, i.e. $C_P = \nabla/(A_M\cdot L_{PP})$. It measures the *longitudinal taper* of the hull. Typical range: **0.60 to 0.85**.

* **(iii) Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$:** The ratio of the midship-section submerged area $A_M$ to the area of the bounding rectangle $B\cdot T$, i.e. $C_M = A_M/(B\cdot T)$. It measures the *fullness of the cross-section* amidships. Typical range: **0.90 to 0.99**.

> **[Mark Distribution: 1 mark each definition]**

---

### Q2. State the relationship between $C_B$, $C_P$ and $C_M$. Explain its significance in ship design. **[3 Marks]**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Retest]`

**Model Answer:**

The relationship is:

$$C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$$

**Significance:**

1. It shows that the *overall fullness* ($C_B$) is the product of two independent design choices: the *longitudinal distribution* ($C_P$) and the *transverse fullness* ($C_M$).
2. Two ships with identical $C_B$ can have very different $C_P$–$C_M$ combinations — e.g., a fine warship ($C_P$ low, $C_M$ high) vs. a stubby tanker ($C_P$ high, $C_M$ very high).
3. Designers can tune one coefficient without necessarily changing the other, giving freedom in hull-form optimisation.

> **[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for relation, 2 marks for significance]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Convention:** Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** (full 14 marks).

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — Hull Size Computation from Coefficients

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module-4 Internal Choice Q1]`

A **bulk carrier** has the following particulars:

* Length between perpendiculars, $L_{PP} = 180$ m
* Breadth (moulded), $B = 30$ m
* Design draft, $T = 11.5$ m
* Block coefficient, $C_B = 0.78$
* Prismatic coefficient, $C_P = 0.82$
* Wetted surface coefficient (Tscherny), $C_s = 2.68$

The ship is to operate in **seawater** ($\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³).

#### (a) Compute the **displacement volume $\nabla$**, **displacement mass $\Delta$** (in tonnes), and the **midship section coefficient $C_M$**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Displacement Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= L_{PP}\cdot B\cdot T\cdot C_B \\
       &= 180 \cdot 30 \cdot 11.5 \cdot 0.78 \\
       &= 62\,100 \cdot 0.78 \\
       &= 48\,438 \;\;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Displacement Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho_{sw}\cdot \nabla / 1000 \\
       &= 1025 \cdot 48\,438 / 1000 \\
       &= 49\,648.95 \;\;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Or equivalently $\Delta = 1.025 \times 48\,438 = 49\,648.95$ tonnes)

**Step 3 — Midship Coefficient (using identity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= \frac{C_B}{C_P} = \frac{0.78}{0.82} = 0.9512
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Results:** $\nabla \approx 48\,438$ m³, $\Delta \approx 49\,649$ tonnes, $C_M \approx 0.951$.

#### (b) Compute the **wetted surface area $S$** using Tscherny's formula. The ship sails at **$V = 14$ knots** in seawater with $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Determine the **Reynolds number $R_n$** and the **ITTC-1978 frictional resistance coefficient $C_F$**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wetted Surface Area (Tscherny):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= C_s\cdot\sqrt{\nabla \cdot L_{PP}} \\
  &= 2.68 \cdot \sqrt{48\,438 \cdot 180} \\
  &= 2.68 \cdot \sqrt{8\,718\,840} \\
  &= 2.68 \cdot 2952.77 \\
  &= 7\,913.4 \;\;\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016 \;\;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{V\cdot L_{PP}}{\nu} \\
    &= \frac{7.2016 \cdot 180}{1.19\times 10^{-6}} \\
    &= \frac{1296.288}{1.19\times 10^{-6}} \\
    &= 1.0894 \times 10^9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — ITTC-1978 Frictional Coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_n) &= \log_{10}(1.0894 \times 10^9) = 9.037 \\
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{(9.037 - 2)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{(7.037)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{49.52} \\
    &= 1.515 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Results:** $S \approx 7\,913$ m², $R_n \approx 1.089 \times 10^9$, $C_F \approx 1.515 \times 10^{-3}$.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Ship Size and Froude / Reynolds Scaling

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module-4 Internal Choice Q2]`

A **geometrical scale ratio** of $\lambda = L_{ship}/L_{model} = 25$ is used for towing-tank testing of a frigate. The ship particulars are:

* $L_{PP} = 125$ m, $B = 14.5$ m, $T = 4.5$ m, $C_B = 0.52$, $C_P = 0.65$
* Design speed: $V_s = 28$ knots

Test conditions: freshwater with $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Use **Froude similarity** for the wave system and compute the corresponding model speed.

#### (a) Compute the **ship's displacement volume $\nabla_s$**, **displacement mass $\Delta_s$ in tonnes (seawater)**, and the **midship coefficient $C_M$**. Determine the **model length $L_m$**, **model speed $V_m$**, and **model Reynolds number $R_{n,m}$**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ship displacement volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_s &= L_{PP}\cdot B\cdot T\cdot C_B \\
        &= 125\cdot 14.5\cdot 4.5\cdot 0.52 \\
        &= 8\,156.25\cdot 0.52 = 4\,241.25 \;\;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Ship displacement mass (seawater):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_s &= 1.025 \times 4\,241.25 = 4\,347.28 \;\;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Midship coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= \frac{C_B}{C_P} = \frac{0.52}{0.65} = 0.800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Model length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_m &= \frac{L_{PP}}{\lambda} = \frac{125}{25} = 5.0 \;\;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Ship speed in m/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= 28\times 0.5144 = 14.4032 \;\;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Model speed (Froude similarity $F_n$ equal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{n,model} &= F_{n,ship} \\
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} &= \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_{PP}}} \\
V_m &= V_s\cdot\sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_{PP}}} = V_s / \sqrt{\lambda} \\
V_m &= \frac{14.4032}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{14.4032}{5} = 2.8806 \;\;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Model Reynolds number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n,m} &= \frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{2.8806\cdot 5.0}{1.0\times 10^{-6}} = 1.4403 \times 10^7
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Results:** $\nabla_s \approx 4\,241$ m³, $\Delta_s \approx 4\,347$ tonnes, $C_M \approx 0.800$, $L_m = 5.0$ m, $V_m \approx 2.88$ m/s, $R_{n,m} \approx 1.44\times 10^7$.

#### (b) Compute the **ship's Reynolds number $R_{n,s}$** (seawater, $\nu_s = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s), the **ITTC-78 $C_F$ for both ship and model**, and the **% difference in frictional coefficient** between model and ship. Comment on the *size effect*. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ship Reynolds number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n,s} &= \frac{V_s \cdot L_{PP}}{\nu_s} = \frac{14.4032 \cdot 125}{1.19\times 10^{-6}} \\
       &= \frac{1800.4}{1.19\times 10^{-6}} = 1.513 \times 10^9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — $C_F$ for ship:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_{n,s}) &= \log_{10}(1.513\times 10^9) = 9.180 \\
C_{F,s} &= \frac{0.075}{(9.180 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{51.55} = 1.455\times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — $C_F$ for model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_{n,m}) &= \log_{10}(1.4403\times 10^7) = 7.158 \\
C_{F,m} &= \frac{0.075}{(7.158 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{26.62} = 2.818\times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage difference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\Delta C_F &= \frac{C_{F,m} - C_{F,s}}{C_{F,s}} \times 100 \\
               &= \frac{2.818 - 1.455}{1.455}\times 100 \\
               &= 93.7\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on size effect:**

The model has a $C_F$ about **94 % larger** than the full-scale ship. This **size effect** arises because Reynolds number scales linearly with length — a 25× shorter model at Froude-similar speed has 25× lower $R_n$, falling in a regime where the boundary layer is comparatively thicker and turbulence less developed. The frictional resistance per unit area is therefore higher at model scale, and the **ITTC-78 correlation (or its 1957 predecessor)** is the standard tool to correct this discrepancy when extrapolating model-test results to full scale.

> ✅ **Results:** $R_{n,s} \approx 1.513\times 10^9$, $C_{F,s} \approx 1.455\times 10^{-3}$, $C_{F,m} \approx 2.818\times 10^{-3}$, $\%\Delta C_F \approx 94\,\%$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in "Size" problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit mix-up for $\Delta$:** Always state the unit explicitly. $1.025 \cdot \nabla$ gives *tonnes*; $\rho g \nabla$ gives *newtons*. Many students write "$49\,648$ kN" when they actually computed tonnes — full 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1.025$ factor** for seawater (using $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) — silently produces an answer that is ~2.5 % low. Examiners will notice.
> 3. **Confusing $L_{PP}$ with $L_{OA}$** in formula application. Most resistance/size formulas use $L_{PP}$. Substituting $L_{OA}$ loses the full 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Mixing $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ direction:** It is *not* $C_P = C_B \cdot C_M$ or $C_M = C_B/C_P$ in general — the **identity** is $C_B = C_P\cdot C_M$. Get the direction wrong, identity is unusable.
> 5. **Speed conversion slip:** $V$ in knots must be multiplied by 0.5144 to convert to m/s. A 1-knot slip (using 1.0) leads to wrong $R_n$ by ~7 %.
> 6. **Skipping units in $\log_{10} R_n$:** The argument of $\log_{10}$ in the ITTC formula is dimensionless — $R_n$ is dimensionless by definition, but writing $R_n = 1.089 \times 10^9$ (no unit) is mandatory for clarity.
> 7. **Tscherny's constant:** Using $C_s = 2.6$ instead of 2.68 changes $S$ by ~3 %. Examiners typically accept 2.6–2.8, but **state the constant you used**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* **Principal dimensions:** $L_{PP}$, $L_{OA}$, $L_{WL}$, $B$, $D$, $T$, $F_B = D - T$.
* **Block coefficient:** $C_B = \nabla/(L_{PP} B T)$ — fullness of the underwater hull. **Range 0.55 – 0.85.**
* **Prismatic coefficient:** $C_P = \nabla/(A_M L_{PP})$ — longitudinal taper. **Range 0.60 – 0.85.**
* **Midship coefficient:** $C_M = A_M/(B T)$ — transverse fullness. **Range 0.90 – 0.99.**
* **⭐ Identity (must remember):** $\;C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$.
* **Waterplane coefficient:** $C_{WP} = A_{WP}/(L_{WL} B)$ — affects stability and wave resistance.
* **Displacement:** $\nabla$ in m³; $\Delta = 1.025 \nabla$ tonnes in seawater.
* **Wetted surface area:** Tscherny $S \approx 2.68\sqrt{\nabla L_{PP}}$; Harvald $S = 1.7 L_{PP} T + \nabla/T$.
* **Size effect on resistance:** Larger $L$ → higher $R_n$ → lower $C_F$ (ITTC-78). Same speed, bigger ship → less frictional resistance per unit area.
* **Froude similarity (waves):** $V_m = V_s/\sqrt{\lambda}$.
* **Reynolds similarity (friction):** Hard to satisfy simultaneously with Froude → use ITTC-78 correlation for extrapolation.
* **Speed conversion:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 m/s = 1.944 knots.
* **Water densities:** Freshwater 1000 kg/m³, Seawater 1025 kg/m³.
* **Kinematic viscosities:** Freshwater $\approx 1.0\times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, Seawater $\approx 1.19\times 10^{-6}$ m²/s at ~20 °C.
* **Hull-type signature:** Tanker = high $C_B$, warship = low $C_B$; container = moderate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic (for exam day):**
> **"L-B-T-B-P-M"** → **L**ength, **B**readth, **T**draft, **B**lock, **P**rismatic, **M**idship coefficients.
> **"C-B = C-P times C-M"** — the golden identity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Section Shape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST501 - SHIP DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Section Shape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In ship hydrodynamics and naval architecture, the **Section Shape** (also called the **Hull Cross-Section** or **Body Plan Section**) refers to the geometric profile of the ship's hull obtained by cutting the vessel vertically with a transverse plane (perpendicular to the centerline and to the base plane) at any specific longitudinal station along the ship's length.

Each section shape is characterized by its:
- **Section Area (A)**: The area enclosed within the section's waterplane cut, measured in m².
- **Beam at Section (B_x)**: The maximum transverse width at that station.
- **Draught at Section (T_x)**: The vertical depth from the keel to the waterline at that station.
- **Section Coefficient (C_x)**: The ratio of the actual submerged section area to the area of its circumscribing rectangle.

The collection of all these sections from the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)** to the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** forms the **Body Plan** of the ship, which is the fundamental blueprint defining hull form.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Sliced Loaf" Concept)

Imagine you take a long **baguette** and slice it into 20 equal cross-sections. Now, lay each slice flat on a table. If you arranged the slices in a row from biggest to smallest, you would get a profile that looks just like a ship's hull from the side.

However, the **shape of each individual slice** (round, oval, flat-bottomed, etc.) is what naval architects call the **Section Shape**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Section Shape Matters in Ship Dynamics:**
> The section shape governs how water flows around the hull. A **rounded U-section** slices through waves smoothly (low resistance), while a **sharp V-section** (typical of destroyers) cuts through waves efficiently at high speed, sacrificing cargo space for hydrodynamic sharpness.

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Section Shapes

| Shape Type | Profile Description | Typical Vessel |
|------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **U-Section** | Flat-sided, wide bottom, rounded bilge radius | Tankers, Bulk Carriers |
| **V-Section** | Sharp, narrow bottom, sloping sides | High-speed Frigates, Patrol Boats |
| **Round Bilge** | Smooth, continuous curve from keel to deck edge | Most Merchant Ships |
| **Hard Chine** | Sharp intersection between bottom and side | Planing Crafts, SWATH vessels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Section shape is studied as a primary design variable influencing **wave-making resistance**, **added mass**, **form drag**, and **damping coefficients** in the equations of ship motion. The midship section coefficient **C_m** is the single most diagnostic section shape parameter.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**: Distance between forward and aft perpendiculars, in **meters (m)**.
- **Design Waterline (DWL)**: The waterline at the ship's **design draught** where section properties are computed.
- **Block Coefficient (C_B)**: Ratio of displacement volume to LBP × B × T (dimensionless).
- **Midship Coefficient (C_M)**: Ratio of midship section area to B × T (dimensionless).
- **Prismatic Coefficient (C_P)**: Ratio of displacement volume to midship section area × LBP (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Section Shape Analysis

### Pillar 1: The Midship Section Coefficient (C_M)
This is the single most important parameter describing section shape. It quantifies how "full" or "fine" the midship section is.

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

- **High C_M (> 0.95)**: Very full U-section, typical of **tankers** → high cargo capacity, higher frictional resistance.
- **Low C_M (< 0.70)**: Fine V-section, typical of **warships** → lower resistance, better seakeeping, reduced capacity.

### Pillar 2: The Prismatic Coefficient (C_P)
Describes the **longitudinal distribution** of the displacement, i.e., how the section area changes from bow to stern.

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot A_M}$$

- **High C_P (> 0.70)**: Full-bodied hull with parallel middle body (e.g., tankers).
- **Low C_P (< 0.55)**: Fine ends with sharp entry and run (e.g., container ships, naval vessels).

### Pillar 3: The Block Coefficient (C_B)
The most commonly cited overall fullness coefficient, combining both shape and distribution.

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

### 2.2 The Fundamental Block–Prismatic–Midship Relationship

By simple algebraic substitution, these three coefficients are linked through the **master equation**:

$$C_B = C_M \times C_P$$

This is a foundational relationship frequently tested in KTU examinations.

## 2.2.1 Why This Relationship Exists — The Logic Chain

Step 1: Write the displacement volume $\nabla$ as the integral of all section areas along the ship's length.

$$\nabla = \int_0^{L} A(x) \, dx$$

Step 2: Approximate the integral using the **mean of all section areas**, which equals the product of midship area and the prismatic coefficient.

$$\nabla = L_{BP} \cdot A_M \cdot C_P$$

Step 3: Substitute the midship area using $A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T$.

$$\nabla = L_{BP} \cdot (C_M \cdot B \cdot T) \cdot C_P$$

Step 4: Divide by the circumscribing rectangular volume $L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$.

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} = C_M \times C_P$$

## 2.3 Section Area Curve (SAC) — The Visual DNA of Hull Form

The **Section Area Curve** is a plot of the submerged cross-sectional area $A(x)$ against the longitudinal position $x$ from AP to FP.

- The **area under the SAC** equals the **displaced volume** $\nabla$.
- The **peak of the SAC** is the **midship area** $A_M$.
- The **slope of the SAC** at the bow indicates **entry sharpness**; at the stern, it indicates **run fullness**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Typical Range | Practical Significance |
|-----------|---------|---------------|-------------------------|
| Midship Coefficient | $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ | **0.60 – 0.98** | Section shape "fullness" |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot A_M)$ | **0.55 – 0.85** | Longitudinal taper |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | **0.40 – 0.90** | Overall fullness |
| Master Identity | $C_B = C_M \cdot C_P$ | Universal | Algebraic linkage |
| Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L_{BP} \cdot B)$ | **0.65 – 0.95** | Initial stability, added mass |
| Longitudinal C.G. Position | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{\nabla} \int_0^L x \cdot A(x) \, dx$ | 0 – 1 (from AP) | Trim, resistance balance |
| Section Area at Station $x$ | $A(x) = C_x \cdot B_x \cdot T_x$ | 0 – A_M | Hull form definition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
